title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane

## Status
Published (ADR-017)

## Decision summary
Retention policies are evaluated in the **control plane**. Data plane components (log/telemetry stores, purge jobs, exporters) consume a **resolved “effective policy snapshot”** produced by the control plane and do not independently recompute precedence/validation rules.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] Retention policies are evaluated in the **control plane**. Data plane components (log/telemetry stores, purge jobs, exporters) consume a **resolved “effective policy snapshot”** produced by the control plane and do not independently recompute precedence/validation rules.


We will:
- Centralize evaluation, precedence rules, and validation in the control plane (Retention Policy Service + evaluator module).
- Cache effective policy snapshots in the control plane (in-memory + Redis) with explicit invalidation on policy changes.
- Make policy changes and evaluation outputs auditable via immutable change history and audit log events.

## Context
The “Retention controls + compliance reporting pack” program requires consistent enforcement of customer-configurable retention policies across multiple data classes and deployment modes (Hosted / Dedicated / Private). The platform includes multiple storage systems and pipelines (request/response logs, audit logs, traces/metrics, artifacts) where TTL enforcement is implemented via scheduled purge jobs and/or native TTL features.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] The “Retention controls + compliance reporting pack” program requires consistent enforcement of customer-configurable retention policies across multiple data classes and deployment modes (Hosted / Dedicated / Private). The platform includes multiple storage systems and pipelines (request/response logs, audit logs, traces/metrics, artifacts) where TTL enforcement is implemented via scheduled purge jobs and/or native TTL features.


Key requirements:
- Consistent behavior and terminology (TTL, purge semantics, legal hold, exceptions).
- Strong auditability for customer security reviews and internal audits.
- Operational safety: idempotent purge jobs, observable lag, and safe rollouts.
- Avoid duplicate logic across many services and minimize drift in precedence rules.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] - Avoid duplicate logic across many services and minimize drift in precedence rules.


## Problem statement
Where should retention policies be evaluated and resolved into enforceable settings?

Two broad options:
1) Evaluate in the data plane (each service/store computes applicable policy based on tenant + region + data class + overrides).
2) Evaluate in the control plane (one canonical evaluation engine; data plane consumes effective results).

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] 2) Evaluate in the control plane (one canonical evaluation engine; data plane consumes effective results).


## Drivers
- **Auditability:** Single source of truth for “what was the policy at time T?”
- **Consistency:** Avoid divergent implementations of precedence rules.
- **Performance and reliability:** Data plane should be lightweight; control plane must scale and avoid hot paths.
- **Change safety:** Policy changes must be tracked, reviewed, and rolled out predictably.
- **Compliance reporting:** Report pack needs authoritative exports of current and historical retention config.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: Option A: Evaluate in data plane
text:
[...] - **Compliance reporting:** Report pack needs authoritative exports of current and historical retention config.


## Considered options
### Option A: Evaluate in data plane
Each store/purge job would fetch raw policy objects and compute effective values locally.

Pros:
- Reduced dependency on control plane for effective policy reads.
- Potentially simpler request path if policy is needed inline.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: Option B: Evaluate in control plane (chosen)
text:
[...] - Potentially simpler request path if policy is needed inline.


Cons:
- Multiple implementations of precedence/validation (high drift risk).
- Harder to audit and reproduce effective policy at time T across services.
- Increased blast radius when rules change.

### Option B: Evaluate in control plane (chosen)
A centralized evaluator resolves raw policy + defaults + overrides into an effective snapshot. Data plane uses the snapshot.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] A centralized evaluator resolves raw policy + defaults + overrides into an effective snapshot. Data plane uses the snapshot.


Pros:
- Canonical evaluation behavior; lower drift.
- Central audit trail and reproducible effective policy at time T.
- Enables standardized exports for compliance reporting.

Cons:
- Control plane becomes a dependency for policy distribution.
- Requires caching/invalidation and resilience plan for outages.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: The control plane evaluator is responsible for
text:
[...] - Requires caching/invalidation and resilience plan for outages.


## Decision
We choose **Option B**: evaluate retention policies in the **control plane**.

### What “evaluation” includes
The control plane evaluator is responsible for:
- Validation of policy inputs (min/max TTL by tier, data-class support, region constraints).
- Precedence rules across:
  - Product-tier defaults (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
  - Tenant-level policy
  - Data class overrides (e.g., request_logs vs audit_logs)
  - Region overrides (when supported)
  - Legal hold and approved exceptions
- Computing a normalized effective representation used by enforcement systems.
- Producing a stable export format for compliance packages (see ADR-018).

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: What is *not* evaluated in the data plane
text:
[...] - Producing a stable export format for compliance packages (see ADR-018).


### What is *not* evaluated in the data plane
Data plane services do not:
- Interpret tier defaults.
- Apply precedence rules.
- Decide whether an override is valid.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] - Decide whether an override is valid.


Data plane services only:
- Read an effective snapshot (or receive it via config distribution).
- Enforce TTL/purge according to the effective values.

## Effective policy snapshot
### Snapshot characteristics

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: The effective snapshot is a read-optimized, explicit representation that answers
text:
[...] ## Effective policy snapshot
### Snapshot characteristics

The effective snapshot is a read-optimized, explicit representation that answers:
- For this tenant + region, what is the retention TTL per data class?
- Are there holds/exceptions that block deletion for a data class?
- When was this computed and from which raw config version?

Minimum fields (conceptual; exact schema tracked in the policy service doc):
- tenant_id
- region (or “global”)
- snapshot_version (monotonic)
- policy_revision_id (raw policy revision)
- generated_at
- data_classes: effective_ttl_days per class
- holds: active holds and scope (data class, time window where applicable)
- notes: warnings (e.g., disabled logging mode for a class)

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: Data classes referenced in evaluation must match Security terminology
text:
[...] - notes: warnings (e.g., disabled logging mode for a class)


### Data class alignment
Data classes referenced in evaluation must match Security terminology:
- request_logs (request/response logs)
- prompts_and_completions (only if explicitly stored; may be disabled by configuration)
- audit_logs
- traces
- metrics
- artifacts (e.g., eval datasets where applicable)

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] - artifacts (e.g., eval datasets where applicable)


## Caching strategy
We cache effective snapshots in the control plane to avoid recomputation and reduce latency.

### Cache layers
1) **Process-local cache** in the Retention Policy Service (fast path)
- Key: (tenant_id, region)
- TTL: 30 seconds
- Eviction: LRU bounded by tenant count

2) **Redis distributed cache** (shared across service replicas)
- Key: retention:effective:{tenant_id}:{region}:{policy_revision_id}
- TTL: 10 minutes
- Negative caching: 60 seconds for “no policy configured” (tenant uses defaults)

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: Invalidation is event-driven with TTL backstop
text:
[...] - Negative caching: 60 seconds for “no policy configured” (tenant uses defaults)


### Cache invalidation
Invalidation is event-driven with TTL backstop:
- On policy create/update/delete OR hold/exception change:
  - Increment policy_revision_id
  - Publish invalidation event (internal bus topic: retention.policy.changed)
  - Evict local cache entries for affected tenant_id across replicas
  - Allow Redis entries to expire naturally OR evict by prefix when feasible

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...]   - Allow Redis entries to expire naturally OR evict by prefix when feasible


### Consistency model
- Effective snapshot reads are **eventually consistent** with target propagation under 60 seconds.
- Purge jobs must tolerate a short propagation delay and rely on verification metrics (see Monitoring).

## Auditability and change history
### Requirements

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: We must be able to answer
text:
[...] ## Auditability and change history
### Requirements

We must be able to answer:
- Who changed retention settings?
- What changed (diff), when, and why (reason/issue link)?
- What was the effective retention at any point in time?

### Control plane audit trail

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: On each change, emit audit events (exportable via the audit-log-exporter)
text:
[...] 
### Control plane audit trail

The Retention Policy Service will:
- Store an immutable revision log for retention policy objects and hold/exception objects.
- Record the actor (user/service), authentication context, source (Console/API/Terraform), and request ID.

### Audit log events
On each change, emit audit events (exportable via the audit-log-exporter):
- retention_policy.created
- retention_policy.updated
- retention_policy.deleted
- retention_hold.created
- retention_hold.released
- retention_exception.approved
- retention_exception.revoked

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] - retention_exception.revoked


Each event must include:
- tenant_id
- policy_revision_id
- actor_id + actor_type
- changed_fields summary
- justification (free text + ticket link when provided)

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] - justification (free text + ticket link when provided)


### Evidence expectations
Compliance report generation will include:
- Current effective snapshot export
- Change history for a requested time window
- Audit log coverage summary of retention change events

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] - Audit log coverage summary of retention change events


## Operational and reliability considerations
### Control plane dependency
Data plane enforcement must not hard-fail if the control plane is temporarily unavailable.

Mechanisms:
- Purge jobs operate on the **last known effective snapshot** persisted locally (per store) with:
  - max_staleness: 24 hours for continuing enforcement
  - alerts if snapshot age > 2 hours (configurable per environment)
- If no snapshot has ever been fetched, jobs must fall back to **secure tier defaults** (favor longer retention vs accidental over-deletion) and emit a high-severity alert.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
section_header: Why “favor longer retention” on missing policy
text:
[...] - If no snapshot has ever been fetched, jobs must fall back to **secure tier defaults** (favor longer retention vs accidental over-deletion) and emit a high-severity alert.


### Why “favor longer retention” on missing policy
Accidental deletion is typically irreversible and creates customer impact. Temporary over-retention is treated as a compliance risk and must be surfaced via alerts and evidence, but is safer than permanent data loss. This posture is explicitly documented in the retention controls overview and risk assessment.

### Performance
Effective snapshot evaluation is not on the request serving path for inference traffic. Enforcement reads occur via scheduled jobs and admin/reporting flows.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] Effective snapshot evaluation is not on the request serving path for inference traffic. Enforcement reads occur via scheduled jobs and admin/reporting flows.


## Security considerations
- Policy write endpoints require Security Admin / Org Admin privileges (RBAC enforced in control plane).
- Hold/exception actions require additional approval workflow as documented in Legal Hold & Exceptions.
- All policy and hold objects are classified as internal configuration; exports for customers must be scoped to their tenant.
- Snapshots must not include sensitive request content; only configuration metadata.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] - Snapshots must not include sensitive request content; only configuration metadata.


## Monitoring and verification
To ensure evaluation + distribution behaves correctly, we will track:
- retention_effective_snapshot_age_seconds{tenant,region}
- retention_policy_propagation_lag_seconds{tenant,region} (time from change event to observed snapshot update in data plane)
- retention_policy_eval_failures_total (by error class: validation, datastore, cache)

Verification dashboards and alerts are defined in the Retention Controls Verification Dashboards page.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] Verification dashboards and alerts are defined in the Retention Controls Verification Dashboards page.


## Consequences
### Positive
- Single, testable evaluation engine and precedence rules.
- Stronger compliance story: reproducible effective policy + audit trail.
- Lower engineering overhead across stores/purge jobs.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] - Lower engineering overhead across stores/purge jobs.


### Negative / tradeoffs
- Requires robust caching/invalidation and snapshot distribution.
- Control plane changes (schema/rules) must be managed carefully with backwards compatibility.

## Implementation notes (non-normative)
- Evaluator module lives with the control plane Retention Policy Service and is used by:
  - Console read views (effective retention display)
  - Export endpoints for compliance pack
  - Data plane snapshot fetch endpoint
- Policy changes must be tagged with a policy_revision_id and persisted before publishing invalidation.
- Data plane consumers should use ETag/If-None-Match semantics to reduce bandwidth and avoid thrash.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] - Data plane consumers should use ETag/If-None-Match semantics to reduce bandwidth and avoid thrash.


## Rollout plan
1) Land evaluator + snapshot endpoint behind feature flag (internal tenants only).
2) Enable for audit_logs and request_logs enforcement stores first.
3) Add traces/metrics and artifact classes once enforcement paths are ready.
4) Turn on compliance pack exports using the same snapshot representation.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
text:
[...] 4) Turn on compliance pack exports using the same snapshot representation.


## Open questions
- Private/on-prem deployments: confirm whether snapshot distribution is pull-based only (recommended) or supports push for restricted networks.
- Region override semantics: confirm which tiers allow per-region differences and how to represent “global” vs explicit region entries.
- Legal hold scope: whether holds can be time-windowed per class in v1 or only “hold all” for a class.

## References
- Retention policy schema and validation rules
- Legal hold and retention exceptions policy
- Data deletion and TTL enforcement standards
- ADR-018: Compliance report pack export format

title: Cohort-driven Shadow Validation Playbook
text:
Cohort-driven Shadow Validation Playbook

Overview:

This playbook describes a repeatable, cohort-driven approach to shadow (non-production) validation that combines traffic mirroring, cohort stratification, and statistical evaluation to detect drift, regressions, or QoE degradations before a model rollout. It is intended for use by ML Engineers, Product Reliability, and SRE when validating model variants (quantized models, finetunes, or new topologies) against real traffic slices.

Goals:
- Expose subtle, slice-specific regressions that aggregate metrics miss.
- Provide deterministic acceptance gates with clear thresholds and statistical tests.
- Keep validation cost-bounded by adaptive sampling and prioritized cohorts.

Scope and Non-Goals:
- Scope: live shadowing and replay for text-generation and embedding endpoints; cohort metrics for latency, token cost, response-stability, and semantic fidelity.
- Non-Goals: synthetic adversarial fuzzing (see separate fuzz playbook) and complete production failover automation.

Definitions:
- Shadow traffic: duplicated requests sent to a candidate model without impacting production responses.
- Cohort: a named subset of traffic defined by request attributes (customer_id, route, prompt-template, language, token_count, session_age).
- Response-stability-index (RSI): composite metric measuring output drift against baseline using embedding cosine similarity, edit distance, and semantic score.

When to run a cohort-driven shadow validation:
- Before a model promotion that affects >5% of production request volume.
- When introducing cross-workload optimizations (e.g., new quant profile, KV-cache changes, batch scheduling).
- After detecting slice-specific degradations in continuous telemetry.

Ownership and Roles:
- Requesting owner: Feature/Model owner (creates cohorts and success criteria).
- Shadow operator: SRE / Infra (sets up mirroring, monitors resource usage).
- Evaluator: Applied ML / Quality (runs statistical tests, interprets slice failures).
- Approver: Product owner or Reliability lead (approves promotion or rollback).

High-level Workflow (stepwise):
1) Define cohorts (3–10 per run):
   - Use historical telemetry to choose meaningful slices (high-value customers, long-context, multimodal inputs).
   - Example cohort definitions: "high-long-context-english" (tokens > 2048), "enterprise-integration-route" (route = /api/v1/generate && customer_tier = enterprise).
2) Choose sampling policy and budget:
   - Default: mirror 10% of cohort traffic for 72 hours or until 5,000 requests per cohort are collected, whichever comes first.
   - For sensitive cohorts, reduce to 1–2% with extended duration.
3) Configure shadow routing (example manifest):
   - Provide network-level mirroring rules and candidate endpoint. See appendix for JSON/YAML snippets.
4) Instrument telemetry and tagging:
   - Ensure every mirrored request is tagged with: cohort_id, shadow_run_id, baseline_model, candidate_model, sample_rate.
   - Log raw prompts, token counts, latency, final cost estimate (tokens * model_cost_rate).
5) Compute per-cohort metrics (real-time and batch):
   - Latency P95 and P99 (ms).
   - Token cost delta (%).
   - RSI (median per cohort) and drift fraction (percent responses with RSI < threshold).
   - Functional correctness checks: reranking loss, presence/absence of required fields for structured outputs.
6) Statistical evaluation and thresholds:
   - For continuous metrics (latency, cost): use bootstrap confidence intervals with 10k resamples. Gate if candidate P99 > baseline P99 + 20% and p < 0.01.
   - For semantic/fidelity metrics (RSI): require median RSI >= baseline median - 0.03 and two-sided Mann-Whitney U test p > 0.05.
   - For categorical regressions (required fields missing): Fisher exact test, require failure rate delta < 0.5 percentage points with 95% CI.
7) Adaptive triage and probe amplification:
   - If a cohort shows borderline failure (0.01 < p < 0.05), automatically increase sampling for that cohort by up to 5x for an additional 24 hours.
8) Runbook actions on failures:
   - Soft fail (slice flagged): pause promotion, notify owners, request focused remediation (prompt-tune, quant-fix, dataset augmentation).
   - Hard fail (critical customer slice degraded or functional correctness violated): immediate rollback path and incident creation.
9) Promotion decision and documentation:
   - Successful run: produce a validation artifact containing per-cohort summaries, raw probe examples (anonymized), and final decision. Attach to release ticket.

Metric and Alerting Configuration (table):
| Metric | Aggregation | Gate | Action on breach | Ownership |
|---|---:|---|---|---:|
| Latency P99 | per-cohort | > baseline + 20% (p<0.01) | Pause promotion, trigger perf triage | SRE |
| Token cost delta | median % | > +15% | Cost review, consider quant profile | Finance/Feature owner |
| RSI median | median similarity | < baseline - 0.03 (MWU p<0.05) | Quality investigation, escalate | Applied ML |
| Required-field loss | failure rate | delta > 0.005 absolute | Immediate rollback for critical routes | Product |

Sampling and Budgeting Guidelines:
- Minimum sample per cohort for significance: 500 requests. If unavailable, mark cohort as "insufficient data" and schedule an extended run.
- Budget cap per run: token budget defined in run config (default 10M tokens). Operator may truncate tests if budget exhausted and annotate results.

Data Privacy and Redaction:
- Always redact PII before storing mirrored requests. Use the pipeline redaction rules: replace emails, SSNs, API keys, and long identifiers with deterministic tokens.
- For enterprise customers with confidentiality flags, request explicit authorization before mirroring; otherwise exclude those cohorts.

Operational Playbook (quick steps):
1) Create run ticket with cohort definitions and success criteria.
2) Request SRE to enable mirror manifest with run_id and sample_rate.
3) Confirm telemetry tags are present in the mirror stream.
4) Validate that baseline and candidate endpoints are reachable.
5) Monitor dashboards (per-cohort) for first 6 hours. If any hard fails occur, follow hard-fail incident checklist.

Promotions, Canary Integration and Rollout Path:
- If all cohorts pass: promote candidate to canary (5–10% production, canary duration 48–96 hours) with continuous cohort monitoring.
- If a small number of low-impact cohorts fail: consider targeted mitigations (prompt templates, per-route fallback) and rerun shadow validation.

Appendix A — Example Shadow Routing YAML:
```yaml
shadow_run_id: cohort-shadow-2025-09
mirror_rules:
  - route: /v1/generate
    sample_rate: 0.1
    cohorts: [high-long-context-english, enterprise-integration-route]
candidate_endpoint: https://shadow-candidate.internal:8443/generate
baseline_endpoint: https://prod-model.internal:8443/generate
storage: s3://redwood-shadow-archive/cohort-shadow-2025-09/
```

Appendix B — Example cohort SQL (for data-team):
```
SELECT request_id, customer_id, route, token_count, language, timestamp
FROM request_logs
WHERE token_count > 2048 AND language = 'en' AND timestamp BETWEEN '{{start}}' AND '{{end}}';
```

Appendix C — CLI snippets:
- Start run: redwood-shadow run start --id cohort-shadow-2025-09 --manifest ./shadow.yaml
- Increase sampling for cohort: redwood-shadow run update --id cohort-shadow-2025-09 --cohort high-long-context-english --sample-rate 0.5
- Archive results: redwood-shadow artifacts bundle --id cohort-shadow-2025-09 --out ./artifacts.tar.gz

Troubleshooting common issues:
- Low sample counts: increase sample_rate for affected cohorts, extend window, or create synthetic probe generator limited to the target cohort patterns.
- High resource usage on candidate host: coordinate with SRE to throttle mirror throughput or scale candidate pool; prioritize critical cohorts.
- Inconsistent telemetry tags: stop the run, patch instrumentation, and restart to ensure validity of per-cohort aggregates.

Audit and Reporting:
- Every shadow run must attach an artifact containing anonymized examples (<=100 per failed cohort), a per-cohort metric CSV, and the approval decision.
- Maintain a run index (spreadsheet or internal DB) with run_id, start/end times, outcome, and link to artifacts for 12 months.

Notes and rationale:
- Cohort-driven validation focuses our signal-to-noise on high-value slices rather than aggregate metrics that can mask regressions. Adaptive sampling reduces cost while enabling focused amplification where it matters. Statistical gates balance sensitivity and false positive rates to avoid unnecessary rollbacks.

Contact and escalation:
- Primary owner: Maya Chen (applied-ml)
- SRE shadow contact: Diego Alvarez
- On-call escalation: Pager duty "model-eval-oncall"

End of playbook.

title: Probe Pruning and Adaptive Sampling Playbook
text:
Probe Pruning and Adaptive Sampling Playbook

Summary

This playbook defines the probe pruning and adaptive sampling system used by the Eval Harness to keep continuous evaluations cost-effective while preserving signal for regression detection. It formalizes: probe scoring, pruning thresholds, an adaptive sampler that reallocates budget to high-variance slices, scheduler policies for burst/steady modes, and a safe rollout path for any changes.

Motivation

Large-scale continuous evaluation produces a long tail of low-value probes (very stable slices, duplicated semantics, or highly correlated prompts) that consume budget and increase noise in alerting. By pruning probes and adaptively sampling, we achieve:
- 30–60% reduction in probe execution cost for stable workloads
- Faster regression detection on volatile slices
- Lower false-positive alert rates from noisy low-variance probes

Scope

title: Probe Pruning and Adaptive Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] Scope


This document applies to:
- The Eval Harness scheduler (service: eval-sequencer)
- Probe catalog artifacts (probe registry and metadata)
- Continuous monitors and alerting rules tied to eval-sentinels

Not in scope: manual one-off experiments, offline research-curated benchmark runs.

Definitions

- Probe: a single stimulus (prompt + context) executed against a model variant and recorded with outputs and telemetry.
- Slice: logical grouping of probes (e.g., language, intent, length bucket).
- Stability score: running statistic (0..1) where 1 == perfectly stable across N recent runs.
- Importance score: business-weight multiplier (0.1..10) assigned by owners.

High-level architecture

1) Probe registry enriches probes with base metadata (slice, created_at, owner_team, importance).
2) Eval-sequencer assigns probes to worker queues according to adaptive-sample policy.
3) Pruner service periodically marks probes as "prune_candidate" based on scoring rules and retention config.
4) Audit logger records all prune actions and supports rehydration for 90 days.

Key metrics and thresholds (recommended defaults)

- Metric: Stability score (rolling 30-day window)
  - Thresholds: stable if >= 0.98, semi-stable if 0.95–0.98, unstable if < 0.95
- Metric: Sample variance (per-slice)
  - Action: reallocate +20% budget to slices with variance in top 10%
- Metric: Duplicate similarity (embeddings cosine)
  - Prune if cosine >= 0.995 to a higher-ranked probe in same slice
- Minimum retained probes per slice: 50 (unless slice cardinality < 50)

Pruning ruleset (ordered)

1) Structural deduplication: within a slice, group probes with embedding cosine >= 0.995 and keep the highest-importance/most-recent item.
2) Stability floor: for probes with stability >= 0.99 and age > 21 days, mark prune_candidate.
3) Low-importance culling: importance <= 0.2 and stability >= 0.97 -> prune if slice has > minimum retained probes.
4) Manual holdouts: probes with flag "do_not_prune" are preserved.

Adaptive sampling algorithm (overview)

The adaptive sampler reallocates probe budget per scheduling epoch (default 24h) based on per-slice signal. Steps:
- Compute per-slice priority = mean(importance * max(1, variance_score * 10)) * recent_violation_factor
- Normalize priorities to available budget (tokens / probe cost estimate)
- Ensure hard floor: every slice must receive >= 1% of epoch budget unless explicitly disabled

title: Probe Pruning and Adaptive Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] - Ensure hard floor: every slice must receive >= 1% of epoch budget unless explicitly disabled


Pseudocode:

for each epoch:
  load slices S
  for s in S:
    compute importance_s, variance_s, violation_factor_s
    priority_s = importance_s * max(1, variance_s * 10) * violation_factor_s
  scale priorities so sum(priority) == epoch_budget
  assign probes to queue respecting per-slice minimums and per-probe dedup constraints

Scheduler policies

- Steady mode: evenly distributed probes across 24h with jitter; used for baselining.
- Burst mode: directed execution for top-K priority slices when on-call triage requests immediate re-eval or after new model rollout. Burst windows capped at 2h and require SRE approval.
- Canary mode: small fraction (default 2%) of new probe templates are exercised for 72h before being added to main catalog.

Configuration example (YAML snippet)

title: Probe Pruning and Adaptive Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] Configuration example (YAML snippet)


# eval-sampler-config.yaml
epoch_period: 24h
min_slice_budget_pct: 0.01
min_probes_per_slice: 50
stability_window_days: 30
dedup_cosine_threshold: 0.995
burst_mode_max_window: 2h
canary_fraction: 0.02
prune_scan_interval: 48h
prune_retention_days: 90

Rollout and change control

title: Probe Pruning and Adaptive Sampling Playbook
section_header: Any change to pruning thresholds or adaptive sampler weights must follow this path
text:
[...] Rollout and change control


Any change to pruning thresholds or adaptive sampler weights must follow this path:
1) Draft change proposal in the eval-harness repo with benchmarks (description, config diff, expected savings/risks).
2) Submit PR and tag reviewers from applied-ml-and-evals and eng-sre.
3) Run controlled experiment (A/B) for 7 days: 10% traffic on new config vs 90% control. Record: detection latency for seeded regressions, cost delta, alert noise.
4) If experiment meets pass criteria (<=5% degradation in detection latency, >=10% cost improvement, no increase in high-severity alerts), schedule staged rollout: 10% -> 30% -> 60% -> 100% over 7 days with monitoring gates.
5) Post-rollout retrospective and update playbook if necessary.

Pass/fail gate checklist for A/B

- Regression detection latency: no more than 5% worse than control
- Alert precision on top-10 slices: not decreased by >3 percentage points
- Cost per epoch: >=10% improvement expected; if less, require product sign-off
- No increase in customer-facing incidents attributed to eval changes

Operational runbook (quick actions)

- If critical slice goes missing from schedule:
  1) Query pruner audit logs: "pruner.events?probe_id=<id>"
  2) Rehydrate probe from archival store (rehydrator CLI): "rehydrator restore --probe <id> --reason emergency"
  3) Temporarily mark slice as "holdout" in probe registry and set min_slice_budget_pct to 0.05
- If adaptive sampler is starving a high-importance slice:
  1) Pause prune job: "kubectl scale deployment/pruner --replicas=0 -n eval"
  2) Manually bump importance for affected slice in registry UI or via API
  3) Re-run epoch with debug logging enabled and capture schedule plan

Observability and alerts

title: Probe Pruning and Adaptive Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] Observability and alerts


Monitor these dashboards and alerts:
- Dashboard: Eval Sampling Overview (latency, cost, per-slice budget allocation)
- Alert: eval-sampler:budget-deviation > 25% for 2 epochs -> paged to applied-ml-oncall
- Alert: prune:unexpected-retention-drop when retained_probes_count < 60% expected -> pager
- Alert: canary-failures: > 3 seeded-regressions missed in 72h -> paged to product-owner and applied-ml-oncall

Data retention and auditability

- Pruner decisions are logged in audit store for 90 days.
- Full probe artifacts (inputs, outputs, embeddings) retained for 365 days for investigations.
- Rehydration API supports re-executing archived probes against historical models for reproducibility.

Safety & false-positive mitigation

- Never prune a probe used in a currently open incident ticket.
- Prioritize retention of probes tagged as regulatory or compliance-critical.
- For any prune action that would drop a probe with importance >= 5, require two approvers (applied-ml lead + product).

Troubleshooting checklist

- Symptom: sudden drop in per-slice probe count
  - Check prune job logs for recent sweeps and decisions.
  - Verify dedup threshold change in config history.
- Symptom: missed seeded regression in canary
  - Inspect canary fraction; confirm canary traffic was delivered.
  - Increase canary_fraction to 5% for next experiment if flakiness persists.

Ownership and contacts

- System owner: applied-ml-and-evals team (oncall: applied-ml-oncall rotation)
- Pruner service owner: runtime-quality squad (primary: Marcus Nguyen)
- Scheduler owner: eval-platform squad (primary: Aisha Patel)
- SRE escalation: eng-sre oncall (see runbook for page routing)

Appendices

A. Example dedup query (CLI)

title: Probe Pruning and Adaptive Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] A. Example dedup query (CLI)


# Find duplicates within slice=faq
probe-embeddings search --slice faq --similarity-threshold 0.995 --output csv | head -n 50

B. Example schedule inspection API

GET /api/v1/scheduler/plan?epoch=2026-02-09T00:00:00Z
Response fields: slice_id, assigned_probes, estimated_cost_tokens, priority_score

C. Change log
- 2025-11-12: Initial draft and internal review (Priya Shah)
- 2026-01-05: Added burst/canary policies and safe rollout gates (Liam O'Connor)
- 2026-02-10: Tightened dedup cosine default to 0.995 and clarified rehydration retention (Sofia Martinez)

If you have questions, file an issue in the eval-harness repo and tag @applied-ml-and-evals.

title: Evaluator Backpressure and Priority Fairness Playbook
text:
Evaluator Backpressure and Priority Fairness Playbook

Overview

This playbook documents the design and operational procedures for the distributed evaluator backpressure subsystem and priority-fairness scheduler used by Redwood's Eval Mesh. It targets evaluator-sidecar and orchestrator components responsible for running probes, scoring outputs, and returning telemetry while respecting product SLAs and evaluator resource constraints.

Goals
- Prevent evaluator overload during spikes in probe load or slow downstream services.
- Ensure tenant and slice priority classes receive fair shares of evaluation budget.
- Provide deterministic and observable behavior for on-call engineers.
- Make graceful degradation decisions (deferred probes, sampled execution, degraded fidelity) explicit and reversible.

Scope
- Applies to: eval-orchestrator, evaluator-sidecar, probe-priority-agent, and the probe-queue services running in the Eval Mesh.
- Not in scope: probe generation policies and annotation pipelines (handled by Probe Fabrication).

Background and Motivation
- We run heterogeneous probes (shadow traffic, synthetic seeds, long-context functioncall tests) across a fleet of evaluators. Peaks (e.g., model rollouts, heavy shadow validation) can saturate CPU/GPU and cause unexpected increases in latency and cost.
- Previous incidents (see incident-2025-eval-thundering.json) showed cascading failures when thousands of long-context probes simultaneously retried after transient KV-cache warmup failures.

Design Principles
1) Observable decisions: every backpressure action must emit an event with reasoning tags (reason, affected_slice, priority_class, resource_util).
2) Predictable fairness: priority classes map to weight buckets; allocation follows weighted fair queuing with bounded latency targets.
3) Multi-tier degradation: prefer fidelity reduction (e.g., use draft evaluator config -> sample fewer seeds) before dropping high-priority probes.
4) Recovery-first rollbacks: automated scaling/backpressure policies must be paired with an explicit rollback and a health-check gating policy.

Key Terms
- priority_class: {gold, silver, bronze, infra}.
- probe_token: logical unit charged against a probe budget.
- soft_limit: policy threshold that triggers degradation; not an immediate reject.
- hard_limit: reject threshold requiring immediate shedding.

Architecture and Components
- eval-orchestrator: central controller that assigns probes to evaluator pools and updates policies via /api/v1/policy-sync.
- evaluator-sidecar: local agent on evaluator hosts that enforces per-host queueing, backpressure decisions, and emits telemetry to eval-metrics-service.
- probe-queue: Redis-backed sharded queue used for short-lived probe bursts.
- probe-priority-agent: service that converts business priority into scheduling weights and budgets.

SLOs and Operational Thresholds
- Probe completion SLO (P95): 2.5s for short probes (<512 tokens), 95s for long probes (>=4096 tokens) in gold class for hosted evaluation.
- Queue wait soft_limit: host CPU util >= 70% or queue length > 250 -> trigger soft degradation.
- Queue wait hard_limit: host CPU util >= 92% or queue length > 1000 -> reject non-infra probes.
- Backpressure event emission: 100% of decisions must emit to eval-metrics-service with keys: decision_type, priority_class, queue_len, cpu%, mem%.

Priority Class Weights (default)
- gold: weight 8
- silver: weight 4
- bronze: weight 1
- infra: weight 12 (reserved for infra-critical probes)

Backpressure Strategy (high level)
1) Admission Control: evaluator-sidecar applies per-priority leaky-bucket admitting tokens at rate = base_rate * weight.
2) Soft Degradation: when soft_limit hit, execute in this order:
   a) Reduce sample rate by 50% for bronze/silver probes.
   b) Convert >1K-token long probes to trimmed probes (context pruning rules applied).
   c) De-prioritize functioncall-heavy probes by requeueing with lower routing priority.
3) Hard Reject: when hard_limit triggered, reject bronze probes first, then silver; gold and infra should remain unless host is dangerously saturated.
4) Circuit Breaker for downstream dependencies: if downstream scorer latency > 2x baseline for 30s, temporarily pause new probes that require that scorer and route to fallback scorer.

Detailed Procedure: Host-side Enforcement (evaluator-sidecar)
1) Monitor: collect per-host CPU%, GPU util (if present), queue_length, avg_probe_latency (sliding 1m).
2) Evaluate thresholds in order: if cpu% >= 92% OR queue_length > 1000 -> hard_limit path. Else if cpu% >= 70% OR queue_length > 250 -> soft_limit path.
3) Soft Limit Actions (atomic):
   - Emit event: POST /telemetry/backpressure with payload: {host,reason,queue_len,priority_sampled}.
   - Apply adaptive sampler: for priority_class in [bronze, silver], set sample_rate = max(0.1, current * 0.5).
   - Trim contexts: call local priming microservice to apply configured context_trim_rules (see config table).
4) Hard Limit Actions (atomic):
   - Reject policy: return HTTP 429 with X-Backpressure-Reason header.
   - Requeue infra probes to dedicated infra pool when possible.
   - Trigger autoscaler webhook: POST /ops/autoscale-requests with reason "evaluator_hard_limit".

title: Evaluator Backpressure and Priority Fairness Playbook
text:
[...]    - Requeue infra probes to dedicated infra pool when possible.
   - Trigger autoscaler webhook: POST /ops/autoscale-requests with reason "evaluator_hard_limit".

Configuration Matrix (reference)
| config_key | default | description | recommended change for model-rollout |
|------------|---------|-------------|------------------------------------|
| host_soft_cpu_pct | 70 | host-level soft CPU threshold | +5 during slow rollout warmup |
| host_hard_cpu_pct | 92 | hard limit for host-level CPU | unchanged |
| queue_soft_len | 250 | soft queue length | lower to 200 for high-contention tenants |
| queue_hard_len | 1000 | hard queue length | unchanged |
| base_rate_per_weight | 10 probes/s | base admission rate per weight | increase in steady-state after scaling |

Examples: CLI and Policy YAML
- Query current host backpressure state
  $ rmeshctl host-status --host eval-host-12 --format json

- Example policy (eval-orchestrator/policies/prod-priority-policy.yaml)
  ---
  priority_weights:
    gold: 8
    silver: 4
    bronze: 1
    infra: 12
  host_soft_cpu_pct: 70
  host_hard_cpu_pct: 92
  queue_soft_len: 250
  queue_hard_len: 1000
  base_rate_per_weight: 10

Telemetry and Dashboards
- Metrics emitted: eval.backpressure.decisions.count, eval.backpressure.rejected.count, eval.queue.length, eval.latency.p95.short_probes, eval.priority.share.
- Alert rules (SRE):
  - P1: eval.backpressure.rejected.count > 500 in 5m -> Incident (pager)
  - P2: eval.queue.length.avg > 750 for 15m -> Slack alert (#eval-ops)
  - P3: eval.priority.share.gold < 60% for 10m when gold load > 1000 -> Ops review.

Runbook: On-call Steps for P1
1) Acknowledge pager and open incident channel.
2) Query eval-orchestrator /api/v1/pools to identify affected pools.
3) Run: rmeshctl drain-pool --pool-id <pool> --grace 120s to stop new probes to pool.
4) If root cause is autoscaler lag, trigger manual scaling: POST /ops/autoscale-requests with scale_delta +2 nodes.
5) If immediate relief required, flip policy to emergency-priority-policy (feature flag) which: reduces bronze sample to 5% and routes gold to dedicated pool.
6) Record decision in incident timeline and emit retrospective tags for regression triage.

Operational Play Scenarios
- Gradual Model Rollout: pre-warm evaluators for 10m, then ramp probes at 10% per minute while observability confirms p95 latency < SLO. If soft_limit hits, pause ramp and investigate probe-type mix.
- Sudden Spike (Third-party scorer outage): circuit-break scorer calls; route light-weight scoring fallback; mark affected probes with tag scorer_fallback=true for later replay.

Testing and Validation
- Unit tests for admission controller deterministic behavior across weight combinations.
- Chaos tests: inject evaluator-sidecar CPU pressure and verify soft->hard transitions are logged and observable.
- Shadow rollouts: deploy new backpressure policy to 10% of evaluators for 48h before full rollout.

Rollout Checklist
1) Create policy PR in eval-orchestrator (include traffic simulation results).
2) LGTMs from reviewers (Evan Morris, Lina Fernández).
3) Canary deploy to canary-pool (10% fleet) and monitor priority_share, rejected.count, p95 latency for 48h.
4) Gradual ramp (25% -> 50% -> 100%) with 12h observation windows.
5) Post-rollout audit and update playbook with lessons learned.

Ownership and Contacts
- Policy owners: Priya Khandelwal (applied-ml), Marcus Li (infra).
- On-call rotation: eval-oncall (see roster).
- Escalation: if autoscaler fails to add capacity within 15m, escalate to infra-sre lead (pager).

title: Evaluator Backpressure and Priority Fairness Playbook
text:
[...] - On-call rotation: eval-oncall (see roster).
- Escalation: if autoscaler fails to add capacity within 15m, escalate to infra-sre lead (pager).

Appendices
A. Context trimming rules (example)
- remove backreference sentences beyond 1024 tokens unless anchor markers exist.
- collapse multi-turn persona prompts to last 2 turns for trimmed probes.

B. Event schema (telemetry/backpressure)
- decision_type: soft_degrade | hard_reject | sampler_adjust | route_shift
- reason: cpu | queue_len | downstream_latency
- priority_class: gold | silver | bronze | infra
- host: hostname
- queue_len: integer
- cpu_pct: float

C. FAQ
Q: How do we treat long-context (>16k) probes during soft-limit?
A: Prefer trimming with context_trim_rules and, if trimming insufficient, sample at 10% and enqueue for background replay once load decreases.

Document History
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Khandelwal.
- 2026-02-20: Added runbook steps and CLI examples after triage of incident-2026-eval-spike.

Related reading
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/operational-probe-economics-and-priority-contract-2026.json
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/telemetry-driven-prompt-surgeon-and-repair-playbook-2025.json

title: Operational Eval Sweep and Prioritization Framework
text:
Operational Eval Sweep and Prioritization Framework

SUMMARY
This document describes the Operational Eval Sweep and Prioritization Framework (OESPF), an opinionated set of processes and configuration conventions to run continuous, cost-aware evaluation sweeps across production workloads, quantized artifacts, and prompt perturbations. The framework balances risk (quality regressions) and cost (eval compute) with clear prioritization and automated handoffs into regression triage.

Scope
- Applies to eval orchestration performed by eval-orchestrator and probe-farm for hosted, dedicated, and private model variants.
- Covers sweep definition, priority scoring, scheduling policies, telemetry collection, alert thresholds, and integration points for triage/runbooks.
- Not a replacement for unit-level evals or offline researcher experiments.

Goals
1) Surface high-impact regressions early with minimal eval spend.
2) Ensure repeatable priority decisions across workloads and teams.
3) Provide deterministic handoffs to the regression-triage playbooks when thresholds are exceeded.

Definitions
- Sweep: a single, parameterized pass of probes (a set of prompts + seeds) run against a model or artifact.
- Probe: individual synthetic or sampled request used for evaluation.
- Priority Score: numeric score (0-100) used to order sweeps for execution.

Sweep types and use cases
1) Smoke Sweep (high-priority, low-cost): 50 probes per workload; run on every model push to hosted. Purpose: fast fail detection.
2) Sensitivity Sweep (medium-priority): targeted perturbation families (paraphrase, tokenization noise, slot-shuffle); 200 probes. Purpose: detect semantic instability and tokenization sensitivity.
3) Quant-sensitivity Sweep (high-cost, targeted): runs only for artifacts flagged by quant-profile changes; includes per-layer microbenchmarks and 1k prompt subset.
4) Regression Probe Sweep (on-demand): triggered by telemetry anomaly or customer report; merges production traces with synthetic probes.

Priority scoring algorithm (example)
Priority = round(min(100, 0.4*Risk + 0.3*CustomerImpact + 0.2*ArtifactChangeScore + 0.1*ResourceUrgency))

title: Operational Eval Sweep and Prioritization Framework
text:
[...] Priority scoring algorithm (example)
Priority = round(min(100, 0.4*Risk + 0.3*CustomerImpact + 0.2*ArtifactChangeScore + 0.1*ResourceUrgency))

Where:
- Risk: model lineage delta score (0-100) from model-watcher.
- CustomerImpact: weighted by active routes using model (0-100).
- ArtifactChangeScore: 0-100 based on quantization delta or kernel changes.
- ResourceUrgency: incremental based on backlog age and SLA commitments.

Scheduling policies
- Real-time queue: priority >= 80 -> scheduled within 30 minutes.
- Batch queue: priority 40-79 -> scheduled during next nightly slot.
- Low queue: priority < 40 -> opportunistic execution when idle.
- Quotas: team-level daily compute budgets enforced by eval-orchestrator.

Config schema (YAML example)
```yaml
sweep_name: sensitivity-2026-10
model_variant: redwood/gptx-7b-q4f
type: sensitivity
probe_count: 200
priority_overrides:
  customer_critical: true
schedulers:
  queue: batch
  retry_policy:
    max_attempts: 2
    backoff: exponential
stability_checks:
  coherence_threshold: 0.88
  semantic_shift_alert_delta: 0.05
telemetry_exports:
  prometheus_metrics: [eval_duration_seconds, probe_failure_count, response_coherence]
  storage: s3://eval-results/prod-sweeps/
```

Concrete thresholds and SLOs
- Eval completion SLO: 99th percentile eval_duration_seconds < 300s for real-time queue.
- Alerting rule: raise a "sweep-regression" when response_coherence drops by >= 5% relative to baseline AND semantic_shift >= 0.04 on >= 10 probe slices.
- Escalation: Critical if customer_impact >= 80 and quality delta >= 3% on high-traffic routes.

Telemetry and observability
- Instrumentation: eval-orchestrator emits traces (trace_id), per-probe metrics, and artifacts (serialized responses).
- Prometheus metrics: eval_queue_length, eval_success_count, eval_failure_count, eval_duration_seconds_bucket.
- Dashboards: "Operational Eval Health" (per-model, per-route) and "Sweep Priority Backlog".

Failure modes and mitigations
- Stalled sweeps: watchdog kills runs exceeding 2x expected duration; auto-requeue with reduced probe_count.
- Probe flakiness: if probe_failure_count > 10% for a sweep, mark as unstable and quarantine probe family.
- False positives (semantic metric drift): require 2 consecutive sweep confirmations within 48h before automated rollbacks.

Integration with regression triage and oncall
1) When a sweep crosses the "sweep-regression" alert rule, eval-orchestrator creates a ticket in regression-triage with: sweep_id, top failing slices, baseline snapshot, and a link to artifacts in S3.
2) The regression-triage playbook (see related_pages) is responsible for bisect steps, suspected root-cause tags (quant-rollback, prompt-shift, model-weight-drift), and recommended mitigations.
3) Oncall flow:
   - Pager triggers for Priority >= 90 + customer_impact >= 80.
   - Runbook steps: confirm alerts, gather signatures (traces, sample diffs), run a narrow canary rollback, and notify CS if customer routes were affected.

Operational runbook (short run steps)
1) Verify sweep artifacts: `aws s3 ls s3://eval-results/prod-sweeps/<sweep_id>/`
2) Check dashboard slices and top-failing prompts.
3) If failure confirmed: open regression-triage ticket and tag as `high-priority`.
4) Apply mitigation: temporary model pin or automatic fallback to parent model variant via smart-routing.
5) Follow up: schedule a quant-sensitivity sweep after any rollback to verify health.

title: Operational Eval Sweep and Prioritization Framework
text:
[...] 4) Apply mitigation: temporary model pin or automatic fallback to parent model variant via smart-routing.
5) Follow up: schedule a quant-sensitivity sweep after any rollback to verify health.

CLI snippets
- Kick off a one-off sensitivity sweep:
  `rwd-evalctl start-sweep --model redwood/gptx-7b --type sensitivity --probes 200 --priority 65`
- Query sweep status:
  `rwd-evalctl get-sweep --id sweep-202610-abc123 --format json`

Ownership and contacts
- Primary owner: Applied ML Platform (Maya Singh) — applied-ml@redwood.ai
- Escalation: Runtime SRE (oncall alias: runtime-sre@redwood.ai)

Change log
- 2025-09-30 (Maya Singh): Initial draft and rollout of priority algorithm.
- 2026-02-18 (Maya Singh): Added quant-sensitivity gating rules and confirmed telemetry export schema.

Appendix: Examples of prioritization decisions
- Example A: A small quant change (ArtifactChangeScore 30) for a model used by 3 enterprise routes (CustomerImpact 80) -> Priority ≈ 62 -> Batch queue but with customer_critical override can promote to Real-time.
- Example B: Kernel-level update (ArtifactChangeScore 70) + high lineage drift (Risk 75) -> Priority ≈ 78 -> Nightly batch but with auto-accelerate if backlog_age > 24h.

References
- model-watcher lineage scoring (internal doc)
- regression-triage playbook (related_pages)
- eval-orchestrator v3 API spec (internal)

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
section_header: The root causes we have observed include
text:
CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation

## Summary
Our evaluation harness previously produced intermittent metric drops (e.g., ~0.04 BLEU on a single run) for the quantized-v2 onboarding suite that disappeared on rerun. This page documents the current (2026) standard for reducing nondeterminism, preventing CI flakes, and making regressions actionable.

The root causes we have observed include:
- Stochastic decoding (temperature/top_p sampling) without per-example RNG control.
- RNG state not consistently propagated to all relevant libraries/ops.
- GPU-level nondeterminism (kernel selection, atomic ordering), especially in quantized/int8 paths.
- Cross-hardware differences (e.g., A100 int8 kernels vs legacy fallback paths).

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
section_header: What changed since the initial investigation
text:
[...] - Cross-hardware differences (e.g., A100 int8 kernels vs legacy fallback paths).


## What changed since the initial investigation
The original debugging thread identified sampling parameters and missing explicit generators as primary contributors. The current harness incorporates the same core ideas, with the following updates:
- We now treat deterministic decoding as the default for per-commit CI gates for onboarding suites.
- The determinism flag is implemented as `--deterministic` / `deterministic: true` in eval config, and it hard-enforces decoding parameters (see below).
- The runner always constructs and passes an explicit `torch.Generator` for generation, derived from a root seed plus a stable per-example index.
- CI gating moved from single-run metrics to median-of-repeats, and flakiness is measured explicitly.

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...] - CI gating moved from single-run metrics to median-of-repeats, and flakiness is measured explicitly.


## Definitions
### Deterministic (harness-level)
A run is considered deterministic (for CI purposes) if repeated executions on the same hardware + container image + model revision produce identical decoded token sequences for the same prompts.

Notes:
- Hardware drift (A100 vs K80) is not expected to be bitwise identical.
- Some quantized kernels may still be nondeterministic even when framework-level deterministic flags are enabled.

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...] - Some quantized kernels may still be nondeterministic even when framework-level deterministic flags are enabled.


### Flake
A flake is a CI failure caused by nondeterministic variation rather than a real change in model/code.

## Recommended CI strategy (current)
### 1) Per-commit (PR) gate: deterministic quick eval
Purpose: prevent random sampling jitter from blocking merges.
- Environment: standardized runner image + target GPU class when available (A10 preferred for cost; A100 acceptable).
- Mode: `deterministic: true`
- Repeats: `--repeat 3`
- Decision rule: gate on the median metric across repeats; also fail if output hashes differ across repeats (for suites that require bitwise stability).

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...] - Decision rule: gate on the median metric across repeats; also fail if output hashes differ across repeats (for suites that require bitwise stability).


### 2) Nightly: multi-seed + tolerance-based comparison
Purpose: catch real regressions and quantify expected variance.
- Environment: target infra (A100 for quantized-v2)
- Mode: `deterministic: false` (sampling allowed when the eval is designed to measure sampled behavior)
- Seeds: 3 seeds (default: 13, 37, 101)
- Decision rule: compare against the golden baseline using metric tolerance thresholds; alert on sustained shifts (median across seeds).

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
section_header: 3) On failure: auto-trigger repro debug job
text:
[...] - Decision rule: compare against the golden baseline using metric tolerance thresholds; alert on sustained shifts (median across seeds).


### 3) On failure: auto-trigger repro debug job
Purpose: make investigations fast and data-rich.
- Auto-collect artifacts listed in the Logging and Artifacts section.
- Include a run summary that states whether variance is:
  - decode-level (different token sequences)
  - metric-only (same outputs but metric computation drift)
  - environment-level (hardware/container mismatch)

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...]   - environment-level (hardware/container mismatch)


## Deterministic decoding mode (required behavior)
When `deterministic: true`, the harness MUST enforce:
- `do_sample = false`
- `temperature = 0.0`
- `top_k = 1`
- `top_p = 1.0`
- `num_beams = 1` (unless the suite explicitly tests beam search)
- Explicit generator passed into generation APIs
- Stable batching order (no data-loader shuffling; stable sort by example_id)

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...] - Stable batching order (no data-loader shuffling; stable sort by example_id)


Rationale:
- Simply setting a global seed is insufficient when generation draws randomness in parallel or across different batch shapes.

## RNG handling (current standard)
### Root seed and per-example seeds
- The runner accepts a `--seed` root seed.
- For each example, derive a stable per-example seed:
  - `example_seed = root_seed + example_index`
- Construct a generator on the active device and pass it explicitly to generation.

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...] - Construct a generator on the active device and pass it explicitly to generation.


Operational guidance:
- Ensure `example_index` is stable across runs (do not use worker-local indices unless they are globally consistent).
- If sharding is used, include `shard_id` in the seed derivation only if shard assignments are stable across runs.

### Framework flags
We enable deterministic flags when `deterministic: true`:
- `torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True)`
- `torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True`
- `torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False`

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...] - `torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False`


Caveat:
- Quantized/int8 kernels may not fully respect these flags. Treat the flags as best-effort rather than a guarantee.

## Hardware notes (quantized-v2)
- A100 int8 paths can differ from legacy GPU fallbacks.
- CI determinism requirements are scoped to a single target hardware class per suite.
- The quantized-v2 onboarding suite is pinned to A100 for nightly baselines.

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...] - The quantized-v2 onboarding suite is pinned to A100 for nightly baselines.


## Metric gating rules
### Per-commit deterministic gate
- Compute metric per repeat.
- Report: median, min, max, and standard deviation.
- Fail conditions:
  - Any repeat differs in output token hash (for deterministic suites).
  - Std dev exceeds threshold (default threshold: 0.005 BLEU-equivalent; suite owners may override).

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...]   - Std dev exceeds threshold (default threshold: 0.005 BLEU-equivalent; suite owners may override).


### Nightly tolerance-based gate
- Compare nightly medians to golden medians.
- Default tolerances (may be tuned per suite):
  - BLEU: 0.03 absolute
  - chrF: 0.02 absolute
  - Task accuracy: 0.5 percentage points absolute

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
section_header: Each eval run must emit the following into the run artifact directory
text:
[...]   - Task accuracy: 0.5 percentage points absolute


## Logging and artifacts (required)
Each eval run must emit the following into the run artifact directory:
- `run_metadata.json`: model revision, container digest, GPU type, torch version, eval suite name, config snapshot
- RNG details:
  - root seed
  - per-example seed derivation scheme
  - `torch.get_rng_state()` (hex or base64)
  - `torch.cuda.get_rng_state_all()` (per device; hex or base64)
- Generation parameters:
  - do_sample, temperature, top_p, top_k, num_beams, max_new_tokens
- Outputs:
  - `outputs_run_{i}.jsonl` for each repeat
  - include decoded text and decoded token ids
- Summary:
  - `metrics_run_{i}.json`
  - `summary.json` with median/std and output hash comparisons

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
section_header: Required runner options for reproducibility testing
text:
[...]   - `summary.json` with median/std and output hash comparisons


## Runner CLI (current)
Required runner options for reproducibility testing:
- `--repeat N` : runs the same suite N times with the same config and seed
- `--deterministic {true|false}` : enforces deterministic mode when true
- `--seed SEED` : sets the root seed

title: CI Eval Determinism and Flake Mitigation
text:
[...] - `--seed SEED` : sets the root seed


Example:
- `evals/onboarding --suite quantized-v2 --repeat 3 --deterministic true --seed 42`

## Troubleshooting playbook
If you see nondeterminism with `deterministic: true`:
1) Confirm the run is on the expected hardware class and container digest.
2) Confirm output hashes differ (decode nondeterminism) vs metrics differ (metric computation drift).
3) If decode nondeterminism:
   - verify generator is passed explicitly
   - verify stable ordering of examples and stable batch shape
   - check for any remaining sampling knobs being set by a suite override
4) If still nondeterministic on A100 quantized kernels:
   - capture artifacts and escalate to eng-serving-runtime for kernel-level investigation
   - consider switching the suite’s CI gate to tolerance-based comparison if bitwise repeatability is unattainable

## Ownership and change control
- Owner: Applied ML & Evals
- Any change to determinism semantics (`--deterministic` behavior, seed derivation, gating thresholds) requires review from Applied ML & Evals + Serving Runtime.

title: deterministic-inference-testing-and-eval-harness-standards-2026
text:
deterministic-inference-testing-and-eval-harness-standards-2026

Overview
This document defines standards and an actionable harness for deterministic testing and evaluation of inference behavior across Redwood Inference serving modes (hosted, dedicated, private). The goal is to make regression detection predictable and actionable while acknowledging realistic sources of non-determinism in modern LLM stacks. This standard applies to model onboarding, model variant releases, runtime changes that touch kernels/scheduling/quantization, and CI gating for production rollouts.

Goals
- Detect semantic regressions and unintended output drift with low false positive rate.
- Provide reproducible test artifacts (prompt sets, seeds, environment spec) that can be executed locally and in CI.
- Define acceptable nondeterminism envelopes and tolerance thresholds tied to SLOs and eversioning policies.
- Integrate with observability and alerting so failures produce clear remediation playbooks.

Scope and applicability
- Applies to: model-serving teams, runtime engineers, evaluation engineers, release engineers.
- Not a replacement for human review on subtle safety regressions; it's a mechanical test gate.

Definitions
- Deterministic run: executing a prompt set with fixed environment spec where token-level outputs are byte-identical between two runs.
- Semantic-equivalence pass: outputs may differ at token level but meet a pre-specified metric threshold (e.g., repetition of facts, intent match, BLEU/ROUGE/embedding similarity).
- Golden Prompt Set: curated set of prompts and expected assertions used as a regression baseline.
- Environment spec: tuple {model artifact id, quantization profile, kernel build tag, runtime flags, RNG seed, hardware class}.

Principles
1) Make the environment spec explicit and reproducible in every test artifact.
2) Surface both deterministic and semantic signals; pass the strictest gate required by the release type (hotfix vs regular rollout).
3) Keep prompt sets small but representative; maintain a larger corpus for offline diagnostics.
4) Integrate with observability to capture token-level traces, embedding diffs, and performance counters for failed runs.

Test categories and gates
- Unit-level deterministic checks (pre-commit/CI fast): small prompt set (~20 examples) with fixed seed and environment spec. Gate: byte-equality required for library/runtime changes that claim no algorithmic change.
- Semantic regression tests (CI merge gates): Golden prompt set (~200 examples) run with nominal configuration. Gate: metric thresholds (embedding similarity >= 0.88 average; exact-match for critical safety prompts).
- Canary/Production shadowing (pre-rollout): run full golden corpus against canary variants in shadow mode under representative latency conditions. Gate: no critical safety failures, metric degradation < 2% for NLU tasks, resource regressions within performance budget.
- Stress and non-determinism stress tests: repeated runs (n=50) to measure output variance and tail-latency interplay. Gate: variance curve must be documented; excessive variance triggers runtime investigation.

Golden prompt set construction and maintenance
- Ownership: each model family has an owner (team + service). Owners maintain 3 prompt sets: smoke (20), regression (200), audit (2000).
- Items must include: input text, expected assertions (regex/fuzzy), tags (safety, factuality, latency-sensitive, formatting), and confidence thresholds.
- Versioning: store prompt sets in Git (eval-harness repo) with changelogs and a required reviewer list for edits.
- Labeling: label each example with acceptance criteria and whether exact-match is required.

Environment specification format (stringified YAML in test metadata)
- Required fields: model_artifact_id, model_checksum, quant_profile, kernel_tag, runtime_version, rng_seed, hardware_class, batch_shape, request_timeout_ms.
- Example: 
  ---
  model_artifact_id: redwood-turbo-v1
  model_checksum: 9f8b4c2a
  quant_profile: int8-o16-mixed
  kernel_tag: nv-kernel-2026-01
  runtime_version: rrt-2.4.1
  rng_seed: 16021993
  hardware_class: gpus-rtx-8000
  batch_shape: [1, 64]
  request_timeout_ms: 1200

Acceptable nondeterminism and tolerance enums
- REPEATABLE_BYTE: request must be byte-equal when environment spec unchanged. Use only for narrow low-level changes.
- SEMANTIC_EQUIV_HIGH: embedding cosine >= 0.92 AND top-1 intent unchanged.
- SEMANTIC_EQUIV_MED: embedding cosine >= 0.88 or BLEU >= 0.6.
- STRUCTURAL_TOLERANCE: output JSON schema stable but token-level differences allowed.

Metric definitions and thresholds
- Embedding similarity: mean cosine between baseline and candidate embeddings per example. Thresholds as above.
- Intent match: top-1 intent label equality for intent-classification examples.
- Entity F1: used for structured extraction tasks.
- Latency delta: p95 latency change must be <= +10% and absolute <= configured SLAs.
- Cost-per-token delta: must be <= +5% for same accuracy band unless approved.

title: deterministic-inference-testing-and-eval-harness-standards-2026
text:
[...] - Latency delta: p95 latency change must be <= +10% and absolute <= configured SLAs.
- Cost-per-token delta: must be <= +5% for same accuracy band unless approved.

CI integration and gating policy
1) Pre-merge: run unit deterministic checks. Fail fast for byte-level regressions.
2) Merge: run semantic regression suite in parallel; require no critical safety failures and metrics within thresholds.
3) Release candidate: run canary shadowing and non-determinism stress test; attach report to release ticket.
4) If any gate fails, checkout-to-incident-playbook: link to triage template and assign owner.

Observability instrumentation for test failures
- When a regression is detected capture and store: request payload, returned tokens, embeddings, runtime counters (kernel timings, GPU util, memory stats), and system logs.
- Tag traces with test-run-id, environment-spec, and commit-sha.
- Emit SLI events for: deterministic-failure, semantic-drop, latency-regression, cost-regression. Each SLI must map to an alert rule in the deployments' alerting stack.

Diagnostic playbook (on failure)
1) Reproduce locally using the exact environment spec in the failing CI artifact.
2) If deterministic mismatch: bisect recent runtime/kernel commits, re-run unit tests.
3) If semantic degradation: run per-example diffs (embedding vs baseline); prioritize top-K regressions by severity tag.
4) For hardware-specific anomalies: run across alternative hardware_class values.
5) If unresolved, open a release-blocker with reproducible artifact and attach to the model-family issue tracker.

Coding and API-level expectations for tests
- Test harness must expose a stable client API: run_suite(env_spec_yaml, prompt_set_id, options) -> structured report JSON.
- Reports must include metadata fields listed in this doc and be archived in the eval-harness artifacts bucket with deterministic naming: <model_artifact_id>-<promptset>-<commit>-<date>.json
- Unit test code must be in Python/Go with explicit RNG seeding. No tests should rely on system timezone or ephemeral system state.

Performance and infra considerations
- Parallelization: harness runs may be parallel but must log batch_shape and ordering to reconstruct token stream deterministically.
- Resource accounting: non-production canary runs must tag requests with traffic_tag=canary to avoid skewing customer billing and metrics.
- Cost budgeting: adopt cost-per-suite ceiling configured per model; overages require product approval.

Rollout and release decision guidance (short form)
- Hotfix to runtime kernel: requires passing deterministic unit checks + regression suite with SEMANTIC_EQUIV_HIGH for safety examples.
- Model variant with quantization: requires regression suite + canary shadowing under target hardware_class.
- Non-critical infra tweak: if only performance counters change and deterministic tests pass, allow staged rollout with increased sampling.

Auditing and traceability
- Every regression run must be archived with linked ticket id and reviewer.
- Golden prompt set changes require two reviewers and an entry in CHANGELOG.md in the eval-harness directory.

Appendix: example CI job (GitHub Actions snippet)
- name: Run inference regression suite\  run: |
    docker run --rm -v $GITHUB_WORKSPACE:/wrd --env-file .env redwood/eval-harness:latest \
      run_suite --env-spec ./envs/redwood-turbo-v1.yaml --prompt-set regression_v2 --output /wrd/artifacts/$GITHUB_SHA.json
Appendix: short ADR summary
Decision: Adopt a two-track testing contract combining strict deterministic unit checks for low-level runtime changes and semantic regression gates for model/quantization changes. Require explicit environment specs and integrate with observability to minimize noisy false positives.
Rationale: Single-mode testing produced noisy alerts and missed subtle semantic drift. A two-track approach provides fast feedback for kernel/runtime engineering while preserving broader semantic checks that reflect user-facing quality.

title: deterministic-inference-testing-and-eval-harness-standards-2026
text:
[...] Decision: Adopt a two-track testing contract combining strict deterministic unit checks for low-level runtime changes and semantic regression gates for model/quantization changes. Require explicit environment specs and integrate with observability to minimize noisy false positives.
Rationale: Single-mode testing produced noisy alerts and missed subtle semantic drift. A two-track approach provides fast feedback for kernel/runtime engineering while preserving broader semantic checks that reflect user-facing quality.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Increased CI cost. Mitigation: tiered suites and sampling for non-critical branches.
- Risk: False sense of security from metric thresholds. Mitigation: require human review for safety-tagged prompts and periodic audit runs.

Owners and contact
- Standard steward: Priya Srinivasan (model-serving team)
- Eval harness owner: Marcus Li (applied-ml-and-evals)
- Runtime steward: Evelyn Park (eng-serving-runtime)

Change log
- 2026-01-12: Created initial draft (Priya).
- 2026-02-18: Incorporated reviewer feedback: clarified env-spec fields, added canary guidance, tightened thresholds (Marcus, Evelyn).

title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
text:
Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook

Overview

This playbook defines the telemetry schema, compression strategies, and scheduling policy used by the Eval Harness probe fabric to keep long-running evaluation suites stable and cost-effective. It is intended for ML Engineers, Evaluations SREs, and Probe Stewards responsible for probe lifecycle, telemetry ingestion, and archive retention.

Goals
- Preserve signal fidelity for slice-driven evaluations while reducing long-term storage and network cost.
- Provide deterministic scheduling priorities so that critical probes (regressions, high-SLO slices) are executed and retained in full fidelity.
- Allow safe lossy compression and sampling for low-priority probes with automated recovery if anomalies are detected.

Scope

title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
text:
[...] 
Scope

This document covers:
- Telemetry signals and minimal wire schema for probes.
- Compression tiers and codecs recommended for each signal type.
- Priority scoring and scheduling algorithm for the probe fabric.
- Rollout and monitoring runbook for compression changes.
- Ownership and escalation paths.

Definitions
- Probe: a single evaluation request or synthetic workload trace used by the Eval Harness.
- Anchor probe: a small set of high-fidelity probes kept uncompressed for drift detection.
- Telemetry bucket: logical grouping of telemetry (request, response, model_meta, embeddings, runtime).

Telemetry schema (minimal)

title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
section_header: All probes must emit the following top-level fields (JSON wire format)
text:
[...] 
Telemetry schema (minimal)

All probes must emit the following top-level fields (JSON wire format):
- probe_id (string)
- timestamp_utc (ISO8601)
- model_variant (string)
- prompt_template_id (string)
- input_tokens (int)
- output_tokens (int)
- latency_ms (float)
- success (bool)
- response_hash (string, SHA256)
- meta_tags (list[string])
- raw_response (string | optional, see compression tiers)

Signals and retention requirements
| Signal name | Use cases | Retention (hot) | Archive fidelity | Comments |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| latency_ms | SLO tracking, slice analysis | 90 days | float retained | 1ms resolution |
| response_hash | regression detection | 365 days | hash string | small footprint |
| raw_response | behavioral triage, annotation | 30 days (default) | tiered (see compression) | may be compressed or sampled |
| embeddings | clustering/semantic drift | 90 days | float32 or quantized int8 | store quant meta |
| model_meta | model config, quant profile | forever | full | small text |

Compression tiers and recommended codecs

title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
text:
[...] 
Compression tiers and recommended codecs

Tier definitions:
1) Tier-A (Anchor/high-fidelity)
- Criteria: top 0.5% probes by priority score and 100 probes per model per day.
- Codec: none (store raw_response and token-level logits if available).
- Storage: hot, uncompressed.
2) Tier-B (Operational high-value)
- Criteria: probes used in recent regressions, customer-facing slices, labeled probes.
- Codec: Brotli level 6 for raw_response; embeddings stored as float16.
- Storage: hot for 30–90 days, then move to compressed archive.
3) Tier-C (Economy)
- Criteria: background stress probes, synthetic diversity probes below priority threshold.
- Codec: gzip level 6 or Snappy for fast decompress; optionally strip token-level metadata.
- Sampling: retain 1-in-N sampling by slice with dynamic N (see scheduling).
4) Tier-D (Discardable summaries)
- Criteria: probes older than archive window and low priority.
- Storage: store only response_hash + compact summary (first 256 chars) + metrics.

Compression rules matrix (text table)
- raw_response: Tier-A none; Tier-B Brotli; Tier-C gzip/Snappy; Tier-D summary only.
- embeddings: Tier-A float32; Tier-B float16; Tier-C int8 quantized with metadata.
- logits/token-level: Tier-A only; remove in Tier-B/C.

Priority scoring & scheduling (algorithm)

title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
section_header: Priority score (PS) is a weighted linear combination where higher is more important
text:
[...] 
Priority scoring & scheduling (algorithm)

Priority score (PS) is a weighted linear combination where higher is more important: 
PS = w_s * slice_criticality + w_u * user_impact + w_r * recency_boost + w_f * failure_rate_boost + w_c * compute_cost_factor
Where:
- slice_criticality: [0..1] derived from coverage matrix (manual mapping per product slice).
- user_impact: [0..1] function of daily active users affected (log-scaled).
- recency_boost: decays exponentially (recent anomalies get higher weight), recency_boost = exp(-delta_days / tau).
- failure_rate_boost: proportional to recent failure/ regression signal frequency (0..1).
- compute_cost_factor: negative term to penalize extremely expensive probes (normalized 0..1).
Default weights: w_s=0.35, w_u=0.25, w_r=0.20, w_f=0.15, w_c=0.05. Weights are configurable per workspace.

Scheduling policy (high-level)
1) Compute PS for new probes on ingest.
2) If PS >= 0.8 -> schedule immediate, Tier-A retention (no compression).
3) If 0.5 <= PS < 0.8 -> schedule within 2 hours, Tier-B retention/compression.
4) If 0.2 <= PS < 0.5 -> enqueue for batch window, Tier-C with dynamic sampling.
5) If PS < 0.2 -> optional sampling (background only), Tier-D summaries only.
6) During capacity constraints, preempt Tier-C and Tier-D first; Tier-A and Tier-B preserved.

Dynamic sampling policy
- Sampling rate per slice S is computed as: N = max(1, round(base_rate / (1 + importance_score)))
- base_rate default = 100 (keep 1 in 100). importance_score is normalized slice_criticality * 10.
- Example: slice_criticality=0.8 -> importance_score=8 -> N = round(100 / 9) = 11 -> keep ~1 in 11

Rollout & change control (compression changes)
1) Author prepares a change request with: compressed codecs, affected probe-types, rollback steps, cost delta estimate, and test dataset.
2) Run A/B shadow for 72 hours on 1% of traffic. Collect metrics: response_stability_index, hash_collision_rate, retrieval_latency, cost delta.
3) If response_stability_index degradation <= 0.5% and hash_collision_rate < 0.01% -> expand to 10% for 48 hours.
4) Full rollout if both checks pass and SRE sign-off.
5) Emergency rollback: disable codec at proxy, fallback to Tier-B storage.

Monitoring & alerting

title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
section_header: Essential metrics to publish to the Eval Mesh dashboard
text:
[...] 
Monitoring & alerting

Essential metrics to publish to the Eval Mesh dashboard:
- probe_ingest_rate (per model, per slice)
- compression_ratio_histogram (per codec)
- response_stability_index (RSI) rolling 24h/7d
- retrieval_latency_p95 (archive reads)
- anchor_probe_delta (distance between anchor response and compressed-rehydrated response)
- regression_detection_rate (alerts/day)
