title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
text:
[...] - anchor_probe_delta (distance between anchor response and compressed-rehydrated response)
- regression_detection_rate (alerts/day)

Alert thresholds (examples):
- RSI drop > 2% in 1h -> P1 oncall page.
- anchor_probe_delta increase > 0.02 L2-norm -> investigate compression bias.
- compression_ratio drop more than 50% from expected -> storage misconfiguration.

Operational runbook: compression incident (summary)
1) Pager triggers: RSI P1.
2) Triage: identify affected model_variant and start time window.
3) Run quick-replay on anchor probes (stored uncompressed) and compare hashes.
4) If compression codec suspected, rollback codec via feature flag; rehydrate Tier-B from archived uncompressed backup for critical windows.
5) Postmortem: include regression triage tags and escalate to model-onboarding if root cause is model-output shift.

Ownership and contacts
- Probe Fabric Owners: Marcus Li (primary), Aisha Patel (secondary)
- Eval Harness SRE: Ravi Desai
- Applied ML: Evelyn Chen (doc owner)

Appendices

title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
section_header: A: Example YAML for retention policy (Eval Harness config)
text:
[...] 
Appendices

A: Example YAML for retention policy (Eval Harness config)
---
retention_policies:
  - name: anchor
    criteria: "priority >= 0.95 or tag:anchor"
    retention_days: 365
    compression: none
  - name: high_value
    criteria: "tag:labelled or recent_regression:true"
    retention_days: 90
    compression: "brotli:level=6"
  - name: default
    criteria: "true"
    retention_days: 30

title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
text:
[...]     criteria: "true"
    retention_days: 30

    compression: "gzip:level=6"
---

B: CLI examples
- Inspect probe priority: eval-harness-cli score --probe-id <id> --model <name>
- Force uncompressed retention: eval-harness-cli retention set --probe-id <id> --policy anchor

C: Example checksums and rehydration snippet (pseudo-code)

title: Probe Telemetry Compression & Scheduling Playbook
text:
[...] 
C: Example checksums and rehydration snippet (pseudo-code)

# verify response fidelity after decompression
resp = decompress(stored_blob)
if sha256(resp) != stored_meta.response_hash:
  raise FidelityMismatch("hash mismatch after decompression")

D: FAQ
Q: How do we choose tau for recency_boost?
A: Default tau is 7 days for user-facing slices and 2 days for internal stress tests. Tune based on burstiness.

Q: Can we apply quantized embeddings to Tier-A?
A: No — Tier-A anchors must keep float32 to act as fidelity references. Tier-B may use float16.

Change log
- 2026-02-12: Initial draft by Evelyn Chen.
- 2026-03-01: Added sampling formula and rollout thresholds; reviewers added.

References
- Eval mesh telemetry contract: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/eval-data-provenance-and-reproducibility-standard-2026
- Probe privacy budget playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quality-guardrails/probe-privacy-budget-and-annotation-retention-playbook-2024

title: Cohort-driven Eval Scheduling and Priority Protocol
text:
Cohort-driven Eval Scheduling and Priority Protocol

Overview

This playbook defines the cohort-driven evaluation scheduling and priority protocol used by Redwood's eval-orch service to allocate evaluator resources, reduce noisy variance across workloads, and ensure high-value experiments get stable, timely runs. It describes how cohorts are defined, how priority scores are computed, resource windows and backfill rules, integration with caching/quantization-aware profiles, and operational runbooks for common scenarios.

Why this exists

- Reduce cross-workload interference that causes unstable eval signals (especially for latency-sensitive and quantized workloads).
- Make scheduling decisions reproducible and auditable so regressions can be traced to scheduling artifacts rather than model changes.
- Balance cost, timeliness, and statistical fidelity when allocating constrained evaluator capacity.

Scope and applicability

- Applies to eval runs managed by eval-orch (interactive and batch evaluators) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private eval fleets.
- Covers cohort construction, priority computation, preemption/backfill policies, telemetry signals used for autoscaling, and required annotations on eval requests.
- Does NOT cover model acceptance criteria (see production-model-certification-checklist) or user-facing rollout policies.

Definitions

- Cohort: A grouping of eval jobs that share workload characteristics (latency sensitivity, sequence-length distribution, quant profile, dataset type, and customer tier).
- Priority Score: A computed numeric score used to order job execution. Higher means run earlier.
- Evaluator Pool: Logical group of workers with similar hardware and kernel profiles (e.g., a100-40g-int8-pool).
- Fidelity Band: Statistical group that indicates required sample size and stability budget (low/medium/high).

Cohort construction rules

1) Mandatory tags on every eval request (API/SDK):
   - workload_type: {chat, completion, embedding, rerank}
   - seq_len_bucket: {short:<128, medium:128-1024, long:>1024}
   - quant_profile: {fp16, int8, q4_0, q4_1}
   - latency_sensitivity: {low, medium, high}
   - customer_tier: {self_serve, commercial, enterprise}

2) Cohort assignment algorithm (pseudo):
   - Step A: Bucket by evaluators_supported_hardware and quant_profile.
   - Step B: Sub-bucket by latency_sensitivity + seq_len_bucket.
   - Step C: Tag with fidelity_band using heuristics: high fidelity for enterprise & regression triage jobs; medium for feature-tracking; low for exploratory.

Priority score calculation

title: Cohort-driven Eval Scheduling and Priority Protocol
section_header: Priority score (PS) is a weighted linear combination
text:
[...] Priority score calculation


Priority score (PS) is a weighted linear combination:

PS = W_tier * TierScore + W_latency * LatencyScore + W_fidelity * FidelityScore + W_staleness * StalenessScore - W_cost * EstimatedCostRank

Where:
- TierScore: self_serve=10, commercial=30, enterprise=60.
- LatencyScore: low=5, medium=20, high=50.
- FidelityScore: low=5, medium=25, high=45.
- StalenessScore: monotonic hours since request submission normalized to [0,10].
- EstimatedCostRank: cost rank of the run on normalized scale [0,10].
- Default weights: W_tier=1.0, W_latency=1.2, W_fidelity=1.4, W_staleness=0.6, W_cost=0.8.

Notes: weights are tunable via eval-orch config; score is clipped to [0,100]. Priority ties are broken by request id timestamp.

Resource windows and capacity allocation

- Each Evaluator Pool exposes N concurrent slots (configured per-pool).
- Pools are partitioned into: baseline_capacity (70%), burst_capacity (20%), preemptible_backfill (10%).
- Baseline capacity honors enterprise SLAs and scheduled high-priority cohorts. Burst capacity serves transient spikes for commercial workloads. Backfill runs lower-priority & exploratory evals and may be preempted.

Preemption and backfill rules

1) Preemption is allowed only for backfill jobs. Baseline and burst slots cannot be preempted without explicit approval.
2) When a higher-priority job (PS delta >= 15) is queued and there is no free baseline slot, eval-orch will attempt to reclaim a backfill slot.
3) Preempted jobs receive an attempt to checkpoint partial results to the artifact store; if checkpointing fails, they are marked for fast-resume (see runbook).

Cache & quantization-awareness

- Cohorts maintain a small KV cache affinity window (default 15 minutes) per pool to preserve KV-cache hotness and reduce cold-start noise.
- Quantization profile mismatches between requested eval and pool hardware lower the effective PriorityScore by 10 points to avoid noisy mismatch runs.

Fidelity bands and statistical budgets

Table: Fidelity band definitions

| Fidelity | Min samples | Max variance budget (std) | Run window (hours) | Typical use-cases |
|----------|-------------|---------------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Low      | 250         | 0.15                      | 72                  | exploratory dev, feature probes |
| Medium   | 1,000       | 0.07                      | 48                  | performance tracking |
| High     | 5,000       | 0.02                      | 24                  | regression triage, release gating |

Example YAML eval-orch job config

```yaml
job_id: "eval-2026-0009"
workload_type: "chat"
seq_len_bucket: "medium"
quant_profile: "int8"
latency_sensitivity: "high"
customer_tier: "commercial"
fidelity_band: "medium"
priority_overrides: null
requested_pool: "a100-40g-int8-pool"
expected_sample_count: 1200
max_run_time_hours: 12
annotations:
  team: "search-ranking"
  experiment: "rerank-quant-int8-v2"
```

Telemetry signals and SLOs (what eval-orch tracks)

title: Cohort-driven Eval Scheduling and Priority Protocol
section_header: Priority score (PS) is a weighted linear combination
text:
[...] 
Telemetry signals and SLOs (what eval-orch tracks)

- Queue latency P95 <= 2 hours for baseline capacity cohorts.
- Job start P95 <= 1 hour after dequeuing.
- Checkpoint success rate >= 99% for runs > 30 minutes.
- Preemption noise rate (jobs preempted more than once) < 0.5%.
- Fidelity convergence: median variance/day reduction >= 30% for medium/high bands.

Operational procedures and runbooks

1) Fast resume for preempted runs
   - Trigger: job preempted and checkpoint saved to artifact store.
   - Steps:
     1. Verify checkpoint exists in s3://eval-artifacts/<job_id>/checkpoint/ .
     2. Requeue job with resume=true and original priority.
     3. Monitor memory and kv-cache warming metrics for first 5 minutes.
     4. If failure to resume, mark job as resume_failed and notify owner (Slack group: #eval-ops).

2) Handling runaway variance in a cohort
   - Trigger: variance > band threshold for 3 consecutive runs.
   - Steps:
     1. Quarantine cohort (stop scheduling new medium/high runs to the same pool).
     2. Spawn diagnostic shadow runs on isolated pool (a100-debug-pool) with identical seeds.
     3. Collect kernel-level traces and KV-cache hit rates.
     4. Open an incident in Incidents Jira (component: eval-orch) and assign to Applied ML on-call.

3) Emergency rebalancing (SLA breach imminent)
   - Trigger: enterprise SLA breach predicted within 15 minutes due to capacity loss.
   - Steps:
     1. Pause all backfill jobs immediately.
     2. Drain burst capacity and move eligible jobs to preemptible nodes.
     3. If still insufficient, execute cross-region fallback policy (route to compatible pools in another region).

Integration points and hooks

- eval-orch -> telemetry-pipeline: emits job lifecycle events (queued, started, checkpointed, preempted, completed).
- eval-orch -> optimizer-service: requests cost estimates and suggested batching parameters.
- eval-orch -> evaluator-agent: gRPC control plane for running and checkpointing jobs.
- eval-orch -> kv-cache-service: affinity hints for cache warmup.

Metrics and dashboards to create

- Evaluator Pool: concurrent usage, slot occupancy by cohort, burst/backfill ratio.
- Job lifecycle: queue latency distribution, preemption counts, resume success rate.
- Cohort health: variance over time, sample completion rate, checkpoint success.

Ownership and contacts

- Primary owner: Applied ML Platform (team alias: applied-ml-eval-platform@redwood.com).
- On-call for eval-orch: eng-sre rotation (see eng-sre/oncall-rotations).
- Incident escalation: Applied ML lead (Marco Hernandez) -> Eng Platform manager (Lena Ortiz).

Change history

- 2026-01-18: Initial published version (Aisha Patel).
- 2026-02-02: Adjusted default weights and added cache affinity window (reviewers: Marco Hernandez, Rina Sato).

Appendix: common troubleshooting commands

- List queued jobs: `curl -sS https://eval-orch.internal/api/v1/jobs?state=queued | jq .`
- Drain backfill on pool: `eval-orch ctl drain --pool a100-40g-int8-pool --mode backfill`
- Force resume job: `eval-orch ctl resume --job eval-2026-0009`
- Inspect checkpoint: `aws s3 ls s3://eval-artifacts/eval-2026-0009/checkpoint/`

title: Cohort-driven Eval Scheduling and Priority Protocol
section_header: Priority score (PS) is a weighted linear combination
text:
[...] - Force resume job: `eval-orch ctl resume --job eval-2026-0009`
- Inspect checkpoint: `aws s3 ls s3://eval-artifacts/eval-2026-0009/checkpoint/`

References

title: Multi-Granularity Response Correction Orchestrator
text:
Multi-Granularity Response Correction Orchestrator

Overview:

This document describes the Multi-Granularity Response Correction Orchestrator (MG-RCO): an automated system and operational runbook for detecting, synthesizing, validating, and rolling out corrections to model outputs at different granularities (token, turn, and conversation/session). MG-RCO is designed to integrate with the eval harness, prompt catalog, model onboarding flows, quantization profiles, and regression triage processes so fixes are safe, measurable, and reversible.

Why we built MG-RCO:
- Persistent long-tail quality issues require targeted repairs at different levels (e.g., tokenization errors vs. conversational context drift).
- Manual patching is slow and risky; a reproducible orchestrator enables faster mean-time-to-fix while preserving production SLOs.
- Corrections must be validated across quantized variants and shadow fleets to avoid regressions introduced by performance optimizations.

Scope and definitions:
1) Granularity levels:
   - Token-level: fixes that alter prompt encoding, tokenization, or scoring of a small lexical pattern (e.g., punctuation handling, Unicode normalization).
   - Turn-level: fixes that affect a single exchange (prompt + immediate response) such as hallucination suppression or instruction tightening.
   - Session-level: multi-turn stateful repairs (context window trimming, memory anchors, persona resets).

2) Correction types:
   - Prompt rewrite: automated or templated prompt modifications applied at request-time.
   - Post-filter/heuristic: deterministic filters that modify or suppress outputs (e.g., remove PII patterns).
   - Model artifact patch: small model re-ranking weights or delta weights applied in runtime (rare, requires rigorous rollout).
   - Eval-driven synthetic label: generated training/eval examples used for model fine-tuning or on-the-fly re-ranking.

Design principles:
- Minimal blast radius: prefer prompt rewrites and post-filters before considering model artifact patching.
- Evaluability: every correction must be covered by an automated eval suite (unit-level + slice-level + mixed-SLO shadowing).
- Quant-compatibility: corrections must be probed against quantization profiles and KV-cache behaviors.
- Observability: clear telemetry for candidate impact, rollback triggers, and SLOs.

System architecture (textual diagram):
- Detection layer: anomaly detectors, probe triggers, and incident inputs (Sentry alerts, eval regressions, customer tickets).
- Orchestrator core: stores correction recipes, coordinates patch generation, runs validation workflows, and coordinates rollouts.
- Evaluator adapters: run correction-aware eval harness jobs (shadow, canary, full-fidelity replay).
- Synthesis engine: template-based rewrite generator + small LLM-based rewriter used with caution (sandboxed).
- Safety & guardrails: static checks, cost budget checks, quant-compatibility checks, and approval gates.

Key integrations:
- Eval harness: MG-RCO registers a validation job type 'correction-validation' that runs the candidate against the canonical probe set plus a targeted slice.
- Prompt catalog: correction recipes are linked to prompt-set IDs; canonicalized templates are versioned in the prompt registry.
- Model onboarding: when a correction requires model artifact changes, MG-RCO triggers a model-onboarding check including preflight probes and shadow promotions.
- Quantization profiles: MG-RCO runs candidate corrections across the active quant profiles for the target model (e.g., int8, q4_0) and fails candidates that show coherence or hallucination regressions beyond thresholds.
- Regression triage: every correction generates a traceable incident ticket containing hypothesis, test matrix, and blame candidates for regression investigators.

Operational runbook: detection to production (numbered steps)
1) Detection & intake:
   - Trigger sources: automated regression alert, probe-sentinel, SLO breach, customer-reported case.
   - Create MG-RCO intake with: input example(s), TLDR of failure class, target service/model, urgency level.
2) Preliminary triage (human-in-loop):
   - Triage engineer assigns granularity and correction preference (prompt rewrite, filter, model patch).
   - If evidence points to quant or KV-cache interaction, mark "quant-sensitive" and notify runtime team.
3) Candidate generation:
   - For prompt rewrites: run template synthesizer + 3 LLM-variant rewrites (sandboxed) and canonicalize.
   - For filters: author a deterministic rule with unit tests.
   - For model patches: create a small re-ranking delta and schedule offline evaluation (requires cross-team pre-approval).
4) Automated validation:
   - Launch correction-validation job in eval harness with matrix: 
     * Baseline model (prod variant) + candidate correction (shadow)
     * Quantized variants (all active quant profiles)
     * Long-context session replay (if session-level)
     * Adversarial prompt suite for the affected slice
   - Pass criteria: no regression on anchor slices, >X% improvement on failure slice, latency increase < 5% absolute, token cost delta within configured budget.
5) Human review and safety gates:
   - Reviewer checklist includes: privacy scan, hallucination regression check, prompt-rewrite bias scan, and cost impact.
   - If any gate fails, mark candidate as 'rework' and loop back to step 3.
6) Canary rollout:
   - Deploy correction as a shadow for 10% of requests (not visible to end-users) and run live evaluators for 48 hours.
   - Monitor MG-RCO dashboard metrics (see Metrics section) and run a scheduled rollback test at 24 hours.
7) Gradual promotion:
   - If canary passes gates (no service-impacting regressions, stable latency/cost), promote to 1%, 5%, 25%, 100% over daily steps with quorum checks at each stage.
8) Post-promotion validation and closure:
   - Run post-promotion audit: tranche sample of live requests replayed through eval harness and recorded.
   - Close the intake with a postmortem if the correction involved model artifact changes.

Acceptance criteria and SLOs for corrections:
- Functional: the failure-slice pass rate increases by at least the configured uplift (default 20% relative).
- Non-functional: 99th-pctl latency increase <= 5% absolute and 95th-pctl latency increase <= 3% absolute.
- Cost: token-cost delta <= 10% for heavy-use endpoints unless compensated by a business case.
- Safety: zero new high-severity safety findings on eval sentinel suite.

Sample correction spec (JSON used by orchestrator, example):
{
  "id": "corr-2026-0892",
  "target_model": "redwood-chat-v2.1",
  "granularity": "turn",
  "type": "prompt_rewrite",
  "recipe_version": "v1",
  "validation_matrix": ["baseline", "q4_0", "int8"],
  "pass_thresholds": {"slice_uplift_pct": "20", "max_latency_p95_pct": "3"},
  "owner": "priya.nair@redwood.com"
}

(Orchestrator fields are stored in the correction registry; the example above is textual only.)

Validation matrix example (table as text):
| Axis | Baseline | Shadow+Correction | Quantized int8 | Long-context replay | Adversarial slice |
|------|----------|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|-------------------|
|Metric: failure-slice accuracy| 0.42 | 0.66 | 0.63 | 0.61 | 0.55|
|Pass?| No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Partial|

Telemetries and dashboards to monitor:
- Correction-intake rate and median time-to-first-triage.
- Correction validation pass/fail counts per model and per quant-profile.
- Canary impact charts: slice uplift, overall quality delta, latency, token-cost.
- Regression triage link: each correction creates a triage artifact with provenance (eval jobs, seed probe IDs).
- Audit trail: who approved, reviewer notes, artifact versions (prompt template id, model id, quant profile id).

Automated rollback rules (examples):
- If production error rate for affected route increases by > 5% RELATIVE within a 30-minute window, auto-rollback.
- If failure-slice regression reaches > 10% relative compared to baseline at canary stage, auto-rollback.
- If cost per 1k tokens rises above the configured cost budget for the route, rollback and notify finance-runbook.

Troubleshooting checklist (common failure modes):
- Candidate passes offline but fails in-shadow: check for telemetry mismatch (tokenization differences, header prompts), verify request canonicalization.
- Quant regressions: run quant-shadow replay harness, inspect KV cache alignment and attention kernel fallbacks.
- Drift reappearance after promotion: schedule probe refresh; consider session-level anchors rather than brittle rewrites.

Governance and change control:
- Low-risk corrections (prompt rewrites + filters) are auto-approvable with two reviewers.
- Medium-risk (synthetic label changes, LLM-generated rewrites) require cross-team approval from Applied ML and Safety.
- High-risk (model artifact deltas) must go through full model-onboarding acceptance, SRE performance sign-off, and security review.

How MG-RCO complements existing playbooks:
- Eval harness: runs the canonical correction-validation jobs and contributes new job types and example seeds.
- Prompt sets: canonical prompt templates are versioned and mapped to correction recipes to preserve provenance.
- Model onboarding: when a patch crosses into model artifacts, MG-RCO invokes the onboarding acceptance matrix (preflight probes, shadow promotion).
- Quantization profiles: MG-RCO requires per-profile passes and stores results in the correction artifact.
- Regression triage: every failed candidate generates a triage artifact that is consumable by the regression huntbook team.

Ownership and contacts:
- Runbook owner: Priya Nair (Applied ML)
- Runtime / Quant contact: Jonah Baker (Serving Runtime)
- Evaluator and probe infra: Alex Chen (Eval Infrastructure)
- Emergency rollback pager: eng-sre oncall (see internal pager rota link)

Appendix A — Example CLI commands:
- Create intake: redwood-mgro intake create --title "Chat hallucination on payments" --examples-file ./examples.json --urgency P2
- Run quick validation: redwood-mgro validate --id corr-2026-0892 --matrix baseline,q4_0 --timeout 2h
- Promote to canary: redwood-mgro promote --id corr-2026-0892 --stage canary --percent 10

Appendix B — Quick decision heuristics table (text):
- If failure pattern is syntactic and deterministic -> prefer filter.
- If failure pattern requires context rewriting but is stable -> prefer prompt rewrite.
- If failure pattern is model-internal (semantic hallucination not fixed by prompt) -> model artifact path (requires onboarding).

Related pages and artifacts:
- Link to canonical prompt registry: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/prompt-sets-and-benchmarks/prompt-registry-index (internal)
- Model onboarding acceptance matrix: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/onboarding-acceptance-rubric-kvcache-quant-and-prompt-calibration-2026
- Eval harness job types reference: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/evaluator-interchange-format-and-federation-spec-2026

Revision notes:
- 2025-11-10: Initial draft and prototype runbook.
- 2026-02-04: Added quant-profile validation requirements after a production regression test.
- 2026-09-12: Expanded canary promotion policy and automated rollback rules.

This page is intended to be a living runbook and design spec. Use the reviewers above for changes that affect validation thresholds or the rollout cadence.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
text:
Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations

Summary:
This document defines the telemetry contract and operational workflow required to run edge and private shadow evaluations against customer-managed (Private) and Dedicated pools. The contract balances fidelity for quality checks (coherence, hallucination, embedding drift) with privacy, cost, and storage constraints specific to private deployments. It is intended for applied ML engineers, SREs running shadow orchestration, and oncall/regression teams performing triage and rollbacks.

Goals and scope:
- Provide a minimal, well-documented telemetry payload that all shadow evaluators must emit when running in Private and Dedicated environments.
- Specify sampling, anonymization, retention, and encryption requirements to meet customer privacy/KMS constraints.
- Define quality and regression gate metrics, thresholds, and alerting integration for early detection during shadow rollouts and quant/optimize migrations.
- Describe triage playbook for any failing slices discovered by the shadow probes, including ownership and rollback criteria.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
section_header: Each shadow probe record must contain the following top-level fields (string or numeric types only)
text:
[...] - Describe triage playbook for any failing slices discovered by the shadow probes, including ownership and rollback criteria.


Why this contract exists:
Private and Dedicated deployments run under different constraints than Redwood-hosted evals: limited egress, stricter PII rules, and heterogeneous hardware/quantization variants. Without a standard contract, shadow telemetry is inconsistent and expensive to analyze. This contract ensures we get the telemetry needed to detect regressions while respecting privacy and cost.

Telemetry payload spec (minimal required fields):
Each shadow probe record must contain the following top-level fields (string or numeric types only):
- probe_id: unique id for the probe run (uuid)
- tenant_id: hashed tenant id (sha256 hex) or obfuscated alias; raw customer IDs MUST NOT be sent outside the private boundary unless agreed (see privacy section)
- deployed_model: model name + version (e.g., redwood-7b-v2-quant8)
- deployment_variant: one of [hosted, dedicated, private]
- quant_profile: label of quant profile used (e.g., q8_fast_fp16_fallback)
- hardware_tag: e.g., a100-40gb, t4-16gb, edge-x86-32g
- prompt_family: short token describing probe set (e.g., billing_faq_anchors_v3)
- seed_context_hash: sha1 of the prompt/context after canonicalization (for deduping; non-reversible)
- latency_ms: observed end-to-end request latency in ms (int)
- tokens_input: token count of input (int)
- tokens_generated: token count generated (int)
- top_level_metrics: comma-separated string of key metrics, e.g., coherence:0.82,hallucination:0.03,embed_cos_sim:0.67
- error_code: empty string or standardized code (see appendix error-code-table)
- timestamp_utc: ISO8601 timestamp

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
text:
[...] - timestamp_utc: ISO8601 timestamp


Optional fields (when privacy & egress allow):
- raw_response_snippet: up to 512 characters after PII redaction
- annotator_score: numeric human score if annotator was used (0..1)
- kv_cache_hit: boolean (true/false)

Sampling and rate limits:
- Default sampling rate for private shadow probes: 2% of production traffic or 1000 probes/day per tenant (whichever is smaller) unless explicitly configured for a campaign.
- For Dedicated pools without egress, sampling may be increased during controlled experiments with customer approval; update deployment manifest.
- System must support stratified sampling by prompt_family to ensure rare slices are captured (min 5 probes per slice per day if available).

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
text:
[...] - System must support stratified sampling by prompt_family to ensure rare slices are captured (min 5 probes per slice per day if available).


Privacy, encryption, and retention:
- All telemetry leaving customer-managed network must be encrypted with the customer KMS key (AES-256-GCM envelope encryption).
- tenant_id must be hashed; any reversible identifier requires documented consent and a signed customer exception.
- raw_response_snippet must undergo automated PII redaction pipeline before export. Use our redaction library version >= v2.1.
- Default retention: 30 days for detailed probe payloads, 365 days for aggregated metrics (daily rollups). For customers with longer retention requirements we must create a retention SLA and enable on-prem analytics hooks.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
section_header: Metric definitions and SLOs (shadow-eval quality gates)
text:
[...] - Default retention: 30 days for detailed probe payloads, 365 days for aggregated metrics (daily rollups). For customers with longer retention requirements we must create a retention SLA and enable on-prem analytics hooks.


Metric definitions and SLOs (shadow-eval quality gates):
- Coherence (slice median): unit 0..1; alert threshold < 0.70; action: create incident, notify model-owner. Notes: Cohesion of multi-turn replies (see coherence rubric).
- Hallucination rate (slice): unit fraction; alert threshold > 0.05; action: open P1 triage if sustained for 6 hours. Notes: Exclude benign OOD prompts after review.
- Embedding cosine shift: unit delta; alert threshold > 0.12; action: schedule regression-bisect, tag model-version. Notes: Compare against anchor baseline.
- Latency p95: unit ms; alert threshold > 2x baseline or > 1200ms; action: throttle rollout, rollback if tail persists. Notes: Baseline computed per hardware_tag.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
text:
[...] - Latency p95: unit ms; alert threshold > 2x baseline or > 1200ms; action: throttle rollout, rollback if tail persists. Notes: Baseline computed per hardware_tag.


Alerting and integration:
- Expose shadow-eval metrics to the Ops dashboard with tenant-scoped anonymized view. Use metric names: shadow.coherence.*, shadow.hallucination.*, shadow.latency.*.
- Create automatic incidents when thresholds are breached for >= 30 minutes across >=3 prompt_families.
- Add annotation to incidents indicating whether the failing probes used quantized model variants.

Workflow: Shadow rollouts and gating (sequence):
1. Prepare: define probe campaign with prompt_families, sampling rate, quant_profile, and expected traffic window. Owner: Applied ML engineer.
2. Dry run: execute offline shadow run on a representative dataset; compute baseline metrics and generate an acceptance ticket.
3. Shadow deploy: route sampled traffic to shadow instance (no user-visible change). Emit telemetry per contract.
4. Monitor: dashboard shows live slice metrics. Automated gates evaluate every 5 minutes.
5. Escalate: if a gate crosses alert threshold, follow triage playbook (see next section).
6. Decision: canary -> promote, continue shadowing, or rollback per prescribed criteria.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
text:
[...] 6. Decision: canary -> promote, continue shadowing, or rollback per prescribed criteria.


Regression triage playbook (short-form):
- Initial assessment (15 minutes):
  1. Confirm the alert: verify metric, affected prompt_families, and hardware_tag.
  2. Check deployment context: quant_profile, kv_cache_hit, batch size, runtime kernel revision.
  3. Correlate with infra alerts (GPU errors, memory pressure).
- Isolation (30 minutes):
  4. Reproduce with a deterministic probe replay locally (use replay-tool with probe_id).
  5. Run welcome/regression bisect if applicable (see bisect playbook link).
- Remediation (TBD cadence):
  6. For quant-induced regressions: switch to fallback profile (q_fallback) and validate.
  7. For model-quality regressions: escalate to model-owner and create a rollback if degradation persists > 2 hours.
  8. Document root cause and add a probe to the longtail suite if it's a novel failure mode.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
text:
[...]   8. Document root cause and add a probe to the longtail suite if it's a novel failure mode.


Ownership and responsibilities:
- Applied ML: define probe_families, baseline acceptance criteria, and annotation rubrics.
- Eng-SRE: ensure telemetry pipelines, encryption, retention and dashboards are functioning; own incident runbook execution if infrastructure is implicated.
- Runtime/Serving team: expose runtime tags (kernel revision, batch size) in telemetry and provide kernel rollback knobs.
- Data-privacy/legal: approve any tenant identifier exceptions and retention extensions.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
section_header: Appendix A - Example telemetry payload (redacted, human-readable)
text:
[...] - Data-privacy/legal: approve any tenant identifier exceptions and retention extensions.


Appendix A - Example telemetry payload (redacted, human-readable):
- probe_id = 11111111-2222-3333-4444-555555555555
- tenant_id = 9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08
- deployed_model = redwood-7b-v2-quant8
- deployment_variant = private
- quant_profile = q8_fast_fp16_fallback
- hardware_tag = a10g-24gb
- prompt_family = billing_faq_anchors_v3
- seed_context_hash = a0c6f0e8c7b1e3
- latency_ms = 420
- tokens_input = 42
- tokens_generated = 128
- top_level_metrics = coherence:0.85,hallucination:0.00,embed_cos_sim:0.71
- error_code = (empty)
- timestamp_utc = 2026-02-07T18:22:31Z

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
section_header: Appendix B - On-prem agent config (human-readable excerpt)
text:
[...] - timestamp_utc = 2026-02-07T18:22:31Z


Appendix B - On-prem agent config (human-readable excerpt):
agent name: shadow-forwarder
batch_size: 500
encrypt_with_kms: true
kms_key_alias: alias/customer-tenant-123
max_outbound_ops_per_min: 60
retention_days: 30
allowed_fields: probe_id, deployed_model, quant_profile, hardware_tag, prompt_family, seed_context_hash, latency_ms, tokens_input, tokens_generated, top_level_metrics, error_code, timestamp_utc

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
text:
[...] allowed_fields: probe_id, deployed_model, quant_profile, hardware_tag, prompt_family, seed_context_hash, latency_ms, tokens_input, tokens_generated, top_level_metrics, error_code, timestamp_utc


Common commands and quick references:
- Run a localized shadow replay: replay-tool --from probe_log --filter prompt_family=billing_faq_anchors_v3 --replay-count 200
- Fetch recent failing probes: rw-metrics query --metric shadow.hallucination.rate --since 2h --filter quant_profile=q8_fast_fp16_fallback --output csv
- Trigger temporary OpenEgress approval for extended sampling: infra-ticket create --type egress-exception --reason "Quant stress test" --owner eng-applied-ml

Governance and exceptions:
- Any deviation from the minimal payload requires a documented exception signed by Data-Privacy and the customer (if tenant-owned).
- Extended retention beyond 90 days requires Security signoff and must include a justification for forensic needs.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
section_header: Onboarding checklist to adopt the contract
text:
[...] - Extended retention beyond 90 days requires Security signoff and must include a justification for forensic needs.


Onboarding checklist to adopt the contract:
1. Add the telemetry contract to the deployment manifest (deployment-config).
2. Verify the agent config matches allowed_fields and encryption settings.
3. Execute a dry run with anonymized sample and verify metrics match local baseline within tolerance.
4. Schedule a 2-week shadow window and monitor.
5. If stable, include probes in daily longtail sweep and add new failing examples to probe-farm.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
text:
[...] 5. If stable, include probes in daily longtail sweep and add new failing examples to probe-farm.


Related references and docs:
- shadow-to-prod-eval-continuity-guidelines (crosswalk)
- quant-impact-synthesis-for-prompt-families (on quant analysis and profiles)
- regression-bisect-and-blame-assistant (triage automation)

Document change log:
- 2026-01-12 (Maya Chen): initial draft and contract specification.
- 2026-02-10 (Diego Alvarez): added sampling strategy and retention clarifications.
- 2026-02-15 (Priya Raman): updated metric thresholds and triage timings after pilot run.

title: Edge Shadow Telemetry Contract for Private and Dedicated Evaluations
text:
[...] - 2026-02-15 (Priya Raman): updated metric thresholds and triage timings after pilot run.


Appendix C - Error-code table (short):
- E000: OK
- E100: Model timeout
- E200: Runtime OOM
- E300: Quantization fallback triggered
- E400: PII-redaction-failed

If you have questions or would like a walkthrough, contact the Applied ML team at applied-ml@redwood.example.com or create an adoption ticket in the Eng Platform board.

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook

Summary
-------
This playbook defines a repeatable, automated evaluation and control strategy for diagnosing and preventing regressions caused by the KV (key-value) cache in Redwood's serving runtime. It combines synthetic probe design, production shadow comparisons, and a small battery of controlled ablation experiments to: 1) detect behavioral regressions introduced by cache changes, quantization, or kernel swaps; 2) attribute regressions to cache interactions; and 3) provide safe remediation and rollback criteria.

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...] This playbook defines a repeatable, automated evaluation and control strategy for diagnosing and preventing regressions caused by the KV (key-value) cache in Redwood's serving runtime. It combines synthetic probe design, production shadow comparisons, and a small battery of controlled ablation experiments to: 1) detect behavioral regressions introduced by cache changes, quantization, or kernel swaps; 2) attribute regressions to cache interactions; and 3) provide safe remediation and rollback criteria.


Scope
-----
- Applies to models and service routes that use prefix / KV caching (all autoregressive and chat-style routes).
- Intended for use during: model onboarding, quantized rollout, runtime kernel upgrades, and any change to cache policy.
- Not intended for model algorithmic changes unrelated to token caching (those have separate evals).

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...] - Not intended for model algorithmic changes unrelated to token caching (those have separate evals).


When to run
-----------
- Pre-promotion: mandatory for any model variant that will be deployed with cache enabled (required gate).
- On quantized or perf-optimized rollout (canary): run full playbook on canary traffic and shadow runs.
- Continuous: run lightweight probes daily on a stratified sample of production keys.

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...] - Continuous: run lightweight probes daily on a stratified sample of production keys.


Key definitions and assumptions
-------------------------------
- "Prefix" refers to any cached prior tokens including system prompts and shared conversation context.
- "Cache hit" means the runtime reused KV state for an attention window slice.
- Tests compare three configurations: cache=on (baseline), cache=off (cold), cache=partial (first-n tokens disabled).

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...] - Tests compare three configurations: cache=on (baseline), cache=off (cold), cache=partial (first-n tokens disabled).


Metrics and signals to monitor
------------------------------
1) Behavioral / quality signals (primary)
- Semantic drift score (dev-set BLEU/ROUGE proxy + embedding cosine delta): absolute drop > 4% triggers investigation.
- Response divergence rate: fraction of responses with token-level edit-distance > 10% between cache=on and cache=off. Alert if > 3%.
- Functioncall fidelity: mismatched function call name or argument schema between modes. Any mismatch is P1.

2) Operational signals (secondary)
- Latency delta (p50/p95) comparing cache on vs off. Expect p50 improvement; if p95 increases > 15% investigate.
- Token churn / repeated-subword inflation: sudden increases can indicate inconsistent tokenization or cache corruption.

3) Privacy / leakage checks
- Cross-user prefix contamination checks: 0 tolerated leaks. Automatic fail if any non-empty overlap of private tokens across user IDs is detected.

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...] - Cross-user prefix contamination checks: 0 tolerated leaks. Automatic fail if any non-empty overlap of private tokens across user IDs is detected.


Probe design and synthetic workloads
-----------------------------------
- Probe families:
  * Priming-probes: long system messages that exercise KV compression and eviction.
  * Prefix-perturbation probes: replay same user prompt with small prefix alterations to detect brittle reuse.
  * Stateful-dialog probes: multi-turn interactions that depend on KV for referent resolution.
  * Functioncall anchors: prompts designed to trigger deterministic functioncall outputs so mismatches are unambiguous.

- Probe dataset composition: 2k probes stratified by context length (0-256, 256-1024, 1024-8192) and intent type (instruction, QA, multi-turn, functioncall).

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...] - Probe dataset composition: 2k probes stratified by context length (0-256, 256-1024, 1024-8192) and intent type (instruction, QA, multi-turn, functioncall).


Experiment matrix (minimum cells)
--------------------------------
- Configurations: [cache=on, cache=off, cache=partial(n=64)] x [model variant: float32, int8/quantized] x [traffic: synthetic, 1% shadow production].
- Measurements: run each cell with seeds=3, sample size per cell >= 1k probes, capture logits, tokens, KV-access traces, and timing.

Acceptance criteria (example gate)
----------------------------------
- No P1 behavioral mismatches (functioncall fidelity, toxic output regression).
- Semantic drift score delta <= 4% vs baseline (cache=off).
- Response divergence rate <= 3%.
- Latency p95 must not increase by more than 10% on canary traffic.
- Zero cross-user contamination events.

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
section_header: Runbook: triage steps when a cache-related regression is detected
text:
[...] - Zero cross-user contamination events.


Runbook: triage steps when a cache-related regression is detected
----------------------------------------------------------------
1) Confirm signal: reproduce locally with the failing probe set across the three cache modes.
2) Collect artifacts: full request/response pairs, KV traces, kernel logs, quantization artifacts, and deterministic seed.
3) Narrow the change set: is the regression associated with (a) cache policy change, (b) kernel swap, (c) quantization variant, (d) eviction/compaction regression? Use binary bisect on deployments.
4) Quick rollback path: revert to last known-good cache policy or kernel; if regression persists, disable cache for the route and escalate.
5) Root-cause experiments:
   - Re-run targeted probes with increased context length to stress eviction logic.
   - Replay the exact token sequence with and without compressed KV encodings.
   - If quantization suspected, test float32 vs quantized weights with cache on/off.
6) Remediation options:
   - Patch: fix KV encoding/decoding mismatch or eviction bug.
   - Configuration: reduce cache window size or switch to partial caching strategy.
   - Long term: add sentinel checks in runtime to detect corrupted KV entries.

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...]    - Long term: add sentinel checks in runtime to detect corrupted KV entries.


Automation recommendations
--------------------------
- CI integration: add a KV-cache-aware test stage to model promotion pipeline that runs full matrix on PRs touching runtime or quantization code.
- Canary automation: when enabling cache on a canary, automatically run the playbook with synthetic probes + 1% shadow traffic and block rollout on failures.
- Alerting: create dedicated SLOs and alerts for the primary behavioral signals with playbook links in the alert body.

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...] - Alerting: create dedicated SLOs and alerts for the primary behavioral signals with playbook links in the alert body.


Canary / rollout policy (example)
---------------------------------
1) Stage 0: internal synthetic only, pass acceptance criteria.
2) Stage 1: 0.5% shadow production traffic + synthetic; hold for 24h, require no P1 and metric stability.
3) Stage 2: 1-5% live routing with revert-on-failure; monitor for 72h.
4) Stage 3: graduated ramp with automated health checks every 1h.

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
section_header: Ownership and artifacts to attach to a run
text:
[...] 4) Stage 3: graduated ramp with automated health checks every 1h.


Ownership and artifacts to attach to a run
-----------------------------------------
- Owner: ML Infra / Serving Runtime team (primary), Model Onboarding (secondary).
- Mandatory artifacts for promotion: probe dataset ID, probe results snapshot (CSV), KV trace archive, canary logs, diff against last-good.
- Ticket template: include incident id, failing-probe-ids, command to reproduce, and candidate fix patch link.

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...] - Ticket template: include incident id, failing-probe-ids, command to reproduce, and candidate fix patch link.


Appendix A — sample test YAML
----------------------------
```yaml
playbook_version: 1.2
probe_set_id: kv-probes-v3
matrix:
  - cache_policy: on
    model_variant: float32
  - cache_policy: off
    model_variant: float32
  - cache_policy: partial_n64
    model_variant: int8
seeds: [1001,1002,1003]
min_samples_per_cell: 1000
artifacts_to_collect: [responses.jsonl, kv_traces.tar.gz, logs.tgz, timings.csv]
```

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook
text:
[...] ```


Appendix B — quick CLI examples
--------------------------------
- Run local probe runner (docker):
  docker run --rm -v $(pwd)/probes:/data redwood/eval-runner:latest \"--probes /data/kv-probes.jsonl --config /data/kv-test.yaml\"

- Query results summary (example):
  python tools/kv_eval_summary.py --results s3://redwood-evals/kv/2026-01-12/summary.json

title: KV cache behavioral eval and control playbook

[...]   python tools/kv_eval_summary.py --results s3://redwood-evals/kv/2026-01-12/summary.json


Related pages
-------------
- Link to model onboarding checklist: /applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/inference-readiness-eval-matrix-and-promotion-playbook-2026
- Link to quantization audit: /applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/kv-cache-quant-stability-audit-playbook-2025

Contact / escalation
--------------------
- Primary oncall: infra-ml-sre rotation (see team wiki).
- If P1 behavioral regression: page infra-ml-sre and model-quality leads and open an immediate incident with priority tag KV-CACHE-REGRESS.

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration

Summary
-------
This document describes the local + CI inference evaluation harness used to simulate Redwood serving behavior (routing, batching, KV/prefix cache effects, quantization profiles, and model fallbacks) in offline evaluations. The goal is to provide evaluators, ML engineers, and SREs with a reproducible way to predict production unit economics and quality regressions prior to rollout.

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...] This document describes the local + CI inference evaluation harness used to simulate Redwood serving behavior (routing, batching, KV/prefix cache effects, quantization profiles, and model fallbacks) in offline evaluations. The goal is to provide evaluators, ML engineers, and SREs with a reproducible way to predict production unit economics and quality regressions prior to rollout.


Goals
-----
- Provide a deterministic local runtime that mirrors production-serving decisions (routing policy, batching windows, and cache hit/miss behavior).
- Surface cost and latency estimates that correlate with hosted/Dedicated telemetry.
- Enable CI gating for model onboarding and quantization changes with meaningful SLO-style thresholds.
- Make it easy to run differential experiments (A/B between model/quantization variants) with the same harness.

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...] - Make it easy to run differential experiments (A/B between model/quantization variants) with the same harness.


Scope and non-goals
-------------------
- In scope: request routing simulation, batching emulation, prefix/KV cache semantics, quantization audio, model-variant fallbacks, synthetic load profiles, and metric aggregation compatible with the eval dashboard.
- Out of scope: full GPU kernel-level profiling (use eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook for that), or end-to-end network emulation of multi-region failovers.

Architecture overview
---------------------
1) Harness components
   - controller: orchestrates simulation scenarios and injects routing policies.
   - worker simulator: single-process inference executor that runs model artifacts via the local inference shim (see Local runtime). It supports a pluggable model backend: ONNX-RT, torchscript, and a lightweight quantized runtime (qsim).
   - cache emulator: in-memory KV that supports TTLs, LRU, and prefix caches with configurable hit-rate curves.
   - load generator: replays production trace snippets or generates synthetic arrivals (Poisson, bursty, periodic).
   - metrics collector: emits the same metric names we use in the production console (r/latency_p50_ms, r/latency_p95_ms, r/tokens_per_sec, cost/est_per_k_token, eval/quality_wer).

2) Data flow (textual diagram)
   - [input events] -> controller (routing decisions) -> cache emulator (hit/miss) -> worker simulator (batching & model execution) -> metrics collector

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...]    - [input events] -> controller (routing decisions) -> cache emulator (hit/miss) -> worker simulator (batching & model execution) -> metrics collector


Local runtime and dev setup
---------------------------
Prerequisites
- macOS/Linux with docker and Python 3.10+
- redis (optional for persistent cache experiments)
- access to model artifacts (local-path or internal artifact registry)

Quickstart (local)
1) Clone the repo: git clone git@github.com:redwood-inference/eval-harness.git
2) Create virtualenv and install: python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt
3) Start harness controller in dev mode: ./harnessctl start --mode=local --config examples/local-dev.yaml
4) Run a smoke scenario: ./harnessctl run-scenario simple-chat --trace examples/traces/guest-chat-2025-11.json
Expected output: metrics printed to stdout and written to results/ with a scenario-level summary JSON.

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...] Expected output: metrics printed to stdout and written to results/ with a scenario-level summary JSON.


Example configuration (YAML)
---------------------------
# examples/local-dev.yaml
controller:
  routing_policy: 'latency-first'
  fallback_order: ['gptx-small', 'gptx-int8', 'gptx-qa-tiny']
  max_concurrent_requests: 32
batching:
  enabled: true
  max_window_ms: 15
  max_batch_tokens: 4096
cache_emulator:
  type: 'prefix'
  default_ttl_s: 3600
  hit_rate_curve: 'decay:0.85;burstiness:0.1'
quant_profiles:
  - name: 'int8-fast'
    backend: 'qsim'
    topk: 256
    scaling: 'per-channel'

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...]     scaling: 'per-channel'


CLI usage examples
------------------
- Run long simulation and output metrics: 
  ./harnessctl run-scenario production-snapshot-2026-01 --out results/prod-snapshot-2026.json --duration 2h
- Run variant comparison (A vs B):
  ./harnessctl compare --left config/gptx-1.yaml --right config/gptx-1-int8.yaml --trace examples/traces/high-throughput.json
- Emit a deterministic trace slice for debugging:
  ./harnessctl sample-trace --seed 42 --outfile debug/trace-seed-42.json

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...]   ./harnessctl sample-trace --seed 42 --outfile debug/trace-seed-42.json


Metric definitions and thresholds
--------------------------------
Below are the canonical metric names collected by the harness and suggested thresholds for CI gating. These align to console metrics (prefix r/ and cost/). Use these thresholds in PR checks for onboarding and quantization changes.

Table (textual)
- metric: r/latency_p95_ms  | description: simulated 95th percentile latency including batching overhead | CI gate: must not increase by > 15% vs baseline
- metric: r/latency_p50_ms  | description: median latency | CI gate: must not increase by > 10%
- metric: cost/est_per_k_token | description: estimated cost per 1k tokens using current batching & quantization profile | CI gate: absolute increase must be < $0.10 (or relative < 20%)
- metric: eval/quality_ae (average edit distance) | description: quality delta vs baseline | CI gate: quality delta must be within -0.02 (i.e., no more than 2% drop)

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...] - metric: eval/quality_ae (average edit distance) | description: quality delta vs baseline | CI gate: quality delta must be within -0.02 (i.e., no more than 2% drop)


Quality guardrails and CI gating
-------------------------------
Use the harness to produce a per-commit artifact that CI stores alongside unit tests. The onboarding pipeline (applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding) should include the following steps:
1) Run small-sweep (10k requests) against the onboarding dataset; collect metrics.
2) Run targeted prompt-regression suite (see prompt-sets-and-benchmarks/regression-suite) and compute aggregate quality metrics.
3) If any CI gate from the previous section fails, create a triage ticket with attached harness run artifact and set label: regression/auto-fail.

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...] 3) If any CI gate from the previous section fails, create a triage ticket with attached harness run artifact and set label: regression/auto-fail.


Reproducible experiments and seed management
-------------------------------------------
- All stochastic components (sampling, dropout mimics, trace sampling) must accept a --seed parameter and embed the seed into the scenario result JSON.
- For fairness across runs, harness supports "deterministic execution mode" that removes timing noise (useful for pure model-quality comparisons).

Quantization-aware evaluation
----------------------------
- The harness supports plugging in quantization profiles: symmetric/asymmetric, per-channel scaling, and custom dequantization shims.
- To approximate perf differences, qsim uses a cost model calibrated against eng-serving-runtime/gpu-fleet-and-capacity benchmarks. Calibration parameters are stored in examples/calibration/int8-calibration.json.
- Recommended workflow for a quantization rollout:
  1) Run purely functional regression (deterministic mode) with quantized model to validate quality.
  2) Run throughput-latency simulation with realistic trace and cache settings.
  3) Run CI gating and, if passed, schedule a limited Dedicated rollout with canary traffic.

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
section_header: Example result snippet (JSON-like, for human readability)
text:
[...]   3) Run CI gating and, if passed, schedule a limited Dedicated rollout with canary traffic.


Differential analysis and result artifacts
----------------------------------------
- Output format: each scenario emits a result JSON with the following top-level keys recorded as strings in the artifact: run_id, seed, variant, metrics_csv_path, summary_table_path, raw_traces_path. (CI systems will parse these keys; see example below.)
- Use harnessctl compare to produce a delta report: a markdown file that lists metric deltas, significance test (bootstrap by default), and a plot bundle (pngs).

Example result snippet (JSON-like, for human readability)
------------------------------------------------------
{"run_id": "supplychain-2026-02-04T12:00Z", "variant": "gptx-1-int8", "seed": "42", "metrics_csv_path": "results/supplychain/metrics.csv", "summary_table_path": "results/supplychain/summary.md", "raw_traces_path": "results/supplychain/traces.json"}

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
section_header: Ownership, rollout checklist, and runbook links
text:
[...] {"run_id": "supplychain-2026-02-04T12:00Z", "variant": "gptx-1-int8", "seed": "42", "metrics_csv_path": "results/supplychain/metrics.csv", "summary_table_path": "results/supplychain/summary.md", "raw_traces_path": "results/supplychain/traces.json"}


Ownership, rollout checklist, and runbook links
------------------------------------------------
- Owner: Applied ML - Eval Platform (team alias: amle-harness)
- Oncall: applied-ml-and-evals rotation (see eng-oncall for schedule)
- Rollout checklist (short):
  1) Verify harness calibration is within tolerance using examples/calibration/benchmark-2026-01.json.
  2) Attach harness artifact to PR and confirm CI gates.
  3) Run the limited canary in Dedicated for 24 hours with traffic caps and compare live metrics to harness predictions.
  4) Gradual rollout with model pin and rollback plan.

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...]   4) Gradual rollout with model pin and rollback plan.


Troubleshooting common failures
------------------------------
- Symptom: latency_p95 much higher than harness prediction.
  - Check: did CI use a production-like cache hit-rate? Review cache_emulator.hit_rate_curve.
  - Action: re-run with increased cache realism OR annotate the mismatch in the triage ticket.
- Symptom: quality metric regresses only in throughput scenarios.
  - Check: batching window and token truncation policies. Some models behave differently under batched padding.
  - Action: test with deterministic mode and per-example isolation to confirm root cause.

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
section_header: Appendix: recommended default thresholds (for local dev and CI)
text:
[...]   - Action: test with deterministic mode and per-example isolation to confirm root cause.


Appendix: recommended default thresholds (for local dev and CI)
-------------------------------------------------------------
- p50 latency: baseline * 1.1
- p95 latency: baseline * 1.15
- cost/est_per_k_token: baseline + $0.05 OR baseline * 1.2 (whichever is smaller)
- quality_ae: no worse than baseline - 0.02

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...] - quality_ae: no worse than baseline - 0.02


Related reading and links
------------------------
- Model onboarding checklist: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/onboarding-playbook
- Runtime architecture notes: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/overview
- Quantization profiles guide: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/README

title: Serving Simulation Eval Harness: local and CI integration
text:
[...] - Quantization profiles guide: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/README


Change log
----------
- 2025-11-12: initial draft (Camille Ortega)
- 2026-02-04: added quantization calibration notes and CI gating thresholds (Aisha Patel)

Contact and feedback
--------------------
For questions or to request new simulation features, open a ticket in the applied-ml-and-evals backlog or contact the harness owner team (amle-harness@redwood.internal).

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol

Summary
-------
This document defines a reproducible protocol and decision matrix for evaluating throughput vs. fidelity tradeoffs for candidate serving configurations (model variants, quantization profiles, batching policies, and routing tiers). It provides acceptance gates used by model-onboarding, infra, and SRE to decide whether a configuration can be promoted to: hosted baseline, dedicated pool, or private rollout. The goal is to make acceptance deterministic, measurable, and automatable.

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...] This document defines a reproducible protocol and decision matrix for evaluating throughput vs. fidelity tradeoffs for candidate serving configurations (model variants, quantization profiles, batching policies, and routing tiers). It provides acceptance gates used by model-onboarding, infra, and SRE to decide whether a configuration can be promoted to: hosted baseline, dedicated pool, or private rollout. The goal is to make acceptance deterministic, measurable, and automatable.


Scope and audience
------------------
- Scope: LLM inference configurations tested in the Redwood eval-harness and serving-simulation environments. Includes hosted, dedicated, and private serving modes.
- Audience: Applied ML engineers, serving runtime owners, SRE, and product owners conducting promotions and rollouts.

Why this protocol exists
------------------------
Teams frequently make tradeoffs (e.g., int8 quantization + increased batch size) that increase system throughput and lower per-token cost but may reduce output fidelity. Without a consistent accept/reject rulebook, rollouts cause regressions or unnecessary conservatism. This protocol standardizes:
- a throughput/fidelity taxonomy
- measurement methodology (inputs, metrics, statistics)
- promotion gates and rollback triggers
- automation knobs for CI/CD integration

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...] - automation knobs for CI/CD integration


Definitions
-----------
- Throughput (T): tokens/sec or requests/sec measured end-to-end under the target serving config and arrival pattern. We report tokens/sec at stable-state and 95th percentile request throughput.
- Fidelity (F): application-level quality signals computed against the production prompt set. Typical signals: semantic-similarity (embedding cosine), exact match (EM) for extraction tasks, BLEU/ROUGE for summarization, and a Response Stability Index (RSI) used across Redwood.
- Baseline: the currently-promoted configuration (model + quant + batching) used as the reference for delta calculations.

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
section_header: Throughput buckets and fidelity budget matrix
text:
[...] - Baseline: the currently-promoted configuration (model + quant + batching) used as the reference for delta calculations.


Throughput buckets and fidelity budget matrix
--------------------------------------------
We classify candidate configs into buckets and apply corresponding fidelity budgets. Buckets are evaluated at the stable-traffic point (defined below).
Table: Bucket | Target throughput (req/s) | Allowed fidelity delta (vs baseline) | Example use-cases
- LOW | < 50 req/s | delta_F <= 0.03 | low-volume prototypes, experiments
- MEDIUM | 50–500 req/s | delta_F <= 0.02 | product features with moderate load
- HIGH | 500–2,000 req/s | delta_F <= 0.01 | core product routes, user-facing completion
- EXTREME | > 2,000 req/s | delta_F <= 0.005 | high-throughput embeddings, batch reprocessing

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...] - HIGH | 500–2,000 req/s | delta_F <= 0.01 | core product routes, user-facing completion
- EXTREME | > 2,000 req/s | delta_F <= 0.005 | high-throughput embeddings, batch reprocessing

Notes:
- delta_F is expressed in normalized units appropriate to the metric (e.g., cosine similarity delta, BLEU delta). Teams must map each fidelity metric into a normalized [0,1] scale in their benchmark definitions.
- For composite workloads (mixed NLU + generation), compute a weighted average fidelity delta using traffic-weighted slice weights (see Measurement Methodology).

Measurement methodology
-----------------------
1) Testbed and isolation
   - Use the Redwood eval-harness in a dedicated test cluster (shadow-serving mode) with isolated GPU pool to avoid noisy neighbors.
   - Seed the harness with the production prompt catalog subset (see Prompt Set Selection).
2) Traffic profile
   - Use two phases: warmup (5 minutes) and stable run (20 minutes). Warmup allows KV cache and batching mechanisms to reach steady state.
   - Arrival distribution: Poisson for request arrival with configurable lambda to hit desired req/s. Sequence length distribution must mirror production percentiles (p50, p90, p99).
3) Metrics to record
   - Throughput: tokens/sec, requests/sec (mean and 95/99).
   - Latency: p50/p95/p99 per-request time-to-first-token and time-to-last-token.
   - Cost proxy: average GPU-seconds per 1000 tokens.
   - Fidelity: per-sample similarity scores (embedding cos), task-specific scores (BLEU/ROUGE/EM), and RSI.
   - Stability: token-level divergence rate (fraction of samples with top-3 token differences vs baseline).
4) Statistical test
   - For fidelity: run paired bootstrap on the prompt catalog (N=10k resamples) to produce a 95% confidence interval for delta_F. The candidate passes if the upper bound of the two-sided 95% CI <= allowed delta for the bucket.
   - For throughput and latency: require p95 latency less than the SLO target for the route (default SLOs listed in Observability Standards). Throughput must be >= bucket lower-bound for 90% of runs.

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...]    - For throughput and latency: require p95 latency less than the SLO target for the route (default SLOs listed in Observability Standards). Throughput must be >= bucket lower-bound for 90% of runs.


Prompt Set Selection
--------------------
- Use the production prompt catalog slice with stratification over slices that represent different behavior modes: short completions, long-form, instruction-following, extraction, and embeddings.
- Minimum sample sizes: 2,000 examples for single-task workloads; for multi-slice workloads, ensure each slice has >= 500 examples.

Promotion gates (automated)
---------------------------
1) Smoke gate (automated CI):
   - Run a 5-minute smoke harness with a small canonical prompt set (N=200). Pass criteria: no p99 latency regression > 2x baseline and median fidelity delta <= 0.01.
2) Canary gate (gradual rollout):
   - Gradual traffic ramp: 1%, 5%, 10%, 25% each 30 minutes or longer until at target. At each step, run a short fidelity check (N=500 stratified) and telemetry checks (error rate, p95 latency).
   - Automated abort if: fidelity CI upper bound exceeds bucket threshold or error rate increases > 3x baseline for 10 minutes.
3) Full promotion gate:
   - Full 20-minute stable run as defined in Measurement Methodology. Pass if fidelity, throughput, and latency requirements satisfied and no new anomalies in logs/traces.

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...]    - Full 20-minute stable run as defined in Measurement Methodology. Pass if fidelity, throughput, and latency requirements satisfied and no new anomalies in logs/traces.


Rollback and regression triage triggers
-------------------------------------
- Immediate rollback triggers (automated):
  - Production fidelity alert: continuous monitoring detects an alert where a rolling 1-hour fidelity metric deviates by more than the allowed delta with p<0.01.
  - Error spike: 5x increase in 5xx error rate sustained for 10 minutes.
  - Latency SLO breach: p95 latency > 2x SLO for 15 minutes.
- Triage playbook summary:
  1. Capture failing sample set (use the fidelity-alerting hook to store inputs and outputs).
  2. Run replay in the eval-harness with both baseline and candidate configs. Produce diffs (token-level and embedding diffs).
  3. If the issue is traceable to quantization/precision (see Quantization Sensitivity Checklist below), treat as high priority for rollback to previous quant-profile.
  4. Open a regression investigation issue with tags: regression, fidelity, rollout-id, model-version. Assign owner from oncall rotation.

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...]   4. Open a regression investigation issue with tags: regression, fidelity, rollout-id, model-version. Assign owner from oncall rotation.


Quantization Sensitivity Checklist (short)
-----------------------------------------
- Confirm per-layer activation ranges were collected with representative data (not synthetic truncations).
- Re-run calibration with variable sequence lengths; some activations are length-dependent.
- If tokenizer-sensitive failure: compare tokenization deltas and map to prompt failure modes.
- Fallback option: promote with mixed precision (e.g., int8 for attention + fp16 for MLP) and re-evaluate.

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...] - Fallback option: promote with mixed precision (e.g., int8 for attention + fp16 for MLP) and re-evaluate.


Automation and CI integration
-----------------------------
- CI job: eval-throughput-fidelity (parametrized by candidate-id, baseline-id, bucket)
- Exit codes:
  - 0: pass smoke/canary/full gates
  - 2: fidelity fail
  - 3: latency fail
  - 4: infrastructure/noise (inconclusive)
- Artifact storage: harness produces a standardized results.json and artifacts/ (diffs, embeddings, raw traces). Store under s3://redwood-eval-artifacts/{candidate-id}/{run-id}/
- Example CLI:
  python -m redwood_eval.run --config configs/throughput_fidelity.yaml --candidate-id v2-int8-512batch --baseline-id v2-fp16 --bucket HIGH

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...]   python -m redwood_eval.run --config configs/throughput_fidelity.yaml --candidate-id v2-int8-512batch --baseline-id v2-fp16 --bucket HIGH


Example benchmark config (YAML)
------------------------------
- name: high-throughput-embed-eval
  bucket: HIGH
  prompt_set: /datasets/prod/embeddings/stratified_20k
  arrival: poisson
  target_reqs_per_sec: 750
  seq_length_percentiles: {p50:64, p90:512, p99:1024}
  metrics: [embedding_cosine, tokens_per_second, p95_latency]

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...]   metrics: [embedding_cosine, tokens_per_second, p95_latency]


Sample results.json snippet (human-readable)
-------------------------------------------
{
  "candidate_id": "v2-int8-512batch",
  "baseline_id": "v2-fp16",
  "bucket": "HIGH",
  "throughput_tokens_sec": 15400,
  "p95_latency_ms": 420,
  "fidelity_delta_embedding_cosine": 0.007,
  "fidelity_delta_bleu": 0.003,
  "fidelity_ci_upper": 0.009,
  "pass": true
}

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...] }


Operational runbook (quick)
---------------------------
1) Alert fires: check alert dashboard and rollout-id.
2) Pull artifacts from s3 for the offending candidate-run.
3) Execute replay harness locally: python -m redwood_eval.replay --run s3://... --baseline baseline-id --candidate candidate-id
4) If high-confidence fidelity regression: rollback via rollout controller (PATCH route to set active config = baseline) and notify stakeholders.
5) Postmortem: owner creates a short triage note with root cause and remediation within 72 hours.

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...] 5) Postmortem: owner creates a short triage note with root cause and remediation within 72 hours.


Decision examples and rationale
------------------------------
- Example A: int8 + aggressive batching yields 2.5x throughput for HIGH bucket with delta_F=0.012 and CI upper=0.015 -> fail. Rationale: exceeds allowed delta (0.01).
- Example B: mixed-precision reduces cost 25% with delta_F=0.006 and CI upper=0.008 in HIGH bucket -> pass and promote to dedicated pool canary.

Responsibilities and ownership
------------------------------
- Applied ML: owns prompt catalog and fidelity metrics mapping.
- Serving runtime: owns harness environment and kernel/quant profiles.
- SRE: owns canary rollout orchestration and automated rollback tooling.
- Product/PO: sets bucket assignment for routes and approves exception requests.

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...] - Product/PO: sets bucket assignment for routes and approves exception requests.


Open questions and extensions
---------------------------
- Automating slice-weight discovery from traffic telemetry to reduce manual weight errors.
- Standardizing an embedding-threshold mapping that converts cosine delta to user-visible metric loss.

FAQ
---
Q: Can we relax fidelity thresholds for non-user-facing internal jobs?
A: Yes — buckets are guidelines. For internal batch workloads set bucket to EXTREME with negotiated thresholds recorded in the promotion ticket.

Q: What if throughput fluctuates across runs?
A: Re-run harness up to 3 times. If variance > 10% between runs, mark the run as inconclusive and require infrastructure noise triage before promotion.

title: Throughput–Fidelity Tradeoff Matrix and Acceptance Protocol
text:
[...] A: Re-run harness up to 3 times. If variance > 10% between runs, mark the run as inconclusive and require infrastructure noise triage before promotion.


Related pages
-------------
- Serving simulation harness: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/serving-simulation-eval-harness-2026
- Quantization benchmarks: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/workload-transferability-and-quant-benchmarks-2026

Appendix: scoring example
------------------------
- Composite fidelity score S = sum(slice_weight_i * normalized_metric_i). Normalization docs and code are in the eval-harness repo under redwood_eval/normalizers.py.

If you plan to use this protocol for an upcoming model promotion, create a promotion ticket and attach: candidate id, baseline id, bucket selection, and three harness run artifacts. The eval-harness team will help validate the configuration during the first promotion.

title: functioncall-emergent-regression-simulator-2026
text:
functioncall-emergent-regression-simulator-2026

Overview

This document describes the Functioncall Emergent Regression Simulator (FERS) — a reproducible harness and failure-library designed to synthesize and stress emergent failure modes for function-calling models during shadow, canary, and quantized rollouts. FERS is intended to detect behavioral regressions that do not appear in standard prompt suites: e.g., degraded function argument extraction, hallucinated tool endpoints, corrupted JSON shapes, and latent tokenization-induced misalignment under quantization.

Goals

- Provide a catalog of synthetic failure generators targeting functioncall interfaces.
- Define a runtime harness (eval-meson) that can inject failures into shadow traffic and produce deterministic failure traces.
- Supply acceptance criteria and automated triage signals to gate rollouts (shadow -> canary -> prod) for models with functioncall usage.
- Standardize remediation and rollforward steps for regressions discovered by the simulator.

Scope and applicability

- Applies to models exposed via Redwood API's chat/functioncall surface and internal rerankers that emit structured payloads.
- Intended for model onboarding, quant migration, and canary validations across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments.
- Not intended as a replacement for human annotator evaluations; FERS augments evaluator workflows by surfacing falsifiable, reproducible failure traces.

Definitions

- Failure generator: deterministic or stochastic transformer that mutates prompts, responses, or function signatures to simulate a class of regression.
- Anchor probe: a canonical prompt-and-response pair used for day-zero comparison and SLI calculation.
- Emergent regression: behavior change that appears only under composite conditions (e.g., long context + quant + functioncall).

Failure-library (catalog)

Below is an excerpt of the curated failure generators. The full library is in repo: git://infra/eval-meson/fers/failure-library.

| id | target | description | intensity knobs | expected signal |
|----|--------|-------------|-----------------|----------------|
| FCF-01 | function-args | Drop a random required arg from the model-invoked JSON payload | drop_rate (0.0-1.0) | increase in missing-arg errors; schema-violation count up |
| FCF-02 | json-escape | Inject mis-escaped control characters into string args | char_freq, char_set | parser exceptions or silent truncation |
| FCF-03 | endpoint-halluc | Replace real tool names with plausible-but-invalid endpoints | substitution_map_size | 4xx/5xx tool routing anomalies; increased fallback triggers |
| FCF-04 | token-shift | Shift token boundaries to simulate tokenization drift (quant-sensitive) | shift_window, seed | coherence drop on long-context slices; KV-cache misses |
| FCF-05 | type-corrupt | Replace numeric args with strings and vice-versa | corruption_rate | type-mismatch traces in downstream services |

Design and architecture

High-level components: eval-meson (harness), probeforge (probe generator), sidecar-evaluator (per-tenant evaluator-sidecar), and synth-catalog (failure library).

- eval-meson: Gateway that replays or mirrors shadow traffic and coordinates injection via a pluggable failure step graph. Supports deterministic seeds for reproducible traces.
- probeforge: DSL for composing multi-step probes (priming -> invoke -> verify). Probe definitions are stored as YAML manifests and can target live shadow traffic, synthetic sessions, or archived traces.
- sidecar-evaluator: Runs near the serving tier (dedicated/private) and evaluates functioncall stability at socket-level to catch protocol anomalies before they hit upstream tooling.
- synth-catalog: Versioned package of failure generators with semantic versioning. Each generator includes a risk score and recommended mitigation patterns.

Operational flow (high level)

1) Author creates a probe manifest in probeforge (see example below).
2) eval-meson schedules the probe against a shadow cohort or synthetic session set.
3) Failure generators run according to configured intensity; traces are recorded to S3 and the eval-meson results store.
4) Automated analyzers compute SLI deltas vs. anchors and emit triage tickets if thresholds are breached.
5) Triage flow routes to the model-oncall and quant-steering desk with recommended rollback/mitigation actions.

Probe manifest example (YAML)

---
name: invoice-extraction-longcontext
mode: shadow
seed: 20260610-invoice-001
steps:
  - priming: file://probes/primers/invoice-context-12k.txt
  - call: { model: gpt-4x-functioncall-v0, max_tokens: 256, function: extract_invoice }
  - failure_inject:
      id: FCF-04
