      params: { shift_window: 128, seed: 42 }
  - verify: { schema: file://schemas/invoice-v2.json, allow_missing_fields: false }

Running the simulator (CLI examples)

title: functioncall-emergent-regression-simulator-2026
section_header: Schedule a multichoort run with increasing intensity
text:
[...] Running the simulator (CLI examples)


# Run a single probe against archived trace set
$ eval-meson run --probe invoice-extraction-longcontext --archive s3://eval-archives/tenant-a/2026-06-01 --seed 42

# Schedule a multichoort run with increasing intensity
$ eval-meson schedule --manifest ./manifests/quant-compat-multi.yaml --cohorts tenant-a,tenant-b --ramp 0.1:0.5:1.0 --notify --deadline 2026-06-20

Acceptance criteria and gating rules

title: functioncall-emergent-regression-simulator-2026
section_header: All rollouts that include functioncall-capable models must pass the following checks in the shadow stage before canary
text:
[...] Acceptance criteria and gating rules


All rollouts that include functioncall-capable models must pass the following checks in the shadow stage before canary:

- Anchor coherence SLI: delta <= 1.5% against promoted baseline on the anchor probes after 1000 probe runs.
- Schema conformance: no more than 0.2% schema violations on critical fields (invoice_id, total_amount) across the shadow cohort.
- Tool stability: < 0.5% increase in tool fallback rate when endpoint-hallucation generators are enabled at intensity=0.25.
- Token-shift sensitivity: coherence p95 drop < 2% under FCF-04 intensity=0.5 for long-context probes.

If any single gating rule fails, the model must not proceed to canary and the model owner must open a regression investigation within 1 business day.

Monitoring, alerts and triage signals

title: functioncall-emergent-regression-simulator-2026
functioncall-emergent-regression-simulator-2026
text:
[...] - Reproducibility score (0-1): fraction of injected failures that reproduce across seeds (low score indicates non-deterministic failure)


Alerting thresholds (examples):

- P0: schema violation rate >= 1% sustained for 5 minutes -> page oncall + create incident ticket.
- P1: anchor SLI delta >= 3% for 30 minutes -> notify model owner and schedule regression run.
- P2: reproducibility score < 0.3 on high-severity failure generators -> send to evaluation desk for manual analysis.

Automated triage rules

- If schema violation rate > 0.5% && reproducibility >= 0.75 -> auto-open a regression-investigation ticket with attached failure traces and suggested blame candidates (quant, tokenizer, prompt-preprocessor).
- If token-shift sensitivity triggers with low tool fallback changes -> flag quant as likely cause and pin a quant_shadow cohort for deeper replay.

Investigation and remediation checklist (numbered runbook)

1. Confirm reproducibility: re-run failing probe with seed and verify failure trace (eval-meson replay --trace <id>).
2. Compare traces across promoted baseline and candidate model; capture tokenization diffs.
3. If tokenization drift suspected, run token-shift diagnostic (probeforge diagnostics token-diff).
4. If quant-sensitive: route to quant-steering; run mixed-precision shadow with FCF-04 intensity sweep.
5. If function-arg or JSON corruption: run sidecar-evaluator at socket level and check intermediate tool ingress logs.
6. Apply mitigations: prompt-rewrite, schema-relaxation (short-term), or quant rollback (if severity high).
7. Re-run gated probes and ensure all acceptance criteria pass before resuming rollout.

Owner and escalation matrix

- Library owner: Sasha Kim (applied-evals).
- Harness owner: Marcus Liu (eval infra).
- Quant desk: Diego Ramirez (applied-ml).
- Oncall: model-oncall rotation (see team wiki for schedule).

Change controls and release cadence

- synth-catalog updates require a PR with: description of new generator, risk score, two reviewers, and reproducibility report (>= 10 deterministic seeds).
- Minor patch releases (catalog patch) on a two-week cadence. Major changes that affect gating thresholds require sign-off from quant desk and evaluation committee and a 1-week canary run across two tenants.

Appendix A — Example failure trace snippet (trimmed JSON)

{
  "probe_id": "invoice-extraction-longcontext",
  "seed": 42,
  "failure_gen": "FCF-04",
  "events": [
    {"t":"2026-06-10T12:01:02Z","stage":"call","tokens_before": 10234},
    {"t":"2026-06-10T12:01:03Z","stage":"inject","desc":"shifted tokens at 100-228"},
    {"t":"2026-06-10T12:01:04Z","stage":"verify","result":"schema_violation","field":"total_amount"}
  ]
}

Appendix B — Quickcheck list before submitting a promo PR

- [ ] Add anchor probe + baseline results to PR description.
- [ ] Include reproducibility score and failure traces for all enabled generators.
- [ ] Confirm no schema regressions on critical fields (invoice_id, user_id, total).
- [ ] Run token-shift diagnostic at intensity 0.5 and include report.

Contacts and references

- Eval harness repo: git://infra/eval-meson
- Failure library index: git://infra/eval-meson/fers/failure-library/index.yaml
- Probe DSL docs: confluence://applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/probe-dsl-reference-2025

Changelog

title: functioncall-emergent-regression-simulator-2026
text:
[...] 
Changelog

- 2026-02-15: Initial draft by Sasha Kim.
- 2026-04-02: Added token-shift generator and acceptance thresholds (reviewers: Anya Patel).
- 2026-06-10: Finalized gating rules and automation triage flows (publish).

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol

Summary
-------
This document defines a company-wide protocol for running hybrid shadow evaluations across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments using a federated orchestration model, with a strong focus on privacy-aware probe retention and telemetry aggregation. It standardizes: probe manifest formats, federation coordination patterns, privacy budget enforcement (DP), retention tiers, aggregation windows, and operational runbooks for triage and promotion.

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] This document defines a company-wide protocol for running hybrid shadow evaluations across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments using a federated orchestration model, with a strong focus on privacy-aware probe retention and telemetry aggregation. It standardizes: probe manifest formats, federation coordination patterns, privacy budget enforcement (DP), retention tiers, aggregation windows, and operational runbooks for triage and promotion.


Scope
-----
Applies to:
- Evaluation harness teams (shadow/eval orchestrator)
- Model onboarding and quantization teams (model owners)
- Platform & infra (orchestration connectors)
- Privacy & legal for DP/retention policies
- Customer Success for private deployment constraints

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] - Customer Success for private deployment constraints


Not in scope:
- Non-eval production logs (use observability standards)
- Long-term archival of raw PII-laden user content beyond defined retention windows

Goals and success metrics
------------------------

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] Goals and success metrics
------------------------

Primary goals:
1) Provide consistent, low-latency shadow evaluation across environments while preserving tenant isolation.
2) Limit retention of raw probe payloads without sacrificing actionable triage signals.
3) Enable cross-tenant, cross-region federated aggregation for fleet-level signal analysis.

Success metrics (monthly):
- Shadow orchestration success rate >= 99.0% (jobs launched and results returned within expected window).
- Aggregate fidelity-preservation score <= 0.03 MRR delta for promoted models vs baseline on evaluation slices.
- Privacy budget overage events = 0 (epsilon budget violations).
- Mean time to first-triage (probe anomaly) <= 4 hours.

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] - Mean time to first-triage (probe anomaly) <= 4 hours.


System architecture (overview)
----------------------------
1) Probe Fabric: central registry of probe manifests (immutable IDs) and lightweight shards delivered to orchestrators.
2) Local Shadow Engine: runs inside each deployment mode (hosted/dedicated/private) that executes probes, applies local DP mechanisms, and emits telemetry.
3) Federation Coordinator: central or regionally replicated controller that aggregates pre-processed telemetry from Local Shadow Engines.
4) Aggregation Layer: deterministic, privacy-aware reducers that compute slice-level metrics and produce evaluated artifacts (reports, diffs, alerts).

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] 4) Aggregation Layer: deterministic, privacy-aware reducers that compute slice-level metrics and produce evaluated artifacts (reports, diffs, alerts).


Key properties:
- Immutable probe manifests allow reproducible runs.
- All raw payloads are treated as sensitive by default; local pre-processing removes or hashes candidate PII before any external transfer.
- The federation supports pull-based and push-based modes depending on private deployment networking constraints.

Probe manifest schema (human-readable)
-------------------------------------

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
section_header: Each manifest must be stored in the Probe Fabric and include the following fields
text:
[...] Probe manifest schema (human-readable)
-------------------------------------

Each manifest must be stored in the Probe Fabric and include the following fields:
- probe_id: string (immutable)
- created_by: engineer name or service
- description: short text explaining intent and slice
- input_template: single-line template with placeholders
- expected_labels: list of label names used by evaluators
- priority: low|medium|high
- retention_tier: raw|redacted|aggregate
- max_runtime_seconds: integer
- seed_policy: seed|randomized|deterministic-with-seed
- dp_policy_id: optional reference to a DP policy (see Privacy section)

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] - dp_policy_id: optional reference to a DP policy (see Privacy section)


Example manifest (YAML snippet)
------------------------------
manifest:
  probe_id: "pf-2026-0012"
  created_by: "maya.chen"
  description: "multi-turn coherency slice: long-context, system prompts"
  input_template: "<SYSTEM> \nUser: {question} \nAssistant:"
  expected_labels: ["coherence_score","toxicity_flag"]
  priority: medium
  retention_tier: redacted
  max_runtime_seconds: 30
  seed_policy: deterministic-with-seed
  dp_policy_id: dp-epsilon-0.5-std

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...]   dp_policy_id: dp-epsilon-0.5-std


Privacy, retention and DP enforcement
-------------------------------------
Retention tiers (policy):
- raw: raw request + raw response (allowed only for limited ops debugging and audited by privacy). Max retention 7 days.
- redacted: PII removed or replaced, text minimally redacted (stable identifiers hashed). Maximum retention 90 days.
- aggregate: only statistical aggregates or bucketing retained. Maximum retention 365 days.

Default policy: all probe runs default to redacted unless explicitly approved for raw by Privacy and the model owner.

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] Default policy: all probe runs default to redacted unless explicitly approved for raw by Privacy and the model owner.


Differential privacy enforcement:
- All telemetry leaving a private deployment must apply the local DP shim (Laplace or Gaussian depending on the metric) before transmission.
- Standard privacy policies: dp-epsilon-0.5-std (per-probe epsilon = 0.5), dp-epsilon-2.0-experiment (per-probe epsilon = 2.0, requires approval).
- Federation Coordinator validates DP policy IDs on ingestion and rejects telemetry without a valid DP header.

Telemetry fields and schema (table)
----------------------------------
| field_name | type | description | retention_tier | allowed_in_private_pull? |
| probe_id | string | manifest ID | aggregate | yes |
| run_id | string | unique run UUID | aggregate | yes |
| model_variant | string | model version tag | aggregate | yes |
| slice | string | evaluation slice name | aggregate | yes |
| latency_ms | int | end-to-end run latency | aggregate | yes |
| token_count | int | tokens consumed | aggregate | yes |
| fidelity_delta | float | metric delta vs baseline | redacted | yes |
| raw_response_hash | string | SHA256 of response after redaction | redacted | yes |
| annotation_flags | list | labels from annotators | redacted | no (annotator PII removed) |
| dp_policy_id | string | policy id applied locally | aggregate | yes |

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] | dp_policy_id | string | policy id applied locally | aggregate | yes |


Federation modes and networking patterns
----------------------------------------
1) Push-mode (hosted & dedicated): Local Shadow Engine pushes pre-aggregated telemetry to Federation Coordinator via HTTPS with mutual TLS.
2) Pull-mode (private / VPC): Federation Coordinator cannot reach the private network. Local Shadow Engine exposes an authenticated pull endpoint; coordinator fetches aggregated bundles on schedule.
3) Hybrid: Local Shadow Engine pushes to a customer-controlled mirror S3 / bucket. Federation Coordinator consumes from the mirror in read-only mode.

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] 3) Hybrid: Local Shadow Engine pushes to a customer-controlled mirror S3 / bucket. Federation Coordinator consumes from the mirror in read-only mode.


Operational runbook: shadow-run-create -> monitor -> triage
----------------------------------------------------------
1) Create probe manifest in Probe Fabric. Tag retention_tier and dp_policy.
2) Schedule a shadow run via the eval-orchestrator CLI (example below).
3) Local Shadow Engines acknowledge within 60s, run probes, and emit local metrics.
4) Federation Coordinator aggregates within the configured window (default 15 minutes) and computes slice-level metrics.
5) If aggregate fidelity_delta > threshold (0.05) on any high-priority slice, auto-open a triage ticket and page the eval-oncall.
6) For privacy flags or DP violations, Federation marks run as failed and notifies Privacy team; no further aggregation occurs.

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
section_header: launch a single-run against a manifest across all reachable environments
text:
[...] 6) For privacy flags or DP violations, Federation marks run as failed and notifies Privacy team; no further aggregation occurs.


CLI example (eval-orchestrator)
-------------------------------
# launch a single-run against a manifest across all reachable environments
eval-orchestrator run --manifest pf-2026-0012 --deadline 1800 --priority medium --notify-team eval-team

Config snippet for federated push (JSON)
---------------------------------------
{
  "federation_mode": "push",
  "endpoint": "https://federation.redwood.internal/v1/ingest",
  "mTLS_cert": "/etc/ssl/fed-client.pem",
  "aggregation_window_minutes": 15
}

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
section_header: Promotion to staged-serving must satisfy both quality and operational gates
text:
[...] }


Canary and promotion criteria
----------------------------
Promotion to staged-serving must satisfy both quality and operational gates:
- Quality: aggregated fidelity_delta <= 0.02 across critical slices (intent, safety, coherence) with p-value < 0.05 vs baseline.
- Cost/latency: 95th percentile latency increase <= 10ms and token cost delta <= 3%.
- Stability: no more than 2 regression incidents in the last 14 days.
- Privacy: no DP policy violations and retention tier is compatible with target environment.

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] - Privacy: no DP policy violations and retention tier is compatible with target environment.


Escalation and triage ownership
-------------------------------
- Probe steward: owns manifest lifecycle and labeling (assigned by applied-ml).
- Eval-oncall: primary responder for any fidelity/regression alerts.
- Privacy-eng: owns DP policy approvals and investigations.
- Infra-platform: responsible for federation endpoints and mTLS rotation.

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] - Infra-platform: responsible for federation endpoints and mTLS rotation.


Triage checklist (first responder)
---------------------------------
1) Verify the run_id and manifest ID.
2) Check Federation ingestion logs for DP header and reject reasons.
3) Examine slice-level metrics; identify top 3 regressions by impact.
4) If raw payload is required for debugging, request Privacy approval with a justification ticket (include hash of response and time window).
5) If regression is operational (latency/throughput), escalate to infra-platform.

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] 5) If regression is operational (latency/throughput), escalate to infra-platform.


Audit and compliance
--------------------
- All manifest creations and edits are immutably logged in Probe Fabric (write-once audit log retained 3 years).
- DP policy changes require dual approval (Privacy + Applied ML lead) and are recorded.
- Random spot checks: Privacy team runs monthly audits on raw-release approvals.

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
section_header: Operational metrics and dashboards to build
text:
[...] - Random spot checks: Privacy team runs monthly audits on raw-release approvals.


Operational metrics and dashboards to build
-----------------------------------------
- Shadow job success rate by environment (hosted/dedicated/private)
- Probe retention counts by tier and age histogram
- DP policy compliance rate and most common rejected runs
- Slice-level fidelity_delta heatmap (rolling 7d)
- Mean time to first triage and triage backlog size

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] - Mean time to first triage and triage backlog size


Appendix A: Example DP header (HTTP)
-----------------------------------
X-DP-Policy-Id: dp-epsilon-0.5-std
X-DP-Epsilon: 0.5
X-DP-Algorithm: gaussian

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
section_header: check local shadow engine status\kubectl -n eval get pods -l app=local-shadow-engine -o wide
text:
[...] X-DP-Algorithm: gaussian


Appendix B: Minimal troubleshooting commands
-------------------------------------------
# check local shadow engine status\kubectl -n eval get pods -l app=local-shadow-engine -o wide
# tail federation ingestion logs\stern federation-ingestor -n infra

Appendix C: Change log
-----------------------
- 2025-09-02: initial draft (Maya Chen)
- 2025-10-14: added DP policy table and retention tiers (Arjun Patel)
- 2026-01-15: clarified pull-mode for private deployments and added CLI examples (Lena García)

title: Hybrid Shadow Federation and Probe Retention Protocol
text:
[...] - 2026-01-15: clarified pull-mode for private deployments and added CLI examples (Lena García)


Notes and open questions
------------------------
- Should redacted tier include automated named-entity replacement vs hashing? Proposal: hashing by default; replacement optional with explicit approval.
- Investigate lightweight homomorphic aggregation for certain safety counts to reduce DP noise while preserving utility.

References
----------
- Probe Fabric spec (internal): /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/probe-fabric-spec
- Privacy budgeting playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quality-guardrails/probe-privacy-budget-and-annotation-retention-playbook-2024

Contact
-------
For questions or approvals, contact Maya Chen (maya.chen@redwood.ai) or the Applied-ML oncall channel (#eval-oncall).

title: Cascade Probe Resilience and Repair Playbook
text:
Cascade Probe Resilience and Repair Playbook

SUMMARY
This page describes detection, emergency mitigation, and long-term prevention for cascading failures in the eval harness probe pipeline. We define failure modes that commonly cause large-scale evaluation regressions, provide an emergency runbook to restore safe continuity, and list durable design changes and configuration standards to prevent recurrence.

Scope
- Applies to the eval-harness execution plane (probe generator, sequencer, shadow router, and annotator ingestion queue).
- Includes interactions with: quantized shadow artifacts, KV cache warmup probes, and external annotator workers.
- Does NOT cover model training pipeline bugs or user-facing product incidents unless they are caused by an eval probe cascade.

Why this matters
- A single overloaded probe generator or stalled annotation queue can amplify into systemic false-positive regressions, wasted compute, and production instability for private/dedicated shadowing.
- Detecting and containing cascades early preserves evaluation fidelity, reduces noise for oncall, and keeps rollout gates (canaries, quant rollbacks) actionable.

Symptoms (observable metrics)
- Probe-failure-rate (probe.errors / probe.total) > 5% for 30m.
- Sequencer queue latency (p95) > 10s or steady growth for 15m.
- Annotation backlog > 10k unprocessed items AND ingestion rate falling > 30% vs baseline.
- Spike in duplicate probes / replays (indicates retry storm).
- Sudden divergence between shadow and synthetic control probes (indicates quantization or cache mismatch).

Common root-cause patterns
1. Backpressure amplification: downstream annotator lag triggers probe retry policy which pushes more load upstream.
2. Retry storms: aggressive retry policy (low jitter, high concurrency) + transient infra failure -> exponential traffic.
3. Quantization-induced behavioral skew: quantized model variant returns unexpected tokens and triggers extra post-processing or longer traces.
4. Sequencer scheduling inversion: priority inversion where low-priority bulk probes starve critical control probes.
5. Resource exhaustion: ephemeral node OOM or GPU out-of-memory causing probe workers to exit repeatedly and restart.
6. Telemetry blindspots: missing sampling of failed probe payloads, preventing root-cause attribution.

Emergency mitigation runbook (fast path)
Run this immediately when alerts indicate a spike matching the symptoms above. The goal is to stop amplification and buy time for a careful rollback/repair.

Ownership: eval-platform oncall (primary), model-onboarding (secondary), infra-sre (escalate when node-level OOMs are present).

Immediate steps (0–15 minutes)
1) Acknowledge pager and post incident stub in #eval-incidents with tags: cascade, severity:high.
2) Throttle probe ingestion: flip the sequencer rate-limit toggle to 20% of normal via the admin console OR run:

   # temporarily reduce probe rate to 1/5
   curl -X POST "https://admin.internal/eval/sequencer/rate" -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"target_fraction":0.2,"reason":"cascade-mitigation"}'

3) Disable aggressive retries: change probe retry policy to exponential backoff with jitter and caps. Apply the emergency policy:
   - base: 1s, factor: 2, jitter: true, max_retries: 3, max_backoff: 300s

4) Pause non-essential shadow experiments: set shadow-router tag multipliers to 0 for low-priority experiments.

5) Open a telemetry capture window for failed probes: enable full payload logging for the failing probe family for 15 minutes, write logs to a quarantined S3 prefix: s3://eval-debug/quarantine/<incident-id>/

6) If annotation backlog > 50k, spin up temporary annotator workers (runbook for autoscale: infra-sre) OR toggle manual expedited labeling for high-priority slices.

Stabilization checks (15–60 minutes)
1) Confirm probe-failure-rate trending down to < 1% and queue latency p95 below 5s.
2) Validate that retry counters are not growing and duplicate probe counts fall.
3) If quantized variant involved, temporarily pin evaluation routing to the float reference or last-known-good quant artifact.

Rollback / repair decisions (60–240 minutes)
- If rollback to last-known-good brings system back to healthy levels, schedule a postmortem and leave the rollback in place until root cause fixed.
- If the cascade is caused by a specific probe family, disable that family and create a prioritized mitigation ticket.

Post-incident diagnosis (post-stabilization)
- Collect the quarantined payloads, probe logs, and a compressed execution trace from the sequencer for the incident window.
- Run the automated bisect script against probe artifacts (see related: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/automated-regression-bisect-and-blame-assistant-2026).
- Classify root cause into one of the patterns above and tag the incident with: root_cause_pattern, estimated_impact_tokens, affected_models.

title: Cascade Probe Resilience and Repair Playbook
text:
[...] - Run the automated bisect script against probe artifacts (see related: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/automated-regression-bisect-and-blame-assistant-2026).
- Classify root cause into one of the patterns above and tag the incident with: root_cause_pattern, estimated_impact_tokens, affected_models.

Long-term prevention (design and configuration changes)
1) Default retry-safety policy (applied to all probe agents):
   - max_retries: 3
   - base_backoff_s: 0.8
   - max_backoff_s: 300
   - jitter: true
   - circuit_breaker_threshold: 10 failures / 60s per probe-family

2) Sequencer safety knobs:
   - prioritized control probe lane (reserved QPS 50) that cannot be backfilled by bulk probes.
   - soft-ttl on probes: default 48 hours; probes older than TTL are tombstoned.

3) Probe family metadata enforcement: every probe must declare: owner, priority (critical/high/normal), expected-cost-tokens, safe-fallback-route. The sequencer rejects unlabeled probe families.

4) Annotation autoscale thresholds: proactively provision extra workers when backlog > 5k for 10m, with a hard cap tied to budget alerts.

5) Shadow routing durability: automatic pin-to-reference when divergence metric (shadow_vs_reference_coherence) falls below 0.85 for two consecutive windows.

6) Telemetry coverage: sample 100% of failing probe payloads for first 10m of a failure, then 5% afterwards. Ensure redaction pipeline before storage.

Operational SLOs and alert thresholds (proposal)
- Probe success rate: 99.5% over 24h for control probe families.
- Sequencer queue latency p95: < 5s.
- Annotation backlog: < 5k unprocessed with smoothing window 1h.
- Automatic circuit-breaker trips: manual review required after 3 trips in a 7-day window.

Config reference (table)
| config_key | recommended_default | rationale |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------|
| probe.ttl_hours | 48 | Prevents stale probes from amplifying long-tail issues |
| probe.max_retries | 3 | Limits retry storms while preserving transient resilience |
| sequencer.control_qps | 50 | Reserved QPS for critical control probes |
| annotator.autoscale_backlog | 5000 | Trigger autoscale before latency degrades |
| retry.jitter | true | Breaks synchronization between failing workers |

Example emergency policy snippet (YAML)
apiVersion: eval/v1
kind: EmergencyPolicy
metadata:
  name: cascade-mitigation-2026
spec:
  sequencer:
    target_rate_fraction: 0.2
  retryPolicy:
    base: 1s
    factor: 2
    jitter: true
    maxRetries: 3
    maxBackoff: 300s

Ownership and escalation matrix
- eval-platform-oncall: primary (pager)
- model-onboarding: secondary (model-related failures)
- infra-sre: node-level resource exhaustion and autoscale failures
- applied-ml: assists with probe design and quant failure analysis

Post-incident actions (must be completed within 5 business days)
1) Fill incident template: link to logs, root-cause classification, and remediation plan.
2) Create a changelog entry in the probe family registry with the action taken and retention policy changes.
3) Run a targeted smoke eval against the affected family after fixes and collect before/after coherence scores.

title: Cascade Probe Resilience and Repair Playbook
text:
[...] 2) Create a changelog entry in the probe family registry with the action taken and retention policy changes.
3) Run a targeted smoke eval against the affected family after fixes and collect before/after coherence scores.

Pre-deployment checklist for new probe families
1) Declare probe metadata (owner, priority, expected_token_cost, fallback_route).
2) Run local burst test: 10k probes at scaled intervals to verify sequencing and ttl handling.
3) Nightly shadow run for 48h with metrics gated (success rate >= 99.7%).
4) Quantized artifact check: run quant-sensitivity probe suite and ensure shadow_vs_reference_coherence >= 0.9.

Appendix A — Useful diagnostic commands
- Inspect sequencer queue: kubectl logs deploy/sequencer -n eval --since=1h | jq '.queue_stats'
- Toggle rate limit: curl -X POST https://admin.internal/eval/sequencer/rate -d '{"target_fraction":0.2}'
- Capture failed probe payloads (one-off): aws s3 cp s3://eval-logs/failure-window/<id> ./ --profile eval-debug

Appendix B — Example incident timeline (illustrative)
1) 12:03: spike in probe-failure-rate to 8%
2) 12:07 sequencer p95 queue latency rising to 18s
3) 12:12 admin toggles rate to 20% and disables aggressive retries
4) 12:25 queue latency down to 4.2s; failure-rate falling to 0.9%
5) 15:00 rollback of quantized artifact to float reference; proxy pinned to last-known-good

Related reading and links
- Probe lifecycle and TTL enforcement: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/probe-lifecycle-annotation-economics-playbook-2026
- Quant failure modes and repair: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quant-output-degradation-detect-and-repair-protocol-2026
- Automated bisect and blame assistant: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/automated-regression-bisect-and-blame-assistant-2026

Document maintenance
- Owner: eval-platform team
- Review cadence: quarterly or after any incident categorized as cascade.
- Last review: 2026-02-18

End of document

title: Experimental Priming Sandbox and Relabeling Playbook
text:
Experimental Priming Sandbox and Relabeling Playbook

Summary
This playbook documents the Experimental Priming Sandbox: a repeatable, auditable environment for generating, validating, and relabeling priming examples (few-shot anchors, system prompts, and priming templates) before they reach onboarding or shadow fleets. It focuses on safety, cross-tenant isolation, quantization compatibility checks, and a human-in-the-loop relabeling loop to reduce noisy anchors that cause downstream regressions.

Goals
- Provide a lightweight, reproducible sandbox that mirrors production serving characteristics for priming artifacts.
- Detect priming-driven regressions early (semantic drift, hallucination amplification, quant sensitivity).
- Supply an annotated relabeling pipeline that respects annotator privacy and yields high-quality anchors for shadow/onboard promotion.

When to use this sandbox
1. Introducing a new priming family (new few-shot template / system prompt) for a model-suite.
2. Migration of a service to a quantized runtime where priming sensitivity is unknown.
3. Investigating regressions traced to prompt-anchor families in regression triage.
4. Routine pruning and refresh of priming registry prior to major rollout.

Ownership and roles
- Sandbox Owner: Applied ML Platform (primary) — responsible for infra, access control, and monitoring.
- Priming Steward: Service/feature owner — provides candidate priming artifacts and acceptance criteria.
- Annotator Lead: Quality/Labeling — runs relabeling rounds and signs off on dataset quality.
- Evaluator: Applied ML Eng — configures eval harness scenarios and quant checks.
- Security Reviewer: InfoSec (on restricted or customer-sensitive priming artifacts).

High-level workflow
1. Candidate registration: Priming Steward registers new artifacts in the priming registry with metadata (description, expected intents, negative examples, provenance).
2. Synthetic amplification: The harness expands edge cases and counterfactual variants (noise/shuffling, paraphrase, long-context) to probe sensitivity.
3. Shadow simulation: Run priming candidates against a mixed-fidelity shadow mesh (float32 reference + quantized variants) using service-like request traces.
4. Metric collection: Record coherence, hallucination indicators, functioncall stability, token-efficiency, and divergence between float/quant variants.
5. Relabeling loop: Low-confidence or high-disagreement responses are queued for annotators. Relabel results update artifact scores and provenance.
6. Promotion gate: If artifact passes thresholds and annotator sign-off it moves to shadow fleet; otherwise iterate or retire.

Acceptance criteria (promotion gate)
- Coherence score >= 0.85 (service-specific baseline).
- Cross-fidelity divergence (KL-like coherence delta) <= 6% relative.
- No amplification of hallucination markers on anchor-augmented probes (regression delta <= 0.5%).
- Annotator agreement >= 80% on relabeled items.
- Latency overhead <= 5% at p95 when using anchor in production runtime profile.

Sandbox resource configuration (example table)
| Resource | Purpose | Typical size | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Priming harness job | Generate variants and run evals | 2 vCPUs, 8 GB, ephemeral GPU 1xA10 | Short lived, autoscale per queue |
| Shadow mesh | Mixed-fidelity model runners | 4 GPUs (2 float, 2 int8) | Use representative model weights |
| Annotation queue | Human labeling UI + storage | 4 vCPUs, 16 GB, 50 GB storage | S3 bucket with TTL for PII redaction |
| Telemetry store | Probe & telemetry retention | 8 vCPUs, 32 GB DB | Retention 90 days for sandbox artifacts |

Key diagnostic metrics captured
- Per-probe coherence, intent-preservation score, hallucination-signal count.
- Fidelity delta: token-level log-prob divergence between float32 and quantized run.
- Functioncall stability score (normalized rank variance for function results).
- Annotator disagreement (fraction of items needing second pass).
- Priming amplification index: response-length/novel-token expansion over baseline.

Step-by-step: Running a priming experiment
1. Register candidate: Use the priming-registry CLI. Example metadata payload (YAML):

```yaml
name: onboarding-help-template-v2
owner: payments-checkout
intent_tags: ["helpfulness","safety:tokenization"]
description: >-
  Compact few-shot to disambiguate payment error remediation steps.
negatives: ["billing-lookup"]
provenance: manual-author:priya/2026-01-10
```

title: Experimental Priming Sandbox and Relabeling Playbook
text:
[...] provenance: manual-author:priya/2026-01-10
```

CLI command: primingctl register --file candidate.yaml

2. Launch synthetic amplification: primingctl expand --candidate onboarding-help-template-v2 --modes paraphrase,noise,context-elongation --samples 500

3. Schedule shadow run: primingctl trigger-shadow --candidate onboarding-help-template-v2 --mesh float32,int8 --trace sample-trace-202601 --deadline 2h

4. Inspect results dashboard: Open Sandbox -> Experiments -> onboarding-help-template-v2. Review fidelity delta heatmap and functioncall variance table.

5. Queue relabeling: primingctl queue-annotate --threshold divergence>0.06 --annotator-group payments-anchors

6. Annotator workflow: Annotators use the Sandbox Labeler UI. Labeling instructions: preserve intent, flag hallucination, quantify response utility (0..3). Include free-text rationale.

7. Promotion or retire: Evaluator reviews metrics + annotator summary and executes primingctl promote --candidate onboarding-help-template-v2 if gate satisfied. Otherwise primingctl retire --candidate ...

Relabeling policy and privacy controls
- Annotators must never see raw user PII. Sandbox must apply deterministic redaction plus synthetic mask where live traffic is used.
- For customer-sensitive artifacts, require Security Reviewer approval (confidentiality: restricted).
- Relabel decisions and comment history are immutable and stored for 1 year for audit.

Quant-compatibility checks (automated)
- Token-level log-prob comparison across float32 and target quant modes. Report the 95th percentile divergence.
- Identify prompts where quant causes rank inversion for critical tokens (functioncall names, entity identifiers).
- Run targeted micro-benchmarks: repeated long-context with anchored priming to detect KV cache misalignment.

Troubleshooting common failure modes
- High fidelity delta but low annotator disagreement: often cosmetic phrasing differences. Apply normalization (whitespace/casing) and re-evaluate.
- High annotator disagreement: re-run with expanded paraphrase set and add an adjudicator pass.
- Latency regression on p95: try shorter anchors, move heavy examples to background priming, or apply prompt condensation (template compression).

Rollback and incident integration
- Any promoted priming artifact must have a rollback artifact (previous anchor version or empty anchor) and a rollback plan in the promotion ticket.
- If a regression is detected in production tied to priming, follow Regression Triage Playbook: create an incident, capture sample traces, run targeted sandbox replay, and escalate to Model Onboarding for root-cause.

Operational runbooks (quick steps)
- Emergency disable: primingctl disable --candidate <id> (immediate removal from live routing).
- Fast replay: primingctl replay --trace <trace-id> --candidate <id> --to float32,int8 --out /tmp/replay.json
- Extract disagreement set: primingctl exports failures --candidate <id> --min-agreement 0.6 --format ndjson

Housekeeping and lifecycle
- Sandbox artifacts older than 180 days are archived unless actively referenced in onboarding tickets.
- Quarterly priming audit: Priming Steward must run an audit report and retire anchors with low utility (usage < 0.1% and negative impact on fidelity).

Appendix: Example experiment JSON output (trimmed)
```json
{
  "candidate": "onboarding-help-template-v2",
  "runs": [
    {"mesh":"float32","coherence":0.89,"hallucination":0.01},
    {"mesh":"int8","coherence":0.82,"hallucination":0.012}
  ],
  "fidelity_delta": 0.074,
  "annotator_agreement": 0.81
}
```

References and links
- Priming registry spec: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/prompt-sets-and-benchmarks/priming-registry-manifest-2025
- Regression triage: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/regression-impact-index-and-prioritization-framework-2024
- Quant checks: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quant-output-degradation-detect-and-repair-protocol-2026

Contact
- For questions or access requests contact: applied-ml-platform@redwood.ai or Priya Nambiar (priya@redwood.ai)

title: Synthetic User Simulation and Long-tail Eval Protocol
text:
Synthetic User Simulation and Long-tail Eval Protocol

Overview
This document defines the Synthetic User Simulation (SUS) framework and the Long-tail Evaluation Protocol (LTEP) used by Redwood Inference to validate model behavior and serving correctness across rare, compound, and emergent input patterns. The intention is to produce repeatable, auditable simulations that exercise production routing, caching, KV behavior, quantized execution, and fallback logic.

Goals
- Provide a reproducible harness for generating realistic long-tail user requests and multi-turn sessions.
- Measure model and serving stability across compound perturbations (tokenization, prompt templates, kv-cache state, quant noise).
- Produce artifact outputs for triage: deterministic traces, synthetic user IDs, seed manifests, and per-slice metrics.
- Integrate with CI and the eval mesh to block model promotions on regressions of defined slices.

Scope and non-goals
- Scope: multi-turn chat, few-shot templates, embedding drift probes, reranking workloads, and long-context summarization flows.
- Non-goals: full user privacy simulation (do not attempt to rehydrate PII); SUS must use synthetic/generated entities only.

Definitions and terminology
- Session: ordered sequence of user messages and system/tool calls intended to mimic a single interaction.
- Slice: a labeled subset of sessions sharing attributes (e.g., nonstandard tokenization, mixed languages, deep-turn > 40).
- Perturbation: synthetic modification applied to inputs (typos, injection tokens, sentence shuffling, prefix truncation).

High-level architecture
1. Seed Catalog: canonical prompt templates, persona generators, and locale distributions.
2. Perturbation Engine: applies deterministic/noisy transforms to seeds with reproducible RNG seeds.
3. Session Mixer: composes multi-turn sequences, optional tool calls, and stateful KV cache interactions.
4. Runner: executes sessions against target model variants via the eval mesh and records full traces.
5. Analyzer: computes per-slice metrics and produces regression/quality reports.

Example evaluation slices (recommended)
- Tokenization-edge: mixed-encoding, zero-width joiners, and emoji-heavy input.
- Deep-context: session length >= 40 user/system turns; tests KV reuse efficiency.
- Few-shot-template-variance: permutations of demonstration order and answer-key formats.
- Quant-noise-probe: identical prompt executed on fp16, int8, and q4 profiles to surface divergence.
- Cross-lingual/romanization: code-switched tokens and transliteration fallbacks.

Metrics and thresholds
- Semantic-consistency-score (SCS): aggregated BERTScore-type metric compared to golden replies; pass threshold >= 0.82 for critical slices.
- Latency-90 (L90): 90th percentile token-end latency; must be <= 220ms for hosted SLO workloads, <= 350ms for dedicated background workloads.
- Token-delta drift (TDD): percent token count variance vs baseline <= 12% for deterministic prompts.
- Output-stability-index (OSI): fraction of identical deterministic outputs across N=5 seeds; must be >= 0.9 for inference-readiness slices.
- Regression-alert: any delta in SCS > 0.03 or OSI drop > 0.08 triggers triage.

Artifact outputs (required)
- session-trace-<seed>.json: full request/response with headers and timing.
- seed-manifest.csv: seed_id, template_id, RNG_seed, slice_tags.
- metric-report-<model>-<variant>.csv: per-slice metrics and baseline deltas.
- failure-bundle.tar.gz: representative traces, model logs (if available), and runtime config.

Experiment configuration table (example)
| field | description | example |
|---|---|---|
| model_variant | model tag under test | redwood-7b-v2-qat-q4 |
| batch_policy | batching mode | continuous-batch-128 |
| kv_mode | KV cache policy | enable-local, preload-prefix=128 |
| quant_profile | quantization configuration | q8_256_tile_sparse |
| seed_set | synthetic seed catalog version | sus-seedset-v1.4 |
| rng_seed | numeric seed for perturbation reproducibility | 20260202 |
| run_id | unique run id | sus-run-2026-02-02-01 |

Run procedure (step-by-step)
1. Provision: allocate an eval runner in the desired environment (hosted/dedicated/private). Ensure the runner has network egress to the eval mesh.
2. Prepare artifacts: pull seed-set (sus-seedset-v1.4) and perturbation configs.
3. Start run: ./sus-runner --config sus-config.yaml --run-id sus-run-2026-02-02-01 --parallelism 32
4. Monitor: watch L90 and OSI in the run dashboard; abort run if L90 > 600ms for >5 minutes.
5. Collect: when complete, gather session traces and metric-report CSVs. Upload to artifacts store and link to run id.
6. Evaluate: Analyzer computes deltas vs baseline and generates regression-alerts if thresholds breached.

title: Synthetic User Simulation and Long-tail Eval Protocol
text:
[...] 5. Collect: when complete, gather session traces and metric-report CSVs. Upload to artifacts store and link to run id.
6. Evaluate: Analyzer computes deltas vs baseline and generates regression-alerts if thresholds breached.

Example sus-config.yaml (inline)
---
model_variant: "redwood-7b-v2-qat-q4"
batch_policy: "continuous-batch-64"
kv_mode: "preload-prefix=64"
quant_profile: "q4_opt_level_2"
seed_set: "sus-seedset-v1.4"
perturbations:
  - name: typo-insert
    rate: 0.12
  - name: emoji-injection
    rate: 0.05
  - name: prefix-truncation
    max_prefix_len: 512
parallelism: 24
retries_on_failure: 2

title: Synthetic User Simulation and Long-tail Eval Protocol
text:
[...] parallelism: 24
retries_on_failure: 2

output_bucket: "s3://redwood-eval-artifacts/sus/${run_id}/"
---

Failure triage checklist
1. Confirm reproducibility: re-run top failing session with identical rng_seed and model_variant.
2. Compare quantized vs fp baseline: run same seed on fp16 to determine if quantization is primary cause.
3. Inspect tokenization: re-tokenize inputs with dev tokenization tool; compare token boundaries and special token insertion.
4. KV-cache sensitivity: replay session with cache-warmup and cache-cleared modes.
5. Network/runtime noise: confirm CPU/GPU contention and host telemetry (GPU SM utilization, NVLink errors) during failing run.

Integration and gating rules
- Every model promotion to staging must include 3 SUS runs: short-smoke (1k sessions), deep-context (5k sessions, mixed slices), and quant-fidelity sweep (10k sessions across quant profiles).
- Promotion gating: no critical slices with regression-alerts, and aggregate SCS within 0.02 of baseline.
- If gating fails, attach failure-bundle and open a regression ticket with priority tag: regress/auto-block.

Dashboarding and alerting
- Expose per-slice SCS, OSI, L90 and TDD on the eval mesh dashboard.
- Alert channel: #eval-regressions with automated triage summary and link to failure-bundle.
- Weekly digest: long-tail drift trends and top 10 failing seeds.

Ownership and maintenance
- Primary owner: Applied ML — Eval Harness (owner: Nina Patel).
- Secondary owner: Serving Runtime (owner: Evan Morales) for infra and runner reliability.
- Maintenance cadence: seed-set refresh quarterly or after major model architecture changes.

CI integration
1. Add SUS run step to model promotion pipeline (nightly for candidate builds).
2. Store artifacts in immutable storage; reference run_id in promotion PRs.
3. Fail fast: if automated triage marks run as 'block', the promotion pipeline must stop and assign to oncall owner.

Privacy and data hygiene
- All synthetic seeds must be generated from safe-name and safe-entity tables. No PII, real email addresses, or real phone numbers are permitted.
- Seed manifests must include a lineage field that states generator version.

Appendix: quick CLI examples
- Launch single-session replay (developer): ./sus-runner --replay session-trace-20260202-0001.json --model redwood-7b-v2-qat-q4 --output ./replay-output.json
- Run quant sweep: ./sus-runner --config quant-sweep.yaml --sweep-profiles [fp16,q4,q8] --run-id quant-sweep-2026-02-02

References
- eval-harness/promotion-readiness-synthetic-quant-safety-playbook (internal)
- prompt-sets-and-benchmarks/edgecase-probe-generator-and-labeling-guidelines-2026 (internal)

Change log
- 2025-09-18: Document created (Nina Patel).
- 2025-12-07: Added quant-noise-probe slice and artifact naming conventions (Evan Morales).
- 2026-02-02: Formalized CI gating rules and thresholds; published.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection

Overview
========
This playbook describes the Shadow Orchestrator: an orchestrated, repeatable pipeline for running cross-modal (text + image + long-context) shadow tests against production traffic to detect regressions, quantization sensitivity, and early drift. The goal is to exercise the full serving stack and evaluation harness without impacting live responses.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] This playbook describes the Shadow Orchestrator: an orchestrated, repeatable pipeline for running cross-modal (text + image + long-context) shadow tests against production traffic to detect regressions, quantization sensitivity, and early drift. The goal is to exercise the full serving stack and evaluation harness without impacting live responses.


Why this exists
---------------
- Catch subtle cross-modal regressions that only appear under production request composition (multimodal prompts, long context + tool calls).
- Measure behavior differences introduced by model promotions, quantization profiles, or runtime kernel changes.
- Provide a fast, audit-friendly path for risk-aware promotion decisions.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] - Provide a fast, audit-friendly path for risk-aware promotion decisions.


Scope
-----
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where shadowing of selective traffic is supported.
- Supports: text-only, text+image, and long-context (>4k tokens) shadow runs.
- Not intended for full load performance benchmarking (use dedicated perf suite).

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] - Not intended for full load performance benchmarking (use dedicated perf suite).


Key components
--------------
1) Orchestrator controller: schedules shadow runs, enforces traffic fraction, and records run metadata.
2) Probe generator: synthesizes cross-modal probes from prompt catalog and production anchors.
3) Shadow sequencer: replays requests into shadow model variants while preserving KV cache warmup policy.
4) Telemetry collector: aggregates semantic metrics, token deltas, latency/throughput, and KV-cache hit rates.
5) Comparator & alerting: computes divergence metrics and raises triage alerts when thresholds are exceeded.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] 5) Comparator & alerting: computes divergence metrics and raises triage alerts when thresholds are exceeded.


Run definitions and config (example)
----------------------------------
- run_type: cross_modal_shadow
- traffic_fraction: 0.5%  # percent of qualifying requests to fork to shadow path
- probe_mix: [ real-anchors: 70%, synthetic-probes: 30% ]
- variants: [baseline: "v3.1-quant32", candidate: "v3.2-q4f", fallback: "v3.1-quant32"]
- kv_cache_policy: warmup=2 requests, preserve_prefix=true
- telemetry: semantic_similarity=cosine-sbert, token_perturb=edit-distance-tokenized

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
section_header: YAML example for orchestrator (use with CLI)
text:
[...] - telemetry: semantic_similarity=cosine-sbert, token_perturb=edit-distance-tokenized


YAML example for orchestrator (use with CLI)
--------------------------------------------
run_id: crossmodal-2026-02-20-01
selector:
  sources: ["web-api", "mobile-sdk"]
  modalities: ["text", "image", "long-context"]
  min_tokens: 20
  max_tokens: 8192
probes:
  anchor_sampling: {window_hours: 72, sample_rate: 0.001}
  synthetic_rules: ["image-caption-variants", "noisy-instruction-perturbs"]
variants:
  baseline: "models/redwood-v3.1-quant32"
  candidate: "models/redwood-v3.2-q4f"
  fallback: "models/redwood-v3.1-quant32"
schedule:
  parallelism: 8
  batch_size: 16
  max_runtime_minutes: 180
observability:
  metrics: ["semantic_divergence", "token_perturb_rate", "latency_p99_delta", "kv_cache_hit_frac"]
  report_interval_seconds: 60

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...]   report_interval_seconds: 60


CLI examples
------------
- Start run: rwdctl shadow start --config ./crossmodal-run.yaml
- Status: rwdctl shadow status run_id=crossmodal-2026-02-20-01
- Snapshot results: rwdctl shadow export --run crossmodal-2026-02-20-01 --format jsonl

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] - Snapshot results: rwdctl shadow export --run crossmodal-2026-02-20-01 --format jsonl


Telemetry and scoring (what we measure)
-------------------------------------
- Semantic divergence (S): cosine similarity delta between baseline and candidate embeddings; aggregated per-slice.
- Token perturbation rate (T): percent of responses with edit-distance token change > 5%.
- Latency P99 delta (L): relative increase in p99 latency for candidate vs baseline.
- KV-cache instability (K): fraction of requests where key-cache leads to different generation path (measured via prefix prefix-match diagnostics).

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] - KV-cache instability (K): fraction of requests where key-cache leads to different generation path (measured via prefix prefix-match diagnostics).


Alerting thresholds (initial)
---------------------------
- S_mean_delta > 0.08 for any high-importance slice => triage alert.
- T_rate > 0.03 on user-facing prompt family => abort promotion.
- L_p99_increase > 0.15 (15%) across global traffic => performance incident.
- K_instability > 0.05 for multimodal long-context => investigate quantization fallback.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
section_header: Success criteria for a safe candidate run
text:
[...] - K_instability > 0.05 for multimodal long-context => investigate quantization fallback.


Success criteria for a safe candidate run
---------------------------------------
- No alerting thresholds exceeded on top-50 production slices.
- Semantic divergence concentrated in low-impact slices only (<= 5% of overall traffic).
- Regression precision: when failing probes are re-run with deterministic seeds, true positive regression rate > 60% (reduces noisy alerts).

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
section_header: Procedure: Running a cross-modal shadow run (step-by-step)
text:
[...] - Regression precision: when failing probes are re-run with deterministic seeds, true positive regression rate > 60% (reduces noisy alerts).


Procedure: Running a cross-modal shadow run (step-by-step)
--------------------------------------------------------
1) Author a run-config YAML and attach the appropriate probe catalog (see Probe Catalog section below).
2) Tag the candidate model artifact in model catalog and ensure runtime kernels/quant profile are declared.
3) Execute: rwdctl shadow start --config <yaml>. Note owner and run_id.
4) Monitor the run dashboard: focus on semantic_divergence heatmap, token_perturb-slice table, and latency deltas.
5) If any threshold is breached, follow Triage flow (next section).
6) After run completes, publish the run report to the promotion checklist and attach exported JSONL of mismatched pairs.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] 6) After run completes, publish the run report to the promotion checklist and attach exported JSONL of mismatched pairs.


Triage flow (if alerts triggered)
--------------------------------
1) Create a triage ticket in the eval-triage project and link the run_id and top-5 failing probe examples.
2) Reproduce locally with deterministic seeds: rwdctl shadow replay --run <run_id> --seed 42 --limit 50.
3) Classify failure: semantic-quality | hallucination | formatting | latency | kv-cache-path.
4) If quality regression is confirmed: assign to model-onboarding team for rollback or candidate rework.
5) If instability due to quantization: escalate to quant-profiles owner with artifact + kernel logs.
6) Tag customer-impacting examples and notify customer-success if applicable.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] 6) Tag customer-impacting examples and notify customer-success if applicable.


Probe catalog guidance
---------------------
- Prefer a mix: 70% production anchors (anonymized) + 30% synthetic stress probes.
- Include multimodal transforms: rotated/cropped images, compressed images, OCR-noise variants.
- For long-context probes, attach explicit KV-cache priming sequences (3 warmup prompts).

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] - For long-context probes, attach explicit KV-cache priming sequences (3 warmup prompts).


Data handling and privacy
------------------------
- All shadowed request payloads must be anonymized prior to being stored in the eval-mesh: PII redaction, token hashing for user identifiers, and image blurring for sensitive regions.
- Retention: shadow run raw exports retained for 30 days by default; longer retention requires compliance approval.
- When operating in Private deployments, ensure shadow export endpoints route to customer-owned storage where configured.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] - When operating in Private deployments, ensure shadow export endpoints route to customer-owned storage where configured.


Integration and dependencies
---------------------------
- Serving: must support forked request path with independent model routing and consistent headers.
- Eval-mesh: probe delivery, result collection, and reproducible seeds.
- Telemetry pipeline: ingest at 1m granularity and store semantic embeddings in vector-store for post-hoc analysis.
- Model catalog: candidate/baseline artifact tags and quant-profile metadata must be present.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] - Model catalog: candidate/baseline artifact tags and quant-profile metadata must be present.


Roles & responsibilities
------------------------
| Role | Responsibility | Owner |
|------|----------------|-------|
| Run Owner | Create running config, approve probe set | ML Eng (on-call) |
| Probe Curator | Maintain multimodal probe catalog, labels | Applied ML |
| Telemetry Owner | Validate metric correctness and storage | Observability team |
| Quant Owner | Diagnose and fix quant failures | Research/Applied ML |
| Customer-facing comms | Coordinate customer notifications | Customer Success |

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] | Customer-facing comms | Coordinate customer notifications | Customer Success |


Recovery and rollback patterns
------------------------------
- Fast rollback: disable shadow forks for the candidate and re-promote baseline (rwdctl model promote --model-id <baseline>).
- Partial rollback: restrict candidate to internal-region or dedicated pool; run micro-canary before reattempting promotion.
- Fallback routing: orchestrator can automatically route new requests to 'fallback' variant when token_perturb_rate > 0.05 for a sliding 5m window.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
section_header: Synthesis: common failure modes and mitigations
text:
[...] - Fallback routing: orchestrator can automatically route new requests to 'fallback' variant when token_perturb_rate > 0.05 for a sliding 5m window.


Synthesis: common failure modes and mitigations
----------------------------------------------
- Multimodal alignment drift: increase probe coverage for image perturbations and re-evaluate model fusion layer.
- Quantization hallucination spikes: revert to higher-precision quant profile for affected slices and re-run shadow.
- KV cache divergence on long-context: increase warmup count and ensure cache-key normalization across runtime kernels.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] - KV cache divergence on long-context: increase warmup count and ensure cache-key normalization across runtime kernels.


Appendix: useful queries and dashboards
-------------------------------------
- SQL snippet to list top failing slices:
  SELECT slice, count(*) as failures, avg(semantic_divergence) as mean_div 
  FROM shadow_runs.results
  WHERE run_id = 'crossmodal-2026-02-20-01'
  GROUP BY slice
  ORDER BY failures DESC
  LIMIT 50;

- Dashboard: /dashboards/shadow-orchestrator/crossmodal-summary (contains heatmaps, time-series for divergence, token perturb histograms).

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
text:
[...] - Dashboard: /dashboards/shadow-orchestrator/crossmodal-summary (contains heatmaps, time-series for divergence, token perturb histograms).


Change log
----------
- 2026-01-15: Draft created by Maya Chen.
- 2026-02-20: Added YAML example, clarified KV-cache policy, tightened initial thresholds and published (reviewers Luis, Priya, Samir).

Related artifacts and links
-------------------------
- Probe catalog: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/prompt-sets-and-benchmarks/crossmodal-probe-catalog-2026 (recommended anchor set).
- Model catalog docs: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/production-model-certification-checklist-2026.

Contact
-------
For questions or to request a run: open an internal ticket in the #eval-orchestration Slack channel or contact Maya Chen (maya.chen@redwood.ai).

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook

Overview:

This playbook defines the Adaptive Eval Funnel (AEF) — a standardized, cost-aware sampling and prioritization pipeline for running continuous evaluation at scale. AEF is designed to reduce token and compute spend by 40–70% relative to naive full-scan evals while preserving coverage for high-risk slices and regression detection sensitivity.

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] This playbook defines the Adaptive Eval Funnel (AEF) — a standardized, cost-aware sampling and prioritization pipeline for running continuous evaluation at scale. AEF is designed to reduce token and compute spend by 40–70% relative to naive full-scan evals while preserving coverage for high-risk slices and regression detection sensitivity.


Goals:
- Maintain high-fidelity regression detection on high-risk slices (precision >= 0.90 on targeted failure classes).
- Reduce end-to-end eval cost per release by 40% (measured in GPU-minutes and token units).
- Provide reproducible, auditable sampling decisions and a transparent budget allocation for product/tenant workloads.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: applies to model onboarding, nightly and pre-release eval runs, and production regression triage workload mirroring. Does NOT replace small-sample human evaluation for subjective quality checks.
- Audience: applied-ML engineers, eval platform owners, product reliability engineers, and model steward teams.

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] - Audience: applied-ML engineers, eval platform owners, product reliability engineers, and model steward teams.


Definitions:
- Funnel stage: a logical step in the AEF (seed, stratified-scan, focused-verify, heavy-sim).
- Slice: a partition of workload (intent category, customer tier, language, prompt-length bin, etc.).
- Budget unit: our internal token+GPU-minute currency; 1 unit = 10k input tokens + 1 GPU-minute at p3 equivalent.

When to use AEF:
1) Pre-release validation for model promotions.
2) Nightly smoke and slice-focused monitoring for production models.
3) Regression triage when a large surface area prevents exhaustive replay.

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] 3) Regression triage when a large surface area prevents exhaustive replay.


High-level funnel (four stages):
1. Seed discovery (low cost)
   - Objective: locate candidate failing slices cheaply.
   - Inputs: lightweight classifiers, anomaly scoring over telemetry, recent customer Qs, error logs.
   - Typical compute: <5% of total budget.
2. Stratified scan (medium cost)
   - Objective: broad coverage with stratified sampling across prioritized dimensions.
   - Method: stratify by slice weights; draw proportional samples with minimum cell size.
   - Typical compute: 25–35% of budget.
3. Focused verify (targeted)
   - Objective: validate potential regressions with larger, deterministic sample sets per flagged slice.
   - Method: fixed-seed replays, ensemble-checks, human-in-the-loop for ambiguous cases.
   - Typical compute: 30–40% of budget.
4. Heavy simulation (expensive)
   - Objective: full-scan or synthetic amplification for highest-risk or regulatory slices.
   - Triggered only if FDR/precision thresholds not met or downstream business metrics at risk.
   - Typical compute: 10–30% of budget (on demand).

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...]    - Typical compute: 10–30% of budget (on demand).


Sampling configuration (recommended defaults):
| Parameter | Default | Rationale |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| monthly_budget_units | 500 | Enough to run nightly stratified scans + 2 pre-release runs for medium-sized products |
| min_cell_size | 50 | Minimum per-slice sample to estimate proportions with modest variance |
| seed_detector_threshold | 0.7 | Anomaly score threshold to escalate from seed to stratified-scan |
| focused_verify_size | 300 | Per-flagged-slice sample size for deterministic checks |
| heavy_sim_trigger_precision | 0.85 | If focused verify precision < this, run heavy sim |

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] | heavy_sim_trigger_precision | 0.85 | If focused verify precision < this, run heavy sim |


Priority weighting scheme (example):
1) Safety / content policy risk: weight x3
2) High-revenue customers (top 10% ARR): weight x2
3) New prompt patterns / long context: weight x1.5
4) Low-frequency slices: floor sample for minimum visibility

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] 4) Low-frequency slices: floor sample for minimum visibility


Operational procedure (step-by-step):
1) Configure budget and slice definitions in eval-harness config (file: eval-configs/aef.yml). Make sure labels map to telemetry fields and tenant IDs.
2) Run Seed discovery job (weekly/nightly). Command: 
```bash
redwood-eval run --pipeline aef --stage seed --config eval-configs/aef.yml --output s3://eval-outputs/aef/seed/$(date +%F)
```
3) Seed job produces ranked slice candidates. Automatically promote top N where anomaly_score >= seed_detector_threshold to Stratified scan. Document promotions in the run artifact.
4) Run Stratified scan with budget clamps. Use stratified-sampling operator with deterministic RNG seed for reproducibility. CLI: 
```bash
redwood-eval run --pipeline aef --stage stratified --config eval-configs/aef.yml --budget-units 150 --seed 20260301
```
5) Post-process stratified results to compute per-slice metrics (accuracy delta, entropy change, top-k hallucination rate). If any slice metric breaches alert thresholds (see "Monitoring and thresholds"), schedule Focused verify.
6) Focused verify is launched with deterministic seeds and ensemble baselines. Results produce a triage report with suggested root-cause tags (prompt-sensitivity, quantization-delta, kv-cache-regression, model-parameter-shift). Human reviewer assigned automatically for ambiguous failures.
7) Heavy simulation is run only with explicit owner approval; include cost estimate and rollback action in the run ticket.

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] 7) Heavy simulation is run only with explicit owner approval; include cost estimate and rollback action in the run ticket.


Monitoring and thresholds:
- Metrics collected per slice: accuracy_delta, top3_confidence_drop, hallucination_rate, token_cost_per_input, p95_latency.
- Alert thresholds (examples):
  - accuracy_delta <= -0.06 -> auto-priority alert
  - hallucination_rate increase >= 0.03 absolute -> safety review
  - token_cost_per_input increase >= 20% -> cost-investigation
  - p95_latency increase >= 200ms -> perf triage

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...]   - p95_latency increase >= 200ms -> perf triage


Acceptance and escalation rules:
- If a high-weight slice (safety or top-revenue) breaches accuracy_delta <= -0.04 in Focused verify, block promotion and open a model regression incident with incident severity S2.
- If multiple mid-weight slices show correlated failures across 3+ prompts, escalate to Applied ML on-call for bisecting.

Reproducibility and auditability:
- All runs must store: config.yaml, RNG seed, model sha, quant-profile id, inference kernel version, and the full input sample set.
- Use artifact naming convention: aef/{model_sha}/{run-date}/{stage}/{budget-id}.zip

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] - Use artifact naming convention: aef/{model_sha}/{run-date}/{stage}/{budget-id}.zip


Cost control knobs and tradeoffs:
- min_cell_size: raising reduces false positives but increases cost.
- stratified_coverage_ratio: 0.2–0.6 recommended. Lower values favor targeted checks.
- use_kv_cache_replay: toggle; caches reduce token compute for long context but may obscure prompt-sensitivity failures.

Example evaluation config (eval-configs/aef.yml):
```yaml
pipeline: aef
budget_units: 300
priority_weights:
  safety: 3
  revenue: 2
  long_context: 1.5
slices:
  - name: billing-intent
    selectors: ["intent:billing", "lang:en"]
  - name: long-context-technical
    selectors: ["prompt_len:>1024", "topic:technical"]
```

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] ```


Triage playbook (on detection of regression):
1) Confirm reproducibility with Focused verify using stored RNG seed.
2) Run bisect suite across model versions and quant-profiles.
3) If quantization implicated, tag with quant-profile id and set rollback to float-weighted baseline.
4) If prompt cluster implicated, add representative prompts to the regression prompt-set and schedule patch evaluation.
5) Publish incident summary to the model pager channel and attach artifact bundle.

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] 5) Publish incident summary to the model pager channel and attach artifact bundle.


Ownership and SLAs:
- Eval platform owner: Applied ML platform team (oncall rotation: applied-ml-platform@redwood.com) — SLA: respond within 2 business hours for pipeline failures.
- Model steward (per model): required to respond to S2 regression incidents within 4 business hours.

Metrics to report weekly:
- total_budget_units_consumed
- number_of_slices_flagged
- avg_time_to_triage (hours)
- precision_of_seed_to_flag pipeline (measured vs focused verify)

Appendix: investigative tools and helpers
- Commands:
  - List past runs: redwood-eval list --pipeline aef --model <sha>
  - Fetch artifacts: redwood-eval fetch --run-id <id> --dest ./artifacts
- JSON example of a slice flag record:
```json
{"slice_name":"billing-intent","anomaly_score":0.82,"flag_reason":"accuracy_delta:-0.07","run_id":"aef-20260301-strat-42"}
```

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
text:
[...] ```


Related policies and pages:
- See prompt-coverage matrix and drift repair guide for how to define slices and minimum coverage targets.
- See regression triage canonical runbook for incident escalation templates.

Revision history:
- 2026-02-18: initial published by Ava Chen.
- 2026-03-01: clarified heavy-simulation gating and added cost knobs by request of reviewers.

Contact:
- Playbook owner: Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai)
- Platform on-call: applied-ml-platform@redwood.com

title: Evaluability Operational Patterns: Lifecycle & Rollout Contracts
text:
Evaluability Operational Patterns: Lifecycle & Rollout Contracts

Summary:
This document defines operational patterns and acceptance contracts that make a model or configuration 'evaluability-ready' for Redwood Inference. It focuses on the full lifecycle from initial onboarding through progressive rollouts, including the evaluation job shape, telemetry gating, and automatic rollback/mitigation actions when quantitative or qualitative gates fail. The guidance is intended for ML engineers, SREs, and product owners managing model promotion across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Goals and scope:
- Define a concise evaluability contract that every model/service must satisfy before promotion to canary and production.
- Provide a reproducible lifecycle pattern: admission -> shadow -> micro-canary -> ramp -> full release.
- Specify metric bundles, thresholds, and stability windows used for automated gating.
- Document probe composition and synthetic traffic primitives for quant-sensitive workloads.
- Describe interaction points with quantization profiles and KV-cache-sensitive flows.

Key definitions:
1) Evaluability contract: a machine- and human-readable specification attached to a model artifact describing required probes, acceptance metrics, and rollout policies.
2) Probe: a focused synthetic or sampled workload slice exercising a behavior (e.g., long-context decoding, function-calling, rerank).
3) Shadow vs Micro-canary: shadow runs do not serve live traffic; micro-canaries route a small percentage of live traffic with tight SLOs and fast rollback.

Operational pattern (high level):
1. Admission (onboarding): model artifact is registered with metadata including quantization-friendly flags, tokenization fingerprint, and probe manifest.
2. Shadow validation: run the probe manifest against a shadow fleet (same model version but isolated) for 24–72 hours to gather stability and fidelity metrics.
3. Micro-canary: route 0.1%–1% live traffic, enforce short stability window (30–120 minutes), and evaluate live SLO adherence and fidelity sentinels.
4. Ramp: exponential increase with verification windows (1h, 4h, 24h).
5. Full release: model marked as promoted; retention of shadow tracing for 7 days and periodic probes at 6h cadence.

Lifecycle checklist (must be present in model metadata on admission):
- model_id (artifact tag)
- quant-profile-tag (or "none")
- probe-manifest-location (URI)
- owner:team and pager
- required-metrics (list) and alert thresholds
- rollback-policy (immediate | manual-approval | gradual-fallback)

Probe manifest guidance:
- Each probe entry should include: name, description, expected-response-pattern, sample-size, annotation-template, priority (1–5), and affinity (latency/throughput/quant-sensitive).
- Include negative tests and adversarial perturbations alongside nominal cases.
- Design probes to be deterministic where possible (fixed seeds) and provide a re-seeding policy for stochastic tasks.

Example probe manifest (YAML snippet):
```yaml
probes:
  - name: longcontext-coherence-16k
    priority: 1
    sample_size: 120
    affinity: long-context
    expected_signatures: ["coherence_score>=0.85", "no-truncation"]
  - name: functioncall-stability
    priority: 2
    sample_size: 200
    affinity: function-call
    expected_signatures: ["return_schema_match", "latency_p50<180ms"]
```

Metric bundle and gating table:
The evaluability contract relies on a bundle paired with severity tiers for automated actions. Below is the recommended gate matrix used during micro-canary and ramp stages.

| Metric group | Metric | Canary Gate (0.1–1%) | Ramp Gate (1–25%) | Severity if breached | Action |
|--------------|--------|----------------------:|------------------:|---------------------:|--------|
| Latency | p50 / p95 / p99 (ms) | p95 < 300ms | p95 < 500ms | high | rollback or route-to-fallback |
| Quality | fidelity delta (anchor probes) | delta <= 0.03 | delta <= 0.05 | critical | immediate rollback, alert ML-oncall |
| Stability | response variance index (RVI) | RVI < 0.08 | RVI < 0.12 | high | pause ramp, run extended shadow |
| Cost | token-cost-per-request | < 1.2x baseline | < 1.5x baseline | medium | notify infra, consider quant-profile |
| Safety | sentinel-failure-rate | < 0.001 | < 0.002 | critical | block release, run safety sandbox |

Note: gates are expressed relative to the _baseline_ model/service being replaced. Baseline is recorded at admission-time.

Telemetry contract and required signals:
- Core signals: request_id, model_id, version, tenant_id, route, latency_p50/p95/p99, token_count_in/out, embed_dims (if applicable), kv_cache_hit, quant_profile_used.
- Quality signals: anchor_probe_id, anchor_score, human_rating_sample_id (if available).
- Stability signals: seed, randomness_label, prompt_hash, system_prompt_version.
- Safety signals: sentinel_id, fail_type, content_category_tags.

Automated gating engine behavior (implementation notes):
- Windowing: use rolling windows with both short (15m, 1h) and medium (6h, 24h) perspectives. Triggers must evaluate both absolute and trend-based deltas (e.g., 5% worsening over 1h).
- Aggregation: compute weighted metrics by traffic slice; heavy slices can dominate and should be isolated in evaluation.
- Explainability: when a gate trips, the engine emits a short diagnostic bundle (top-3 contributing slices, diff-of-anchors, sample failed requests).

Quantization and KV-cache considerations:
- Quant-sensitive probes: probe manifest must flag probes that are expected to be sensitive to quantization; for these probes require an explicit quant-fallback policy (e.g., route to FP16 variant or increase beam/temperature adjustments).
- KV-cache-aware ramp: for models using persistent KV caches, shadow validation must simulate realistic session patterns (e.g., average sequence length, churn, re-use rate). Include a KV-cache-hit metric and require no >5% drop compared to baseline in early gates.

Rollout policies (examples):
- Immediate rollback: any safety sentinel breach or fidelity delta > 0.07 during micro-canary triggers an immediate rollback to the last stable model.
- Gradual fallback: if latency breach but fidelity is maintained, apply CPU/GPU autoscaling or route to a quantized-but-tested variant for 10 minutes before rollback.
- Manual approval: model changes that touch embedding schema or function-calling require an explicit product/owner manual approval after ramp-green status.

Runbook: responding to a failing gate (steps):
1. Automated action: system pauses rollout and emits diagnostic bundle to #ml-oncall and #infra-ops.
2. Investigator triage (10–30m): owner inspects diagnostics, checks probe samples, and confirms whether breach is distributional or systemic.
3. Quick mitigation (30–90m): apply fallback routing (route 100% of affected slices to baseline), or increase replica capacity if latency-driven.
4. Root cause (RC) investigation (4–72h): pair with SRE to walk traces, reproduce in shadow, and propose permanent fix (model tweak, prompt rewrite, quant-profile change).
5. Post-incident: record regression tickets, update probe manifest if probe failed to detect the issue, and schedule re-run of the lifecycle.

Ownership and SLAs:
- Ownership: the model owner (ML engineer) is responsible for probe manifest correctness and responding to canary alerts. The platform (serving/runtime) team owns telemetry reliability and automated gating implementation. SRE owns capacity and latency remediation knobs.
- SLAs for evaluability pipeline: 99.9% availability for probe orchestration and 99.95% for telemetry ingestion during rollouts.

Acceptance checklist before full promotion (all must be green):
- Shadow pass: no critical sentinel failures for 72h and fidelity delta <= 0.03.
- Micro-canary pass: no critical breaches in 2 consecutive windows (30m and 2h).
