- Telemetry completeness: all required signals present and validated for 24h.
- Rollout policy set: rollback path and fallback variant declared.

Operational examples and CLI snippets:
- Run a probe locally against the shadow fleet:
```bash
rwi eval run --model my-model:v2 --probes probes/longcontext-coherence-16k.yaml --shadow true --window 24h
```
- Query recent gate diagnostics (returns a small JSON bundle):
```bash
rwi eval diagnostics --model my-model:v2 --since 2026-02-28T00:00:00Z
```
(Above CLI returns a zipped bundle; platform UI will render top-10 failed samples inline.)

Appendix: common failure modes and preventive actions:
- Symptom: fidelity delta only in a specific tenant slice. Prevention: add isolation probes per-tenant or cohort and use tenant-aware ramping.
- Symptom: high p99 with stable p95. Prevention: add tail-probing with long sequences and adversarial tokens; consider micro-batching config change.
- Symptom: quantized variant regresses on function-call outputs. Prevention: include structured-output validation probes (schema conformance) in admission.

Questions and next steps:
- Proposed automation: upstream every model artifact with a minimal evaluability contract enforced by CI at merge-time.
- Owners: steward: Aisha Patel (applied-ml); implementation lead: Daniel Kim (eng-serving-runtime).
- Proposed milestones: CI gating POC by 2026-04-01; platform gating beta for internal teams by 2026-06-01.

References and links:
- See also: model promotion guidelines, probe taxonomy index, quantization profile catalog.
- Contact: #applied-ml, #serving-runtime, ml-oncall@redwood.internal

title: Shadow-to-Production: Eval Continuity & Safety Guidelines
text:
Shadow-to-Production: Eval Continuity & Safety Guidelines

Summary:
This document prescribes the end-to-end process for using shadow (mirrored) traffic and continuous eval outputs to safely promote models into production for text generation and embedding workloads. It connects eval-harness artifacts (workload fingerprints, prompt coverage, quantization evaluation), runtime observability, and automated rollback triggers so teams can preserve quality SLOs while iterating quickly.

Goals:
- Ensure production promotions are gated by real-traffic shadow evaluation + synthetic continuity checks.
- Define concrete metrics, thresholds, and automated actions for safe rollback and triage.
- Provide runbook steps and ownership for degradation investigations spanning eval-harness -> runtime -> model-promotion-service.

Applicability:
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployment modes that support request mirroring (shadow routing).
- Target workloads: text-generation/chat, embeddings, and reranking.

Key Concepts and Signals:
1) Shadow run: mirror a sample of production requests to candidate model instances without affecting user-visible responses. Shadow traffic should preserve original request headers and sampling ratios.
2) Continuity test suite: a short, prioritized set of prompts derived from prompt-sets-and-benchmarks that exercise long-tail failure modes for the target workload.
3) Response Stability Index (RSI): a composite signal combining token-level edit distance, semantic-similarity delta (embedding cosine), and policy-safety flags. Range 0-100, lower is better.
4) Token Amplification Ratio (TAR): ratio of mean tokens generated by candidate model to baseline. Flags unbounded or degenerate generation.
5) Quantization Delta: measured difference in essential eval metrics (RSI, semantic-score, latency) between full-precision and quantized variants during shadow runs.

Gating Metrics and Thresholds (default baselines):
- RSI: <= 4.0 relative increase over baseline (if >4.0, fail gate).
- p95 latency: no more than 20% regression vs baseline and absolute p95 <= 1.2s for small-chat profiles.
- TAR: between 0.8 and 1.5 (outside => investigate hallucination/verbosity).
- Semantic Recall (embedding cosine on ground-truth checks): >= 0.92 average for retrieval prompts.
- Safety flags (policy classifier): no more than 0.2% absolute increase in high-severity flags.
- Quantization Delta: RSI increase <= 1.5 and latency improvement >= 10% (otherwise treat quantized variant as incompatible for that workload).

Traffic Sampling and Shadow Configuration:
- Shadow sampling rate: start at 0.1% of requests for initial verification, ramp to 2-5% as confidence increases.
- Sampling strategy: stratified by route and prompt-type fingerprint (use eval-orch fingerprint tagging) so slices are covered proportionally.
- Forge requests exactly as sent by clients (including conversation history) but set header X-Shadow-Run: candidate-model-id.
- Storage: persist shadow responses in the eval-orch datastore for at least 30 days for retrospective triage.

Continuity Test Suite (runtime-synth):
- Run 1: latency-sensitive prompts (short queries, token cap 64) x 200 samples. Check p95 and token tail behavior.
- Run 2: long-context prompts (>= 2048 tokens) x 100 samples. Verify KV-cache correctness and output truncation.
- Run 3: adversarial prompt set (from prompt-sets-and-benchmarks/adversarial-noise) x 300 samples. Measure safety flags and RSI.
- Run 4: embedding recall microbench (100 paired queries). Measure semantic score and embedding variance.

Promotion Workflow (high-level):
1) Preflight: run inference-readiness-eval-matrix locally with model candidate (unit-sized synthetic suite).
2) Shadow rollout stage A: mirror 0.1% traffic for 48 hours. Collect metrics and continuity tests.
3) Review: automated report generated by eval-orch summarizing gating metrics; reviewers assigned (owner + applied-ml) must sign off or open a triage issue.
4) Shadow stage B: increase to 2% mirror for 72 hours if stage A passed. Run full continuity suite nightly.
5) Canary (optional): route 1% of live responses to candidate for actual responses with rollback guardrails and customer-scope opt-outs.
6) Promote to production routing if all gates pass for 7 days; otherwise, rollback to previous stable model and open a regression investigation.

Automated Rollback Triggers (examples):
- Any RSI increase >8.0 vs baseline within a 1-hour window.
- Safety flag spike: >=0.5% absolute increase in critical flags in 15 minutes.
- Latency SLO breach: sustained p95 > 1.2x baseline for 30 minutes or p99 > 2.0x baseline.
- Token amplification: TAR > 2.0 sustained for 10 minutes.
- Quantization catastrophic: semantic recall drop > 0.08 absolute.

Immediate Rollback Procedure:
1) Alerting: Pager duty escalation to oncall-model-owner and applied-ml triage@redwood. Notify customer-success if any customer-visible regressions are observed.
2) Execution: call model-promotion-service API to revert traffic to previous model_id. Example: 
   curl -X POST https://internal.api.redwood/v1/model-promote 
     -H 'Authorization: Bearer $RD_TOKEN' 
     -H 'Content-Type: application/json' 
     -d '{"action":"rollback","target_model_id":"model-1234","reason":"RSI spike"}'
3) Preserve artifacts: snapshot shadow response storage slice, collect profiler traces from runtime-collector, and start automated bisect job if enabled.
4) Postmortem: within 72 hours produce a regression triage that includes top failing prompt slices, quantization parameters, and kernel-level profiling.

Investigation Playbook (triage owner responsibilities):
- Step 0: Confirm alert validity (check noise, infra events).
- Step 1: Compare shadow vs baseline slices in eval-dashboard; prioritize high-severity slices with >0.5 RSI delta.
- Step 2: Run hypothesis checks: quantization delta? prompt-tokenization mismatch? KV-cache context corruption? model artifacts mismatch?
- Step 3: If quantization suspected, re-run quant-eval job with --deterministic-seed and capture full logits for 100 seeds.
- Step 4: If infra suspected, attach runtime profiler traces and coord with eng-serving-runtime for kernel scheduling logs.
- Step 5: If model bug, label and route to model-onboarding team for rollback/retrain.

Quantization Specific Guidance:
- Always include a quantization shadow pass in Stage A for any new quant profile. Record Quantization Delta (RSI, latency, memory) in the promotion report.
- Reject quantized promotion when RSI increases >1.5 and semantic recall drops >0.02 for retrieval/rewrite workloads.
- Recommended quant profiles by workload:
  * chat-medium: int8-gptq-8 + group-size=64 (fast path; verify with long-context tests).
  * retrieval-heavy: fp16 fallback preferred; quantized variants must pass embedding-stability audit.

Observability and Dashboards (what to monitor):
- eval-orch/shadow-metrics: RSI-by-slice, TAR, safety-flag-rate, embedding-delta.
- runtime/latency: p50/p95/p99 per-model and per-route.
- stability/rolling: 1h, 6h, 24h windows for RSI and safety flags.
- promotion-service: model-version lineage and active traffic splits.

Example CLI / YAML snippets:
- Run a targeted continuity eval (eval-orch):
  redwood-eval run --suite continuity-short --model candidate:2026-04-01 --out results/continuity-short.json --samples 200

- Shadow routing rule (kubernetes Ingress annotation):
  annotations:
    redwood.io/shadow-target: "model:candidate-2026-04-01"
    redwood.io/shadow-sample: "0.001"

Ownership and Roles:
- Model Owner (primary): responsible for promotion and initial triage.
- Applied ML (secondary): owns eval-harness suites, continuity tests, and quantization pass criteria.
- Serv‑runtime: responsible for tracing, profiler data, and kernel-level reconciliation when latency/throughput regressions appear.
- Customer Success: notified for production-visible regressions and coordinates customer communication.

Risk Register (common failure modes):
- Silent semantic drift due to tokenization mismatch between training and serving. Mitigation: tokenization fingerprint checks in preflight.
- Quantized hallucinations: mitigation: additional adversarial-run sweep and reject quant-profile if TAR or RSI flags exceed thresholds.
- Data leakage in shadow logs: mitigate by PII scrubbing and strict retention policies (30-day default, 7-day for sensitive customers).

Appendix A — Quick Checklist Before Promotion:
- [ ] Preflight eval matrix passed (unit and synthetic).
- [ ] Shadow stage A (0.1%) passed gating metrics for 48h.
- [ ] Continuity test nightly runs succeeded at 0 regressions for top-5 critical slices.
- [ ] Quantization Delta measured and within thresholds.
- [ ] Monitoring dashboards provisioned and reviewers assigned.
- [ ] Rollback playbook tested within the last 30 days (game-day).

Appendix B — Example Promotion Acceptance Table (fill in in review):
| Metric | Baseline | Candidate | Delta | Pass? | Notes |
| RSI (avg) | 2.4 | 3.1 | +0.7 | Yes | within 4.0 threshold |
| p95 latency | 820ms | 900ms | +9.7% | Yes | under 20% cap |
| TAR | 1.0 | 1.12 | +12% | Yes | acceptable verbosity |
| Safety flags | 0.12% | 0.15% | +0.03% | Yes | low absolute delta |

Document Maintenance:
- Owner: applied-ml team (Marisol Chen).
- Review cadence: quarterly or after any production regression.
- Next scheduled review: 2026-04-01.

Related resources and links:
- eval-harness: orchestration runbooks and runner CLI (link internal)
- prompt-sets-and-benchmarks: canonical continuity and adversarial suites (link internal)
- model-onboarding: inference-readiness and promotion matrix page (link internal)

Contact and escalation:
- applied-ml-oncall@redwood (primary)
- eng-serving-runtime-oncall@redwood (secondary)

This guideline balances safe promotion speed with robust automated protection. Follow the thresholds as defaults and tune per-product with signoff from applied-ml and product owners.

title: Synthetic Mosaic Eval Cookbook: Priming + Canonicalization + Quant-Fallback
text:
Synthetic Mosaic Eval Cookbook: Priming + Canonicalization + Quant-Fallback

Overview

This document describes the "Synthetic Mosaic" evaluation pattern: a composable approach that combines localized priming, canonicalization, and quant-fallback probes to produce robust, cost-aware onboarding signals for edge and mixed-precision LLM services (multilingual, function-call capable). The goal is to detect quality regressions and quantization sensitivity across low-frequency slices while keeping eval cost bounded.

Who this is for
- ML engineers running model onboarding and quant migration for production services.
- Evaluation owners responsible for probe portfolios and telemetry contracts.
- SREs operating mixed-precision fleets and canary rollouts.

Why we need a Mosaic pattern
- Rare-intent and multilingual slices often fail silent quality checks because synthetic probes don’t capture priming/context artifacts.
- Quantization and KV-cache interactions cause subtle coherence regressions only visible under particular priming and canonicalized inputs.
- Running exhaustive full-fidelity evals is cost-prohibitive; we need a low-cost hedging strategy that surfaces high-risk slices for deeper inspection.

Key concepts and definitions
- Mosaic probe: a small, intentional bundle of related probes for a target slice combining (a) primed variants (localized priming), (b) canonicalized transformations (standardized inputs), and (c) quant-fallback comparisons (float baseline vs quantized variant).
- Localized priming: repeatable short-context priming patterns representative of real user prompts (e.g., product-specific examples, local date/time formats, code snippets).
- Canonicalization: deterministic normalization applied to inputs (token-level normalization, whitespace/casing rules, function-call structure canonicalization) to reduce noise and isolate model changes.
- Quant-fallback probe: paired evaluation where the same probe is executed against a float baseline and the intended quantized runtime to measure fidelity delta and emergent failure modes.

Scope and applicability
- Target services: any text-generation or function-call endpoint that will run mixed-precision or quantized models. Works for multilingual and few-shot scenarios.
- Not intended for: high-frequency ad-hoc prompts (use live telemetry probes instead) or purely embedding-only services.

Design components
1) Probe family composition
   - For each service slice, synthesize 6–12 mosaic probes grouped into three cohorts: Primed (4), Canonical (4), Edge-variants (2).
   - Primed cohort: same prompt stem with 3 distinct priming templates (e.g., product example, short narrative, structured list).
   - Canonical cohort: normalized transformations (strip extraneous punctuation, standardize dates to YYYY-MM-DD, canonicalize function-call JSON ordering).
   - Edge-variants: long-context and truncated-context variants to stress KV-cache behavior.

2) Lightweight fidelity scoring (cost-aware)
   - Use a two-stage scoring pipeline: (A) cheap lexical/semantic heuristics (BLEU-lite, token overlap, function-call schema match) and (B) selective human or heavy automated eval for flagged probes.
   - Flagging thresholds are configured in the rollout table below.

3) Quant-fallback delta measurement
   - Execute mosaic probes against float-baseline (f32 or established gold) and quant-target (q4/nn) and calculate: token-level edit distance, function-call schema delta, hallucination indicator score, and perplexity ratio.

Onboarding checklist (step-by-step)
1. Identify service slices for coverage: list top-50 rare-intent slices by traffic fraction <= 0.5% but high business impact.
2. For each slice, synthesize a mosaic probe family (6–12 probes) following the composition rules. Document priming templates.
3. Run baseline executions against the established float-certified model and record reference outputs.
4. Run quant-target executions (same seed/temperature) and collect diffs.
5. Compute cheap heuristics and apply the flagging policy.
6. For flagged probes, schedule a micro-human review or heavy automated eval (semantic embedding similarity + annotator pass).
7. Produce a per-slice decision: promote, require model tweak, adjust quant profile, or block rollout.

Rollout policy and stages
- Shadow (0%): execute mosaic probes in shadow to collect telemetry for 48h. No traffic routing.
- Micro-canary (1–2%): route a tiny traffic shard; enforce synchronous quant-fallback probe runs and fast alerting.
- Canary (10%): require zero flagged probes for 24h before ramp beyond 10%.
- Ramp (25–100%): continuous monitoring with weekly re-eval of mosaic probes.

Config table: flagging thresholds and recommended actions

| Metric | Warning threshold | Action (automated) | Action (human) |
|--------|-------------------:|--------------------|----------------|
| Token edit ratio (quant vs float) | > 0.12 | mark probe flagged | detailed human review |
| Function-call schema mismatch rate | > 2 mismatches / 10 probes | block function-call rollout | annotate and escalate to model-owner |
| Hallucination indicator (heuristic) | > 0.15 | alert on slice | pair with semantic eval |
| Latency delta (quant vs float) | > 20% increase | flag perf regression | SRE triage |
| Cost delta (tokens/response) | > 30% increase | evaluate batching/priming tweak | cost-owner review |

title: Synthetic Mosaic Eval Cookbook: Priming + Canonicalization + Quant-Fallback
text:
[...] | Latency delta (quant vs float) | > 20% increase | flag perf regression | SRE triage |
| Cost delta (tokens/response) | > 30% increase | evaluate batching/priming tweak | cost-owner review |

- Notes: thresholds are intentionally conservative for canary stages and can be relaxed for low-risk slices.

Commands & eval harness snippets
- Example invocation (local evaluator CLI):

  ./evalctl run --suite mosaic --slice-id auth-email-rare --model baseline-f32 --model-compare q4a --seeds 42 --out results/auth-email-mosaic.json

- Minimal YAML config (mosaic probe family):

  suite: mosaic
  slice_id: auth-email-rare
  probes: 8
  priming_templates:
    - "You are a helpful assistant. Example: {product} handles ..."
    - "Short explain: {question}"
  canonicalization:
    date_format: YYYY-MM-DD
    strip_whitespace: true
  quant_targets: [q4a, q8_0]

Monitoring and alerts (metric -> alert rule)
- alert: mosaic_flag_high
  condition: flagged_probes(slice) >= 2 within 1h
  severity: P2
  runbook: follow Troubleshooting section, escalate to model-owner and SRE if function-call mismatch present.

- alert: quant_fidelity_regression
  condition: aggregate_token_edit_ratio > 0.10 over 1k probes
  severity: P1
  runbook: immediate rollback candidate and postmortem.

Troubleshooting and typical failure modes
1) Many canonical probes flagged but primed probes OK
   - Likely: canonicalization rules overly aggressive; re-check tokenization boundary handling. Re-run with less aggressive normalization.
2) Primed probes fail but canonical pass
   - Likely: priming artifacts interact with decoding policy. Test with temperature=0 and deterministic seeds. Consider priming rewrite to shorter anchors.
3) Function-call semantics diverge after quantization
   - Run schema-preserving canonicalization (stable key ordering) and increase float baseline sample size. If mismatch persists, block rollout for function-call routes.
4) High latency delta with quant target
   - Confirm execution backend uses optimized kernels; check kernel selection logs and hardware affinity. If hardware mismatch, route to dedicated capacity.

Operational responsibilities
- Probe synthesis and maintenance: Applied ML (probe-stewards) — primary: Priya Nair.
- Eval executions and scoring pipeline: Eval infra team — primary: Lucas Kim.
- Rollout decisions and business impact signoff: Service owners and product manager.
- SRE on-call for perf regressions: Eng SRE rotation.

Acceptance criteria for promotion
- Zero P1-flagged probes in Canary for 24h.
- Aggregate token edit ratio across mosaic suite < 0.08.
- Function-call schema match >= 99% on the mosaic probes.

Appendix: Example probe templates
- Primed example (support ticket): "Customer: I received a duplicate invoice dated 2025-12-11 for order #12345. Please summarize and next steps."
- Canonical example: "Invoice inquiry | ORDER_ID: 12345 | DATE: 2025-12-11"
- Edge (long context): 3x concatenated previous messages to replicate long-session context.

Related pages and references
- See the Eval Mesh fingerprinting guide for workload selection heuristics.
- See Model Promotion Playbook for cross-team signoff requirements.

title: Synthetic Mosaic Eval Cookbook: Priming + Canonicalization + Quant-Fallback
text:
[...] - See the Eval Mesh fingerprinting guide for workload selection heuristics.
- See Model Promotion Playbook for cross-team signoff requirements.

Review cadence
- Probe portfolio review quarterly and after any major quant profile change.
- Emergency re-eval whenever a production regression is detected.

Contact
- Document owner: Maya Chen (applied-ml).
- For runbook escalations: ping #eval-oncall and #sre-performance.

Change log
- 2026-02-15: Created initial cookbook.
- 2026-03-01: Added rollout thresholds and YAML snippets.

title: Anchor-based workload synthesis and probe seeding playbook
text:
Anchor-based workload synthesis and probe seeding playbook

Summary:

This playbook describes a repeatable process for synthesizing representative workload probes using an anchor-based strategy and for seeding those probes into our eval harness (eval-orchestrator and probe-farm). The goal is to improve coverage of rare but impactful workload slices (e.g., long-context, tokenization edgecases, multimodal anchors) and to make shadow/quantized evaluations more likely to catch regressions before promotion. This document covers architecture, synthesis heuristics, seeding cadence, metrics to monitor, and a triage procedure for failures that surface during these probes.

Motivation:

- Empirical coverage of real traffic is incomplete: production traffic contains long-tail combinations of user intent, formatting, and quantization sensitivity.
- Generic random probes miss structured anchors that reliably trigger failure modes (e.g., specific punctuation patterns, nested JSON payloads, language-mixing).
- Early detection of quantization-induced degradation requires probes that are both workload-anchored and quant-awareness-tagged.

When to use this playbook:

- Before promoting model artifacts from staging -> shadow -> canary.
- Prior to enabling a new quantization profile on Dedicated or Private.
- When telemetry identifies recurring unexplained quality drops on a slice with low sample counts.

Definitions and concepts:

- Anchor: a compact, high-leverage token sequence or feature (e.g., long code block, multilingual snippet, repeated enumerations, math-laden text) known to stress a model or inference path.
- Anchor seed: a synthesized example centered on one or more anchors.
- Probe: a single eval example run through the eval harness with deterministic seeds and telemetry capture.
- Probe farm: the service that schedules, executes, and stores probe runs (internal service name: probe-farm).
- Eval orchestrator: coordinates probe batches and routes runs to shadow/dedicated endpoints (internal service name: eval-orchestrator).

Architecture overview:

- Probe generation pipeline (anchor-extractor -> synth-engine -> probe-store).
- Eval scheduling (eval-orchestrator) that tags runs for quant-profile and routing policy.
- Telemetry collection (kinesis -> eval-store -> enrichment -> metrics).
- Alerting/triage integration (Prometheus alerts, Sentry events, regressions dashboard).

Synthesis heuristics (anchor extraction):

1. Anchor sources:
   - Telemetry-derived fingerprints: extract high-MI tokens from request text when a quality alert was observed.
   - Static catalog: known anchors curated by applied ML (e.g., "triple-backtick code blocks", "nested JSON with arrays", "RTL scripts + emojis").
   - Seeded generative transforms: take high-traffic utterances and apply controlled transforms (lengthening, shuffling lists, injecting noise).

2. Anchor ranking: score anchors by a composite score: frequency_in_failures * rarity_in-prod * severity_impact. Keep top-K anchors per week (K default 250).

3. Compositional probes: combine 1–3 anchors per probe to stress cross-slice interactions. Limit composite probes to combinations that appear in production fingerprints or are logically plausible.

Seeding strategy and cadence:

- Daily: add 50 new single-anchor probes and replay prior failing probes.
- Weekly: add 100 composite-anchor probes prioritized by cross-slice coverage delta.
- Pre-promotion: run a full anchor sweep (all anchors ranked above threshold) through the promotion pipeline (staging -> shadow -> canary).

Probe metadata and tagging (required fields):

- probe_id: UUID string
- anchors: comma-separated anchor tags (e.g., codeblock,long_context,rtl)
- origin: telemetry|catalog|generated
- intended_models: comma-separated model ids (e.g., rwd-7b-v2, rwd-13b-quant8)
- quant_profile: name or "none"
- deterministic_seed: integer (for eval reproducibility)

Sample probe JSON (stored in probe-store):

{
  "probe_id": "uuid-1234-abcd",
  "anchors": "codeblock,long_context",
  "origin": "generated",
  "intended_models": "rwd-7b-v2,rwd-7b-quant8",
  "quant_profile": "q8_telemetry_tuned",
  "deterministic_seed": "42",
  "input": "```python\ndef factorial(n):\n    if n <= 1:\n        return 1\n    return n * factorial(n-1)\n```\nDescribe the complexity and edgecases..."
}

Integration with quantization profiles:

- For each probe, synth-engine must schedule runs across: float baseline, quant-profile variants in the promotion matrix, and fallback policy variants (e.g., hybrid FP16 codepath).
- Track a per-probe quality delta between baseline and quantized variants. We consider a probe failure if: (absolute_quality_drop >= 3 percentage points) OR (semantic-fidelity-score decrease >= 2%). Thresholds are tuned per workload bucket.

Quality signals and telemetry fields to capture:

- generation_latency_ms (median and p95)
- token_count_input and token_count_output
- semantic_similarity_baseline_vs_variant (0-1 float)
- exact_match_for_anchor_tokens (boolean / percent covered)
- logit_gap_or_confidence_delta (float)
- failure_mode_tag (string): hallucination|truncation|formatting_error|execution_mismatch

Alerting and SLOs:

- Probe guard SLO: 99.9% of scheduled probes must complete end-to-end in 24 hours.
- Quality guard: for anchor-ranked probes in the top-100, any model/quant pair with semantic_similarity_baseline_vs_variant < 0.90 triggers a P1 evaluation ticket and blocks promotion.
- Latency guard: p95 generation_latency_ms increase > 2x relative to baseline for top-100 probes triggers investigation (SLO owner: Runtime Performance Team).

Runbook: triage for failing probes (order of operations):

1. Confirm: check probe-run logs in eval-store and the attached artifacts (response diffs, token-level alignments).
2. Re-run: schedule a deterministic re-run with the same seed to rule out transient infra flakiness.
3. Cross-quant check: compare baseline float -> all quant variants. If only quant variants fail, mark as "quant-sensitive" and escalate to Quant Team.
4. Regression bisect: use automated-regression-bisect tool to identify the first failing model commit or kernel change. Attach bisect report to the ticket.
5. Create remediation plan: options include quant-profile tweak, local FP fallback for anchor class, model retune, or probe exclusion if proven noise.
6. Canary rollback policy: if canary fails on >5% of top-100 anchors, rollback canary deployment and create incident.

Owners and responsibilities:

- Probe generation and synth-engine: Applied ML (owner: Priya Desai).
- Probe execution and scheduling: Eval infra (owner: Marco Nguyen).
- Quant-profile tuning & fallback policies: Serving runtime / Quant Team (owner: Lin Zhao).
- Triage and incident coordination: Eng SRE + Applied ML on-call rotation.

Examples and CLI snippets:

- Add a new anchor to the static catalog:

$ rw-probectl add-anchor --name "nested-json-arrays" --pattern '{"items": ["<var>", ...]}' --owner "applied-ml" --tags "json,structure"

- Seed a generated probe batch:

$ rw-probectl seed-batch --source generated --anchors "nested-json-arrays,emoji-mix" --count 50 --quant-profiles "q8_telemetry_tuned" --seed 20260302

- Query recent failing probes:

$ rw-probectl list-failures --since 7d --min-severity 1 --anchor-tag long_context

Configuration examples (YAML snippet for eval-orchestrator routing rules):

routing_rules:
  - name: "quant-sensitive-shadow"
    match_anchors: ["codeblock","long_context","nested_json"]
    prioritize_models: ["rwd-7b-v2"]
    route_to: ["shadow-cluster-01"]
    quant_profiles: ["q8_telemetry_tuned","fp16_fallback"]

Operational notes and best practices:

- Keep anchor catalog explicit and auditable. Each anchor entry must include an author, justification, and production fingerprints if available.
- Limit compositional probes during high-cost evaluation windows to control budget. Flag these to finance when totals exceed monthly threshold.
- If a probe repeatedly flakes, mark with "transient" and add additional diagnostics (GPU traces, kernel selection logs).

Appendix: telemetry schema (CSV header example)

probe_id,run_id,timestamp,model,quant_profile,anchors,latency_ms,semantic_similarity,token_in,token_out,failure_mode

References and related pages:

- Eval mesh and workload fingerprinting (internal reference): /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/eval-mesh-and-workload-fingerprinting-2026
- Quant profile synthesis playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quant-profile-synthesis-and-safety-playbook-2024

Change log:

- 2026-01-15: created initial playbook (Priya Desai).
- 2026-02-20: tightened quant failure thresholds and added routing YAML example (Lin Zhao).
- 2026-03-02: added CLI examples and clarified ownership matrix (Sofia Alvarez).

title: Runtime Shadow Rehearsal and Localized Probe Synthesis Playbook
text:
Runtime Shadow Rehearsal and Localized Probe Synthesis Playbook

Summary

This playbook defines the runtime "shadow rehearsal" pattern: an operationalized workflow that (1) synthesizes localized evaluation probes targeted at tenant cohorts, locales, and latency bands; (2) runs those probes in a low-risk shadow mesh against quantized and mixed-precision artifacts; and (3) performs cost-aware rehearsal passes that validate quality, latency, and coherence before a real rollout. The goal is to detect regressions and quant-induced degradations earlier while bounding evaluation cost and preserving tenant privacy.

Scope

- Applies to model promotion and quantized rollouts for text generation and multi-turn chat services (service names: eval-orch, shadow-mesh, rehearsal-runner).
- Covers probe generation, seed selection, rehearsal execution, gating metrics, and rollback triggers.
- Intended for on-prem, private VPC, and hosted Dedicated deployments.

Why this pattern

- Longtail and locale-specific regressions often escape coarse global evals. Localized probes increase sensitivity to per-tenant failure modes.
- Running rehearsals against quantized artifacts in a shadow mesh simulates production mixing without impacting production traffic.
- Cost-aware caps and staged rehearsal passes allow teams to validate risk under budget constraints.

High-level components

1) Probe Fabricator (probe-fabricator)
   - Responsible for localized probe synthesis: locale-aware lexical meshes, few-shot priming templates, and context anchors.
   - Inputs: seed traffic fingerprints, tenant taxonomy, priming registry, localization lexicons.
2) Shadow Mesh (shadow-mesh)
   - Lightweight runtime that replays probes against quantized variants and the nominal FP32 baseline.
   - Provides instrumentation (response_coherence, fidelity_delta, q_loss, latency_p95, token_emissions).
3) Rehearsal Runner (rehearsal-runner)
   - Orchestrates rehearsal passes, enforces cost caps, aggregates metrics, and writes rehearsal artifacts to eval-orch ledger.
4) Gating Engine
   - Applies SLOs and guardrail rules. Produces pass/fail verdicts and recommended rollout decisions.

Localized probe synthesis strategy

- Taxonomy partitioning: split the tenant base into cohorts along three orthogonal axes: locale (en-US, en-GB, fr-FR, de-DE, ja-JP), intent surface (support, search, compose), and latency band (fast p95 < 150ms, medium 150–500ms, slow > 500ms).
- For each cohort generate probes across these buckets:
  - 40% anchor probes (real user anonymized seeds sampled from last 90 days)
  - 40% synthetic perturbations (paraphrases, tokenization edgecases, punctuation/noise injection)
  - 20% adversarial variants (contrastive prompts designed to stress hallucination and instruction-following)
- Probe counts per rehearsal pass are configurable; default targeted sample: 1k probes per cohort for small services, 10k for high-volume services.

Privacy and provenance

- Anchor probes must be redacted and hashed before leaving telemetry storage (hash-salt per rehearsal job). Raw PII is never exported.
- Provenance metadata attached to each probe includes cohort_id, seed_hash, probe_type, and priming_template_id.

Rehearsal execution modes (staged passes)

1) Dry-run (low-cost smoke)
   - Run: 1–2% of full probe set, quant variants only, no baseline comparison.
   - Purpose: surface obvious failures and infra incompatibilities. Cost cap: $X/day (set by team).
2) Contrast-pass
   - Run: 20% sample, run against FP32 baseline and quant variant(s). Compute fidelity_delta metrics and delta-significance tests.
3) Full-rehearsal
   - Run: 100% probe set, cohort-balanced. Includes heavier metrics (token-level divergence, functioncall stability).

Gating metrics and thresholds

We compute both statistical and decisionable thresholds. Primary signals:

- fidelity_delta (KL-style scoring + templated semantic similarity): fail if cohort mean fidelity_delta > 0.12 and throughput-corrected CI excludes zero.
- response_coherence (annotator-simulated score 0–1): fail if cohort P90 < 0.70 and drop vs baseline > 0.08.
- q_loss (quantization artifact score): fail if absolute q_loss increase > 0.05 and correlated with token_emission anomalies.
- latency_p95: fail if latency_p95 increase > 2x and exceeds target SLO band for that cohort.
- embedding_stability (for retrieval/rerank): fail if cosine shift > 0.06 on average for critical slices.

title: Runtime Shadow Rehearsal and Localized Probe Synthesis Playbook
text:
[...] - latency_p95: fail if latency_p95 increase > 2x and exceeds target SLO band for that cohort.
- embedding_stability (for retrieval/rerank): fail if cosine shift > 0.06 on average for critical slices.

Decision matrix (simplified)

- If any cohort fails fidelity_delta AND response_coherence -> abort rollout for that cohort and open regression ticket.
- If only latency_p95 regression observed but coherence preserved -> engage ops throttling + capacity reroute and run narrow replay.
- If q_loss is high but coherence preserved for anchor-probes -> schedule quant-tuning pass and run targeted micro-rehearsal.

Runbook: step-by-step rehearsal for a scheduled quant rollout

1) Preflight (owner: ModelOnboard Team)
   - Create rehearsal job in eval-orch: include cohort list, probe budget, quant-variant ids, and expected cost cap.
   - Run quick smoke locally with rehearsal-runner dry-run mode: run command below.
2) Dry-run review (owner: Release Lead)
   - Confirm no infra errors in shadow-mesh logs, check instrumentation counters (request_e2e_errors < 0.5%).
3) Contrast-pass (owner: Evaluation Engineers)
   - Run contrast-pass, collect fidelity_delta, response_coherence, and q_loss.
4) Analysis (owner: Applied ML)
   - Run automated delta-significance test (see CLI step). Produce report, tag failing cohorts.
5) Decision (owner: Release Lead + Product)
   - If no failing cohorts -> proceed to phased production rollout.
   - If some cohorts fail -> open triage (link created) and either rollback quant artifact or schedule micro-tunings.
6) Post-rehearsal monitoring (owner: SRE)
   - Enable rehearsal-derived sentinels for 72 hours post-rollout and maintain elevated sampling of failed cohorts.

CLI examples

title: Runtime Shadow Rehearsal and Localized Probe Synthesis Playbook
text:
[...] CLI examples


# Start a dry-run rehearsal (example)
rehearsal-runner start --job-id rjr-20260712-01 --cohorts en-US:support:fast,de-DE:compose:medium --mode dry-run --probe-budget 200 --quant-variant qv-2.1 --cost-cap 200

# Run contrast-pass and upload report
rehearsal-runner start --job-id rjr-20260712-02 --mode contrast --probe-budget 2000 --quant-variant qv-2.1 --baseline mv-1.0 --report-bucket s3://eval-orch/reports/rjr-20260712-02

YAML rehearsal job manifest (example)

job_id: rjr-20260712-02
owner: model-onboard
cohorts:
  - id: en-US-support-fast
    locale: en-US
    intent: support
    latency_band: fast
    probe_target: 1000
  - id: de-DE-compose-medium
    locale: de-DE
    intent: compose
    latency_band: medium
    probe_target: 1000
quant_variants:
  - qv-2.1
baseline: mv-1.0
mode: contrast
cost_cap_usd: "1500"
retention_days: "30"

Interpretation and reporting

- Reports include cohort-level summaries, ranked failure slices, example failing probes, and suggested remediation actions. Attachments include raw witness tokens (hashed) and aggregated diff visualizations.
- Use the provided report template (report-template-v2) for consistency.

Cost control and budgeting

- Rehearsal-runner enforces a hard cost cap at job-level. If cost consumption reaches 90% of cap during a mid-pass, execution pauses, and a partial report is emitted for review.
- Default cost guidance: small service full-rehearsal ~ $800–1,200; large service ~ $4k–8k depending on probe complexity and annotator simulation.

Integration with quant profiles and eval-harness

- Rehearsal jobs reference quant profile IDs from the quantization catalog. Rehearsals must include quant metadata (bitwidth, kernel-profile, stash-policy).
- The probe-fabricator consults the eval-harness priming registry to select few-shot templates, ensuring parity between rehearsal prompts and production priming.

Failure triage and escalation playbook

1) Triage run (owner: Applied ML)
   - Label failing probes by failure mode (hallucination, truncation, functioncall-mismatch, tokenization-error).
2) Cross-team incident (owner: Release Lead)
   - If multiple cohorts fail fidelity_delta or coherence -> declare a Rehearsal Incident (severity: S2) and run causal regression investigation.
3) Rollback policy
   - Immediate rollback recommended when anchor-probe P90 coherence drops below 0.6 for >3 cohorts or when similarity drift >= 0.18 with statistical significance.

Postmortem artifacts and follow-ups

- Create a rehearsal incident dossier containing: rehearsal job manifest, cohort failure matrix, top 50 failing probes, annotator sample, and timeline of actions. Attach to the central incident tracker and link to model-promote PR.
- Follow-ups commonly include: micro-quant-tune, priming-template adjustment, or probe-corpus augmentation.

Ownership and contacts

- Probe Fabricator: Applied ML (owner: Priya Natarajan)
- Rehearsal orchestration: Platform Eval (owner: Marcus Holt)
- Gating & artifacts: ModelOnboard (owner: Sofia Alvarez)
- SRE & post-deploy monitoring: Platform SRE (owner: Liam Chen)

title: Runtime Shadow Rehearsal and Localized Probe Synthesis Playbook
text:
[...] - Gating & artifacts: ModelOnboard (owner: Sofia Alvarez)
- SRE & post-deploy monitoring: Platform SRE (owner: Liam Chen)

Appendices

A) Metrics table (columns: metric, description, alert-threshold, owner)

metric | description | alert-threshold | owner
fidelity_delta | semantic divergence vs baseline | >0.12 cohort mean | Applied ML
response_coherence | simulated annotator coherence 0–1 | cohort P90 < 0.70 | Evaluation
q_loss | quant artifact amplitude | absolute increase > 0.05 | ModelOnboard
latency_p95 | 95th percentile E2E latency | >2x baseline for cohort | SRE
embedding_stability | avg cosine shift per slice | >0.06 | Retrieval Team

B) Delta-significance command (example)

title: Runtime Shadow Rehearsal and Localized Probe Synthesis Playbook
section_header: runs bootstrap CI test comparing metric streams
text:
[...] 
B) Delta-significance command (example)

# runs bootstrap CI test comparing metric streams
python tools/delta_test.py --baseline reports/mv-1.0/metrics.json --variant reports/qv-2.1/metrics.json --metric fidelity_delta --alpha 0.05 --output reports/delta-ci.json

C) Quick checklist before scheduling a full rehearsal

- [ ] Manifest created and reviewed, probes budgeted and redaction confirmed.
- [ ] Quant variant artifacts uploaded to artifact store and kernel-profile declared.
- [ ] Shadow mesh capacity validated and can accept N concurrent rehearsal workers.
- [ ] Cost cap set and billing owner notified.
- [ ] Reviewers assigned and report template linked.

Related links and references

- See: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quant-shadow-cohort-strategy-and-rollback-playbook-2026 for quant fallback patterns.
- See: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/shadow-to-prod-eval-continuity-guidelines-2026 for end-to-end shadow orchestration.

Document revision history

- 2026-02-10: initial draft (S. Alvarez)
- 2026-04-02: added cohort taxonomy and cost guidance (Marcus Holt)
- 2026-07-12: finalized gating thresholds and rollout runbook (S. Alvarez, Priya N.)

title: Precision budgeting and observability manifest
text:
Precision budgeting and observability manifest

Summary
This document defines the Precision Budgeting concept: a compact, auditable manifest that maps workload classes to a quantization "budget" (precision/bitwidth + KV-cache policy + preferred fallback tier) and the observability contract required to safely onboard, operate, and roll back mixed-precision LLM artifacts. The goal is to make quant tradeoffs explicit, measurable, and automatable across the eval harness, onboarding gates, production routing, and regression triage.

Why this exists
- Quantization reduces unit cost but introduces subtle failure modes that are workload-dependent.
- Teams need a single artifact to capture the acceptable quality-budget tradeoffs for a service so the platform can automate routing, monitoring, and rollback decisions.
- This manifest bridges model-onboarding checks, eval harness requirements, quality guardrails, and incident triage signals.

Scope and audience
- Audience: model owners, applied ML engineers, runtime engineers, SREs, and evaluation stewards.
- Scope: inference quantization (dynamic/static/int8/mixed precision), KV-cache interaction policies, probe/eval contracts, and production observability thresholds. Not covered: training-time quant-aware fine-tuning or model architecture changes.

Key concepts
1) Precision budget (single-line): human-readable token that describes the allowable precision for a workload. Example: "pb:service_search_v2=fp16+int8_kv:0.75:fallthrough=dense" meaning: use fp16 base with int8-quantized KV-cache tuned to 0.75 fidelity tolerance, fallback to dense (no quant) if fidelity breaches.
2) Workload class: logical grouping by semantic/latency/stretch (e.g., search-rerank, chat-assistant-short, billing-batch).
3) Fidelity budget: numeric tolerance (0.0–1.0) that maps to acceptable delta on service-level quality metrics (e.g., F1, exact-match, RSI).
4) Observability contract: set of telemetry signals and alert thresholds that must be live before a budget becomes active.

Precision Budget Manifest (recommended schema)
The manifest is a small YAML saved alongside model artifacts and eval reports. The manifest must be generated during the onboarding pipeline and checked into the model service registry. Example YAML (illustrative):

precision_budget: 
  service: service_search_v2
  model_family: rwx-2b
  artifact_tag: rwx-2b-int8-kv-2026-02
  workload_class: search-rerank
  base_precision: fp16
  kv_cache_precision: int8
  kv_cache_policy: per-tenant-disabled
  fidelity_budget: "0.06"  # allowed drop in primary metric (fractional)
  primary_metric: MAP@10
  secondary_metrics: ["latency_p95_ms","response_stability_index"]
  fallback_tier: dense
  canary_percent: 5
  observability:
    telemetry_keys: ["probe_delta_v1","rsi","latency_ms_p95","quant_error_rate"]
    required_retention_days: 14
    alert_rules:
      - name: fidelity-violation
        when: probe_delta_v1 > 0.06 and quant_error_rate > 0.01 for 30m
        action: rollback-to-fallback
      - name: latency-degradation
        when: latency_ms_p95 > 300 for 15m
        action: investigate-scheduling
  owner: team-search
  onboarding_status: gated

Operational readiness checklist
1) Eval harness integration (required)
   - Run quantized artifact through the synthetic eval suite at the same or larger sample as the production shadow set.
   - Metrics to collect: primary_metric (drift), probe_delta_v1 (probe family), response_stability_index (RSI), token-efficiency delta.
   - Run functioncall and streaming probes to cover behavior changes introduced by quant kernels.
   - Automated acceptance if primary_metric drop <= fidelity_budget AND RSI delta <= 0.03.

2) Observability gating (required)
   - The following telemetry must be ingesting to the eval mesh and stored for on-call lookback: probe_delta_v1, rsi, latency_ms_p50/p95/p99, tail_token_repeat_rate, quant_error_rate, cache_hit_ratio.
   - Alerts created in monitoring stack with the same names as manifest alert_rules.

3) Canary and shadowing (required)
   - Start with canary_percent from the manifest (recommend 1–5%).
   - Maintain continuous shadow traffic for 48 hours with per-slice diff reporting every 15 minutes.
   - If any alert fires during canary, automatic rollback must be triggered.

title: Precision budgeting and observability manifest
text:
[...]    - Maintain continuous shadow traffic for 48 hours with per-slice diff reporting every 15 minutes.
   - If any alert fires during canary, automatic rollback must be triggered.

4) Cost benchmarking (informational)
   - Record tokens-per-dollar before and after quantization using the platform cost harness. Target at least 15% cost improvement for int8 KV-cache cases, otherwise re-evaluate quant knob.

Decision matrix: when to accept a quant profile
- Accept if: primary_metric_drop <= fidelity_budget AND RSI_delta <= 0.03 AND no severity>=P2 alerts during 48h canary.
- Reject if: primary_metric_drop > fidelity_budget OR any data-loss or hallucination class > 0.5% on sensitive slices.
- Manual review if: cost saving > 30% but RSI_delta between 0.03–0.07 (requires engineering + product signoff).

Escalation and regression triage playbook
1) First responder: the model owner must triage incoming fidelity-violation alert.
2) Quick triage steps (15 min):
   - Compare shadow metrics vs baseline using the eval harness snapshot.
   - Check quant_error_rate and top-10 failing probes.
   - If failure concentrated on small slice: create probe patch and temporarily increase fallback percent for affected tenant slice.
3) Rollback criteria (automatic): fidelity-violation alert action=rollback-to-fallback.
4) Post-incident investigation (3 day window): run regression bisect using the quant artifact variants (fp16, fp16+int8-kv@low, int8-only) and produce a hypothesis matrix.

Operator runbook (commands)
- Extract probe diffs from eval mesh:
  curl -sS "https://eval-internal/api/v1/probe-diffs?service=service_search_v2&tag=rwx-2b-int8-kv-2026-02" | jq .

- Trigger a controlled rollback via ops CLI:
  redwood ops service rollback --service service_search_v2 --to-tier dense --reason "precision-budget breach"

- Promote revalidated artifact:
  redwood model promote --artifact rwx-2b-int8-kv-2026-02 --env production --manifest precision-budget.yml

Observability metrics and recommended thresholds
- primary_metric (service-specific): fidelity_budget (manifest)
- probe_delta_v1: alert if > fidelity_budget for 30m
- response_stability_index (RSI): alert if drop > 0.03 or absolute < 0.60 for user-facing services
- quant_error_rate: alert if > 0.01 sustained for 30m
- latency_p95_ms: alert if increased > 1.5x baseline or > 300ms (whichever is lower)
- tail_token_repeat_rate: alert if > 0.5%

Onboarding workflow (step-by-step)
1) Model owner: prepare candidate artifact and generate precision_budget manifest in repo.
2) CI: run unit tests + compatibility smoke (tokenization, functioncall shape tests).
3) Eval harness: run full mixed-slice probe sweep (costed budget) and produce artifact-level report.
4) SRE/Platform: verify observability contract (telemetry ingestion + alerts).
5) Product signoff (if manual review flagged).
6) Canary promotion (monitoring enabled).
7) Full rollout if canary passes for configured window.

Best practices and pitfalls
- Avoid single global fidelity numbers: quant sensitivity is probe-family-dependent. Use localized probe budgets for high-risk slices (payments, legal, privacy).
- Always include a KV-cache policy in the manifest: quantizing KV-cache often has larger behavioral impact than base weight quantization.
- Prefer staged quant knob sweeps (int8-low -> int8-mid -> int8-high) rather than one-step jumps.
- Keep an up-to-date regression probe set that contains adversarial, boundary, and functioncall sequences.

Example quick-check list (for reviewers)
- Is there a precision_budget manifest with owner and contact?
- Are all manifest telemetry_keys ingesting and retained for 14+ days?
- Did the quant artifact pass the eval harness acceptance criteria?
- Were canary and shadow windows configured and monitored?
- Is there an explicit fallback_tier and rollback action defined?

Appendix: sample manifest checklist table
- manifest present: YES
- primary_metric defined: YES (MAP@10)
- fidelity_budget: 0.06
- observability hooks: probe_delta_v1, rsi, latency_ms_p95, quant_error_rate
- canary_percent: 5%
- cost_target_improvement: >= 15%
- owner: team-search

title: Precision budgeting and observability manifest
text:
[...] - cost_target_improvement: >= 15%
- owner: team-search

Related documents and references
- Model onboarding: onboarding-fidelity-sla-and-noise-budget-manifest (see model-onboarding)
- Eval harness: quantized-artifact-eval-gate-and-rollout spec
- Regression triage: causal-regression-investigation-handbook

Maintainers and ownership
- Primary: team-search (model owners)
- Platform & tooling: serving-runtime team
- Observability: eng-platform

Revision history
- 2026-02-10: initial draft (Ava Martinez)
- 2026-03-12: added automated alert rules and decision matrix; clarified KV-cache policy guidance (reviewers: Marco D'Souza, Priya Nair, Jonah Reed)

Feedback and next steps
If you are onboarding a quantized artifact, include a link to this manifest in your model service PR and ping the serving-runtime channel for a short readiness review.

title: synthetic-probe-factory-architecture-and-on-demand-api-manifest-2026
text:
synthetic-probe-factory-architecture-and-on-demand-api-manifest-2026

Summary

This document defines the Synthetic Probe Factory: an on-demand service for fabricating, versioning, and serving synthetic evaluation probes (aka probes or synthetic examples) used across Redwood’s eval harness, shadow fleets, and canary pipelines. The factory provides:

- A deterministic probe generation API (REST + batch) with reproducible seeds.
- Metadata, lineage, and artifact registry for probe provenance.
- Integrated cost accounting and per-tenant quota controls.
- Governance hooks for privacy, label reviews, and deprecation workflows.

Goals and non-goals

title: synthetic-probe-factory-architecture-and-on-demand-api-manifest-2026
text:
[...] Goals and non-goals


Goals:
1. Reduce manual probe curation time by 70% for common workload families.
2. Ensure every synthetic probe is reproducible and auditable.
3. Provide a low-latency on-demand path for small probe batches (<= 1k) and a scalable batch path for large fabrications.
4. Integrate with the eval mesh and shadow orchestrator so probes can be routed to private/dedicated environments with safe defaults.

title: synthetic-probe-factory-architecture-and-on-demand-api-manifest-2026
text:
[...] 4. Integrate with the eval mesh and shadow orchestrator so probes can be routed to private/dedicated environments with safe defaults.


Non-goals:
- Replacing human-authored gold datasets. The factory augments, not substitutes, human annotation for high-risk slices.
- Performing large-scale human labeling within the factory; human review tasks are orchestrated to separate tooling (annotator UI).

Motivation

We observed long lead times in producing targeted probes for: emergent instruction patterns, long-context coherency checks, functioncall edge cases, and quantization stress tests. Teams either re-used stale assets or hand-built ad-hoc generators, which caused duplication and provenance gaps. The Synthetic Probe Factory centralizes generation patterns, enforces reproducibility, and embeds cost controls to avoid runaway eval spend.

High-level architecture

title: synthetic-probe-factory-architecture-and-on-demand-api-manifest-2026
section_header: The factory is composed of the following services (logical)
text:
[...] High-level architecture


The factory is composed of the following services (logical):

- Probe API Frontend: exposes /v1/generate and /v1/batch endpoints, authentication, quota checks, and request enrichment.
- Generator Workers: stateless pods implementing generation templates (templating engine + model driver). Each worker tracks seed -> output mapping for deterministic replay.
- Artifact Registry: stores generated probes, metadata, seed, template id, generator version, model fingerprint, and annotations. Backed by an object store + SQL index for fast queries.
- Provenance Ledger: append-only log of generation events (who/when/template/version) for audit and reproducibility.
- Governance Service: policy engine enforcing filters (PII safe-lists, domain restrictions) before artifacts are promoted.
- Cost & Quota Engine: calculates estimated token cost before generation and bills to project accounts.

Diagram (textual)

Client -> Probe API Frontend -> (Quota Check) -> Generator Worker -> Artifact Registry + Provenance Ledger -> Governance Service -> Promotion to Eval Mesh

Key concepts and data model (plain text table)

| Field | Description | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| probe_id | Unique artifact identifier | probe_20260112_03ab |
| template_id | ID of the generator template | tmpl_instr_clarify_v2 |
| seed | RNG seed for deterministic replay | 92734123 |
| model_fingerprint | Model name + commit + tokenizer hash | redwood-open-v1@c3f2 |
| provenance_event | event ids for generation/promotion | ev_4421 |
| tags | probe facets for routing | [functioncall, long-context] |

Generator templates and semantics

A template encodes: prompt skeleton, slot types, slot sampling distributions, post-filters, and a risk flag. Templates live in Git (templates repo) and are versioned. Example template components:

- Slots: user_intent, context_snippet, num_entities, language
- Sampling: distributions (uniform, weighted, noise-injection) with seedable RNG
- Post-filters: syntactic sanity checks, token-length bounds, and optional semantic validators (small model checks)

title: synthetic-probe-factory-architecture-and-on-demand-api-manifest-2026
text:
[...] - Post-filters: syntactic sanity checks, token-length bounds, and optional semantic validators (small model checks)


Template example (YAML snippet):

---
template_id: tmpl_functioncall_edge_v1
description: "Generates user prompts that exercise functioncall resolution and ambiguous arg types"
slots:
  user_intent: {type: choice, values: ["book_flight", "order_food", "schedule_meeting"]}
  payload: {type: generator, model: redwood-synth-v0, max_tokens: 120}
post_filters:
  - max_tokens: 200
  - banned_patterns: ["\u003cscript\u003e", "SSN"]

API surface

1) Generate (single/batch small)\POST /v1/generate\Request JSON:\n{
  "template_id": "tmpl_functioncall_edge_v1",
  "count": 50,
  "seed": 12345,
  "target_tenant": "team-payments",
  "privacy_mode": "redact-sensitive"  
}

title: synthetic-probe-factory-architecture-and-on-demand-api-manifest-2026
text:
[...] }


Response:
- 202 Accepted with batch_id and estimated_token_cost. On completion artifact_ids are available via /v1/artifacts?batch_id=...

2) Batch Fabrication (large asynchronous)\POST /v1/batch\- Accepts CSV or manifest with column mappings; returns job_id.

3) Replay / Reproducibility\GET /v1/replay?probe_id=probe_20260112_03ab\- Returns generation inputs: seed, template_id, generator_version, and model_fingerprint. Optionally triggers on-demand re-generation in a sandboxed environment.

Security and privacy model

- PII blocking: Templates must declare sensitive slots. The Governance Service applies a multi-stage filter: deterministic redaction -> small-model validator -> human review queue for promoted artifacts with non-zero risk score.
- Tenant isolation: Private/dedicated eval runs must use the factory in 'local-only' mode that runs generator workers within the customer VPC (no artifacts leave the VPC).
- Access control: All endpoints require OAuth tokens with scopes: probe:read, probe:write, probe:promote. Promotion to eval mesh requires eval:promote scope + governance approval for restricted templates.

Cost model and quotas

- Pre-flight estimator returns an estimated token cost and runtime estimate. The Cost Engine applies tenant-level multipliers (e.g., dedicated tenants have cost discounts).
- Default quotas: small-burst (<= 1k probes) per project/day = 10 jobs; large-batch jobs limited by monthly credit. Quotas are soft-fail with override workflows for emergencies.

Operational runbook (for SRE / eval-ops)

1) Normal flow: user submits generation request -> ticket created in artifacts index -> generator workers spin up -> artifacts appear in registry -> governance checks run -> artifacts promoted to staging-eval.
2) Failure modes and remediation:
  - Job stuck in PENDING: check generator worker pool autoscaler and queue depth. Remedy: scale up the generator-workers deployment, verify worker logs (task timeout typically 30s).
  - High rejection rate from governance: run sample through local validator with template debug flag, open a template patch PR.
  - Token cost spikes: Identify job via Cost Engine dashboard, revoke risky templates and notify owner.

SLOs and observability

- API availability (Probe API Frontend): 99.95% monthly.
- Median generation latency for single requests (<= 5 probes): 1.2s (target).
- Batch job completion P95 for <= 10k probes: 20 minutes.
- Provenance write success rate: 99.99%

title: synthetic-probe-factory-architecture-and-on-demand-api-manifest-2026
section_header: Telemetry to capture per generation event
text:
[...] - Provenance write success rate: 99.99%


Telemetry to capture per generation event:
- template_id, seed, model_fingerprint, token_usage_estimated, token_usage_actual, governance_decision, tenant_id, job_id

Promotion and governance workflows

Promotion states:
- DRAFT (local, not visible to eval mesh)
- STAGING (visible to internal eval mesh for shadow runs)
- PROMOTED (allowed into canary/cross-tenant synthetic runs)

Promotion checklist (automated + manual):
1. Governance policy pass (automated)
2. Privacy redaction = OK or human review approved
3. Cost estimate within tenant budget
4. Reviewer sign-off for high-risk templates (if template risk_flag == high)

Onboarding checklist for template authors

1. Create template in templates repo with metadata and unit tests (template unit test harness included in repo).
2. Run local generation with seed and attach sample to a PR.
3. Provide risk classification and sample counts.
4. Request template review via the Template Review board (link in Related Pages).

Examples and common usage patterns

- Stress test for quant kernels:
  1. Select template: tmpl_longcontext_compression_stress_v1
  2. Request count: 2k, privacy_mode: strict, target_tenant: internal-quant
  3. Submit batch with replay enabled.

- Small canary for functioncall edge cases:
  1. /v1/generate template_id=tmpl_functioncall_edge_v1 count=200 seed=9876 target_tenant=canary-team
  2. Promote to STAGING and route to shadow fleet with quant-fallback enabled.

Governance exceptions and auditability

- Any promoted artifact that had human review must include reviewer id(s) in the provenance ledger.
- The Provenance Ledger supports export for compliance audits (CSV + manifest). Exports are time-limited signed URLs accessible to compliance roles.

Appendix A: Example artifact record (CSV-friendly single-line)

probe_id,template_id,seed,model_fingerprint,created_by,created_at,tags,governance_state,artifact_uri
probe_20260112_03ab,tmpl_functioncall_edge_v1,92734123,redwood-open-v1@c3f2,a.patel,2026-01-12T14:03:11Z,"functioncall;ambiguous_args",PROMOTED,s3://artifacts/probe_20260112_03ab.json

Appendix B: Troubleshooting checklist for test authors

- If generated probes appear malformed: re-run with debug=true and inspect generator worker logs. Check model_fingerprint for unexpected model drift.
- If reproducibility fails: confirm seed, template_id, and generator_version in the provenance event. If mismatch, file a template version rollback.

Appendix C: Roadmap (next 6 months)

- Q1: Implement VPC-local generator workers for private deployments.
- Q2: Add continuous sampling mode (streaming generation) for real-time synthetic traffic.
- Q3: Integrate an anonymized feedback loop from annotator tool to improve template sampling distributions.

Contact and ownership

- Owner: Applied ML Infra (Aisha Patel) - a.patel@redwood.ai
- SRE liaison: Evan Chu - e.chu@redwood.ai
- Governance & Privacy contact: Maya Lopez - m.lopez@redwood.ai

If you plan to use the factory for a customer-facing canary or regulatory-sensitive domain (finance/healthcare), open a governance request before creating PROMOTED artifacts.

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
text:
Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint

Summary:
This document defines the 'Session Trace Fabricator' — a standardized toolkit and procedure for synthesizing representative long-session traces used to seed evals, stress benches, and shadow workloads across model-onboarding, quantization safety checks, and regression triage. It centers on reproducibility, tenant realism, and quantization compatibility without relying on customer PII.

Scope:
- Audience: ML engineers, evaluators, model-onboarding teams, QA owners, and applied-research.
- Use cases: seeding eval meshes, generating long-context stress profiles, priming registries, creating canary/shadow traffic for quant-aware rollouts, and constructing synthetic probes for rare-slice failure modes.

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
text:
[...] - Use cases: seeding eval meshes, generating long-context stress profiles, priming registries, creating canary/shadow traffic for quant-aware rollouts, and constructing synthetic probes for rare-slice failure modes.


Goals:
- Produce representative sessions that preserve request/response distributions, multi-turn persistence patterns, and function-call behavior while removing sensitive data.
- Ensure generated traces are annotated with fidelity metadata (fidelity_score, priming_vector, quant_compat_flags).
- Provide a repeatable CLI and API for scaled trace generation.

Definitions:
- Session Trace: ordered list of user/system messages, model responses, functioncalls, and associated telemetry (latency, token counts).
- Priming Vector: compact descriptor that encodes the prompt-anchoring context used when fabricating the session (template id, few-shot examples, locale).
- Quant Compatibility Flags: per-trace flags indicating likely sensitivity to quantization (high/medium/low) computed by the Quant Sensitivity Module.

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
text:
[...] - Quant Compatibility Flags: per-trace flags indicating likely sensitivity to quantization (high/medium/low) computed by the Quant Sensitivity Module.


Design Overview:
1) Components:
   - Trace Templates: YAML templates that encode intent flows (e.g., multi-step booking, nested functioncalls, policy reasoning).
   - Session Sampler: probabilistic sampler that mixes templates with real traffic-derived fingerprints.
   - Sanitizer: deterministic redactor + privacy transformer to remove PII and maintain statistical properties.
   - Annotator: attaches provenance fields, priming_vector, and quant_compat_flags.
   - Output Formatter: emits traces to JSONL, parquet, or the Eval Asset Registry.

2) Trace Template spec (summary):
   - id: short string
   - description: one-line
   - intents: ordered list of intent names and branching probabilities
   - example_user_turns: 2–6 canonical utterances per intent
   - expected_function_patterns: optional (e.g., call sequence: [search, enrich, book])
   - context_length_profile: distribution (mean, 90p)

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
text:
[...] |   - { name: confirm_payment, p: 0.20 }


Operational Procedure (step-by-step):
1. Create or select templates for the target workload slice. Record template id and steward.
2. Configure session sampler parameters: target_token_dist, multi_turn_depth, functioncall_rate, locale_mix.
3. Run fabricator in dry-run mode: samples=100, output=synthetic-dryrun.jsonl.
4. Sanitize outputs; run automatic privacy checks (PII-score < 0.01 threshold).
5. Annotator computes quant_compat_flags using the quant-sensitivity model and writes metadata.
6. Seed eval harness: register traces in eval asset registry with tags (service, slice, quant_risk).
7. Run targeted evals (smoke -> fidelity -> cost) and compare to seed acceptance criteria.

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
text:
[...] 7. Run targeted evals (smoke -> fidelity -> cost) and compare to seed acceptance criteria.


CLI examples:
- Dry run (100):
  redwood-eval-fabricator generate --template booking-nested-3step --samples 100 --mode dryrun --out /tmp/booking-dryrun.jsonl

- Produce and register with metadata:
  redwood-eval-fabricator generate --template billing-edgecase-guard --samples 5000 --locale en-US --register --tags "quant-risk:high,service:billing"

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
text:
[...]   redwood-eval-fabricator generate --template billing-edgecase-guard --samples 5000 --locale en-US --register --tags "quant-risk:high,service:billing"


JSON trace example (one-line placeholder):
{
  "session_id": "fabric-20260203-0001",
  "template_id": "booking-nested-3step",
  "turns": [
    {"role":"user","text":"I need a flight to SFO next Tuesday"},
    {"role":"assistant","text":"Do you prefer morning or evening?"},
    {"role":"user","text":"Morning, and a window seat please"},
    {"role":"assistant","function_call": {"name":"search_flights","args": "{...}"},"text":"Searching..."},
    {"role":"assistant","text":"I found a 9:10am option, proceed to payment?"}
  ],
  "telemetry": {"tokens_total": 142, "approx_latency_ms": 320, "kv_cache_deltas": "low"},
  "metadata": {"priming_vector":"pv_3f2a","quant_compat_flags":"medium","fidelity_score":"0.88"}
}

Quantization Compatibility Matrix (example — text table):
| workload_profile | quant_risk | suggested_fallback | test_scope |
| booking-heavy     | medium     | per-turn 8-bit fallback | shadow-run with mixed-precision |
| functioncall-dense| high       | disabled static-quant for fcall path | canary with querier-only |
| chat-short        | low        | aggressive 4-bit allowed | unit-run |

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
text:
[...] | chat-short        | low        | aggressive 4-bit allowed | unit-run |


Acceptance criteria for seeds (minimum):
- Fidelity score >= 0.75 for functional slices (function-call correctness).
- Token distribution KL < 0.07 compared to production fingerprint for the same slice.
- PII-score < 0.01 after Sanitizer.
- Quant-sensitivity: no more than 5% of generated traces flagged as 'high' for low-priority slices.

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
section_header: Seeding the eval mesh (recommended staging flow)
text:
[...] - Quant-sensitivity: no more than 5% of generated traces flagged as 'high' for low-priority slices.


Seeding the eval mesh (recommended staging flow):
1. Register traces in the Eval Asset Registry with labels: slice, priming_vector, template_id, created_by.
2. Assign to eval lanes: smoke, fidelity, cost, stress.
3. Run smoke in a small cohort (10% jobs) -> inspect sample outputs and metrics.
4. Promote to fidelity lane if smoke pass; run side-by-side against current production model for coherence and functioncall accuracy.
5. Run quant-aware shadow with mixed-precision candidates.
6. If discrepancy > thresholds, open an onboarding ticket with Model Onboarding and Quant teams and attach sample failing traces.

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
section_header: Telemetry and Metrics to collect on fabricated traces
text:
[...] 6. If discrepancy > thresholds, open an onboarding ticket with Model Onboarding and Quant teams and attach sample failing traces.


Telemetry and Metrics to collect on fabricated traces:
- sample_coherence: automated scorer vs anchor outputs (0–1).
- token_histogram: distribution vector to compute KL divergence.
- functioncall_recall: fraction of expected fcall sequences matched in output.
- quant_perturbation_sensitivity: delta in fidelity when switching precision.
- privacy_artifact_rate: redaction events per session.

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
section_header: Regression triage playbook (when seeded evals fail)
text:
[...] - privacy_artifact_rate: redaction events per session.


Regression triage playbook (when seeded evals fail):
1. Triage owner (assigned by test-run) collects: failing trace id, model version, eval lane, metric deltas.
2. Re-run the trace under deterministic seeds (seed id logged) to rule out nondeterminism.
3. Compare outputs across: baseline unquantized, candidate-quant, and candidate-unquantized.
4. If failure is quant-sensitive, tag with quant_failure_mode: {lossy-tokenization, kv-cache-inconsistency, functioncall-mismatch}.
5. If failure maps to prompt-priming variance, check priming_vector: run ablation by swapping few-shot examples.
6. Escalate to Model Onboarding if root cause is model drift or decoder-regression; include 10 failing traces and annotated diffs.
7. For infra-induced regressions (latency/timeouts), attach telemetry spans and hand to runtime team runbook 'eval-failure-runtime-handoff'.

Rolling to production: checklist (must be completed before seeding canary):
- [ ] Templates reviewed by a second SME and documented with stewardship.
- [ ] PII-score verification and privacy audit signed off.
- [ ] Quant-compatibility review: flags evaluated and mitigation plan defined.
- [ ] Seeds registered and smoke/fidelity lanes passed.
- [ ] Canary run executed with traffic shadowing and automatic rollback hooks enabled.
- [ ] Post-carry forward plan for trace retirement and storage TTL set (default 90 days).

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
text:
[...] - [ ] Post-carry forward plan for trace retirement and storage TTL set (default 90 days).


Ownership and contacts:
- Fabricator library (code + CLI): eng/eval-platform (owner: Avery Chen).
- Quant sensitivity model: research/optimization (owner: Marco Delgado).
- Privacy/Sanitizer: security-and-compliance (contact: Priya Rao).

Appendix A — Sanitizer rules (high level):
- Deterministic redaction: emails, phone numbers, IPs -> replace with consistent tokens.
- Name hashing: replace name spans with hashed pseudo-identifiers preserving count statistics.
- Numeric bucketing: replace high-precision numbers with bins (e.g., amounts -> <$10, $10-100, >$100).
- Sampling-only linking: do not attempt to reconstruct cross-session customer ids in synth traces.

Appendix B — Recommended storage schema (JSONL fields):
session_id, template_id, created_by, created_at, turns, telemetry, metadata.tags, metrics (coherence, token_kld, quant_sens).

Appendix C — Example test plan snippet (for model onboarding):
1) Objective: ensure new model M_x maintains functioncall fidelity on booking-nested-3step traces.
2) Seeds: 5k booking-nested-3step traces, priming_vector variants=3.
3) Success: functioncall_recall >= 0.95, fidelity_score_delta <= 0.03 vs baseline.
4) Fallback: if quant-perturbation_sensitivity > 0.08, route booking slice to dedicated unquantized pool.

title: Representative Session Trace Fabricator and Eval Seeding Blueprint
text:
[...] 4) Fallback: if quant-perturbation_sensitivity > 0.08, route booking slice to dedicated unquantized pool.


Related reading and links:
- See the Eval Asset Registry README for registration APIs.
- See Quant Sensitivity Module design doc for the quant_compat_flags algorithm.

Change log:
- 2025-11-12: initial draft and internal review.
- 2026-02-03: added quant compatibility matrix and triage playbook updates.

Contacts for feedback: avery.chen@redwood.ai, priya.rao@redwood.ai

title: Compact Priming Fallback Orchestrator and Regression Fastpath
text:
Compact Priming Fallback Orchestrator and Regression Fastpath

Summary

This playbook describes the Compact Priming Fallback Orchestrator (CPFO): an end-to-end pattern for generating and serving highly compressed prompt priming for edge clients while preserving evaluation fidelity, integrating quantization-aware fallbacks, and providing a rapid regression fastpath for operational incidents. The target audience is model-onboarding engineers, eval harness owners, runtime operators, and SREs who manage mixed-precision/edge-serving fleets.

Goals

- Reduce average token cost for long-primed edge requests by >= 35% without exceeding fidelity delta budgets.
- Maintain 99th percentile generation latency SLOs for edge-tail workloads (see Metrics).
- Provide a deterministic, low-latency rollback and regression triage path when fidelity or hallucination error increases.

When to use

title: Compact Priming Fallback Orchestrator and Regression Fastpath
text:
[...] When to use


Use CPFO when:
- Clients attach long priming contexts (>= 512 tokens) frequently and budget/cost is material.
- Deployments use mixed-precision quantized model variants (int8/4bit) where prompt sensitivity may vary.
- You need to shadow edge traffic in private or dedicated deployments for continuous fidelity monitoring.

Architecture (text diagram)

Edge Client -> Compact-Priming Service -> Shadow-Orchestrator -> Runtime Router -> {Quantized Variant, Full FP Variant} -> Eval-Mesh (telemetry sink)

Components and responsibilities

- Compact-Priming Service: token-aware compressor that produces a compact priming representation and a reconstruction hint. Outputs: compressed_prompt, reconstruction_meta. Owned by: eng-developer-experience.
- Shadow-Orchestrator: fabric that duplicates requests into shadow lanes with configurable ratio and fidelity anchors. Owned by: eval-harness team.
- Runtime Router: policy engine (latency-first, cost-first, hybrid) that selects model variant. Owned by: serving-runtime.
- Eval-Mesh: long-term storage and continuous evaluators that compute fidelity deltas, embedding drift, and hallucination scores. Owned by: applied-ml-and-evals.

Design details

1) Prompt compression algorithm (overview)
- Input: priming text, tokenized.
- Steps:
  1. Identify high-entropy windows via n-gram frequency sketch (k=5).
  2. Preserve named entities and functioncall signatures (heuristics + regex whitelist).
  3. Replace low-impact adjectives and boilerplate with canonical placeholders.
  4. Serialize compressed representation as a small delta patch: {anchors, tokens, compress-hints}.
- Output constraints: final compressed token footprint <= 0.45 * original for target slices.

2) Quantization-aware priming profiles
- Profiles: conservative (use int8 fallback only), aggressive (allow int4/packed quant), reconstruction-first (prioritizes fidelity).
- Each profile includes: acceptable fidelity delta (ROUGE/embedding distance), recommended shadow rate, and quant hedge policy.

Configuration table (example)

| profile | fidelity_budget | recommended_shadow_rate | runtime_policy | quant_hedge_threshold |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------------|----------------|-----------------------|
| conservative | <= 0.02 embedding-dist | 10% | prefer fp32 -> int8 fallback | 1.5x fidelity-cost |
| aggressive   | <= 0.05 embedding-dist | 2%  | cost-first, allow int4 | 2.0x fidelity-cost |
| reconstruction-first | <= 0.01 embedding-dist | 20% | preserve anchors -> fp32 | 1.2x fidelity-cost |

Shadow orchestration flow (operational steps)

1. Enable CPFO for a tenant: set profile, shadow_rate, and priming_max_tokens in tenant config.
2. For each incoming edge request: generate compressed_prompt; send live request to runtime per policy.
3. Fork a shadow request to eval-mesh with full uncompressed priming and to a quant-shadow pool.
4. Compare live response vs shadow baseline asynchronously; compute fidelity metrics and publish to topic eval.metrics.cpfo.{tenant}.
5. If fidelity deltas exceed profile thresholds for N=5 consecutive probes, trigger regression fastpath.

Regression fastpath (triage playbook)

Immediate detection triggers: fidelity delta high, hallucination score > threshold, functioncall mismatch.

title: Compact Priming Fallback Orchestrator and Regression Fastpath
text:
[...] Immediate detection triggers: fidelity delta high, hallucination score > threshold, functioncall mismatch.


Fastpath steps (numbered):
1. Auto-quarantine: temporarily disable compression for affected tenant-route (API flag). Timebox: 30m.
2. Promote FP baseline: route traffic to non-quantized variant and set shadow_rate=100% for 10 minutes.
3. Capture artifacts: request/response pairs, tokenization diffs, compressed->reconstructed text, model logits (if available).
4. Oncall steps (SLO 15 minutes to initial triage):
   a. Run automatic delta classifier (script: tools/cpfo/delta_check.py --tenant $ID).
   b. If classifier maps to 'compression-failure', create a triage ticket in the regression board and tag as cpfo-compression.
   c. If classifier maps to 'quant-degradation', escalate to runtime/quantops.
5. Rollback path: if an immediate rollback is required, flip runtime_policy to 'prefer fp32' and reduce shadowing for cost.

Metrics and SLOs (text table)

- SLOs:
  - Latency: p95 end-to-end generation < 420ms for edge small prompts, p99 < 900ms for cold starts.
  - Fidelity: average embedding-distance increase <= profile budget (see config).
  - Cost: tokenized priming cost reduction target >= 35% within 30 days of rollout.
- Signals to monitor:
  - eval.metrics.cpfo.{tenant}.embedding_delta (gauge)
  - eval.metrics.cpfo.{tenant}.hallucination_score (gauge)
  - runtime.latency.tail (histogram)
  - quantops.fallback_rate (counter)

Rollout checklist

1. Lab validation: run 1k synthetic probes with both compressed and full priming; assert fidelity budgets.
2. Shadow stage: enable profile at 5% shadow rate for 48 hours; monitor metrics.
