   - Reserve CriticalFloor credits: all families with label 'safety' or 'regulatory' get at least 5% of total daily credits.
   - Cap per-family spend to MaxPercent (default 20%) unless emergency flagged.
5) Sampling and rotation:
   - Select probes for annotation by sampling in proportion to Priority, but inject RotationLowEntropyFraction (default 10%) of low-priority families uniformly to maintain freshness.
6) Dispatch and logging:
   - Send selected probes to annotation backend with metadata and expected adjudication policy. Write allocation decisions to the probe-allocation ledger (timestamp, family_id, priority_score, credits_allocated, owner).

Table: default family weights and thresholds (example)

| Family label     | FamilyWeight | Critical? | Minimum daily credits | Notes |
|------------------|--------------|-----------|-----------------------|-------|
| safety           | 3.0          | yes       | 50                    | Regulatory and user-safety slices |
| user-facing      | 1.2          | no        | 20                    | High product exposure |
| backend-parser   | 0.8          | no        | 10                    | Non-critical but useful |
| long-context     | 1.5          | no        | 15                    | Historically high entropy |
| rare-edge        | 1.8          | yes       | 25                    | Low frequency but high impact |

title: Annotation Credit Allocation and Entropy Stewardship Framework
text:
[...] | long-context     | 1.5          | no        | 15                    | Historically high entropy |
| rare-edge        | 1.8          | yes       | 25                    | Low frequency but high impact |

Embedding the framework in eval-harness

- Config integration: add a new top-level field in eval-recipe YAML: annotation_allocation:
  - total_daily_credits: int
  - rotation_low_entropy_fraction: float
  - critical_floor_percent: float
  - family_weights: map
  - cost_map: map

title: Annotation Credit Allocation and Entropy Stewardship Framework
text:
[...]   - cost_map: map


Example YAML snippet:

annotation_allocation:
  total_daily_credits: 2000
  rotation_low_entropy_fraction: 0.10
  critical_floor_percent: 0.05
  family_weights:
    safety: 3.0
    user-facing: 1.2
    backend-parser: 0.8
    long-context: 1.5
    rare-edge: 1.8
  cost_map:
    binary: 0.6
    multi_class_3: 2.1
    multi_annotator_consensus: 3.5

Operational runbook: emergency top-up and regression triage

1) Trigger: regression triage or P1 production incident requires immediate re-labeling of a suspect probe family.
2) Immediate action:
   - Owner toggles emergency_flag for family_id in eval console. This bypasses MaxPercent cap and bumps FamilyWeight to EmergencyWeight (default 10x).
   - System allocates EmergencyCredits from a reserved pool (configurable).
3) Post-mortem allocation reconciliation:
   - Annotators' spend is reconciled to real budget. If reserve used, product manager reviews the spend and approves rebalancing of future daily credits.

Integration with quantization profiles and model variants

- When a model variant or quant-profile is promoted to staging, run a CalibrationSweep: sample N probes from top-priority families and recompute EntropyEstimate under the variant. If BitsPerDollar drops below VariantThreshold, flag for extended human review and delay rollout.
- For quantized models that reduce response diversity, the EntropyEstimate should include kernel-aware priors (we store a small per-model variance multiplier in the model catalog).

Telemetry and dashboards

title: Annotation Credit Allocation and Entropy Stewardship Framework
section_header: Expose the following dashboards in the eval console
text:
[...] Telemetry and dashboards


Expose the following dashboards in the eval console:
- BitsPerDollar over time (by family, by model variant)
- AnnotationSpend vs Budget (rolling 7d)
- CoverageFraction heatmap (slices x tenants)
- DriftSignalRate and top families with estimator-human disagreement

Ownership and teams

- Document owner: Aisha Patel (Applied ML)
- Primary implementer: Eval-harness team (eng-applied-ml)
- Annotation operations: DataOps / LabelOps
- Product stakeholder: Feature reliability PMs

Rollout plan (phased)

1) Pilot (2 weeks): enable for 3 families (safety, long-context, user-facing) with a 10% of annotation volume and manual review of allocations.
2) Beta (6 weeks): expand to 25% of families and integrate with annotation backend automatic dispatch. Measure BitsPerDollar and YieldRate.
3) GA (quarterly cadence): full rollout with SLOs on BitsPerDollar and CoverageFraction and automated emergency top-ups.

Audit and governance

- Ledger retention: allocation ledger retained 2 years.
- Monthly review: AnnotationOps and Applied ML review allocation patterns and update family_weights if needed.
- Approval process: any change to CriticalFloor or EmergencyReserve requires sign-off from Product Reliability and Security.

Appendix: sample SQL for BitsPerDollar calculation

-- pseudocode SQL (for analytics team)
SELECT
  family_id,
  SUM(entropy_bits) as total_bits,
  SUM(usd_cost) as total_usd,
  CASE WHEN SUM(usd_cost) = 0 THEN NULL ELSE SUM(entropy_bits)/SUM(usd_cost) END as bits_per_dollar
FROM probe_annotation_results
WHERE annotated_at >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE, INTERVAL 30 DAY)
GROUP BY family_id
ORDER BY bits_per_dollar DESC;

Notes, known limitations, and future work

- Entropy estimates are approximations; early tuning required per-family. Track DriftSignalRate closely during the pilot.
- This framework assumes stable annotation unit costs; vendor churn can invalidate thresholds quickly.
- Future: integrate active learning models for per-probe annotation decisioning and experiment with variable consensus (dynamic annotator counts) to further optimize cost-per-bit.

Related pages and links

- Probe telemetry compression and scheduling (see: ../eval-harness/probe-telemetry-compression-scheduling-playbook-2026).
- Probe curation and annotation charter (see: ../prompt-sets-and-benchmarks/probe-curation-stewardship-and-annotation-charter-2026).

Revision history

- 2025-11-04: Drafted by Aisha Patel.
- 2025-12-10: Pilot config examples added; reviewers Jonah Kim and Mariana Lopez.
- 2026-02-18: Updated with emergency top-up workflow and SQL; published.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability

Purpose
-------
This document defines the Multi-seed Aggregation and Variance Budgeting protocol (MAVB) used by Redwood's eval harness to quantify output stochasticity, attribute variance to sources (prompting, model nondeterminism, quantization, runtime caching), and set actionable variance budgets for production rollouts and regression triage.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] This document defines the Multi-seed Aggregation and Variance Budgeting protocol (MAVB) used by Redwood's eval harness to quantify output stochasticity, attribute variance to sources (prompting, model nondeterminism, quantization, runtime caching), and set actionable variance budgets for production rollouts and regression triage.


Scope
-----
- Applies to all internal evaluation runs that influence model promotion, quantization decisions, or production rollouts (hosted, dedicated, private).
- Intended for use by applied-ML, model-onboarding, runtime, and SRE teams.
- Covers generation (chat/text), reranking, and embedding stability analyses where multiple random seeds or sampling strategies are relevant.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] - Covers generation (chat/text), reranking, and embedding stability analyses where multiple random seeds or sampling strategies are relevant.


Definitions
-----------
- Seed run: One execution of an evaluation workload with a single RNG seed and fixed runtime settings.
- Seed ensemble: A set of N seed runs used to measure variance for a given workload slice.
- Variance budget: A pre-defined acceptable range (σ or interquartile range) for metric fluctuation attributable to nondeterminism.
- Attribution buckets: "prompt variance", "model stochasticity", "quantization impact", "runtime cache/batching".

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] - Attribution buckets: "prompt variance", "model stochasticity", "quantization impact", "runtime cache/batching".


Roles & Ownership
-----------------
- Evaluation Owner (Applied-ML): defines workload slices, target metrics, and baseline model.
- Runtime Owner (Serving Runtime): provides reproducible runtime configs and documents non-deterministic behaviors.
- Onboarding Lead (Model Onboarding): approves variance budgets for model promotion and signs off on triage.
- Regression Lead (Regression Triage): coordinates bisecting and blames assignment when variance exceeds budgets.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] - Regression Lead (Regression Triage): coordinates bisecting and blames assignment when variance exceeds budgets.


High-level protocol summary
---------------------------
1. Define workload slices (by prompt-type / user-slice / sequence length / language / quantization profile).
2. For each slice, run a seed ensemble of N runs (default N=7; N minimum = 5 for preliminary checks).
3. Compute per-metric aggregation (mean, median, stdev, IQR, bootstrap CI) across seeds.
4. Compare against the variance budget table; if exceeded, run attribution steps and trigger triage.
5. Record all raw seed traces, RNG seeds, runtime config, and KV-cache state hash in the eval store.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] 5. Record all raw seed traces, RNG seeds, runtime config, and KV-cache state hash in the eval store.


Determining ensemble size (N)
-----------------------------
- Small slices or quick smoke tests: N = 3–5 (informational only).
- Standard production slices: N = 7 (balance of power and cost).
- High-sensitivity slices (legal, safety): N = 15+ and use stratified sampling across RNG engine settings.
- Guidance: choose N such that 95% bootstrap CI of the primary metric width <= half the variance budget.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] - Guidance: choose N such that 95% bootstrap CI of the primary metric width <= half the variance budget.


Aggregation methods and rationale
---------------------------------
- Use median as the primary central tendency for skewed generation metrics (e.g., ROUGE, BLEU for few-shot outputs).
- Report both stdev and IQR; prefer IQR for heavy-tailed distributions.
- Provide bootstrap percentile CI (1k resamples) for each metric and seed-aggregated score.
- For binary pass/fail checks (safety filters), report the proportion across seeds with Clopper–Pearson 95% CI.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] - For binary pass/fail checks (safety filters), report the proportion across seeds with Clopper–Pearson 95% CI.


Variance budgeting table (example)
----------------------------------
| Slice category            | Primary metric         | Variance budget (IQR) | Notes |
|--------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|-------|
| Short FAQ prompts (<64)  | answer-accuracy @1     | <= 3% points           | typical low variance |
| Long-context summarization | ROUGE-L               | <= 5 pts IQR           | cache-sensitive |
| Multi-lingual short prompts | BLEU (en→xx)         | <= 4 pts IQR           | cross-tokenization noise |
| Safety filter pass rate   | pass-rate (%)         | <= 1.5% points         | high-sensitivity |
| Embedding cosine (µ)      | mean cosine           | <= 0.005               | quantization-sensitive |

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] | Embedding cosine (µ)      | mean cosine           | <= 0.005               | quantization-sensitive |


Attribution workflow
--------------------
1. If variance budget exceeded, re-run the seed ensemble with controlled toggles:
   - Fix RNG engine to deterministic mode (if supported).
   - Disable prefix/KV cache.
   - Run on a reference model binary (unquantized) and then on the quantized artifact.
   - Switch sampling strategy to greedy to isolate sampling noise.
2. For each toggle, compute delta in metric stdev/IQR. Attribute percent of variance explained to each bucket.
3. If quantization explains > 60% of excess variance, fail quantization promotion and open a quant-triage ticket.
4. If runtime cache/batching contributes > 30%, create a runtime investigation with traces and GPU scheduling logs.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] 4. If runtime cache/batching contributes > 30%, create a runtime investigation with traces and GPU scheduling logs.


Regression triage playbook (short)
----------------------------------
1. Tag the failing eval run and mark related traces as P0 for triage.
2. Run an automated bisect across commits and model checkpoints using the eval harness (see CLI below).
3. For suspected infra/runtime causes, reproduce on a minimal dedicated node with identical runtime config.
4. If model change suspected, request an annotated diff of token-level outputs across seeds and an error-slice report.
5. Document conclusion in the regression tracker with blame attribution and corrective action (rollback, quant-tune, prompt-harden).

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
section_header: Example eval config (YAML) used by the harness
text:
[...] 5. Document conclusion in the regression tracker with blame attribution and corrective action (rollback, quant-tune, prompt-harden).


Practical examples and CLI
-------------------------
Example eval config (YAML) used by the harness:

```yaml
name: long-summary-xlang
baseline_model: redwood-open-7b-v2
slices:
  - name: long-docs
    length_range: [1024, 8192]
    prompt_template: templates/long-summary-v2.jinja
seeds: 7
sampling:
  method: top-p
  p: 0.92
  temperature: 0.8
runtime_config:
  kv_cache: enabled
  batch_size: 8
  quant_profile: q4_0
output_metrics:
  - rouge_l
  - readability_score
  - latency_ms
```

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] ```


Run command (eval harness):

$ eval-harness run --config long-summary-xlang.yaml --store /evals/mavb/2026-02-15

Bisect command (example):

$ eval-harness bisect --config long-summary-xlang.yaml --commit-range abc123..def456 --seeds 5 --report /tmp/bisect.html

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] $ eval-harness bisect --config long-summary-xlang.yaml --commit-range abc123..def456 --seeds 5 --report /tmp/bisect.html


Data retention and artifact requirements
---------------------------------------
- Keep raw seed outputs and RNG seeds for 90 days for general audits; 365 days for safety-critical slices.
- Save runtime traces (trace ids) including kernel selection logs, KV-cache hashes, and batching metadata in the eval store.
- Attach quantization artifact id and build metadata when running quantized profiles.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
text:
[...] - Attach quantization artifact id and build metadata when running quantized profiles.


Alerting thresholds and play-trigger rules
-----------------------------------------
- Automated alert when primary metric IQR exceeds budget on three consecutive runs for the same slice.
- PagerRule: if safety-filter pass-rate variance > budget, page Regression Lead and Safety Owner.
- Generate a "variance-drift" ticket automatically when long-term drift exceeds 2x budget over 30 days.

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
section_header: Integration with model onboarding and Optimize
text:
[...] - Generate a "variance-drift" ticket automatically when long-term drift exceeds 2x budget over 30 days.


Integration with model onboarding and Optimize
---------------------------------------------
- Model onboarding adds variance budgets to the promotion checklist. The onboarding job fails if high-sensitivity slices exceed budgets.
- Redwood Optimize can suggest alternative quant profiles when quantization-attributed variance is detected; attach suggested profiles to the quant-triage ticket.

Quick reference checklist
-------------------------
- [ ] Define slices and primary metric
- [ ] Choose N seeds (≥5 for production)
- [ ] Run seed ensemble and compute IQR/CI
- [ ] Compare to variance budget table
- [ ] If exceeded: run attribution toggles
- [ ] If still failed: trigger regression triage
- [ ] Record findings and attach raw traces

title: Multi-seed aggregation and variance budgeting for eval stability
section_header: Appendix A — Example report output (CSV columns)
text:
[...] - [ ] Record findings and attach raw traces


Appendix A — Example report output (CSV columns)
------------------------------------------------
run_id, seed, slice_name, metric_name, metric_value, runtime_config_id, quant_artifact_id, kv_cache_hash, trace_id

Appendix B — Troubleshooting tips
---------------------------------
- High variance but low mean change: likely sampling noise — increase seeds or fix to greedy for diagnostic runs.
- Variance concentrated in long-context slices: look for KV cache fingerprint mismatches or tokenization boundary issues.
- Quantization large jumps in specific tokens: run token-level log-prob difference across seeds and check for Outlier Token Instances (OTIs).

Related pages
-------------
- See also: Stochastic Eval Replay and Determinism Protocol (link above), Quantization Failure Modes and Recovery Playbook, Regression Bisect Assistant.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026

Operational playbook for automatically generating evaluation slices from telemetry and assigning dynamic priorities to each slice for scheduling in the eval harness. Reduces manual slice engineering, improves coverage of regressions in long-tail traffic, and integrates with existing promotion and rollback gating.
Overview
========
This document defines the dynamic slice creation and priority scheduling system used by the Eval Harness. The goal is to automatically synthesize actionable evaluation slices from production telemetry, score them by risk/impact, and schedule them into the evaluation queue with appropriate cadence and resource allocation. This complements static slice catalogs and reduces time-to-detection for regressions appearing in the long tail.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] This document defines the dynamic slice creation and priority scheduling system used by the Eval Harness. The goal is to automatically synthesize actionable evaluation slices from production telemetry, score them by risk/impact, and schedule them into the evaluation queue with appropriate cadence and resource allocation. This complements static slice catalogs and reduces time-to-detection for regressions appearing in the long tail.


Motivation
----------
- Static prompt slice catalogs are brittle and require continuous manual maintenance.
- Many regressions surface first in telemetry hotspots or newly emerging user behavior.
- We need a reproducible, auditable pipeline that:
  - translates telemetry signal into candidate slices,
  - ranks candidates by an explainable priority score,
  - schedules slices to the eval harness under capacity constraints, and
  - surfaces actionable reports for triage and remediation.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...]   - surfaces actionable reports for triage and remediation.


Scope
-----
This playbook covers:
- Slice candidate generation (signals, heuristics, constraints)
- Priority scoring function and weighting defaults
- Scheduler rules for the eval mesh/orchestrator
- Integration steps for model onboarding and promotion gating
- Monitoring, alerts, and troubleshooting
It does NOT cover human annotation workflows or label pipelines beyond basic sample labeling used for slice verification.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] It does NOT cover human annotation workflows or label pipelines beyond basic sample labeling used for slice verification.


Definitions
-----------
- Candidate slice: a compact selector describing a subset of requests (e.g., metadata + prompt signature + token-length bucket).
- Slice fingerprint: deterministic string used to deduplicate candidates.
- Hotspot: continuous telemetry region exceeding configured thresholds (e.g., error-rate > 2x baseline or latency p95 > SLO).
- Priority score: numeric 0-100 determining scheduling urgency.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
section_header: Primary signals used to generate candidates
text:
[...] - Priority score: numeric 0-100 determining scheduling urgency.


Data Sources and Signals
------------------------
Primary signals used to generate candidates:
1. Error telemetry: response errors, function-call failures, safety sentinel hits.
2. Latency anomalies: p50/p95/p99 shifts per model/route/region.
3. Semantic drift indicators: embedding drift, response embedding distance to historical buckets.
4. Volume surges: sudden increase in request share for a specific route or customer.
5. Customer-reported incidents and support tickets (ingested via tag).
6. Canary eval deltas: small canary sets that exhibit performance regressions.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] 6. Canary eval deltas: small canary sets that exhibit performance regressions.


Candidate Generation Algorithm (high level)
-----------------------------------------
1. Ingest normalized telemetry windows (default: 24h, configurable).
2. Identify hotspots by applying per-route baselines and outlier detection (median absolute deviation).
3. For each hotspot, extract candidate selectors:
   - route_id, model_family, customer_tier, locale, device_type, prompt-template-hash, token-length-bucket, top-3 tokens prefix.
4. Generate fingerprint: SHA1(route_id + model_family + template_hash + token_bucket).
5. Deduplicate candidates by fingerprint; keep top-N variants per hotspot (default N=10).
6. Enrich candidates with sample examples and initial lightweight labels (if available).

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] 6. Enrich candidates with sample examples and initial lightweight labels (if available).


Priority Scoring (explainable formula)
-------------------------------------
We use a linear combination of normalized signals to produce a 0-100 priority score. Default weights (tunable):
- Impacted volume weight (w_vol) = 0.30
- Error severity weight (w_err) = 0.30
- Model divergence weight (w_div) = 0.20
- Customer SLA sensitivity weight (w_sla) = 0.10
- Freshness / growth weight (w_growth) = 0.10

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] - Freshness / growth weight (w_growth) = 0.10


Score computation (pseudocode):

score = round(100 * clamp( w_vol * norm(volume_percentile) + w_err * norm(error_z) + w_div * norm(embedding_shift) + w_sla * sla_flag + w_growth * growth_rate, 0, 1))

Where:
- norm(x) maps a signal into [0,1] using historical percentiles.
- error_z is z-score relative to model-route historical error mean.
- sla_flag is 1 for enterprise customers with SLA, 0 otherwise.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] - sla_flag is 1 for enterprise customers with SLA, 0 otherwise.


Priority tiers (operational):
- 80-100: Critical — schedule as high-frequency slot, notify on-call triage.
- 60-79: High — schedule daily batch, include in weekly regression digest.
- 30-59: Medium — schedule weekly with lower resource priority.
- 0-29: Low — schedule opportunistically, aggregated sweeps.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] - 0-29: Low — schedule opportunistically, aggregated sweeps.


Scheduler Rules and Quotas
--------------------------
- Eval Mesh capacity is expressed as concurrent slice slots per hour.
- By default, 40% of capacity reserved for Critical/High tiers, 40% for Medium, 20% for Low.
- Per-customer cap: no more than 10% of total slots to a single customer unless approved by CS.
- Model-family cap: prevent majority of slots going to a single model family (default 50%).
- Aging policy: slices that do not surface issues within 4 weekly runs are downgraded automatically.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] - Aging policy: slices that do not surface issues within 4 weekly runs are downgraded automatically.


Scheduler pseudo-workflow:
1. Pull prioritized candidate queue, sorted by (priority_score, recency, uniqueness).
2. Apply quota constraints and backpressure.
3. Allocate to eval runners with appropriate resource profile (e.g., long-context -> larger GPU mem).
4. Launch evaluation with attached telemetry replay or synthetic generator.
5. Capture artifact: eval result, failure trace, example requests, and metrics.
6. If eval triggers regression gates, escalate via regression triage (see "Triage handoff").

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
section_header: Integration with Model Onboarding and Promotion Gates
text:
[...] 6. If eval triggers regression gates, escalate via regression triage (see "Triage handoff").


Integration with Model Onboarding and Promotion Gates
----------------------------------------------------
- New model versions should be shadowed for 72h with an elevated slice sampling weight to detect early regressions.
- Promotion requires no Critical-tier slices failing and < 10% of High-tier slices flagged in the last 7 days.
- The slice system exports a promotion summary: top failing slices, sample counts, error types, impact estimates.
- Use this export as part of the Model Promotion Checklist (link to onboarding page).

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] - Use this export as part of the Model Promotion Checklist (link to onboarding page).


Example: scheduling API call (CLI)
--------------------------------
```bash
redwood-evalctl schedule-slice \
  --fingerprint sha1:abc123def \
  --priority 87 \
  --runner-type gpu-large \
  --timeout 3600 \
  --examples-file /tmp/slice-examples.json
```

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] ```


Promotion decision table (example)
---------------------------------
| Metric | Threshold | Action |
|---|---:|---|
| Critical slices failing | >0 | Block promotion, immediate triage |
| High slices failing | >10% | Hold promotion, require mitigations and re-eval |
| Medium slices failing | >25% | Require justification for promotion |
| Regression severity (avg) | >= 0.7 (on 0-1 scale) | Block and open priority incident |

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
section_header: Key dashboards to build and monitor
text:
[...] | Regression severity (avg) | >= 0.7 (on 0-1 scale) | Block and open priority incident |


Monitoring, Alerts, and Dashboards
----------------------------------
Key dashboards to build and monitor:
- Candidate generation volume by route/model (24h, 7d)
- Priority distribution histogram and slot utilization
- Recent failing slices table (with links to artifacts)
- Time-to-detection for newly created slices

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] - Recent failing slices table (with links to artifacts)
- Time-to-detection for newly created slices

Alerting thresholds (examples):
- New Critical-tier slice created with error_rate > 5%: page on-call (P1).
- >3 Critical slices fail within 24h for a single model family: open incident.

Ownership and Roles
-------------------
- Ownership: Applied ML (owner) — maintains heuristics, scoring weights, and integration.
- On-call: Eval Platform SRE — handles scheduling failures, runner capacity issues.
- Triage: Model Quality team — receives failing slice artifacts and coordinates fixes.
- Product/CS: notified for customer-impacting slices.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] - Product/CS: notified for customer-impacting slices.


Rollout Plan
------------
1. Prototype phase (2 weeks): run candidate generator in read-only mode; produce candidates and scored outputs to a review queue.
2. Pilot (4 weeks): enable scheduling for non-critical tiers and a small model subset.
3. Gradual ramp (6 weeks): expand to full model fleet and enable high-priority scheduling.
4. GA: enable promotion gating and integrate with release pipelines.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] 4. GA: enable promotion gating and integrate with release pipelines.


Operational SLOs for the system
-------------------------------
- Candidate latency: new candidate discovered and scored within 15 minutes of telemetry window close (SLO 90%).
- Scheduling success: scheduled candidates start running within 10 minutes of allocation (SLO 95%).
- Artifact availability: evaluation artifacts available for triage within 10 minutes of job completion (SLO 99%).

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] - Artifact availability: evaluation artifacts available for triage within 10 minutes of job completion (SLO 99%).


Troubleshooting and Runbook (quick)
----------------------------------
Problem: Candidate queue is empty despite high telemetry volume
1. Check ingestion pipeline latency: kafka_consumer_lag > 0?
2. Confirm hotspot detector thresholds were not misconfigured (path: /config/slicing/hotspot-thresholds.yaml).
3. Verify fingerprinting service is healthy; look for rate-limiting errors.

Problem: Scheduler failing to allocate slots
1. Inspect the eval-mesh capacity dashboard; check for node drains.
2. Verify per-model caps are not saturated.
3. Check runner pod logs (/var/log/eval-runner/*).

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] 3. Check runner pod logs (/var/log/eval-runner/*).


Auditing and Safety
------------------
- Every candidate and scheduling decision is logged to the audit index with: fingerprint, input signals, score breakdown, scheduled-run-id, and owner.
- Slice fingerprints that involve customer PII are redacted; if redaction removes determinism, use deterministic surrogate keys.
- Security review required for any change that increases sampling for customer data beyond a configured threshold.

title: dynamic-slice-creation-and-priority-scheduling-2026
text:
[...] - Security review required for any change that increases sampling for customer data beyond a configured threshold.


Appendices
---------
A. Example candidate JSON (simplified)
```json
{
  "fingerprint": "sha1:abc123def",
  "route_id": "chat-completion:v2",
  "model_family": "redwood-rlf-13b",
  "selectors": {"locale":"en-US","token_bucket":"64-128","template_hash":"t3_4f"},
  "signals": {"volume_pct":0.021,"error_z":2.7,"embedding_shift":0.35},
  "priority_score": 88
}
```
B. Default thresholds (config path: /config/slicing/defaults.yaml)
| key | value | notes |
| hotspot_window | 24h | sliding window used for detection |
| max_candidates_per_hotspot | 10 | prevents explosion |
| critical_threshold | 80 | priority >= this is critical |

C. Links and references
- Eval harness: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/eval-mesh-and-workload-fingerprinting-2026

title: Annotator calibration for long-context streaming evals (playbook)
text:
Annotator calibration for long-context streaming evals (playbook)

Summary

This playbook describes the end-to-end process for calibrating human annotators used in long-context, streaming-style evaluations. It covers selection and priming of annotators, creation of streaming-specific judgment tasks, inter-annotator agreement (IAA) targets, drift detection, telemetry required, and automated remediation steps. The goal is to ensure that annotation quality meets the fidelity requirements of production-facing long-context features (e.g., multi-turn chat with >8k tokens, streaming function-calls, and KV-cache sensitive prompts).

Why this matters

- Long-context streaming evals amplify small annotation inconsistencies because judgments depend on earlier context and partially observed responses.
- Low annotator calibration increases variance in eval metrics and can mask regressions introduced by quantization, KV-cache changes, or prompt rewrites.
- Well-calibrated annotators reduce false-positive regression alerts and speed up triage in the regression playbooks.

Scope

- Applies to internal and partner annotation vendors used for streaming, long-context, and multi-turn eval workloads.
- Does NOT replace safety or legal review for user-sensitive data; see privacy-first-shadow-eval docs for sensitive pipelines.

Owners and escalation

- Ownership: Applied ML — Evaluations (primary), Runtime Reliability (secondary)
- Day-to-day steward: Ivy Chen (applied-ml)
- Escalation: If IAA falls below thresholds for two consecutive weekly sweeps, notify the Evaluations oncall and open an investigation ticket (SOP ID: EVAL-AUD-34).

Definitions and success criteria

- Judgement unit: A single streaming session fragment: includes the conversation prefix up to the turn, the partial response (possibly streaming), and the intended evaluation prompt instructions.
- IAA (Krippendorff's alpha for ordinal labels; Cohen's kappa for binary): Target >= 0.7 for ordinal 3+ labels, >= 0.8 for binary pass/fail for production slices.
- Annotator latency: Median annotation time per judgment unit \u003c= 75s for full 8k-token session fragments (instrumented).
- Drift window: 7-day rolling metrics. Significant drift if metric moves >2.5 standard deviations from baseline.

Prep: tooling and data setup

- Create streaming judgment tasks in the Evaluator UI with the following config fields:
  - session_id (string), prefix_tokens (int), response_stream_chunks (list), expected_behavior_tags (list), quality_sentinels (list).
- Telemetry to capture per judgment unit (minimum):
  - prefix_token_count, response_token_count, stream_chunk_count, chunk_interarrival_ms, annotator_id, annotator_latency_s, annotator_confidence_score, gold_label_id(optional), anchor_probe_flag.
- Provide annotators with a streaming playback widget that can: pause/replay by token range, show temporally-aligned function-call traces, and reveal metadata (model version, quantization profile, KV-cache state).

Calibration procedure (step-by-step)

1) Seed-phase (10 hours ceiling) — establish initial calibration:
   a) Select seed probe set: 500 judgment units across the top 10 production slices (mix of short, medium, long contexts).
   b) Each candidate annotator labels the full seed set.
   c) Compute IAA and per-slice confusion matrices.
   d) Pass criteria: overall IAA >= 0.7, no production slice IAA \u003c 0.6. Those who fail receive focused retraining and re-test on 100 new seed units.

2) Anchoring-phase (daily run, ongoing) — keep annotators anchored to golden examples:
   a) Inject 5–10% anchor probes each day (randomized) with previously adjudicated labels.
   b) Track anchor agreement by annotator (daily) and by slice (rolling 7-day).
   c) Auto-suspend annotator from live tasks if anchor agreement \u003c 85% in a rolling 48-hour window.

3) Drift detection and triage (weekly):
   a) Compute per-slice metrics: label distribution, average annotator confidence, median annotation time, anchor-agreement, and IAA.
   b) Flag slices with any of:
      - IAA drop \u003e= 0.1 absolute vs baseline,
      - anchor agreement \u003c 85%,
      - median annotator latency increase > 2x baseline (could indicate task confusion).
   c) Triage steps (ordered):
      1. Re-run a 100-probe adjudication on the flagged slice.
      2. If adjudicated labels disagree with current anchors, create a re-anchoring batch and notify steward.
      3. If disagreement is between annotators only, schedule focused retraining and a 50-probe retest.

title: Annotator calibration for long-context streaming evals (playbook)
text:
[...]       2. If adjudicated labels disagree with current anchors, create a re-anchoring batch and notify steward.
      3. If disagreement is between annotators only, schedule focused retraining and a 50-probe retest.

4) Continuous quality regression guardrail (integration with eval harness):
   - For every model promotion or quantization change, require a shortform shadow evaluation against an anchored probe set (min 1,000 units) where annotator anchor agreement must remain within +/-2% of baseline.

Guidance for long-context specifics

- Context trimming: Provide explicit priming guidelines to annotators on how to mark relevance when past context exceeds 16k tokens; allow annotators to mark earliest token ranges they used when judging.
- Streaming partial-response judgments: Use a 3-stage label flow: preliminary-pass (after first N tokens), mid-pass (after chunk M), final-pass (full response). Capture timestamps and final label must be used for scoring.
- Function-call fidelity: Expose function-call traces inline; ask annotators to rate (0–2) fidelity of function semantics and argument completeness.

Example task template (JSON snippet)

{\n  "task_id": "stream-annot-0001",\n  "session_id": "s-53042",\n  "prefix_tokens": 6200,\n  "stream_chunks": ["chunk-1", "chunk-2", "chunk-3"],\n  "instructions": "Rate helpfulness (0-3) after full response. Mark if any earlier context changed your judgment.",\n  "anchors": ["anchor-123"]\n}

Automation and dashboards

- Required dashboards (Grafana/Looker):
  - Annotator Health: per-annotator anchor agreement trend, median latency, tasks_completed/day.
  - Slice Quality: per-slice IAA, label distribution delta, anchor coverage.
  - Shadow Promotion Gate: pass/fail for promotions; show detailed diff of anchor labels.

- Alerts (PagerDuty for Evaluations oncall):
  - P1: Overall test-suite IAA \u003c 0.65 for 24h -> create incident.
  - P2: Any production slice anchor-agreement \u003c 80% for 48h -> run triage playbook.

Adjudication and dispute workflow

1. If two annotators disagree on an anchor probe, route to an adjudicator (senior annotator) with context and both judgments.
2. Adjudicator records decision and rationale in the adjudication UI (required).
3. Update the anchor set if adjudicator verdict differs from current anchor; create re-anchoring job for impacted annotators.

Training materials and shrinkage planning

- Create a 60–90 minute calibration module covering: long-context priming, streaming playback usage, function-call traces, and example edge cases.
- Maintain a rolling pool of 1.5x annotators for critical slices to absorb shrinkage.

Rollout checklist for a new streaming eval (pre-launch)

1. Confirm instrumentation captures stream_chunk timestamps and annotator metadata.
2. Run seed-phase with at least 8 annotators and reach pass criteria.
3. Create anchor set (min 500 judged units) and enable automatic injection.
4. Configure dashboard and alerts.
5. Run a dry-run shadow promotion with a known-good model to validate guardrail thresholds.

Failure modes and mitigations

- Symptom: sudden spike in median annotation time. Mitigation: sample recent tasks and check for task UI regressions or unclear instructions.
- Symptom: IAA collapse only on very long contexts. Mitigation: split tasks into two judgment types (context-summarization + judgment) and re-assess.

Related artifacts and links

- Streaming playback widget design doc: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/api-examples/streaming-playback-widget-design (internal)
- Anchor probe manifest schema: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/evaluator-interchange-format-and-federation-spec-2026
- Regression triage playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/model-regression-forensics-playbook-2026

Appendix: Minimal telemetry schema (CSV header)

session_id,prefix_token_count,response_token_count,stream_chunk_count,chunk_interarrival_ms,annotator_id,annotator_latency_s,annotator_confidence,label,anchor_flag,model_version,quant_profile

Contact

For questions or to request re-anchoring support, contact Ivy Chen (applied-ml) or file a ticket in the Evaluations backlog (component: eval-anchors).

title: Annotator calibration for long-context streaming evals (playbook)
text:
[...] 
For questions or to request re-anchoring support, contact Ivy Chen (applied-ml) or file a ticket in the Evaluations backlog (component: eval-anchors).
