title: Probe Reshaper and Quant-Routing Manual
text:
Probe Reshaper and Quant-Routing Manual

Summary

This document defines the Probe Reshaper component of the Eval Harness: a runtime subsystem that transforms incoming evaluation probes (prompt + context bundles) to improve stability under mixed-quant deployments while preserving privacy constraints and probe fidelity. The Reshaper sits in the eval ingress path and cooperates with the Quant Routing policy engine to decide target variants (fp16, q4, q8_0, etc.), prompt rewrites, and when to fall back to higher-fidelity evaluation.

Goals

- Reduce false-positive regressions caused purely by quantization-induced prompt sensitivity.
- Maintain probe privacy (avoid leaking tenant metadata) during on-host transformations and routing decisions.
- Provide deterministic, auditable transformations so eval results are reproducible.
- Minimize additional latency and token-cost overhead across the eval fleet.

Scope and Non-Goals

- In scope: runtime probe normalization, token-level rewrite heuristics, routing decisions to quantized artifact variants, per-probe fidelity annotations, audit logging and provenance metadata.
- Out of scope: model training, core quantizer development, human annotation workflows (except where annotations are needed for rollback debugging).

Terminology

- Probe: an evaluation input consisting of prompt text, metadata (tenant_id, slice_id), and optional attachment(s).
- Reshaping: a deterministic transformation applied to a probe (canonicalization, priming normalization, token-preserving rewrites).

Design overview

1) Ingress flow (high level):
- EvalOrchestrator -> ProbeReshaper -> QuantRoutingPolicy -> ShadowDispatcher -> Runner (target variant)

2) Two-layer decision model:
- Static eligibility checks (probe size, tenant opt-outs, confidentiality flags).
- Dynamic sensitivity estimation (fast SIMD tokenizer pass + learned sensitivity model) that assigns a QuantRiskScore.

Probe Reshaper responsibilities

- Canonicalize whitespace, normalize non-semantic Unicode (NFKC), and normalize date/time mentions into token-short forms when appropriate.
- Apply deterministic priming compression for long-context probes using the PrimingCompact pattern (keeps anchors and last N-turns intact).
- Execute token-preserving obfuscation for tenant-identifying strings (email, org names) when tenant requests privacy gating. Obfuscation is reversible only within the audit enclave (KMS-protected key) and replaced with a stable placeholder for the runner.
- Emit a provenance blob: {reshaper_version, seed, deterministic_hash, transformations_applied} appended to probe metadata for downstream reconciliation.

Quant Routing policy

Routing decisions are the joint output of: static rules, quant risk score, and traffic cost budget. The routing policy maps to target variant IDs and optional fidelity gates (e.g., force high-fidelity when a quality probe fails).

Policy matrix (example)

| QuantRiskScore | ProbeLengthTokens | TenantTier | RouteRecommendation |
|---:|---:|---|---|
| 0-0.15 | <512 | any | q8_0 (fast) |
| 0.15-0.45 | <1024 | standard | q4 + priming-compact |
| 0.45-0.80 | any | standard | shadow-first: run on q8_0 with synchronous high-fidelity replay on fp16 if variance > threshold |
| >0.80 | any | enterprise | fp16 (no quant) |

Note: thresholds are tunable per-service via the RoutingConfig manifest (see examples).

Deterministic Rewrites and Auditing

- All rewrites are deterministic and keyed by probe deterministic_hash and reshaper_version. Repro playbacks should yield identical rewritten prompts.
- The provenance blob is stored alongside probe outputs in the EvalArtifact store. For privacy, reversible obfuscation keys are stored in KMSEnclave and are auditable via the Audit Service.

Prompt rewrite strategies (examples)

title: Probe Reshaper and Quant-Routing Manual
text:
[...] 
Prompt rewrite strategies (examples)

1) Anchor-preserving compression
- Keep anchor sentences (first and last 2 sentences) and compress middle paragraphs by extracting salient bullet points using a light-weight extractor (heuristic based on punctuation + named entity density).
- Use when probe length > 2x median and QuantRiskScore > 0.2.

2) Lexical normalization
- Replace numerals/dates with canonical tokens (e.g., 2026-02-12 -> [DATE_2026]) when the probe is judged to be structurally invariant. This reduces tokenization variance across quant builds.

3) Controlled paraphrase (limited)
- Apply a single-pass paraphrase using the on-host micro-transformer only when sensitivity model predicts reduced variance and tenant has opted-in. Paraphrases are constrained to preserve named entities and function-call signatures.

Example config manifests (YAML snippet)

---
reshaper_version: v2.1
max_rewrite_tokens: 120
paraphrase_allowed: false
privacy_gating: true
sensitivity_model: fast-token-mlr-v1
provenance_fields: [reshaper_version,det_hash,transformations]

title: Probe Reshaper and Quant-Routing Manual
text:
[...] provenance_fields: [reshaper_version,det_hash,transformations]


RoutingConfig example:
---
default_thresholds:
  low: 0.15
  medium: 0.45
  high: 0.80
tenant_overrides:
  enterprise-tenant: {force_fp16: true}
cost_budget_bucket: standard

Operational runbook (step-by-step)

1) Onboarding a new probe suite:
  a) Register the probe set with the Eval Registry including expected fidelity label (anchor, high-fidelity, smoke).
  b) Run the local Reshaper CLI to preview transformed probes: eval-reshaaper --manifest manifest.yml --preview > previews.tar.gz
  c) Submit preview to the shadow farm with routingConfig=test to generate a delta heatmap.

2) Investigating a regression suspected to be quant-sensitive:
  1. Identify failing probes via Eval Dashboard. Note provenance blob for each probe.
  2. Re-run failing probes with --force-fp16 on the Reshaper CLI to compare diffs.
  3. If diffs disappear on fp16, tag the slice with quant-risk and schedule targeted annotation.
  4. Optionally enable synchronous shadow replay (q8_0 + fp16) for the impacted slice for 24–72 hours.

CLI examples

- Preview transformations:
  eval-reshaper preview --input probes.jsonl --manifest routing-test.yml --out previews.tar.gz

- Run a single probe through the reshaper locally and print provenance:
  echo '{"prompt":"How do I reset password?","metadata":{"tenant":"acme"}}' | eval-reshaper transform --print-provenance

Monitoring and SLOs

- Reshaper CPU P95 per-probe latency: < 25ms (target); alert if > 50ms on median traffic.
- Provenance attach rate: 100% for all reshaped probes.
- Determinism assertion failure rate: < 0.01% (identified by hash mismatch between preview and runtime).
- QuantRouting misclassification (observed variance > 0.05 between selected variant and fp16 baseline): < 2% of traffic per week.

Telemetry and dashboards

- Key dashboards: ProbeReshaper-Latency, QuantRisk-Distribution, ShadowReplay-DeltaHeatmap, Provenance-Integrity.
- Important alerts: sudden shift in QuantRisk distribution, increase in deterministic mismatch, rapid growth of privacy-gated probes.

Privacy and security considerations

- Tenant-identifying obfuscation: only stable placeholders and deterministic hashes are present outside the audit enclave. Restore requires KMS permissions and a documented audit trail.
- Provenance storage: provenance blobs for restricted probes are stored with confidentiality flag and retention policy aligned with legal.

Rollout checklist (canary -> global)

1) Unit tests for all rewrite strategies and determinism checks.
2) Local preview and manual review by Probe Stewards.
3) Shadow run against q8_0 + fp16 for 48 hours on a representative slice.
4) Validate telemetry and SLOs on canary fleet (1% traffic).
5) Gradual rollout with 5/20/50/100% stages, validating misclassification and latency quotas at each stage.

Ownership and contacts

- Component owner: Jordan Patel (Applied ML Platform)
- Oncall rotation: applied-ml-eval-oncall@redwood.internal (week-based)
- Reviewers: Dr. Mei Chen (Evaluator models), Rafael Gomez (Quant infra)

title: Probe Reshaper and Quant-Routing Manual
text:
[...] - Oncall rotation: applied-ml-eval-oncall@redwood.internal (week-based)
- Reviewers: Dr. Mei Chen (Evaluator models), Rafael Gomez (Quant infra)

Appendix: troubleshooting quick hits

- If deterministic hashes differ between preview and runtime: check reshaper_version mismatch; ensure feature flags match and pull the latest manifest from ConfigStore.
- If QuantRiskScore is anomalously high for short probes: verify tokenization model alignment (tokenizer revisions are listed in quant-profile manifest).

Changelog

- 2026-02-12: v1.0 initial draft.
- 2026-03-04: v2.1 add privacy gating, provenance schema, and updated routing thresholds.

References

- Eval Mesh topology cookbook (internal): /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/eval-mesh-topology-and-slotting-cookbook-2026
- Quant shadow fidelity simulator: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/quant-shadow-fidelity-simulator-2026

Feedback and next steps

- Submit PRs to the Reshaper repo and attach unit test results to the manifest. For feature requests or disputes about routing thresholds, open an RFC in the Applied ML team board and tag the reviewers.

title: Telemetry‑Led Probe Feedback and Onboarding Contract
text:
Telemetry‑Led Probe Feedback and Onboarding Contract

Overview

This page defines the telemetry-first contract used to operate probe pipelines, the incremental annotation feedback loop, and the onboarding acceptance criteria for probes and probe-driven model rollouts. It bridges the eval harness, prompt/benchmark catalogs, and the model onboarding lifecycle so that telemetry quality, privacy gating, and operational SLAs are enforced consistently across dedicated, hosted, and private deployments.

Goals
- Ensure that probes (synthetic or live) have measurable telemetry footprints and provenance.
- Close the loop from runtime telemetry -> probe prioritization -> annotation -> model onboarding decisions.
- Provide a minimal contract that teams must implement to onboard probe sets to the central eval mesh.
- Protect PII and tenant privacy with gating rules while keeping annotation latency predictable for operational rollouts.

Scope
This contract applies to: probe fabricators, evaluator-sidecars, the probe registry, the annotation platform, and model-onboarding pipelines. It does NOT replace safety reviews, human-in-the-loop policy checks, or legal compliance signoffs for regulated datasets.

Definitions
- Probe: a single prompt + context + metadata record used to exercise a model.
- Probe Telemetry: runtime signals produced when a probe executes (latency, tokens, kv-cache hit, response stability score, failure flags).
- Probe Fingerprint: stable identifier computed from prompt canonicalization + metadata (sha1 of schema + seed).
- Incremental Annotation: repeated human labeling over time for the same probe to measure drift.

Telemetry Contract (required fields)
All probe executions submitted to the eval mesh must emit the following telemetry fields in JSON. The evaluator-sidecar is the primary emitter; if probes are run in remote sandboxes, an ingest adapter must map local fields to these names.

- probe_id: string (probe fingerprint, immutable)
- probe_version: string (semantic version or date stamp)
- tenant_id: string (or "system" for platform probes)
- run_id: string (unique per execution)
- timestamp_utc: ISO8601 string
- model: string (model-name@artifact-hash)
- seq_len_input: integer
- seq_len_output: integer (if streaming, final token count)
- tokens_consumed: integer
- latency_ms_p50: integer
- latency_ms_p95: integer
- kv_cache_hit: boolean
- failure_code: string | null (see failure taxonomy)
- response_stability_score: float (0.0-1.0)
- telemetry_schema_version: "v1"

Minimum Provenance Metadata (included with registry entry)
- authored_by: string (owner/steering team)
- created_date: YYYY-MM-DD
- priming_template_id: string (if applicable)
- dataset_acl: one of [public, internal, restricted]
- privacy_level: one of [none, low, medium, high]

Privacy & Gating Rules
1) Any probe with privacy_level == high must be processed exclusively in a gated sandbox and annotated with redaction-aware UI.
2) Tenant probes (dataset_acl == restricted) require tenant opt-in before external annotation vendors are permitted access; a summary-only derived probe may be produced for cross-tenant benchmarks.
3) Telemetry payloads must never contain raw user content for high/medium privacy probes; content must be replaced by a content_hash and metadata.

Probe Lifecycle: from Fabrication to Onboarding
1. Fabrication: probe generator produces probes with required metadata (probe_id, fingerprint, priming_template_id).
2. Registration: push preview to probe-registry API (see example below). Registry validates metadata and assigns initial priority.
3. Shadow Execution: probes run in shadow for at least 1k executions or 72h to generate baseline telemetry.
4. Telemetry Review: eval team inspects response_stability_score, kv_cache_hit distributions, and failure_code rates.
5. Annotation: prioritized probes are scheduled for incremental annotation. Fast-turn probes (SLA <= 48h) go to internal annotators; others to vendor/contract annotators per privacy gating.
6. Onboarding Decision: based on acceptance rubric (see table below) the probe set is accepted, requires remediation, or rejected.

Registration API Example (curl)
curl -X POST https://probe-registry.internal/api/v1/register -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '\n{\n  "probe_id": "pf_7e1a2b3f",\n  "priming_template_id": "anchored-system-2026-01",\n  "author": "team-qa",\n  "privacy_level": "low",\n  "dataset_acl": "internal",\n  "samples": 100\n}\'\n
Acceptance Rubric (summary)
| Metric | Threshold | Action |
|---|---:|---|
| response_stability_score (median) | >= 0.85 | Accept |
| failure_rate (code != null) | <= 1.5% | Accept |
| kv_cache_hit (median) | >= 10% for long-context probes | Accept |
| annotation_agreement (krippendorff) | >= 0.7 | Accept |
| privacy_level | not high (unless gated) | Conditional |

title: Telemetry‑Led Probe Feedback and Onboarding Contract
text:
[...] | annotation_agreement (krippendorff) | >= 0.7 | Accept |
| privacy_level | not high (unless gated) | Conditional |

If any "Conditional" rows fail, remediation steps must be logged and a re-run scheduled.

Annotation Feedback Loop (operational runbook)
1. Priority scoring: telemetry-derived priority = w1*(1-response_stability) + w2*failure_flag + w3*DRIFT_SCORE. Default weights: w1=0.5, w2=0.3, w3=0.2.
2. Queueing: high-priority probes enter the fast-track queue (SLA 48h); medium get 5‑day SLA; low are batched monthly.
3. Labeling: annotators are given the latest runtime outputs and a small context window. Annotator UI shows redaction annotations for privacy-level medium/high.
4. Drift detection: newly collected annotations are compared to previous anchor labels; if annotation_agreement < 0.6, a regression triage ticket is auto-created.
5. Rollforward decision: if anchor-aligned metrics improve and telemetry shows stable latency and token economics, probe set is marked as accepted for model promotion.

Regression Triage Fast Path (what to do when annotation_agreement drops)
1. Create regression ticket with: probe_ids (sample 20), last 3 run_id's telemetry, anchor labels, new labels.
2. Triage owner inspects failure_code surface and response_stability deltas.
3. Hypothesis matrix: model, quant-profile, priming-template, runtime-kernel.
4. If likely infrastructure/quant issue -> route to eng-serving-runtime; if prompt drift -> route to prompt stewardship.
5. If urgent customer-facing regression -> trigger shadow rollforward rollback per the Incident Playbook.

Operational SLAs and SLOs (probe system)
- Probe registration validation latency: p99 < 5s.
- Shadow-execution sampling window: min(1000 executions, 72h).
- Annotation turnaround: fast-track p95 <= 48h, medium p95 <= 5 days.
- Telemetry retention for active probes: 90 days; for retired probes: 365 days (metadata only).

Ownership and Contacts
- Probe Registry: team-eval-infra@redwood.ai (primary)
- Annotation Platform: data-ops-annotations@redwood.ai
- Model Onboarding: ml-onboard@redwood.ai
- Escalation (security/privacy): security@redwood.ai

Remediation Patterns and Examples
- Low response_stability_score (median < 0.7): check for quantized rollout. Run targeted shadow with full-precision model and compare response_stability delta.
- Elevated failure_rate with failure_code == KERNEL_OOM: capture kernel profile and escalate to runtime-kernel team with example run_ids.
- Annotation disagreement concentrated on a single prompt family: create priming-template variant and re-run targeted shadow.

Checklist: Onboard a New Probe Set
1. Generate probe set with required metadata (probe_id, priming_template_id, privacy_level).
2. Submit registration request and confirm registry validation.
3. Run shadow for baseline telemetry (wait for sampling window).
4. Review telemetry dashboard (response_stability, failure_rate, kv_hit).
5. Request annotation and monitor labeling SLA.
6. Apply acceptance rubric and record decision & remediation.
7. If accepted: tag probe set as "approved:v1" and schedule promotion into named eval-bundle.

Notes and Implementation Guidance
- Fingerprints must be stable across canonicalization changes; include canonicalization salt in registry metadata to enable re-hash if canonicalization rules change.
- Use content_hash for any redacted payloads; avoid storing raw text in telemetry streams for privacy_level != none.
- The telemetry_schema_version must be incremented for any non-backwards-compatible change; registry will reject older schema submissions.

Appendix: Example Telemetry JSON (v1)
{\n  "probe_id": "pf_7e1a2b3f",\n  "probe_version": "2026-02-01",\n  "tenant_id": "platform",\n  "run_id": "r_20260603_5f3c",\n  "timestamp_utc": "2026-06-03T18:22:31Z",\n  "model": "redwood-13b@sha256:abc123...",\n  "seq_len_input": 512,\n  "seq_len_output": 128,\n  "tokens_consumed": 640,\n  "latency_ms_p50": 32,\n  "latency_ms_p95": 98,\n  "kv_cache_hit": true,\n  "failure_code": null,\n  "response_stability_score": 0.92,\n  "telemetry_schema_version": "v1"\n}

Related pages and references
- See eval mesh topology cookbook for slotting guidance and quota allocation.
- See onboarding eval-playbook for promotion gates and mixed-SLO guidance.

Revision history
- 2026-02-15: Draft published by Priya Menon.
- 2026-03-10: Added privacy gating rules and annotation SLA. (Reviewer: Ethan Caldwell)
- 2026-06-03: Clarified acceptance rubric thresholds and added regression triage fast path. (Reviewer: Sofia Ramirez)

title: Slice Conciliation and Probe Handoff Spec
text:
Slice Conciliation and Probe Handoff Spec

Summary

This document describes the Slice Conciliation and Probe Handoff specification: a lightweight, repeatable process and file format for moving evaluation artifacts (probes, slice definitions, annotations, telemetry anchors) from the Eval team into Model Onboarding and Production Shadow pipelines. The goal is to reduce friction, make evaluation intent explicit, and provide deterministic gates that protect quality and enable rapid rollback.

Who should read this

- Evaluators and QA engineers running the eval harness
- Model onboarding engineers promoting artifacts to shadow and canary
- Runtime and infra engineers who own shadow/traffic mirror plumbing
- SREs and incident responders who will triage model regressions

Principles

1. Explicit intent: every probe bundle must include a short rationale and acceptance criteria.
2. Repeatability: slice manifests are machine-readable and replayable by the eval harness and onboarding pipelines.
3. Minimal surface area for handoff: only the fields needed for gating, telemetry anchors, and traceability are required.
4. Guardrail parity: the same acceptance checks run in shadow must be runnable locally by evaluators.

Terminology

- Probe bundle: a tarball (or S3 prefix) containing prompts, expected labels/anchors, telemetry extractor config, and a slice manifest.
- Slice: a named filter for traffic or probes (e.g., "payment-instructions-vague", "long-context-32k").
- Anchor: a short list of golden examples used to detect statistical drift during promotion.
- Conciliation: the process of reconciling overlapping slices and defining the canonical slice set for onboarding.

File formats and example manifest

All probe bundles must include a single JSON manifest named slice_manifest.json at the root. Minimal manifest schema (human-readable example):

```
manifest_version: "1.0"
bundle_id: "rb-probe-2026-031"
created_by: "riley.chen@redwood.ai"
short_description: "Banking instruction clarity probes for quant-sensitivity and RLHF-regression detection."
slices:
  - id: "payment-instructions-vague"
    query: "intent:payment AND contains:amount_phrase AND tokens_between:0-60"
    expected_failure_mode: "hallucinated_amounts"
    acceptance:
      max_response_entropy_delta: "0.25"
      min_instruction_follow_rate: "0.88"
  - id: "long-context-16k"
    query: "context_length:>8192"
    acceptance:
      max_token_inflation_pct: "5"
anchors:
  - id: "payment-anchor-01"
    seed_prompts: "anchors/payment_anchor_01.json"
telemetry_extractors:
  - name: "perplexity_window"
    path: "tools/extractors/perplexity.py"
    params: "window=32,seed=42"
run_repro_command: "eval-harness run --bundle ./rb-probe-2026-031 --output ./results"
```

Note: The actual manifest must be valid JSON (the example above is YAML-ish for readability). Required keys: manifest_version, bundle_id, created_by, short_description, slices (list), anchors (list), telemetry_extractors (list), run_repro_command.

Conciliation workflow (high level)

title: Slice Conciliation and Probe Handoff Spec
text:
[...] 
Conciliation workflow (high level)

1. Eval creates probe bundle and registers bundle_id in the Eval registry.
2. Automated sanity checks run locally: schema validation, tokenization parity, and a 10-sample anchor run.
3. Eval owner opens a handoff request in the onboarding queue, attaching the bundle_id and a 1-paragraph rationale.
4. Conciliation job compares incoming slices against the canonical slice index. If overlaps > 30% by sample identity, a reconciliation item is created and assigned to both parties.
5. Once conciliation resolves (either accept new slice, merge with existing, or mark as variant), onboarding schedules a shadow promotion.

Acceptance and gating checks (run in both eval harness and shadow)

- Statistical gates:
  - Anchor fidelity: mean token-level KL divergence (model vs baseline) < 0.35 over anchors.
  - Instruction-following: fraction of anchor tests passing policy tests >= 0.88.
  - Response stability: Response Stability Index (RSI) delta <= 0.06 compared to baseline.
- Performance gates:
  - Median p95 latency increase on slice traffic <= 15% during shadow.
  - Memory and kv-cache growth < 12% (measured on representative sequence lengths).
- Safety gates:
  - Safety sentinel pass rate (automated) >= 99.2% on anchors.

Conciliation decision matrix (table)

| Condition | Action | Owner | Required artifact |
|---|---|---:|---|
| New slice, low overlap (<30%) | Accept as new slice ID | Eval owner -> Onboarding | slice_manifest.json, anchors |
| High overlap (>30%), semantics match | Merge with existing slice (track alias) | Onboarding | reconciliation diff, merged manifest |
| High overlap, semantics conflict | Hold -> manual review | Eval + Onboarding | review notes, adjudicator tag |
| Anchor fails locally but passes baseline | Reject bundle -> fix probes | Eval owner | failed-anchor report |

Operational runbook (triage steps when a shadow shows regression)

1. Triage ticket created by Shadow Job: include bundle_id, slice_id, failing metrics, and example request IDs.
2. Run local repro with `eval-harness run --bundle <bundle_id> --slice <slice_id> --seed 0..4` and capture diffs.
3. If repro successful, run `bisect-model-variants --start <previous_promoted> --end <candidate>` to identify the first model introducing delta.
4. If bisect points to a recent model promotion, trigger automatic rollback per Deploy Rollback playbook and notify stakeholders.
5. If bisect inconclusive, escalate to Model Regression oncall and attach probe bundle and anchor outputs.

Quick CLI examples

- Validate manifest locally:
  eval-harness validate-manifest ./slice_manifest.json

- Run a targeted probe slice locally:
  eval-harness run --bundle ./rb-probe-2026-031 --slice payment-instructions-vague --samples 200 --output ./tmp/results.jsonl

- Produce anchor report for onboarding:
  eval-harness anchor-report --bundle ./rb-probe-2026-031 --out ./tmp/anchor_report.json

Telemetry and observability contract

Every shadow run must emit a standard set of telemetry fields (JSON lines) tagged with bundle_id and slice_id:

- bundle_id (string)
- slice_id (string)
- request_id (uuid)
- model_variant (string)
- latency_ms (number)
- token_count (int)
- perplexity_window (number)
- rsi (number)
- safety_flag (bool)

These fields should be mapped into the central eval metrics pipeline. Dashboards must include: slice-level RSI trends, anchor KL trends, median latency by slice, and failure rate by anchor tag.

title: Slice Conciliation and Probe Handoff Spec
text:
[...] 
These fields should be mapped into the central eval metrics pipeline. Dashboards must include: slice-level RSI trends, anchor KL trends, median latency by slice, and failure rate by anchor tag.

Promotion checklist (before scheduling canary)

1. Manifest schema validated (CI job).
2. Anchor fidelity within thresholds on eval harness.
3. Conciliation status: accepted or merged.
4. Shadow-run smoke pass (>= 95% success on non-safety anchors).
5. Resource perf check on representative instance type passed.
6. Runbook and rollback owner assigned.

Ownership and contacts

- Eval owner (creates bundle): role/eval-engineer@redwood.ai
- Onboarding owner: role/model-onboard@redwood.ai
- Shadow operator (if automated): eng-platform-shadow@redwood.ai
- Regression oncall: pager oncall-model-regress@redwood.ai

Appendix A — Example reconciler output (diff summary)

```
reconciler_summary: {
  "incoming_slice": "payment-instructions-vague",
  "matched_existing": "payment-instructions",
  "overlap_pct": 42,
  "resolution": "merged_as_alias",
  "merged_fields": ["expected_failure_mode","anchors"]
}
```

Appendix B — Minimal local smoke test script

```
#!/bin/bash
BUNDLE=$1
SLICE=$2
set -e
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -U redwood-eval-harness
eval-harness validate-manifest $BUNDLE/slice_manifest.json
eval-harness run --bundle $BUNDLE --slice $SLICE --samples 50 --output ./smoke.jsonl
python tools/anchor_check.py --input ./smoke.jsonl --anchors $BUNDLE/anchors --threshold 0.88
```

Notes and open questions

- Should we add a canonical schema registry for slice definitions to avoid subtle tokenization mismatches? Proposal: small JSON Schema + example canonical tokenizer config embedded in manifest.
- When slices are merged, how should historical metrics be backfilled? Current plan: annotate historical dashboards with alias resolution, do not rewrite historic IDs.

References

- Eval harness: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/eval-mesh-and-workload-fingerprinting-2026
- Onboarding readiness matrix: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/inference-readiness-eval-matrix-and-promotion-playbook-2026
- Regression triage: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/causal-regression-investigation-handbook-2026

Change log

- 2026-02-10 (Riley Chen) — Initial draft and example manifests.
- 2026-03-01 (Riley Chen) — Added conciliation decision matrix, telemetry contract, and CLI examples.

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection

Summary:
This document describes our standard for generating amplified test collections from a small set of real failures (latent or observed) to uncover related regressions and emergent failure modes across model variants, quantization profiles, and serving configurations. It bridges the eval-harness, prompt set curation, model onboarding and regression triage processes by describing a repeatable pipeline, danger taxonomy, recommended mutation strategies, and concrete CI / runtime checks to catch latent failures earlier in the lifecycle.

Why we need test amplification:
- Hand-labeled or in-field failures are often sparse. Small seeds may hide families of correlated failures that only appear under slightly different prompts, context truncation, or KV-cache conditions.
- Amplification reduces expensive human annotation by generating prioritized synthetic variants that trigger edge behaviors.
- It helps map failure surfaces across deployment axes: model family, sequence length, batch size, quantization format (int8/4/FP16), and routing fallbacks.

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] - It helps map failure surfaces across deployment axes: model family, sequence length, batch size, quantization format (int8/4/FP16), and routing fallbacks.


Intended audience:
- Applied ML engineers owning eval harness integration and model onboarding
- Runtime engineers validating quantization/latency trade-offs
- SRE and QA teams running pre-release gating suites
- Solutions engineers investigating customer-exposed regressions

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] - Solutions engineers investigating customer-exposed regressions


Goals and acceptance criteria:
- A documented amplification pipeline that converts a seed corpus (N >= 5 failing examples) into a prioritized test bundle of 200–2,000 variants within 24h.
- Amplified tests must include metadata: mutation type, expected failure signal (classification/semantic divergence/unsafe output), trigger conditions (seq_len, kv_cache_percent), and reproducibility flags.
- CI gates must detect statistically significant degradation at pre-defined thresholds (see SLOs table).

SLOs and alert thresholds (recommended):
- Latent Failure Coverage: amplification should increase unique failure triggers by >= 3x relative to seed set in a single run (target: 3x–10x).
- Regression detection sensitivity: if model A → model B causes a mean response-stability-index (RSI) drop > 5% on amplified bundle, block promotion and raise P1 review.
- Quantization amplification check: any amplified test that flips a safety classifier label (safe → unsafe) or changes top-1 intent should be flagged as a critical regression.

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] - Quantization amplification check: any amplified test that flips a safety classifier label (safe → unsafe) or changes top-1 intent should be flagged as a critical regression.


Failure taxonomy (for labeling amplified tests):
1) Semantic drift — generated variant changes desirable intent despite surface similarity.
2) Safety flip — content becomes policy-violating after transformation or quantization.
3) Hallucination spike — previously factual response is now fabricated or inconsistent.
4) Truncation sensitivity — longer or shorter context changes answer in unacceptable ways.
5) Cache-dependent nondeterminism — responses diverge when KV cache reuse is simulated.
6) Tokenization edgecases — whitespace/punctuation/emoji changes tokenization and triggers an error or change in behavior.

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] 6) Tokenization edgecases — whitespace/punctuation/emoji changes tokenization and triggers an error or change in behavior.


Amplification strategies (ordered by priority):
1. Orthographic and punctuation perturbations
   - Add/remove punctuation, swap hyphens vs em-dash, normalize unicode.
   - Example: "state-of-the-art" → "state of the art", add zero-width space.
2. Context slicing and prefix variation
   - Prepend neutral sentences, repeat tokens, truncate at common tokenization boundaries to simulate windowing.
3. Shot/anchor perturbations
   - Insert or remove few-shot examples, scramble example order, or substitute placeholders with semantically-similar distractors.
4. Numeric and unit transformations
   - Convert units, change date formats, replace numbers with words, shift magnitude (e.g., 1,000 → 1000).
5. Adversarial paraphrasing (semantic-preserving)
   - Use templated paraphrasers with controlled diversity (e.g., back-translation only when quality score > 0.85).
6. KV-cache and incremental decoding simulation
   - Re-run prompt with parts of previously generated tokens pre-filled into cache to surface cache-boundary bugs.
7. Mixed-mode quantization toggles
   - Simulate int8/int4 artifacts on candidate models using fast emulator or sliced inference harness to reveal quantization-specific failures.

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...]    - Simulate int8/int4 artifacts on candidate models using fast emulator or sliced inference harness to reveal quantization-specific failures.


Automated pipeline (high level):
1) Seed selection: accept seed failures from bug tracker, field telemetry, or offline evals. Seeds must include failing_response, expected_response, trace_id, and context snapshot.
2) Mutation generation: apply amplification strategies producing labeled variants and collect provenance.
3) Filtering & scoring: run lightweight filters (toxicity, token-length bounds, trivial duplicates). Score variants according to novelty (embedding distance from seed), syntactic variance, and model sensitivity (probe with a fast model).
4) Prioritization: select top-K for full-run depending on test budget and desired coverage.
5) Full-run evaluation: execute amplified bundle across target matrix (model versions, quant profiles, serving configs). Capture metrics: RSI, safety_class, topk_intent_changes, latency, token_count.
6) Triage and triage metadata: generate triage deck with failing examples grouped by failure taxonomy and suggested root causes (quantization, prompt-windowing, prompt-template mismatch).

Runbook: Amplify-to-Triage — step-by-step
1. Create a ticket in the regression board with seed examples and desired target matrix.
2. Run amplification CLI: redwood-eval amplify --seed-file seeds.json --budget 1000 --strategies "orthographic,paraphrase,cache" --out amplified.ndjson
   - Example: redwood-eval amplify --seed-file /var/lib/seeds/field-failures.json --budget 500 --out /tmp/amplified.ndjson
3. Validate amplified.ndjson: use the provided validator to check metadata and duplicate ratio: redwood-eval validate /tmp/amplified.ndjson
4. Submit amplified bundle to CI gate: redwood-ci run-amplified --bundle /tmp/amplified.ndjson --matrix "stable-v2,stable-v3|int8|kv100,kv10"
5. If CI flags regressions: open a regression triage session, attach the generated triage deck, and assign owners.
6. For high-severity safety flips: immediately block promotion and notify Security/Policy via the escalation channel.

Amplified bundle format (required fields) — example ndjson line (single-line JSON per test):
{
  "id": "amp-20260112-0001",
  "seed_id": "seed-42",
  "mutation": "orthographic:dash-normalized",
  "mutation_description": "dash replaced with spaces",
  "expected_signal": "semantic-divergence",
  "notes": "from field-incident #1234",
  "provenance": "amplify:v1"
}
(Our validator requires each amplified item to include at least id, seed_id, mutation, and provenance.)

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] (Our validator requires each amplified item to include at least id, seed_id, mutation, and provenance.)


Filtering heuristics and budgets:
- De-duplication threshold: normalized-levenshtein < 0.15 => drop.
- Toxicity filter: use safety-model v3 to pre-filter expected unsafe variants unless the seed is unsafe.
- Budget allocation: 50% orthographic/context, 25% paraphrase, 25% cache/quant tests by default. Adjust per workload.

Integration points with existing systems:
- Eval harness: amplified bundles are a first-class artifact. Use the eval-harness runner with --amplified flag and provide matrix yaml.
- Model onboarding: require amplified-bundle smoke tests before promotion to staging. Add an entry to the model promotion checklist: "amplified test run (K=500) with zero critical safety flips".
- Quality guardrails: wire amplified failure counts into the response-stability-index dashboard and create an alert when growth rate > 2x/week.
- Quantization profiles: add a per-profile amplification pass to detect quant-specific failure modes and store fingerprints in the quant profile registry.

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] - Quantization profiles: add a per-profile amplification pass to detect quant-specific failure modes and store fingerprints in the quant profile registry.


Example evaluation matrix YAML (text table):
- models: [stable-v2, stable-v3, experimental-quantized-v1]
- quant_profiles: [fp16, int8_fast, hybrid4]
- kv_cache_percent: [0, 10, 100]
- seq_lengths: [64, 512, 2048]

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] - seq_lengths: [64, 512, 2048]


Triage decision rubric (quick):
1) Critical safety flip or P0 customer-impacting behavior → immediate rollback/hold and cross-team incident.
2) High semantic regressions on high-frequency seed slices → revert or prioritize hotfix.
3) Low-frequency hallucination increase < 10% → schedule a targeted eval and shadow rollout.
4) Quant-only diff: treat as quant regression; either tune quant profile or mark as acceptable with documentation.

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] 4) Quant-only diff: treat as quant regression; either tune quant profile or mark as acceptable with documentation.


Ownership and responsibilities:
- Applied ML (eval-harness) team: maintain amplification tool, mutation library, and validator.
- Runtime/Quant team: supply fast emulators for quanted inference and validate model-specific failure fingerprints.
- SRE/QA: operate CI gates and define budget for full-run amplification checks.
- Product / Solutions: prioritize seed list and annotate customer-critical seeds.

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] - Product / Solutions: prioritize seed list and annotate customer-critical seeds.


Operational knobs and configuration examples:
- amplification.config (YAML text example):
  strategies: [orthographic,paraphrase,cache,quant_toggle]
  budget: 800
  dedup_threshold: 0.15
  prioritize_by: [novelty,seed_frequency]
  safety_filter: true

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...]   safety_filter: true


Common pitfalls and mitigations:
- Over-amplification (noise): enforce novelty scoring and cap mutations per seed to 50.
- Synthetic variants that are invalid downstream: validate for required context signatures before including in full run.
- Amplification bias: monitor distribution across language, tokenization patterns, and user geographies to avoid skew.

Metrics to collect (telemetry suggestions):
- Amplified-to-failure ratio (unique failing variants / total amplified)
- Time-to-triage (amplified bundle processed to triage ticket creation)
- Regression false positive rate (CI flag that is not reproducible in field)
- Per-model quant flip rate (safety or intent flips attributable to quant profile)

Appendix: useful commands and quick snippets
- Generate an amplified bundle: redwood-eval amplify --seed-file seeds.json --budget 500 --strategies all --out amplified.ndjson
- Validate bundle: redwood-eval validate amplified.ndjson --schema amplify-v1
- Run in CI: redwood-ci run-amplified --bundle amplified.ndjson --matrix matrix.yaml --notify-on-fail # triggers triage job

title: Test Amplification and Latent Failure Detection
text:
[...] - Run in CI: redwood-ci run-amplified --bundle amplified.ndjson --matrix matrix.yaml --notify-on-fail # triggers triage job


References and related reading:
- See eval-harness smoke-and-fidelity-eval-workflow for CI integration notes.
- For regression bisect automation, see automated-regression-bisect-and-blame-assistant.

Glossary:
- Amplification: automated generation of synthetic test variants from seed failures.
- RSI (response-stability-index): internal metric that aggregates semantic and token-level divergence across runs.
- Quant flip: a change in a model response category attributable to quantization (FP16 → int8, etc.).

Contact and escalation
- Primary owner: Maya Chen (applied-ml) — maya@redwood.ai
- Escalation channel: #amplified-regressions (PagerDuty on-call forwarded for critical safety flips)

Change log:
- 2025-11-08: created initial draft.
- 2025-12-10: added quant-toggle strategy and example ndjson format.
- 2026-01-12: updated SLOs and added triage rubric after pilot runs.

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel

Summary:
This manual describes the operational role of the Field Fidelity Wrangler: the engineer responsible for ensuring fidelity when transitioning models through shadow meshes, mixed-precision (quant) handoffs, and final promotion to production. It consolidates practical checklists, thresholds, runbooks, and sample commands for day-to-day handoffs and regression triage.

Scope:
- Applicable to shadow-mesh validation, quantization handoff windows, and multi-slice probe orchestration for Hosted, Dedicated and Private deployments.
- Intended audience: Applied ML engineers, runtime SREs, model onboarding stewards, and evaluator operators.

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...] - Intended audience: Applied ML engineers, runtime SREs, model onboarding stewards, and evaluator operators.


Goals:
- Maintain end-to-end fidelity budget: Delta_coherence <= 2.5% (service-level) and Response-stability-index (RSI) drop <= 0.03 in promotion windows.
- Ensure quantization sentinel coverage across 6 canonical slices per service.
- Reduce manual rollbacks by standardizing automated triage signals and ownership handoffs.

Key definitions:
1) Shadow mesh: a fabric of shadowed requests routed to candidate models for fidelity evaluation without affecting live responses.
2) Quant-sentinel: a small, representative probe set that surfaces degradation specific to quantized kernels (coherence, hallucination, functioncall fidelity).
3) Field Fidelity Wrangler: primary oncall/owner for a model promotion window.

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...] 3) Field Fidelity Wrangler: primary oncall/owner for a model promotion window.


Ownership and RACI:
- Field Fidelity Wrangler (FFW): active owner during onboarding and promotion window. Responsible for coordinating shadow runs, triage, and final approval.
- Model Owner: approves release when FFW signoff is green.
- Evaluator Team: generates and annotates probe labels; maintains probe budget.
- Runtime SRE: ensures shadow mesh capacity and flags infra regressions.

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
section_header: Pre-promotion checklist (must be completed before canary)
text:
[...] - Runtime SRE: ensures shadow mesh capacity and flags infra regressions.


Pre-promotion checklist (must be completed before canary):
1. Shadow mesh: 24h stable run with at least 5M tokens or 50k requests sampled across target slices.
2. Telemetry: full request-context trace available, with token-level latency and token-cost histograms.
3. Sentinels: Quant-sentinel executed and annotated; RSI and coherence metrics reported.
4. Safety gates: safety sentinel pass (no critical safety sentinel failures in 1000 checks).
5. Rollback plan: documented rollback steps and must-have artifacts in S3 (/redwood/onboard/artifacts/<model-id>/).

Operational runbook: Shadow -> Quant Handoff -> Canary (numbered, stepwise):
1) Initialize mesh:
   - Create shadow-route in router service: POST /admin/routes {
       "name": "mesh-<team>-<model>-vX",
       "percent_shadow": 100
     }
   - Command example (CLI):
     rwdctl routes create --name mesh-payments-bart-v2 --shadow-percent 100 --target model:bart-v2
2) Run baseline probes (0h): run probe-suite with seed=stable-2026 and collect baseline artifact. Use evaluator-sidecar: eval-run --suite baseline --seed stable-2026 --out s3://redwood/evals/<id>/baseline.json
3) Run quant-sentinel: instantiate quant-aware evaluator with mixed-precision kernels: eval-run --suite quant-sentinel --config quant-hedge-1.yaml --out s3://.../quant-sentinel.json
4) Monitor live metrics for 2x SLO windows (default SLO window = 15m):
   - latency.p50 <= target_latency * 1.15
   - error.rate <= 0.5%
   - RSI_drop <= 0.03
5) If any sentinel breaches, trigger triage (see "Regression triage flow").
6) After 24h with green sentinels and no infra anomalies, initiate canary: 1% traffic to candidate, observe for 4 hours, then 5%, 15% with progressive hold times.

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...] 6) After 24h with green sentinels and no infra anomalies, initiate canary: 1% traffic to candidate, observe for 4 hours, then 5%, 15% with progressive hold times.


Probe composition and examples:
- Probe inventory (minimum set):
  | slice_name | purpose | example_count | annotation_required | notes |
  |------------|---------|---------------|---------------------|-------|
  | auth_edge  | long-context auth flows | 1,200 | yes | checks token redaction, functioncall fidelity |
  | billing_calc | numeric reasoning | 800 | yes | verifies exactness and rounding |
  | multi-turn-support | coherence over 8 turns | 1,000 | yes | stress kv-cache and priming |
  | codegen_small | correctness in python | 600 | binary | unit test-able output |
  | safety_tort | safety traps | 400 | reviewed | must be no-critical |
  | short_latency | micro-latency probes | 2,000 | no | measure p95/p99 tails |

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...]   | short_latency | micro-latency probes | 2,000 | no | measure p95/p99 tails |


Quant-sentinel thresholds (operators):
- Coherence-delta (semantic similarity vs baseline): <= 0.025 per slice.
- Token-level hallucination rate: <= 0.8% absolute increase.
- Functioncall-argument fidelity (exact match): >= 98.5% across functioncall-capable slices.
- Latency p95 increase due to KV load: <= 20ms absolute.

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
section_header: Alerting and monitoring configuration (recommended dashboard panels / alert rules)
text:
[...] - Latency p95 increase due to KV load: <= 20ms absolute.


Alerting and monitoring configuration (recommended dashboard panels / alert rules):
1. Panel: RSI (service, per-slice) — alert if RSI_drop > 0.03 for 30m.
2. Panel: Coherence delta — alert if coherence-delta > 0.025 for 15m.
3. Panel: Quant-sentinel failures — alert on >3 failing probes in 10m.
4. Panel: Tokenized cost delta — paging if cost_per_token increase > 10% and accompanied by latency regression.

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...] 4. Panel: Tokenized cost delta — paging if cost_per_token increase > 10% and accompanied by latency regression.


Regression triage flow (fast path):
1) Auto-detect: evaluator pipeline marks regression_flag = true and attaches top-3 delta traces.
2) FFW reviews signal in 10 minutes: check slices, annotate likely root cause (quant, prompt-shift, infra).
3) If quant suspected: trigger quant-rollback to previous quant profile and re-run quant-sentinel.
   - CLI: rwdctl quant rollback --model bart-v2 --to-profile q-profile-2026-08-21
4) If prompt-shift suspected: freeze model promotion and run prompt-anchoring probe augmentation (see "Prompt anchoring" below).
5) If infra suspected: assign to runtime SRE, escalate and pause canary.
6) Document findings in the regression journal and tag PR release as blocked until cleared.

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...] 6) Document findings in the regression journal and tag PR release as blocked until cleared.


Prompt anchoring and repair (short procedure):
1. Identify top 5 failing prompts from probe output.
2. Create anchored prompt set by adding 10 perturbations per prompt (noise, paraphrase, context-drop).
3. Re-run eval-harness with the anchored set.
4. If repairs reduce RSI_drop by >50% and coherence-delta < threshold, proceed to targeted soft-rollout with additional monitoring.

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...] 4. If repairs reduce RSI_drop by >50% and coherence-delta < threshold, proceed to targeted soft-rollout with additional monitoring.


Example config snippets (YAML):
- quant-hedge-1.yaml:
  model: bart-v2
  profiles: [q8_ggml, q4_hybrid]
  hedging_policy:
    fallback: fp16
    max_latency_increase_ms: 15
    sentinel_slice: quant-sentinel

- shadow-route-spec.yaml:
  route_name: mesh-payments-bart-v2
  shadow_percent: 100
  sample_keys: [tenant_id, request_type]

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...]   sample_keys: [tenant_id, request_type]


Safety and annotation gating:
- Safety sentinel thresholds: no critical safety verdicts in last 1000 probe responses; >95% of flagged ambiguous responses must be human-reviewed before promotion.
- Annotation SLA: evaluator team must return labeled sentinel results within 72 hours for critical slices.

Rollback artifacts and required evidence (table):
  | artifact | location | owner | required to rollback? |
  |---------|----------|-------|-----------------------|
  | baseline eval artifact | s3://redwood/evals/<id>/baseline.json | Evaluator Team | yes |
  | quant artifact | s3://redwood/evals/<id>/quant-sentinel.json | Evaluator Team | yes |
  | router route config | /config/router/routes.yaml | Runtime SRE | yes |
  | model package (prev) | s3://redwood/models/<model-id>/vX/ | Model Owner | yes |

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...]   | model package (prev) | s3://redwood/models/<model-id>/vX/ | Model Owner | yes |


Operational tips and known gotchas:
- Long-context probes must be seeded with realistic system prompts; synthetic short-cuts often hide quant regressions.
- When mixing q4/q8 profiles, prefer shadowing with q8 to expose worst-case hallucination behavior early.
- Do not rely solely on aggregate RSI; per-slice signals often reveal correlated regressions masked by frequent slices.

Example triage log entry (template):
- timestamp: 2026-08-24T14:32Z
- model: bart-v2
- owner: asha.mehta@redwood.com
- signal: quant-sentinel coherence-delta=0.038 on slice billing_calc
- immediate_action: triggered quant rollback to q-profile-2026-07-15; paused canary at 1%
- next_steps: re-run quant-sentinel, augment prompt anchors for billing_calc, re-evaluate in 6h

Appendix: evaluation CLI quick reference:
- Start shadow run: rwdctl shadow start --route mesh-payments-bart-v2 --duration 24h --sample 50k
- Run evaluator suite: eval-run --suite quant-sentinel --model-model-id bart-v2 --out /tmp/q.json
- Promote model: rwdctl promote --model bart-v2 --to production --approval-note "FFW green"

title: Field Fidelity Wrangler Manual: shadow-mesh handoff and quant-sentinel
text:
[...] - Promote model: rwdctl promote --model bart-v2 --to production --approval-note "FFW green"


Revision history:
- 2026-03-12: created by Asha Mehta
- 2026-06-10: added quant-sentinel thresholds (Diego Campos review)
- 2026-09-01: clarified probe inventory and canary hold times (Priya Raman review)

Related resources and links:

- Quant profiles registry: s3://redwood/quant-profiles/
- Shadow orchestration dashboard: https://grafana.internal.redwood.com/d/shadow-meshs

Contact:

title: Longtail Continuous Scan Playbook for Production Evals
text:
Longtail Continuous Scan Playbook for Production Evals

Summary

This playbook describes the Longtail Continuous Scan (LCS) process: an automated, prioritized, and privacy-conscious approach to running small, frequent, and broad-spectrum evaluations of models in production-like conditions. LCS targets tail distributions and low-frequency prompt slices that commonly surface quality regressions after deployments, quantization, or major prompt/handler changes.

Scope

- Applies to Redwood studio-managed models and verified open-model variants used in Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.
- Covers evaluation orchestration, slicing, scheduling, metric thresholds, alerting, and first-response triage for long-tail failures.
- Excludes heavy offline human-labeling efforts (those are handled by the annotation pipeline documented separately).

Objectives and SLOs

- Detect regression signals originating from: model updates, quantization changes, routing fallback, KV-cache invalidation, and prompt-surface changes.
- Time-to-detect: median 6 hours from the first anomalous tail observation.
- Time-to-triage (initial owner assignment): 2 hours after a severity-2 alert.
- False-positive rate target: < 12% for automated alerts after calibration.

Glossary

- Longtail slice: A prompt/user pattern with low request volume but high business risk (e.g., legal disclaimers, financial templates, multi-step instructions).
- Scan job: A configured run of LCS that samples prompts, model variants, and runtime settings.
- Seedset: Curated minimal prompt examples used to bootstrap a slice.

Architecture overview

1) Controller: Scheduler service that farms jobs to the eval harness.
2) Runner pool: Ephemeral workers that execute model calls (can run against hosted endpoints, dedicated pools, or shadow VPC endpoints).
3) Aggregator: Time-series store and comparator that computes stability metrics and drift signals.
4) Alerting layer: Rule engine that applies thresholds and enriches signals with provenance to emit alerts to PagerDuty/Slack.

Scan types and when to run them

- Smoke Scan (hourly): 100 weighted prompts across high-priority slices. Fast checks for immediate regressions after traffic-shaping or small rollouts.
- Focus Scan (every 6 hours): 500 prompts concentrated on business-critical low-volume slices and recent failing seeds. Includes edge-case adversarial prompts.
- Exploration Scan (daily): 3k prompts sampled stratified by locale, token-length buckets, user-agent signals, and model-quantization variant. Used for drift discovery and slice expansion.

Prioritization and budgeting

- Each customer and route is assigned a priority weight (0-10) by product owners. Priority influences sampling probability.
- GPU budget caps: LCS jobs cannot exceed 8 GPU-hours per region per day by default; request increases via capacity requests.

Data handling and privacy

- Do not store raw customer prompt text unless explicitly consented. All LCS jobs use pseudonymized prompt keys and salted hashing.
- Seedsets that contain customer data must be tagged "PII-sensitive" and routed to a private shadow cluster with KMS-protected logs.
- Logs kept for debug: sanitized prompt hash, token counts, model variant, latency, and top-3 token-probability deltas. Retention: 30 days by default.

Evaluation metrics and thresholds

title: Longtail Continuous Scan Playbook for Production Evals
text:
[...] Evaluation metrics and thresholds


Primary metrics (automatic):
- Response Stability Index (RSI): measures distributional shift over n-gram/embedding space. Threshold: RSI delta > 0.12 triggers a warning; > 0.25 triggers an incident.
- Semantic Recall (SR): fraction of ground-truth keyphrases present in model outputs for structured tasks. Threshold: drop > 6% (relative) triggers a high-priority alert.
- Safety-Trigger Rate (STR): rate of policy-flagging tokens per 1k responses. Threshold: absolute STR > 4 and increase > 2x baseline triggers immediate review.

Secondary metrics (profiling):
- Median latency (ms), 95th percentile latency, kv-cache hit ratio, and truncated-output rate.

Regression detection heuristics

- Multi-evidence consensus: an alert requires at least two metric classes to exhibit degradation (e.g., RSI + SR), unless the metric is a safety hit.
- Rolling-window confirmation: short blips are suppressed using a 3-sample exponential smoothing prior to alerting.
- Model-variant correlation: anomalies that correlate only with a single variant (e.g., quantized-int8) tag the alert as "quant-suspect".

Triage playbook (first 6 steps)

1) Alert enrichment: The aggregator appends last-known rollout id, model-commit, quant-profile id, and route config.
2) Owner assignment: PagerDuty integration assigns the alert to the model-owner rotation.
3) Immediate checks (automated):
   a) Verify model-commit changed in the last 24 hours.
   b) Check KV cache flush events or recent cache eviction logs.
   c) Examine autoscaler events and pod restarts for the model cluster.
4) Canary rollback: If the regression aligns to the canary rollout id and the rollback window is open, trigger automated rollback to prior canary snapshot.
5) Shadow replay: Launch focused shadow-run against previous known-good variant for 200 seeds. Compare diffs and attach to the ticket.
6) Human review: If automated checks are inconclusive, assign to applied-ml for semantic review and label impacted slices.

title: Longtail Continuous Scan Playbook for Production Evals
text:
[...] 6) Human review: If automated checks are inconclusive, assign to applied-ml for semantic review and label impacted slices.


Detailed triage checklist (owners should follow):
- Verify reproducibility using the seedset with deterministic RNG.
- Re-run with and without quantization to isolate numeric sensitivity.
- Re-run with cached KV disabled to test cache-sensitivity regressions.
- If safety flags increased, run the safety-deep-eval (1000 synthetic adversarial prompts) before public rollback.

Observability, alert templates, and runbook snippets

title: Longtail Continuous Scan Playbook for Production Evals
text:
[...] Observability, alert templates, and runbook snippets


Alert template (Slack / PagerDuty summary):
- Title: LCS Alert: {metric} regression on {route} (severity {1|2|3})
- Body: One-line summary, metric deltas, affected model-variants, last rollout id, link to aggregator graph, and link to reproduction job.

Suggested Grafana panels:
- RSI time-series with slice decomposition (lines per locale/token-length).
- SR per variant as bar-chart with error bands.
- Top-10 diffs in token-probability mass as a table.

Automation recipes and example configs

title: Longtail Continuous Scan Playbook for Production Evals
text:
[...] Automation recipes and example configs


Example LCS job config (YAML, abbreviated):

```
job_id: lcs.focus.20260305
priority_weight: 8
slices:
  - id: legal-disclaimer
    seedset: s3://lcs/seeds/legal-v3.csv
    sample_ratio: 0.6
  - id: financial-templates
    seedset: s3://lcs/seeds/finance-v2.csv
    sample_ratio: 0.3
variants:
  - model: rdx-gpt-13b
    quant_profile: quant-int8-a
  - model: rdx-gpt-13b
    quant_profile: fq-16
budget:
  max_gpu_hours: 2
  max_concurrent_workers: 6
output_policy: hash-only
```

title: Longtail Continuous Scan Playbook for Production Evals
section_header: CLI example to kick off a Focus Scan against a private endpoint
text:
[...] ```


CLI example to kick off a Focus Scan against a private endpoint:

$ rdx-eval run --job-config s3://lcs/configs/lcs.focus.20260305.yaml --target private-shadow --concurrency 4 --tags longtail,focus

Onboarding checklist for a new model into LCS

1) Register model variant and quant profiles with the aggregator catalog.
2) Provide initial seedsets for priority slices (min 40 seeds per slice).
3) Configure run-time limits and private-vpc access if seeds contain customer-derived patterns.
4) Run a baseline Exploration Scan and upload baseline metric snapshots to the baseline store.

Ownership and escalation

- LCS Controller: eng-infra (owner: Priya Gupta)
- Aggregator & rules: applied-ml (owner: Arjun Patel)
- On-call triage: model-owner rotation (list maintained in Oncall Playbook)

Related pages and artifacts

- Seedset schema: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/seedset-schema-spec-2025
- Safety deep-eval: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/continuous-safety-eval-pipeline-and-integration-guide-2026
- Quantization safety checklist: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quantization-failure-modes-and-recovery-playbook-2026

Appendix: sample alert payload (JSON)

{
  "alert_id": "lcs-20260305-8fb2",
  "route": "/api/v1/generate",
  "metric": "RSI",
  "delta": "0.31",
  "top_slices": ["legal-disclaimer", "long-form-instructions"],
  "variants": ["rdx-gpt-13b:quant-int8-a"],
  "links": {"repro_job": "https://internal.redwood/eval/12345"}
}

This playbook will be revised as LCS matures; for questions or improvement proposals, contact the owners listed above.

title: Adaptive Eval Mixer: Scheduling, Safety & Cost-aware Mixing
text:
Adaptive Eval Mixer: Scheduling, Safety & Cost-aware Mixing

Overview:

This document describes the Adaptive Eval Mixer (AEM) — a scheduling and mixing policy for the eval harness that balances safety coverage, latency-sensitive probes, and cost budgets across hybrid workloads (shadow, synthetic, and live-mirrored traffic). AEM is intended for use by the eval-harness service (eval-mixer-service) and the probe-orchestrator layer when routing probes to private/dedicated/hosted model endpoints.

Goals:
- Maintain a minimum safety probe coverage (SLO) across critical slices while minimizing incremental token cost.
- Prioritize low-latency functional checks for production-impacting routes.
- Adapt probe mix to changing workload fingerprints and telemetry (cold-starts, quantized deployments, new model versions).
- Provide clear roll-forward and rollback criteria when the probe mix detects regressions.

Scope and Audience:
- Applies to eval-harness orchestration and the probe scheduling stage; integrates with eval-mesh and promotion pipelines.
- Intended readers: SREs, Applied ML Engineers, Oncall evaluators, Release engineers.

Motivation and Problem Statement:
Teams running continuous evaluations face three competing constraints: safety (need broad behavioral coverage), fidelity (samples that reflect production slices), and cost (token/compute budgets for evaluators). Existing static schedules either overspend or leave blind spots when workload distributions shift. AEM provides a lightweight, adaptive control loop to rebalance probe types and priorities with explicit safety cordons.

Design Summary:
1) Probe classes:
   - Safety Probes: curated sentinel prompts and adversarial probes (high priority).
   - Functional Probes: workload-derived samples (medium priority).
   - Smoke Probes: short health checks and latency-sensitive triggers (high priority, low cost).
   - Quant Probes: model/quantization-specific stress tests (expense varies).

2) Mixer Policy Layers:
   - Hard Safety Floor: a minimum percentage of probe budget always reserved for Safety Probes per model-route slice (default 15%).
   - Priority Buckets: smoke > safety > functional > quant; each bucket has a configured concurrency cap and token cap.
   - Adaptive Rebalancer: every 10 minutes assesses telemetry and shifts share between functional and quant probes using an exponential moving average (EMA) of failure rate and token burn.

3) Cost Smoothing and Throttles:
   - Daily token budget per model name and environment (e.g., prod-shadow: 1.2M tokens/day).
   - Burst window: allow 2x budget for 30 minutes if safety health is green; otherwise capped to 85% of baseline.

Config (defaults):
| setting | default | description |
|---|---:|---|
| safety_floor_pct | "15" | percent of probe budget reserved for safety probes per slice |
| smoke_concurrency | "50" | max concurrent smoke probes per region |
| functional_share_initial | "60" | initial percent share for functional probes |
| quant_share_initial | "10" | initial percent share for quant probes |
| rebalancer_interval_sec | "600" | seconds between rebalancer runs |
| token_budget_prod_shadow | "1200000" | tokens/day for prod-shadow workload |

Mixer algorithm (high level):
1. Gather slice telemetry (failure_rate, p95_latency, token_burn) for the last monitoring window.
2. If any safety sentinel tripped in last window: raise safety priority and pause quant probes for 30 minutes.
3. Compute desired_share for functional vs quant using:
   desired_share_functional = clamp(functional_share_current * (1 + alpha*(failure_rate - target_failure)), 30%, 90%)
   desired_share_quant = 100% - safety_floor_pct - desired_share_functional - smoke_share_estimate
4. Apply smoothing with EMA (beta=0.3) to avoid oscillations.
5. Enforce token budget caps and per-region concurrency constraints.

Telemetrics and SLOs:
- Safety coverage SLO: ">= 99% of canonical sentinels executed per 24h window".
- Probe latency SLO (smoke probes): p95 < 120ms for synthetic network and p95 < 300ms for private deployments.
- Daily token budget utilization alert: > 85% of daily budget for any model-route triggers a cost review.

Integration points and components:
- eval-mixer-service: central mixer that exposes a scheduling API (/v1/mixer/schedule).
- probe-orchestrator: worker pool that accepts schedules and stimulates endpoints.
- telemetry-collector: ingest probe results and emits metrics (aem.safety.tripped, aem.token.burn).
- rollout-controller: listens for regression events and can pause/reduce functional probe share across slices.

API examples:
1) Request schedule (POST /v1/mixer/schedule):
{
  "model": "redwood-v2-13b-quant",
  "route": "chat-completion-prod",
  "window": "10m"
}
Response:
{
  "schedule_id": "sch-2026022011",
  "buckets": {"smoke": 10, "safety": 60, "functional": 120, "quant": 10},
  "token_budget_alloc": 45000
}

YAML config snippet (eval-mixer.yaml):
rebalancer_interval_sec: 600
safety_floor_pct: 15
smoke_share_estimate: 5
functional_share_initial: 60
quant_share_initial: 10
per_model_daily_token_budget:
  redwood-v2-13b-quant:
    prod-shadow: 1200000
    canary: 300000

Operational procedures (runbook):
1) On safety sentinel failure:
   a) Pager to applied-ml oncall.
   b) eval-mixer automatically pauses quant probes for affected slice for 30 minutes.
   c) Triage steps: reproduce with safety probe locally, capture outputs and tokenization diff, annotate in incident ticket.
2) On budget overrun alert (>85%):
   a) Ownership: cost-ops and model-owner.
   b) Short-term mitigation: set functional_share_initial -= 15%, increase rebalancer_interval_sec to 1200s.
   c) Long-term: re-evaluate probe sampling policy and archive low-value probes.
3) On persistent p95 latency regressions for smoke probes:
   a) Route smoke probes to an alternate region (if available).
   b) If region-level degradation persists, mark route degraded and notify SREs.

Validation and Promotion Criteria for New Mixer Rules:
- Unit tests covering rebalancer math and clamp behaviors.
- Integration test: simulated telemetry scenarios (safety trip, burst, cold-start) with deterministic outcomes.
- Shadow rollout for 48 hours against prod-shadow with feature-flag true; require zero safety sentinel regressions and token spend within 110% of expected.

Rollout plan:
1. Canary: enable AEM on 5% of shadow traffic for 48h.
2. Gradual ramp: 5% -> 25% -> 60% -> 100% over 7 days with holds at each step and automated validation checks.
3. Post-rollout audit: QA to run cross-workload fidelity suite and report anomalies.

Ownership and Contacts:
- Primary owner: Maya Patel (Applied ML Infrastructure)
- Secondary: Diego Alvarez (SRE)
- Oncall: eval-harness rotation (see /confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/oncall-contacts)

Related pages:
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/latency-sensitivity-eval-routing-fallback-protocol-2026
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/inference-readiness-eval-matrix-and-promotion-playbook-2026

Appendix: Example telemetry alert mappings (prometheus rules)
- aem_safety_tripped_total > 0 -> pager to applied-ml (severity P1)
- aem_token_budget_ratio > 0.85 -> email to cost-ops (severity P2)

Notes and Future work:
- Explore reinforcement-learning-based mixing for long-lived models with stable telemetry.
- Add per-customer privacy-aware censors for safety probes when running on private deployments.

Document history:
- 2026-01-15 initial draft by Maya Patel
- 2026-02-20 added rollout plan and runbook items (reviewers Diego Alvarez, Lena Hofstad)

title: Probe Artifact Versioning and Reproducibility Manifest
text:
Probe Artifact Versioning and Reproducibility Manifest

Summary
This document defines the artifact model, versioning policy, and reproducibility guarantees for evaluation probes, synthesized test cases, and derived evaluation artifacts used by the Eval Harness. It prescribes manifest formats, CI gating rules, archival tiers, and an operational playbook for diagnosing non-reproducible runs.

Audience
- Applied ML engineers building evaluation probes and test artifacts
- Evaluator maintainers who run scheduled and on-demand evaluation workloads
- SRE and CI owners responsible for eval artifact storage and retrieval

Goals
1. Provide a single canonical manifest format that captures provenance for every eval artifact (probes, annotation sets, priming templates, seed RNG, quant profiles).
2. Make evaluation runs reproducible end-to-end for at least 90% of scheduled regression/eval jobs (exceptions explained below).
3. Reduce time-to-investigation for non-reproducible failures via deterministic manifest-driven replay.
4. Define retention/archival and access controls appropriate for artifact sensitivity.

Key terminology
- Probe: an individual test case or stimulus used to exercise a model (e.g., prompt + metadata + expected slice tag).
- Artifact: any file produced or consumed by eval jobs (probes, annotations, model weights reference, quant profiles, tokenizer snapshots).
- Manifest: a single JSON (see Appendix) that references artifacts, their checksums, seed values, environment hashes, and toolchain versions.
- Reproducible run: an eval execution that, when replayed using the manifest and identical model binary/tag, produces the same scoring outputs within defined stability bounds.

Artifact model and manifest fields
Every artifact must be registered with the registry service and accompanied by a manifest. The manifest fields below are mandatory unless noted as optional. Store manifests alongside artifacts in the eval-asset S3 bucket path prefixed by service/owner/date/manifest.json.

Table: Mandatory manifest fields (ASCII table)
| field | type | description | example |
| manifest_version | string | manifest schema version | v1 |
| artifact_id | string | stable GUID for the probe batch | probe-batch-20241112-7f3a |
| artifacts | list | list of artifact entries (see entry table) | [ ... ] |
| rng_seed | string | PRNG seed used to synthesize stochastic probes (hex) or "none" | 0x9f1a2b |
| tokenizer_snapshot | string | tokenizer version/hash used for tokenization | token-v202405-abc123 |
| toolchain_hash | string | git commit hash of evaluator & adapters used | d4f7c9e |
| environment_os | string | base image identifier | ubuntu-22.04-eval-2024 |
| quant_profile_ref | string (optional) | reference to quantization profile used during shadow/quant runs | quant-profiles/q1-2025 |
| created_by | string | author or pipeline that produced the manifest | eval-factory-v2 |
| checksum | string | SHA256 checksum of the concatenated artifact contents referenced by the manifest | sha256:... |

Table: artifacts entry fields (per artifact)
| name | path | content_type | checksum | role |
| user-intent-probes.json | s3://eval-assets/probes/2024/11/ .. | application/json | sha256:... | probe_batch |
| annotator-csv | s3://... | text/csv | sha256:... | labels |

Versioning policy (short)
1) Use semantic manifest_version (v1, v2) to signal schema changes. 2) Artifact IDs are immutable. If a probe set is modified, produce a new artifact_id and manifest. 3) All artifacts must include a checksum (SHA256) and a human-readable changelog in the manifest metadata. 4) Atomically publish manifests: a manifest is only considered 'promoted' when it is visible in the registry index and the checksum validates against stored objects.

Reproducibility classes and tolerance windows
Not all eval workloads are fully deterministic. We define three reproducibility classes to set expectations:
- Deterministic (class D): Tokenization + model call + deterministic seeds -> bytewise identical outputs expected.
- Stable (class S): Outputs may differ by formatting or whitespace or minor probabilistic variance. We define stability thresholds: e.g., top-1 label agreement >= 98% and scalar metric delta <= 0.5%.
- Non-deterministic (class N): Scenarios that rely on external stochastic components (human-in-the-loop re-rankers, external API augmentations). Acceptable reproduction is manual triage.

Every eval job must declare its reproducibility_class in the job spec. CI will mark runs that do not meet the class target as "repro-check-failed" and attach the manifest and diffs.

Repro checklist (authoring guidance)
- Record and include PRNG seed (or explicit "none" if omitted).
- Snapshot tokenizer and any normalization code. Include pre-tokenization scripts in artifact list.
- Lock evaluator toolchain at a commit hash; include build artifacts or container image digest.
- Avoid external dependency calls during probe synthesis; if unavoidable, capture response corpus as an artifact.
- Annotator assignments: include annotator hash (anonymized), annotation tool version, and sampling policy.

CI/CD integration and gating rules

title: Probe Artifact Versioning and Reproducibility Manifest
section_header: Gating rules for commit pipelines that change probes, evaluator code, or scheduling
text:
[...] 
CI/CD integration and gating rules

Gating rules for commit pipelines that change probes, evaluator code, or scheduling: 
1. Probe author submits artifact PR that includes: artifact files, manifest draft, unit tests for probes (schema validation), and a smoke script to run the probe batch locally.
2. CI validations: schema validation, checksum verification, tokenization smoke-run, lightweight eval against a canned model snapshot (<= 10 examples) to validate end-to-end path.
3. If CI passes, the artifact is staged into the shadow registry (staging prefix) and a promotion job is scheduled to run against the nightly shadow fleet.
4. Promotion policies: 24h shadow runs with coverage targets (see below) must pass to allow artifact_id to be included in production-scheduled evals.

Coverage targets for promotion
- For standard probe batches: >= 95% execution coverage across probe items in shadow runs.
- For long-tail slices: randomized sampling of 5% of items with priority to unique tokens.
- For quant-sensitivity profiles: all probes targeting quant edgecases must execute at least once in a mixed-precision shadow cohort.

Investigating non-reproducible runs: runbook (operator steps)
1. Collect manifest and the run metadata (job id, fleet view, model tag).
2. Validate artifact checksums: run the manifest checksum verification script.
   - CLI: eval-registry verify --manifest s3://.../manifest.json
3. Re-run locally with the manifest pinned to the same toolchain hash and tokenizer snapshot. Use docker image digest if provided.
   - Example: docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/work eval-evaluator:d4f7c9e /work/run_replay --manifest /work/manifest.json --model-tag redwood/gpt-serve:v3
4. If local replay differs, diff output to identify drift: whitespace/tokenization vs semantic. Use the repro-diff tool: repro-diff --left run1.json --right run2.json --thresholds config.yaml
5. If diff indicates tokenizer drift, compare tokenizer snapshots and token counts per prompt. If toolchain mismatch, escalate to evaluator owners. If stochastic variance, check rng_seed and class.
6. Document the triage in the regression tracker with tags: repro:failed, cause:<tokenizer|toolchain|stochastic|external>, mitigation_plan, and attach the manifest.

Archival, retention, and sensitivity tiers
- Tier A (public-safe): non-PII probes and public prompts. Retain 730 days, hot retrieval.
- Tier B (sensitive but internal): contains potential client-specific or sensitive structure. Retain 365 days, warm. Access requires team role binding.
- Tier C (PII/regulated): encrypt-at-rest, HSM-managed keys, retain 90 days unless retention exception approved by privacy team.
Manifests must include "confidentiality_tier" and link to the access control group in the registry.

Operational metrics to track
- Repro success rate per job type (target: 90% deterministic reproducibility for scheduled regression jobs).
- Mean time to detect (MTTD) for non-reproducible runs (target: < 2 hours from job completion to triage start).
- Ratio of manifest-promoted artifacts to total staged artifacts (target: >= 0.8 after three shadow iterations).

Appendix: minimal manifest example (v1)
{"manifest_version": "v1",
  "artifact_id": "probe-batch-20241112-7f3a",
  "artifacts": [
    {"name": "user-intent-probes.json", "path": "s3://eval-assets/probes/2024/11/user-intent-probes.json", "content_type": "application/json", "checksum": "sha256:abcd...", "role": "probe_batch"},
    {"name": "annotators.csv", "path": "s3://eval-assets/annotations/2024/11/annotators.csv", "content_type": "text/csv", "checksum": "sha256:ef01...", "role": "labels"}
  ],
  "rng_seed": "0x9f1a2b",
  "tokenizer_snapshot": "token-v202405-abc123",
  "toolchain_hash": "d4f7c9e",
  "environment_os": "ubuntu-22.04-eval-2024",
  "created_by": "eval-factory-v2",
  "checksum": "sha256:masterchecksum...",
  "confidentiality_tier": "Tier B"
}

Appendix: helper CLI commands
- Register artifact: eval-registry register --manifest ./manifest.json --bucket eval-assets --owner team-evals
- Verify: eval-registry verify --manifest s3://eval-assets/.../manifest.json
- Replay: eval-replay --manifest /tmp/manifest.json --model-tag redwood/gpt-serve:v3 --output /tmp/replay.json

Ownership and contacts
- Primary: Applied ML Eval Platform (team-evals@redwood.ai)
- Secondary: Model Onboarding (model-onboarding@redwood.ai)
- Pager: eng-oncall (for production shadow failures affecting evaluation SLAs)

Change log
- 2024-11-12: v1 initial draft and publication.
- 2025-06-03: Clarified triage steps and added retention tiers.

title: Probe Artifact Versioning and Reproducibility Manifest
section_header: Gating rules for commit pipelines that change probes, evaluator code, or scheduling
text:
[...] - 2024-11-12: v1 initial draft and publication.
- 2025-06-03: Clarified triage steps and added retention tiers.

Related pages and references

title: Tenant-aware Probe Fabricator and Isolation Eval Playbook
text:
Tenant-aware Probe Fabricator and Isolation Eval Playbook

Summary

Purpose: This playbook describes a reproducible process for fabricating tenant-aware synthetic probes and running isolation-focused evaluations in Dedicated and Private deployments.
