# Increase sampling for a slice by 25%
evalctl adjust --model mythic-7b --slice biomedical_qa --scale 1.25

Considerations for quantization and private deployments:

- Quant profiles often change token cost and variance. AEBSAF ingests quant-profile metadata and increases min_samples by 50% when evaluating a new quant profile for the same architecture.
- Private/on-prem runs may have higher infra surcharge; the scheduler may choose to run exploratory samples in hosted environments when privacy constraints allow.

Troubleshooting and common failure modes:

- Symptom: budget exhausted before smoke phase completes => check per-slice sample_capacity and reduce exploratory fraction or raise overall budget.
- Symptom: disproportionate allocation to low-impact slice => verify impact_score normalization and temperature T. Lower T to flatten distribution.
- Symptom: noisy failure_rate with low sample counts => raise min_samples or enable multi-seed aggregation.

Metrics for auditing and continuous improvement:

- Alert-to-resolution time for regression incidents (goal < 48h)
- False-positive triage rate (goal < 10%)
- Coverage of top-10 business slices across promotions (goal: 100%)
- Budget efficiency: detected-regressions-per-1k-units (trend upwards is better)

Appendix A — Statistical guidance:

- Minimum sample formula for binary metrics (approx):
  n = (Z_{1-alpha/2}^2 * p * (1-p)) / effect_size^2
  Use p=baseline pass rate, effect_size = minimum detectable delta (e.g., 0.05), Z=1.96 for alpha=0.05.

Appendix B — Pseudocode for allocation (reference):

function allocate(budget_units, slices):
  for s in slices:
    s.norm_impact = normalize(s.impact_score)
    s.norm_fail = normalize(s.historic_failure_rate)
    s.norm_var = normalize(s.recent_variance)
    s.base = 0.5*s.norm_impact + 0.3*s.norm_fail + 0.2*s.norm_var
  weights = softmax([s.base for s in slices], T)
  for i, s in enumerate(slices):
    s.allocated = max(min_samples, min(max_samples, round(weights[i]*budget_units / s.unit_cost)))
  apply_stage_split(slices)
  return slices

References and related docs:

- See eval harness scheduling guidelines and smoke-and-fidelity-eval-workflow for stage definitions.
- For regression bisect integration, refer to automated-regression-bisect-and-blame-assistant documentation.

Revision history:

- 2025-11-10: Drafted by María González
- 2026-01-22: Incorporated Reviewer feedback: added quant-profile handling and budget cost model (Leah Chen)
- 2026-02-14: Published after pilot with two onboarding flows (Omar Rahman, Priya Iyer)

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration

Summary:
This document defines the Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness (FPSH), an automated evaluation and CI integration protocol for discovering brittle failure modes in production-facing LLM workloads. FPSH focuses on small, semantically plausible input perturbations and adversarial fuzzing applied across prompt templates, prompt variables, context slices, and auxiliary instructions. The goal is to quantify surface-area sensitivity, prioritize failures by user-impact, and provide reproducible triage artifacts for model promotion decisions.

Why this exists:
- Traditional benchmark suites measure average-case behavior; FPSH targets fragile edge-cases and instability that cause regressions post-rollout.
- Integrates with the existing eval harness to produce actionable alerts for model-onboarding, canary rollouts, and regression triage.

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...] - Integrates with the existing eval harness to produce actionable alerts for model-onboarding, canary rollouts, and regression triage.


Key concepts and metrics:
- Perturbation Sensitivity Score (PSS): a weighted fraction of perturbation variants that cause a functional change (e.g., different classification label, broken JSON, hallucination, or unacceptable toxicity). Lower is better. Range 0.0–1.0.
- Latent Failure Index (LFI): composite score combining PSS, semantic severity, and traffic-weighted exposure. Used for prioritization.
- Response Signature Hash (RSH): deterministic hash of normalized response shape (tokenized skeleton, JSON schema keys, classification label) used to detect structural breaks across seeds and quantization variants.

Scope and use cases:
- Pre-promotion stability checks during model-onboarding pipelines.
- Continuous production scan with sampled shadow traffic for high-risk routes.
- Release blocking checks for Dedicated/Private deploys based on configured LFI thresholds.

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...] - Release blocking checks for Dedicated/Private deploys based on configured LFI thresholds.


Architecture overview (high level):
1) Perturbation generator: rule-based + learned transformations (synonym swap, punctuation/noise, case/whitespace, paraphrase back-translation, context injection/removal, token-level bit-flips for quantization sensitivity).
2) Variant scheduler: maps perturbation bundles to seeds, prioritizes by historical failure density and traffic-mix fingerprints.
3) Harness runner: parallel execution across model variants (fp16, int8, q4_0) capturing RSH and full trace.
4) Aggregator: computes PSS/LFI, groups by prompt slice, surface, and function.
5) Triage artifact generator: packages failing examples, diffed responses, token-level alignments, and recommended next steps.

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...] 5) Triage artifact generator: packages failing examples, diffed responses, token-level alignments, and recommended next steps.


Perturbation types (examples):
- Surface-level: punctuation insertion/removal, repeated characters, whitespace injection, emoji insertion.
- Lexical: synonyms, antonyms swap, numerals spelled-out vs digits, unit conversions.
- Structural: clause reordering, sentence splitting/merging, instruction order changes.
- Semantic-preserving paraphrase: back-translation or small-model paraphrase.
- Context-level: remove system instruction, add irrelevant context, swap long-context slices.
- Noise for quantization: token-level bit flips simulated via quantized emulation.

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...] - Noise for quantization: token-level bit flips simulated via quantized emulation.


Sample perturbation spec (JSON/YAML inline example):
- NOTE: when running in the harness this blob is passed as the `perturb_spec` field.

perturb_spec: |
  - bundle_name: brief-punctuation-noise
    transforms:
      - type: punctuation_jitter
        params:
          prob: 0.25
      - type: whitespace_injection
        params:
          prob: 0.15
  - bundle_name: paraphrase-back-translation
    transforms:
      - type: back_translate
        params:
          src: en
          tgt: fr
          repeat: 2

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...]           repeat: 2


How we schedule runs:
1. Baseline: run 50 perturbation variants for each canonical prompt in the production prompt catalog (see prompt-sets-and-benchmarks).
2. Risk-weighted: route larger budgets to high-traffic and high-severity prompt routes (weights derived from token-cost telemetry and QBR exposure lists).
3. Cross-model matrix: run perturbations against candidate model, current prod model, and a quantized variant to capture cross-variant divergences.
4. Re-run failing variants across 3 random seeds and with deterministic KV-cache enabled to rule out non-determinism.

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
section_header: Example CLI and job config snippets
text:
[...] 4. Re-run failing variants across 3 random seeds and with deterministic KV-cache enabled to rule out non-determinism.


CI integration patterns:
- Pre-merge: lightweight smoke with 5 critical perturbation bundles and RSH baseline check.
- Pre-promote (staging): full FPSH run with PSS/LFI report and triage artifact archive attached to the promotion ticket.
- Post-rollout (nightly): sample shadow traffic perturbations for 1% of routes and alert on LFI delta > 0.05.

Example CLI and job config snippets:
- Run one perturbation bundle locally (example):
  $ rfh run --model candidate:v2 --prompt-file prompts/top-10.json --perturb-spec fuzz-bundles/brief-punctuation.yml --out results/candidate-v2-fuzz.json

- Jenkins / GitHub Action job fragment (YAML):
  name: fpsh-pre-promote
  on: workflow_dispatch
  jobs:
    run-fpsh:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
        - name: Checkout
          uses: actions/checkout@v4
        - name: Run FPSH smoke
          run: |
            rfh run --model $MODEL --prompt-file $PROMPT_FILE --budget 50 --report results/summary.json
            rfh report-archive --input results/summary.json --out artifacts/fpsh-archive.zip

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...]             rfh report-archive --input results/summary.json --out artifacts/fpsh-archive.zip


Report format (summary fields):
- model_id: string
- run_id: string
- perturb_bundles_tested: integer
- pss_overall: float
- lfi_priority_buckets: list[string]

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...] - lfi_priority_buckets: list[string]


Prioritization and triage workflow (numbered):
1. Automatically compute LFI per prompt slice and tag failures as critical/high/medium/low. Thresholds: critical LFI > 0.7, high 0.4–0.7, medium 0.15–0.4.
2. For critical failures, attach triage artifact to an auto-created Jira ticket assigned to the model-owner team and notify the oncall via Slack channel #model-regressions.
3. Triage steps (first pass):
   a. Reproduce locally against the exact model version and quantization profile.
   b. Check RSH differences and token-level alignment to determine structural vs content shift.
   c. Run targeted unit tests from the prompt catalog to measure scope.
4. If triage finds quantization-related divergence, escalate to the quantization-owner with the quant-diff artifact and run the adaptive-quant-eval subroutine.
5. Close with remediation tag (prompt-fix, model-change, routing-rule, or accept-mask) and update the LFI record when fixed.

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...] 5. Close with remediation tag (prompt-fix, model-change, routing-rule, or accept-mask) and update the LFI record when fixed.


Operational SLOs and thresholds:
- CI smoke run start-to-report: < 20 minutes for 5 bundles.
- Full pre-promote run: < 6 hours for up to 5000 perturbation variants (parallel target).
- Nightly shadow scan: completion within 2 hours for sampled routes.
- Alerting: LFI delta > 0.05 or any critical failure triggers paging to model-oncall.

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...] - Alerting: LFI delta > 0.05 or any critical failure triggers paging to model-oncall.


Storage and artifacts:
- All runs emit a results archive: JSON lines of request/perturbation/response/RSH and a summary JSON with aggregated metrics.
- Archives are stored in s3://redwood-eval-archives/<env>/<run_id>/ and indexed in the eval-metadata DB for query.
- Triaged failure artifacts include: original prompt, perturbation recipe, raw responses, token alignments, and recommended fix.

Example triage artifact fragment (textual description):
- prompt_id: route-invoice-summarize-v3
- perturbation: paraphrase-back-translation-bundle-3
- observed: assistant returned free-form explanation instead of JSON schema {lines, totals}
- rsh_diff: schema-key-drop
- recommended_action: add schema-enforcement to prompt wrapper and add unit test to prompt-catalog

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...] - recommended_action: add schema-enforcement to prompt wrapper and add unit test to prompt-catalog


Guidance for model-onboarding teams:
- Include FPSH in the promotion checklist and set the acceptable PSS target per workload class (e.g., conversational agents PSS < 0.12; structured-output routes PSS < 0.05).
- For Dedicated/Private customers, require an LFI review meeting for any LFI classified high+ prior to promotion.
- Document any accepted LFI exceptions with compensating controls (e.g., route-level fallback to a more robust model).

Monitoring and dashboards (what we provide):
- PSS heatmap by prompt-slice and model variant.
- LFI trendline per service across promotions and quantization changes.
- RSH divergence explorer: filter by structural-change, content-shift, or stochastic-variance.

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...] - RSH divergence explorer: filter by structural-change, content-shift, or stochastic-variance.


Ownership and run cadence:
- Owners: Applied ML (evaluation) team owns the harness code and aggregator. Model owner is responsible for triage and fix.
- Cadence: Pre-promote runs triggered per candidate; nightly shadow scans for high-risk routes; ad-hoc runs for post-incident forensic.

Appendix A — Example failure classification rules (simplified):
1. Structural break: RSH indicates missing/extra schema keys -> classify as structural.
2. Semantic flip: classification label changed with confidence delta > 0.25 -> semantic flip.
3. Hallucination: named-entity mismatch against ground-truth with BLEU-like penalty -> hallucination flag.

Appendix B — Integration checklist for repo owners:
- Add a light FPSH job to your CI (5 bundles) and set it to non-blocking by default.
- Configure a pre-promote job that runs full FPSH against the candidate model.
- Ensure model metadata is registered with the eval-metadata DB and that the artifact s3 path is accepted by the aggregator.
- Subscribe the appropriate Slack channels for triage notifications.

Appendix C — Example perturbation-transform (pseudo-code):
- punctuation_jitter(text, prob=0.2):
    for char in text:
      if random() < prob:
        insert one of [".", ",", "!", "?", "..."] at random nearby index

title: Fuzz + Perturbation Sensitivity Harness and CI Integration
text:
[...]         insert one of [".", ",", "!", "?", "..."] at random nearby index


Contacts and escalation:
- Primary: Asha Menon (applied-ml-evals)
- Quantization owner: Priya Ramesh
- SRE oncall: follow oncall rotations in eng-sre/runbooks

References and related pages:
- See prompt catalog and evaluation portfolio for canonical prompt lists.
- For quantization triage, see the quantization-profiles playbooks and adaptive-quant-eval subroutine.

Last reviewed: 2026-02-03 by Martin Kline.

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide

Summary:
This guide describes the probe orchestration and telemetry design for evaluating multimodal models on long‑context workloads. It covers synthetic probe generation, traffic stitching (real + synthetic), telemetry event schema, annotation guidance, privacy constraints, and a step-by-step orchestration playbook for staging -> shadow -> production evaluation. The goal is repeatable, auditable evals that reveal subtle long‑context regressions without significantly impacting production latency or costs.

Scope and audience:
- Audience: applied ML engineers, evaluation engineers, model onboarding owners, and SREs who run shadow/replica evals.
- Scope: multimodal inputs (text + images, video frames, document pages), sequence lengths from 2k to 200k tokens/frames, and workloads that exercise cache/KV behavior and prefix reuse. Not a model training guide.

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
[...] - Scope: multimodal inputs (text + images, video frames, document pages), sequence lengths from 2k to 200k tokens/frames, and workloads that exercise cache/KV behavior and prefix reuse. Not a model training guide.


Motivation and objectives:
- Detect quality regressions that only appear with long coherent context or multimodal grounding (e.g., reference tracking across thousand‑token spans).
- Measure worst‑case and average latency, KV cache miss effects, and token‑level fidelity for long context.
- Provide reproducible telemetry that supports causal triage (see regression triage playbooks).

Definitions:
- Probe: an input (text + optional media) designed to exercise a specific failure mode (forgetting, hallucination, coherence loss, cache invalidation).
- Stitch: the process of combining real customer traffic with synthetic probes in a way that preserves request distribution properties for evals.
- Shadow run: live inference of candidate model behind production routing, with outputs logged but not surfaced to users.

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
[...] - Shadow run: live inference of candidate model behind production routing, with outputs logged but not surfaced to users.


Probe generation pipeline (high level):
1. Collection and slicing:
   - Collect anonymized long‑context sessions from consenting customers or synthetic simulators.
   - Slice sessions into anchors (prefixes) and probes (target continuations) with metadata: anchor_length, last_accessed_kv_size, media_count, modality_mix.
2. Synthesis and augmentation:
   - Generate synthetic continuations using calibrated LLMs with varying temperature and instruction templates to cover edge behaviors.
   - Insert controlled perturbations: coreference swaps, repeated entity aliases, injected contradictions, and repeated image references.
3. Prioritization and sampling:
   - Score probes by risk (semantic drift probability), rarity (frequency in corpus), and resource impact (expected token cost).
   - Create evaluation batches balancing high‑risk low‑frequency probes with representative samples.
4. Annotation:
   - Human labelers annotate ground truth for intent, expected entity continuity, acceptable tolerance windows, and failure tags (e.g., misattribution, drop-off, hallucination).

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
section_header: Telemetry event schema (single flat JSON line per event)
text:
[...]    - Human labelers annotate ground truth for intent, expected entity continuity, acceptable tolerance windows, and failure tags (e.g., misattribution, drop-off, hallucination).


Telemetry event schema (single flat JSON line per event):
- event_id: string (UUID)
- probe_id: string
- session_anchor_hash: string (HMAC with eval key)
- model_version: string
- route: string (internal route id)
- input_length_tokens: integer
- input_media_count: integer
- context_kv_size_bytes: integer
- start_timestamp: ISO8601
- end_timestamp: ISO8601
- latency_ms: integer
- tokens_generated: integer
- cache_hit_ratio: float (0.0-1.0)
- failure_tags: list[string] (may be empty)
- output_digest: string (sha256 of normalized output)
- annotation_verdict: string (match|partial|mismatch|not_applicable)
- annotation_notes: string
- cost_usd_estimate: float

Table: recommended thresholds and alerting rules
| Metric | Warning threshold | Critical threshold | Alert owner |
| Latency 95th | > 1200 ms | > 2000 ms | eng-serving-runtime |
| Cache hit ratio (median) | < 0.6 | < 0.4 | eng-serving-runtime |
| Mismatch rate (slice) | > 3% | > 7% | applied-ml |
| Token generation cost (avg) | > $0.015/token | > $0.03/token | finance-legal |

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
[...] | Token generation cost (avg) | > $0.015/token | > $0.03/token | finance-legal |


Annotation and labeling guidelines:
- Labelers must be provided the anchor, probe, and model output side‑by‑side.
- Use the tolerance window concept: a model can satisfy a probe if the required entity/intent appears within N tokens/frames (N determined per probe type).
- Assign failure_tags using the canonical taxonomy: {forgetting, misattribution, contradiction, hallucination, truncation, multimodal-mismatch}.
- For multimodal references, require linkability: the output must reference the correct image/frame id from the anchor.

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
[...] - For multimodal references, require linkability: the output must reference the correct image/frame id from the anchor.


Privacy and data handling:
- All session_anchor_hash values must be HMACed with an evaluation key stored in the secrets manager; do not store raw PII.
- Synthetic probe generation must not be seeded with customer‑owned textual content unless explicit consent exists.
- Telemetry retention: raw events retained for 30 days in hot storage, 365 days in compressed audited archive. Access requires applied-ml-team role.

Shadow orchestration playbook (stepwise):
1. Prepare candidate probe set and priority lanes (critical, representative, low-risk).
2. Create a shadow deployment: route 0.1-1% of mirrored traffic to shadow cluster for a 24‑48h smoke test.
3. Run combined replay: for heavy long‑context probes, use replay worker to flood the shadow cluster with synthetic sequences at controlled throughput to exercise KV cold/warm states.
4. Collect telemetry and annotations asynchronously; do not add blocking to live requests.
5. Run automated checks: mismatch rate per slice, latency degradation per model_version, cache behavior drift.
6. If checks pass, expand to 5–10% traffic with extended probes for 7–14 days.
7. Final promotion gating: pass SLOs, reviewer signoff (applied‑ml + SRE), and privacy checklist.

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
[...] 7. Final promotion gating: pass SLOs, reviewer signoff (applied‑ml + SRE), and privacy checklist.


Traffic stitching and replay constraints:
- Stitching must preserve inter‑arrival time distributions for representative lanes; synthetic bursts allowed for stress lanes but tagged as such.
- Maintain separate telemetry tags: stitched=true|false, synthetic=true|false, stress_test=true|false.
- Use replay windows to avoid violating customer rate limits or user‑facing SLAs.

Quality gates and rollbacks:
- Hard gates: 95p latency regression > 20% on representative lanes triggers immediate rollback.
- Soft gates: mismatch rate increase > 0.5% triggers investigation workflow but may not auto-rollback.
- Rollback procedure: revert route config, mark model_version as quarantined, create regression ticket with probe references and primary telemetry digests.

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
[...] - Rollback procedure: revert route config, mark model_version as quarantined, create regression ticket with probe references and primary telemetry digests.


Operational considerations and ownership:
- Ownership: applied-ml owns probe catalog and annotation quality. eng-serving-runtime owns runtime telemetry and alerting. eng-sre coordinates shadow routing and safe rollout windows.
- SLOs for eval infra: telemetry ingestion 99.9% within 30s, probe replay worker availability 99% monthly.

Troubleshooting quick list:
- If cache_hit_ratio drops unexpectedly: check KV eviction logs, recent infra changes, and quantized kernel rollouts.
- If annotation backlog grows: spin up labeler elastic workers, throttle synthetic generation, and prioritize critical lanes.
- If output_digest collision rate spikes: confirm normalization pipeline and dedup heuristics.

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
[...] - If output_digest collision rate spikes: confirm normalization pipeline and dedup heuristics.


Examples and snippets:
- Telemetry ingestion CLI (example):
  rinf telemetry ingest --source replay-worker --file probes_batch.json --env staging

- Minimal synthetic probe JSON (single line example):
  {"probe_id":"probe-20241115-001", "anchor":"[doc pages 1-14] ...", "continuation_instruction":"Answer with the list of referenced figures and their captions."}

- Example analysis query (pseudo-SQL):
  SELECT probe_id, model_version, AVG(latency_ms) as p95_latency, SUM(failure_tag='forgetting') as forgetting_count
  FROM telemetry_events
  WHERE stitched=false AND synthetic=false AND start_timestamp >= '2025-01-01'
  GROUP BY probe_id, model_version

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
[...]   GROUP BY probe_id, model_version


Release notes and iteration plan:
- v1.0 (2024-11): baseline probe types, telemetry schema, and shadow playbook.
- v1.1 (2025-01): added multimodal linkability checks, cost_estimate fields, and privacy hardening.
- Next: integrate automatic root cause attribution hooks with regression bisect tool.

Appendix: canonical probe taxonomy (short):
- Coherence-lapse: long-range entity consistency failures.
- Multimodal-mismatch: incorrect image/frame grounding.
- KV-thrash: performance and correctness failures caused by KV cache churn.
- Intent-drift: model shifts intent interpretation mid-session.

title: Multimodal Long‑Context Probe Orchestration and Telemetry Guide
text:
[...] - Intent-drift: model shifts intent interpretation mid-session.


Contacts and escalation:
- Applied ML oncall: applied-ml-oncall@redwood.ai
- Eng serving runtime: runtime-ops@redwood.ai
- Evaluation owner: Maya Thompson (maya@redwood.ai)

Related documents and references:
- See eval mesh and workload fingerprinting for scheduling ties.
- See regression triage playbooks for incident investigation linkage.

Document history:
- 2024-11-15: created by Maya Thompson
- 2025-01-08: expanded telemetry schema and added release notes

title: Zero-Trust Networking and Observability Playbook for Private Deploys
text:
Zero-Trust Networking and Observability Playbook for Private Deploys

Summary:
This playbook describes a hardened, repeatable pattern for private Redwood Inference deployments that combines zero-trust network controls, telemetry-first observability, and an explicit canary-based upgrade & rollback procedure. It is intended for VPC-based and on-premises private deployments (customer-managed control plane or hybrid), and covers preflight checks, network policy templates, metrics/tracing requirements, alert thresholds, canary rollout choreography, and post-rollback remediation steps.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to: Private deployments (VPC, connected colos, and on-prem clusters) running Redwood control plane + serving runtime.
- Not covered: hosted API multi-tenant deployments, experimental developer sandboxes.
- Audience: SRE/Platform, Private Deployments engineering, Customer Success (handoff), and Security reviewers.

Goals:
- Enforce least-privilege communication between services (zero-trust) while preserving performance for inference workloads.
- Provide deterministic observability (metrics, traces, logs) for rollout decisions and postmortem analysis.
- Standardize canary upgrade / rollback steps to limit blast radius and enable fast recovery.

Ownership and SLAs:
- Network controls and policy templates: eng-private-deployments team (Primary: Anna Kim).
- Observability and alerts: eng-platform (Primary: Thomas Keller).
- Canary orchestration & runbook authoring: eng-serving-runtime (Primary: Sanjeev Patel).
- SLO target during rollouts: 99.9% availability for control-plane RPCs, p50 token latency degradation < 20% versus baseline.

Prerequisites (pre-flight checklist):
1) Inventory and baseline:
   - List of cluster endpoints, subnets, and NAT gateways.
   - Baseline latency and token-per-second (TPS) metrics for each model route captured for 72 hours.
   - KMS/HSM access validated, customer KMS key ARN present for encryption-at-rest.
2) IAM & identities:
   - Service identities created and mapped to least-privilege roles.
   - TLS certificates issued and rotated frequently (<= 90 days) for internal mTLS.
3) Observability hooks installed:
   - Prometheus scraping targets, OTLP traces ingestion, and centralized log forwarder (ELK/EFK or Splunk) confirmed.
4) Canary capacity reserved:
   - At least 10% of capacity reserved for canary traffic or isolated node pool with identical instance types.

Zero-Trust Network Pattern (high level):
- Enforce mTLS between all Redwood services and sidecars (control-plane, router, runtime executor).
- Explicit allowlists (not blacklists): only defined service-to-service flows are permitted.
- Short-lived credentials: use ephemeral tokens via Vault or cloud-native STS where supported.

Network policy template (Kubernetes NetworkPolicy example):
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-controlplane-to-runtime
  namespace: redwood-runtime
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: runtime-executor
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          redwood-role: control-plane
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 7070

Notes:
- Apply equivalent rules for egress restrictions where host-level packet filters are required on-prem. Avoid overly broad CIDR ranges (prefer security-group-per-service pattern).

Service Mesh and Proxy considerations:
- Use sidecar proxies (Envoy) only if the latency budget allows; for extremely latency-sensitive model inference, prefer lightweight mTLS without full mesh features.
- If deploying service mesh, enable observability filters (access logs, upstream clusters) and limit retries on inference paths to 0 or 1 to avoid duplicate token costs.

Observability requirements (must-have signals):
- Metrics (Prometheus):
  - request_count{route,model,variant,status_code}
  - token_generation_rate{route,model}
  - p50/p95/p99 latency per route (generation and total end-to-end)
  - kv_cache_hit_ratio and batch_size_distribution
  - GPU_utilization, GPU_memory_free, host_cpu_usage
- Traces (OTel):
  - End-to-end trace spanning API gateway -> control plane -> runtime executor with span attributes: model_id, request_id, token_count, hardware_type
  - Sampling: 100% for errors, 1-5% for success requests (adjust for cost)
- Logs:
  - Structured logs with request_id correlation and log-level mapping (info/warn/error). Include deterministic fields for repro (model_checksum, config_hash).

Dashboard & alerting baseline:
- Canary dashboard tiles:
  - Canary_p50_latency_vs_baseline, Canary_error_rate, Canary_token_cost_delta, Canary_throughput
- Critical alerts (examples):
  - Alert: control_plane_rpc_error_rate > 1% for 5m -> Severity: P1 -> Notify oncall + escalation to eng-serving-runtime.
  - Alert: canary_p95_latency > baseline_p95 * 1.5 for 10m -> Severity: P1 -> Pause rollout and trigger rollback.
  - Alert: kv_cache_hit_ratio < 0.6 for 10m -> Severity: P2 -> Investigate cache warmup or slot misconfiguration.

Prometheus alert rule example (YAML snippet):
- alert: CanaryHighLatency
  expr: (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="runtime", env="canary"}[5m])) by (le))) > (scalar(histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="runtime", env="baseline"}[5m])) by (le))) * 1.5)
  for: 10m
  labels:
    severity: page
  annotations:
    summary: "Canary p95 latency > 1.5x baseline"

Canary upgrade choreography (recommended pattern):
1) Prepare canary image/config in isolated node pool.
2) Run smoke tests against canary control-plane endpoints (liveness, model list, key retrieval) — pass must be 100%.
3) Inject synthetic traffic at low TPS (1-2% of normal) with real prompt shapes and measure: latency, error rate, token cost delta, model output quality (if available).
4) If metrics within thresholds for 30 minutes proceed to 10% production traffic for 60 minutes.
5) Run evaluation harness on a reserved prompt set (sliced by top routes) to detect quality regressions.
6) If still green, ramp to 50% for 30 minutes, then full rollout with automated tapering of the canary node pool.

Rollout thresholds (fail-fast):
- Error rate increase > 0.5 percentage points absolute over baseline for 10m.
- p95 latency regression > 30% for 10m.
- Token cost increase > 10% sustained for 15m without quality improvement evidence.
- Eval harness quality drop (BLEU/ROUGE/embedding-similarity) beyond threshold defined in product route policy.

Rollback procedure (step-by-step):
1) Pause traffic routing system (gateway/mesh) to stop further canary traffic shifts.
2) Re-target affected routes to the previous stable revision. If rollout used node pools, cordon/drain canary pool and uncordon stable pool.
3) Confirm traffic has migrated back and monitor the primary SLOs for 15 minutes.
4) Capture forensic artifacts: call traces, last 100 error logs, sample prompts (sanitized), and config diffs.
5) Open a post-rollback incident with required owners and begin RCA within 24 hours.

Day-2 operational playbook snippets:
- Scaling up for load spikes:
  - Step 1: Observe GPU_utilization > 80% sustained for 10m and backlog growing -> trigger scale policy to add N nodes (N based on model mix).
  - Step 2: Monitor per-node queue latency; if latency improves, increase autoscaler cooldown to 3m.
- Key rotation for private KMS:
  - Precondition: rotation window approved by customer.
  - Steps: rotate KMS key in test environment -> validate decrypt/encrypt operations for runtime -> schedule rotation in maintenance window (low traffic) -> rotate in prod with canary decryption test.
- Telemetry disruption mitigation:
  - If OTLP ingestion fails, fall back to local disk buffer (configurable) and alert SRE. Ensure retention window for buffered telemetry is at least 24 hours.

Hardware and minimal network requirements (table):
- CPU per runtime node: 32 vCPU
- RAM per runtime node: 256 GiB
- GPU: A100 or H100 family recommended (customer can supply equivalent), GPU interconnect: NVLink recommended for multi-GPU models
- Network: 25 Gbps NIC minimum per node for inference clusters
- Storage: 2 TB NVMe local for model shards and KV cache (redundant replication recommended)

(As flat JSON this table is represented as lines)

Acceptance and handoff checklist for Customer Success:
- Signed operational contract specifying SLAs and support windows.
- Customer-run preflight report attached showing baseline TPS, latency, and model usage matrix.
- Mutually agreed escalation path and oncall rotation with contact information.

Examples and troubleshooting tips:
- Symptom: rising token cost with no latency change -> check batch size distribution and batching configuration; a sudden drop in batch size often increases per-token overhead.
- Symptom: intermittent RPC timeouts during rollout -> verify network-policy egress rules and sidecar connection timeouts; consider increasing TCP keepalive and reducing proxy connection pooling limits.

Post-incident RCA requirements (if rollback triggered):
- Timeline of events with 1-minute granularity for the window +/- 30 minutes around rollback.
- Top 10 error traces and a sample of 50 requests (sanitized) demonstrating regression.
- Config diff between baseline and canary, and clear action items to remediate.

References and related artifacts:
- Deployment templates: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/airgap-packaging-and-day2-hardening-2026
- Canary traffic generator (internal tool): repo: infra/canary-generator (see README for usage and synthetic prompt sets)

FAQ (short):
Q: "Can I run full service mesh and still meet <50ms p50 latency for small models?"
A: You may, but validate with a synthetic baseline; full mesh introduces CPU/latency overhead — for sub-50ms SLAs prefer lightweight mTLS.

Q: "What level of telemetry sampling do we recommend for cost-sensitive customers?"
A: Sample 100% of errors, 3% of successes, increase to 10% on canaries.

Appendix: Quick CLI snippets
- Check canary status (kubectl):
  kubectl -n redwood get pods -l env=canary -o wide
- Force rollback to revision v1.2.3 (example):
  redwoodctl rollout set image --service=runtime --image=registry.redwood/v1.2.3 --namespace=redwood-runtime

Document maintenance:
- This playbook should be reviewed quarterly or after any production rollback. Major changes to the serving runtime or the underlying networking model must trigger a review and re-validation of the canary thresholds.

Owners and escalation contacts:
- eng-private-deployments: Anna Kim (primary), sanjeev.patel@redwood.ai (oncall contact)
- eng-serving-runtime: Sanjeev Patel
- eng-platform (observability): Thomas Keller

title: Human-in-the-Loop Eval Orchestration and Reviewer Calibration Playbook
text:
Human-in-the-Loop Eval Orchestration and Reviewer Calibration Playbook

Overview

This playbook describes the orchestration pattern, operational runbooks, and quantitative reviewer calibration procedures for human-in-the-loop (HITL) evaluation in Redwood’s eval-harness. It complements automated probes and synthetic workloads by defining safe, reproducible processes for:
- recruiting and onboarding reviewers (internal and vendor)
- run criteria for calibration passes and acceptance thresholds
- scheduling and cost-aware batching of human judgements
- tooling integrations (Annotation UI, Reviewer Portal, Eval Orchestrator, Telemetry pipeline)

Intended audience
- Applied ML engineers, evaluation owners, labeling stewards, and SREs responsible for eval orchestration and quality guardrails.

Goals and success metrics
- Maintain inter-annotator reliability (Krippendorff alpha) >= 0.70 on primary annotation tasks.
- Keep average label latency <= 48 hours for priority probes, <= 7 days for exploratory probes.
- Ensure reviewer accuracy versus gold sets >= 92% for core binary judgments.
- Contain human-eval costs within budget: target $0.20–$2.00 per probe depending on complexity.

Scope and exclusions
- Applies to annotation workflows that require human judgement: safety, hallucination, instruction-following grading, multi-turn coherence.
- Does NOT replace large-scale external annotation procurement policies (see procurement and vendor-management).

Architecture and integrations
- Eval Orchestrator: schedules probes, assigns to reviewer queues, tracks metadata (probe id, model variant, seed, fingerprint).
- Probe Fabric: generates and signs probe bundles for HITL lanes (includes provenance metadata: generation time, model, seed, quant profile).
- Annotation UI: web app used by reviewers. Implements structured tasks, forced-choice labels, rich-text justification, and confidence slider.
- Reviewer Portal: reviewer profiles, performance dashboard, golden-set exposure, and retraining modules.
- Telemetry Ingest: events for label submission, time-to-first-action, QA flags, and rework rates.

Key definitions
- Probe: a single eval input (prompt + context) dispatched for human judgement.
- Golden set: a curated set of probes with high-agreement labels used for calibration.
- Calibration pass: a scheduled evaluation of reviewers using a golden set to measure agreement and accuracy.
- Tiered reviewer: reviewers assigned tiers (T1 internal, T2 trusted vendors, T3 crowd) with different gating policies.

Reviewer recruitment and tiering
1) T1 (Internal SMEs): Redwood employees or contractors with domain expertise. Used for high-sensitivity safety probes and final adjudication.
2) T2 (Trusted vendors): Vendor teams with ongoing SLAs; must pass quarterly calibration.
3) T3 (Crowd/temporary): Quick triage or exploratory labeling; require multiple redundant judgments and lower trust weight.

Onboarding checklist (minimum)
- ID verification, NDA, and role assignment.
- Two dry-run tasks with feedback and coach review.
- Minimum accuracy on golden set: 90% for T2, 95% for T1.
- Access provisioned to Reviewer Portal and Annotation UI; SSO and RBAC configured.

Calibration lifecycle and schedule
- Weekly micro-calibration: 10 golden probes pushed to all active reviewers in a lane; automated scoring and feedback.
- Monthly formal calibration: 100-quality probes, cross-reviewed, recorded in reviewer history. Failure to meet thresholds triggers remediation.
- Pre-launch gating: any new probe family or prompt-set requires a mandatory calibration pass across two reviewer cohorts.

Calibration metrics and thresholds
- Krippendorff alpha (nominal): >= 0.70 for production lanes.
- Reviewer gold accuracy: >= 92% for T2, >= 95% for T1.
- Inter-decoder consistency (pairwise F1): >= 0.85 for multi-label tasks.
- Rework rate (percentage of judgments flagged for adjudication): < 7%.

Calibration remediation actions
- Soft warning: coach message + next micro-calibration within 48 hours.
- Suspension: if golden accuracy < 80% across two consecutive micro-calibrations, suspend from production lanes.
- Retraining: mandatory retraining session, followed by an unlocked golden-set re-eval.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Eval Orchestration and Reviewer Calibration Playbook
text:
[...] - Suspension: if golden accuracy < 80% across two consecutive micro-calibrations, suspend from production lanes.
- Retraining: mandatory retraining session, followed by an unlocked golden-set re-eval.

Annotation workflow patterns and cost controls
- Priority lanes: small batches (N=5–20) with T1/T2 reviewers and fast turnaround.
- Exploratory lanes: larger batches, T3 reviewers, 3x redundancy, consolidated adjudication by T1 when disagreement > 30%.
- Cost-aware batching: Eval Orchestrator evaluates probe complexity (estimated time) and groups probes per reviewer to target 30–40 minutes per session.
- Dynamic redundancy: increase redundancy for low-agreement slices automatically (e.g., when running Krippendorff < 0.65 for a slice).

Data and privacy considerations
- PII scrubber: all probes pass through the PII filter before presenting to human reviewers unless explicit customer consent exists.
- Data retention: labels and justifications retained 2 years by default; redaction applied per data-management policy.
- Access control: reviewer roles and lane access enforced via RBAC; audit logs forwarded to Audit Logging service.

Runbooks and step-by-step procedures
1) Launching a HITL evaluation for a new prompt family
   a) Create a probe bundle in Probe Fabric with provenance metadata (model, seed, quant profile).
   b) Register the bundle in Eval Orchestrator and tag lane: {lane: safety, priority: high, redundancy: 3}.
   c) Run pre-launch calibration: push 50 golden probes to a pilot cohort. Accept only if Krippendorff >= 0.70 and gold accuracy >= 92%.
   d) If pass, schedule live run with incremental ramps: 5%, 25%, 100% traffic split over 48 hours. Monitor disagreement and rework metrics.

2) Emergency triage for sudden drop in agreement
   a) Alert from Telemetry: slice Krippendorff < 0.60 or rework rate > 12%.
   b) Pause new probe dispatch to live lanes via Eval Orchestrator (cli: evalctl suspend-lane --lane safety).
   c) Dispatch investigation pack: 200 recent probes + 50 golden probes to internal SMEs for adjudication.
   d) Apply rollback or prompt-rewrite recommended by SME adjudication.

CLI / API examples
- Submit probe bundle (YAML payload shown as example):
  ---
  bundle_name: user-instruction-lane-v3
  model_variant: open-llama-13b-q4
  probes_source: s3://eval-buckets/probes/2026/01/
  lane_tags: ["safety","priority-high"]
  redundancy: 3
  golden_set_id: golden-safety-2026-01
  schedule: immediate
  ---
  Command: curl -X POST https://internal-eval-orch/api/v1/bundles -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' -d @bundle.yaml

- Suspend a lane: evalctl suspend-lane --lane safety && evalctl resume-lane --lane safety --when-stable

Adjudication and dispute resolution
- Triage: any probe with >= 2 disagreeing labels is sent to adjudication.
- Adjudicators: T1 SMEs perform final judgment. Adjudication decisions feed back into the golden set.
- Dispute SLA: adjudication complete within 72 hours for priority lanes.

Observability and dashboards
- Reviewer health dashboard: golden accuracy, calibration trend, average label latency, rework rate, per-lane cost.
- Slice-level view: per-prompt-family Krippendorff, redundancy heatmap, majority-label drift.
- Alerts: telemetry rules configured in Console:
  - ALERT: safety-lane-krippendorf-critical if Krippendorff < 0.60 for 30m
  - ALERT: reviewer-suspend if individual golden accuracy < 80% for 2 micro-calibrations

Failure modes and mitigation
- Reviewer fatigue: detect via increased lateness and declining gold accuracy; reduce batch sizes and schedule cooldown.
- Golden set leakage: rotate golden sets monthly and monitor for sudden accuracy spikes.
- Adjudication backlog: implement surge adjudication windows and prioritize backlog by probe criticality.

Operational ownership and contacts
- Evaluation Orchestrator owner: eng-evals-team@redwood.com (primary: Marco Chen)
- Annotation UI owner: dx-annotation@redwood.com (primary: Lina Patel)
- Reviewer Operations: labeling-ops@redwood.com (primary: Omar Ruiz)

title: Human-in-the-Loop Eval Orchestration and Reviewer Calibration Playbook
text:
[...] - Annotation UI owner: dx-annotation@redwood.com (primary: Lina Patel)
- Reviewer Operations: labeling-ops@redwood.com (primary: Omar Ruiz)

Appendix: golden set construction guidelines
- Source: mix of historic production failures, synthetic adversarial probes, and SME-curated cases.
- Size: minimum 500 probes per major lane; balanced across classes and difficulty buckets.
- Refresh cadence: quarterly or after major model/prompts changes.

Appendix: example calibration reporting snippet (CSV)
- reviewer_id,cohort,gold_accuracy,krippendorff,avg_latency_minutes,rework_rate
- r-102,T2,0.94,0.72,32,0.03
- r-209,T3,0.87,0.63,78,0.12

Related procedures and references
- See /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/eval-cost-slice-prioritization-and-resource-budgeting-2026 for budgeting ties.
- See /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention for data retention rules.

Change log
- 2026-01-15: Created initial playbook (Aisha Reynolds).
- 2026-02-10: Expanded remediation procedures and added CLI examples (reviewers: Marco Chen, Lina Patel).

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models

Summary:
This playbook defines a reusable set of synthetic workload profiles and a stress-test protocol for validating inference correctness, latency SLOs, cost-accuracy tradeoffs, and quantization stability across Redwood Inference runtimes (hosted, dedicated, private). It is intended for applied ML engineers, runtime owners, and SREs who run large-scale pre-production and canary tests.

Purpose and goals:
- Provide a canonical library of synthetic traces that emulate real customer workloads (short chat, long-compose, retrieval-augmented prompts, embedding bursts).
- Surface regressions caused by quantization (INT8/INT4), KV-cache interactions, batching and kernel selection.
- Verify latency and tail behavior under sustained load and cold-cache scenarios.
- Standardize pass/fail criteria and triage steps for regression alerts.

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
[...] - Standardize pass/fail criteria and triage steps for regression alerts.


Audience:
- Applied ML engineers onboarding new models or quantization profiles.
- Runtime and serving teams validating kernel optimizations.
- SRE and test-automation teams running nightly stress suites.

Prerequisites and test environment:
1) Test cluster: dedicated test pool named `stress-test-pool` (A100/80GB or A10G depending on profile).
2) Services: `inference-service`, `runtime-proxy`, `batcher`, and `metrics-exporter` running with test tags.
3) Dataset seeds: production-sampled seed files in `s3://redwood-evals/seeds/2026/` (obfuscated customer ids).
4) Canary model artifacts: model vX.Y quantized variants (FP16, INT8, INT4) stored in model registry with tags `canary` and `quant-test`.

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
[...] 4) Canary model artifacts: model vX.Y quantized variants (FP16, INT8, INT4) stored in model registry with tags `canary` and `quant-test`.


Test profiles (table):
| profile_id | short_name | description | typical_seq | QPS | target_95p_latency |
|-----------:|------------|-------------|------------:|----:|------------------:|
| P1 | chat-short | Multi-turn chat with short replies, token lengths 8-64 | 8-64 tokens | 100 qps | <= 120 ms |
| P2 | compose-mid | Single-turn compose with 256-token output distribution | 64-256 tokens | 30 qps | <= 300 ms |
| P3 | long-context | Long context (news article ~2048 tokens) with KV cache stress | 1024-4096 tokens | 5 qps | <= 1.2 s |
| P4 | embed-burst | Embedding bursts: 512-dim embeddings, small inputs | 1-32 tokens | 500 qps | <= 80 ms |
| P5 | mixed-realtime | Mixed traffic: 70% P1, 20% P2, 10% P4, respecting burstiness | variable | 150 qps | P1-95p <= 150 ms |

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
[...] | P5 | mixed-realtime | Mixed traffic: 70% P1, 20% P2, 10% P4, respecting burstiness | variable | 150 qps | P1-95p <= 150 ms |


Profile generation rules:
- Use production distribution percentiles: sample prompt lengths from P0/P25/P50/P75/P95 distributions.
- Inject content diversity: include code, tables, conversational tokens, and multilingual samples in 12% of traces.
- Cache state simulation: for P3 run with a warm cache (90% KV hit) and a cold-cache run (0% KV hit) to measure delta.

Config template (YAML) for harness (example):
```yaml
profile: P3
model: open-mistral-v1-canary
variant: int8
duration: 1800 # seconds
qps: 5
concurrency: 20
seed_file: s3://redwood-evals/seeds/2026/long-context-seed.jsonl
headers:
  X-Test-Run: synthetic-stress-2026-02
metrics_exporter: prometheus://metrics-exporter:9090
```

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
[...] ```


Execution steps (canonical run):
1) Provision `stress-test-pool` and ensure cluster autoscaling disabled for test.
2) Deploy canary model variant to `inference-service` with tag `canary/stress/2026-02`.
3) Start `metrics-exporter` and validate Prometheus scrape targets: `batcher`, `runtime-proxy`, `inference-service`.
4) Run baseline FP16 P1-P5 profiles for 30 minutes each, capture APM traces and tokens breakdown.
5) Run quantized variants (INT8, INT4) with identical seeds; run both warm-cache and cold-cache modes.
6) Run long-duration soak (>= 3 hours) for P5 mixed-realtime profile to measure degradation patterns.
7) Upload artifacts: raw responses, latency histograms, token counters, and cost model inputs to `s3://redwood-evals/results/<run-id>/`.

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
section_header: Key metrics to collect (labels map to Prometheus metrics)

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
[...] - runtime_fallback_count_total (counter)


Pass/fail and alert thresholds:
- Functional correctness: BLEU/ROUGE/stability checks against FP16 baseline. Fail on > 3% drop in semantic consistency (task-dependent).
- Latency: 95th percentile must be within 20% of FP16 baseline for the same profile and hardware OR within the absolute thresholds in the profile table.
- Error budget: if runtime_fallback_count_total increases by > 2x baseline across a run, mark as failed.
- Cost delta: unit token cost projected > 1.4x compared to FP16 indicates regression for cost-sensitive tiers; flag for product review.

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
[...] - Cost delta: unit token cost projected > 1.4x compared to FP16 indicates regression for cost-sensitive tiers; flag for product review.


Triage playbook (ordered):
1) Reproduce: re-run failing profile with same run-id and extended logging enabled.
2) Check metrics: examine kv_cache_hit_ratio, batch_size_distribution, and gpu_utilization. Low KV hit or smaller batch sizes indicate workload skew.
3) Kernel mismatch: inspect runtime logs for kernel selection warnings (tagged `kernel-selector.warn`). If present, escalate to serving-runtime team.
4) Quantization degradation: run small targeted eval suite (100 deterministic prompts) to compute semantic drift. If drift concentrated on numeric or code outputs, tag as `quant-numbering-regression`.
5) Fallback & OOM: if runtime_fallback_count_total > threshold, collect heap profiles and GpuMem logs, open internal ticket `RUNTIME-FTB-<run-id>`.
6) Communication: add regression summary to the nightly evaluation digest and notify `#applied-ml-evals` with subject `STRESS-FAIL <profile> <model> <run-id>`.

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
[...] 6) Communication: add regression summary to the nightly evaluation digest and notify `#applied-ml-evals` with subject `STRESS-FAIL <profile> <model> <run-id>`.


Ownership and cadence:
- Test owner: applied-ml team (owner contact: evelyn.cho@redwood.ai).
- SRE support: eng-sre on-call for `stress-test-pool`.
- Nightly runs: automated P1-P3 smoke every night at 02:00 UTC. Full synthetic stress suite (all profiles) runs weekly on Saturday 03:00 UTC.
- Gate for rollout: all canary model releases require a successful synthetic stress run against P1-P5 before traffic migration.

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
[...] - Gate for rollout: all canary model releases require a successful synthetic stress run against P1-P5 before traffic migration.


Automation and artifact retention:
- Harness lives in `repo: infra/eval-harness` under `synthetic-profiles/`.
- Results retained for 90 days in S3; summarized metrics pushed to TSDB.
- CI integration: Pull requests that modify quantization profiles must reference a `stress-test-run-id` demonstrating non-regression.

Interpreting results (examples):
- Scenario A (INT8 shows +15% 95p latency only for P3 cold-cache): likely KV-cache alignment or slower INT8 kernel for long sequences. Next step: run `kernel-benchmark --seq 2048 --variant int8` and compare micro-benchmarks.
- Scenario B (INT4 shows semantic drift on code snippets but latency OK): revert INT4 for canary and open issue against `applied-ml/quantization` for targeted post-training calibration.

Appendix A — Useful CLI snippets:
- Start run: `python tools/run_synthetic.py --config configs/P3-int8.yaml --out s3://redwood-evals/results/$(date +%s)-p3/`
- Collect traces: `kubectl logs -l app=inference-service -n stress-test --since=2h > inference-service-logs.gz`
- Query metrics example: `promql: rate(request_duration_seconds_sum{profile=\"P1\",model_variant=\"int8\"}[5m])`

Appendix B — Known limitations and future work:
- Current seeds underrepresent streaming token-by-token patterns used by low-latency SDKs; plan to add P6 streaming profile Q3 2026.
- Does not currently simulate multi-tenant noisy neighbors; a separate noisy-neighbor harness is scheduled for H2 2026.

title: Synthetic Workload Profiles and Stress Test Protocol for Inference and Quantized Models
text:
[...] - Does not currently simulate multi-tenant noisy neighbors; a separate noisy-neighbor harness is scheduled for H2 2026.


Related pages and references:
- See quantization stability audit playbook for KV-cache specific guidance.
- See serving-simulation-eval-harness for microbench orchestration examples.

Change log:
- 2025-09-10: Initial draft and canonical profile list (E. Cho).
- 2025-11-02: Added P5 mixed-realtime and cost gating rules (M. Ruiz).
- 2026-02-15: Updated pass/fail thresholds and added INT4 semantic-drift guidance (A. Patel).

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
text:
Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration

Summary
-------
This playbook defines a probe-scheduling strategy and orchestration pattern for running mixed-precision (MPX) evaluation workloads across the eval-harness. It centralizes how probes (synthetic requests, prompt slices, and adversarial cases) should be scheduled, prioritized, and routed to candidate model/quantization variants so we can detect quality regressions and quant-degradation early without overspending GPU cycles.

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
text:
[...] This playbook defines a probe-scheduling strategy and orchestration pattern for running mixed-precision (MPX) evaluation workloads across the eval-harness. It centralizes how probes (synthetic requests, prompt slices, and adversarial cases) should be scheduled, prioritized, and routed to candidate model/quantization variants so we can detect quality regressions and quant-degradation early without overspending GPU cycles.


Goals
-----
- Detect regressions introduced by mixed-precision and quantization changes within 24 hours of a rollout candidate being staged.
- Prioritize probes that historically surface degradations while preserving sampling diversity to catch new failure modes.
- Keep incremental compute overhead <= 12% of production-like dedicated shadow capacity.
- Provide deterministic scheduling outputs for reproducible triage and automated alerts.

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
text:
[...] - Provide deterministic scheduling outputs for reproducible triage and automated alerts.


Scope
-----
Applies to: eval-harness orchestration, model-onboarding shadow deployments (hosted, dedicated, private), quantization profiles used by production-serving. Excludes: offline batch-only research experiments and one-off ad hoc label collections.

Definitions
-----------
- Probe: a single test case derived from the prompt catalog (single- or multi-turn) with metadata (slice, expected behavior, anchor model output).
- MPX candidate: a model variant that uses mixed-precision kernels (e.g., int8+f16 hybrid) or a quantized artifact targeted for private/dedicated deployment.
- Probe Farm: the eval-harness pool service that schedules probes to worker lanes.
- Priority Score: numeric score used by scheduler to order probes; see algorithm below.

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
text:
[...] - Priority Score: numeric score used by scheduler to order probes; see algorithm below.


High-level Architecture
-----------------------
1. Probe Catalog (Prompt Sets): persisted in S3 + metadata in the eval database. Each probe has tags: slice, risk_score, cost_estimate, anchor_hash, last_seen_failure.
2. Scheduler Service (probe-scheduler): computes a daily schedule manifest for each MPX candidate.
3. Orchestrator (eval-orch): reads manifests and executes probes on the shadow fleet, collecting telemetry to ingestion topic eval-telemetry-prod.
4. Analyzer (probe-analyzer): computes signal (response stability index, semantic-embedding divergence, length-normalized token-delta) and writes alerts to PagerDuty and regression-triage queue.

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
section_header: We categorize probes into 4 types with default target proportions for a daily run
text:
[...] 4. Analyzer (probe-analyzer): computes signal (response stability index, semantic-embedding divergence, length-normalized token-delta) and writes alerts to PagerDuty and regression-triage queue.


Probe Types and Weighting
------------------------
We categorize probes into 4 types with default target proportions for a daily run:
- High-risk regressors (15%): known slices that have produced past regressions (high precision recall).
- Random coverage (30%): uniformly sampled to detect unknowns.
- Adversarial perturbations (20%): syntactic and semantic noise variants.
- Long-context & KV-cache sensitive (35%): multi-turn sequences emphasizing cache stability.

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
section_header: Priority score formula (used to sort queue)
text:
[...] - Long-context & KV-cache sensitive (35%): multi-turn sequences emphasizing cache stability.


Priority score formula (used to sort queue):
priority = clamp(0.0, 1.0, 0.5 * normalized_risk + 0.3 * age_factor + 0.2 * impact_estimate)
Where: normalized_risk in [0,1] from probe metadata; age_factor = min(days_since_last_run/30,1); impact_estimate derived from upstream product usage weight.

Scheduling Algorithm
--------------------
1. For each MPX candidate, fetch last-run outcomes and compute a reweight table for probes.
2. Reserve N high-priority slots (N = ceil(0.12 * daily_budget_slots)) for probes with last_seen_failure = true.
3. Fill remaining slots by sampling per target proportions with stratified sampling across languages, device types, and traffic cohorts.
4. Annotate schedule with preferred lane (gpu-v2, cpu-sim, or quant-sim) and estimated token-cost.
5. Persist manifest and start execution window.

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
text:
[...] 5. Persist manifest and start execution window.


Operational Runbook: daily execution
-----------------------------------
1. probe-scheduler triggers at 02:00 UTC and emits manifests per candidate.
2. eval-orch pulls manifests and runs probes over a 2-hour window. Long-context probes are rate-limited to spread memory pressure.
3. probe-analyzer runs streaming checks and posts signals:
   - Response Stability Index (RSI) drop > 0.07 -> create regression triage ticket (severity P2).
   - Embedding Cosine Drift > 0.12 on median of slice -> add to regression queue.
   - Token Delta > 20% on top-k token distribution -> flag for quantization failure-mode.
4. If more than 5 distinct probes fail automatic gates for the same candidate, automatically set deployment status to HOLD and notify Model Onboarding channel (@model-onboard).

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
text:
[...] 4. If more than 5 distinct probes fail automatic gates for the same candidate, automatically set deployment status to HOLD and notify Model Onboarding channel (@model-onboard).


SLOs and Alert Thresholds
-------------------------
- Coverage: At least 80% of manifest probes must complete successfully within the 2-hour window.
- Latency: median probe execution latency <= 800ms per probe on gpu-v2 lane.
- Detection window: regressions must be detected and a ticket created within 6 hours of detection.

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
text:
[...] - Detection window: regressions must be detected and a ticket created within 6 hours of detection.


Gates and Automated Actions
--------------------------
- Soft Gate: RSI drop between 0.03–0.07 -> create a low-priority regression ticket; schedule follow-up re-run with expanded adversarial cases.
- Hard Gate: RSI drop > 0.07 or Embedding Drift > 0.12 -> set candidate to HOLD and notify oncall and onboarding owners.
- Emergency Fallback: If an MPX candidate is promoted to dedicated and field telemetry indicates correlated errors, the Routing Service will fallback to the anchor model for 1 hour while triage proceeds.

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
text:
[...] - Emergency Fallback: If an MPX candidate is promoted to dedicated and field telemetry indicates correlated errors, the Routing Service will fallback to the anchor model for 1 hour while triage proceeds.


Promotion Checklist (for MPX candidate)
--------------------------------------
- [ ] All scheduled probes pass soft gates in two consecutive daily runs.
- [ ] No hard gate triggers in the last 7 days.
- [ ] Token-cost delta within expected budget +/- 8%.
- [ ] Shadow-to-field calibration run completed (see 'shadow-rollforward' playbook).
- [ ] Owners and validators listed in onboarding ticket have signed off.

title: Probe Scheduling for Mixed-Precision Evaluation Orchestration
text:
[...] - [ ] Owners and validators listed in onboarding ticket have signed off.

reserved_high_risk: 150

Config table: scheduler tunables
--------------------------------
| key                         | description                                    | default  | notes |
|-----------------------------|------------------------------------------------|----------|-------|
| daily_budget_slots          | total probe executions per candidate per day  | 1200     | scales with candidate priority |
| reserved_failure_slots_pct  | percent reserved for recent failures          | 12       | set to 0 for experimental runs |
| long_context_rate_limit_ms  | per-probe cooldown for long-context probes    | 1500     | avoid GPU memory thrash |
| sample_seed                 | deterministic seed for sampling               | 42       | increment per run for variance |

[...] | sample_seed                 | deterministic seed for sampling               | 42       | increment per run for variance |


Telemetry and Metrics (collected per-probe)
--------------------------------------------

- tokens_in, tokens_out, wall_ms, gpu_mem_peak_mb
- response_hash, embedding_vector_id, rsi_score, token_delta_percent
- annotations: human_label (if escalated), triage_ticket_id

Triage Flow and Responsibilities
--------------------------------

- Deep Triage: Applied ML (Regression Squad) -> reproduce with debug flags, compare kernel traces, KV cache traces.
- Ops Action: If a resource bottleneck is suspected (gpu_mem_peak_mb > threshold 90%), engage eng-infra to reprofile.

section_header: Appendix: common failure modes and remediation
text:
[...] aws s3 cp s3://eval-manifests/mpq-model-v3-int8-hybrid/2025-02-10/manifest.yaml -


Appendix: common failure modes and remediation
-----------------------------------------------
1) Token distribution collapse (spike in token_delta_percent)
   - Likely cause: quant kernel top-k instability.
   - Remediation: tighten quantization calibration, run quant-sim with higher precision for top-k.
2) Embedding semantic shift
   - Likely cause: fused kernel numerical drift or tokenizer mismatch for rare unicode.
   - Remediation: run tokenizer normalization pass, add targeted probes for affected Unicode ranges.
3) Intermittent OOM on long-context probes
   - Likely cause: batch packing + long context combinatorics.
   - Remediation: reduce pack size for long-context lane and increase long_context_rate_limit_ms.
