Ownership and contacts:
- Doc owner: Daniela Kim (Applied ML, Eval Infrastructure)
- Primary contact: Marcus Li (applied-ml-eval oncall)

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026

Summary
-------
This playbook defines an operational pattern and a concrete implementation template for adaptive probe retargeting: the automated process by which our eval harness reassigns probe traffic (synthetic and seeded probes) across workload slices in response to telemetry. The goal is to concentrate evaluation budget on emerging hotspots, reduce wasted probes on stable slices, and recover coverage for rare long-tail slices without human manual scheduling.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] This playbook defines an operational pattern and a concrete implementation template for adaptive probe retargeting: the automated process by which our eval harness reassigns probe traffic (synthetic and seeded probes) across workload slices in response to telemetry. The goal is to concentrate evaluation budget on emerging hotspots, reduce wasted probes on stable slices, and recover coverage for rare long-tail slices without human manual scheduling.


Scope
-----
Applies to: eval-harness orchestration (probe scheduler), probe-farms, offline-to-online continuity, and the telemetry ingestion pipeline.
Not covered: manual annotation workflows, external customer experiments (those follow customer-specific guardrails).

Motivation
----------
- Telemetry shows that a small subset of slices (10% of slices) account for >60% of quality alerts; static probe schedules under-sample these hotspots.
- Manually adjusting probe targets is slow and reactive.
- We need a closed-loop that uses signals (error-rate, response-stability-index, embedding-drift, latency anomalies) to retarget probes autonomously and transparently.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] - We need a closed-loop that uses signals (error-rate, response-stability-index, embedding-drift, latency anomalies) to retarget probes autonomously and transparently.


Principles
----------
- Safety-first: never divert >40% of total probe budget without human signoff for the first 24h of a retargeting policy change.
- Explainability: all retarget decisions must be logged with rationale and signal deltas for auditing and triage.
- Reversibility: every retarget event must include an automatic rollforward and rollback TTL with checkpoints.
- Budget-awareness: respect slice-level and global token/credit budgets.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
section_header: Signals and thresholds used for retargeting
text:
[...] - Budget-awareness: respect slice-level and global token/credit budgets.


Signals and thresholds used for retargeting
-----------------------------------------
- Quality alerts (QA): aggregated score where QA >= 0.7 triggers high-priority candidate.
- Response Stability Index (RSI): RSI drop of >0.12 over 6h for a slice marks it for review.
- Embedding Drift (cosine): mean embedding shift > 0.08 vs baseline for a week.
- Latency tail (p95/p99): p99 increase > 2x baseline AND p95 > 1.5s.
- Error budget burn rate: slice-level SLO burn > 3x expected.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] - Error budget burn rate: slice-level SLO burn > 3x expected.


Priority tiers (example)
----------------------
1. Tier A — immediate retarget: QA >= 0.7 OR RSI drop + error-burn.
2. Tier B — increased sampling: embedding drift sustained 48h OR p99 latency bump.
3. Tier C — exploratory probe seeding: low-traffic slices with sporadic failures or new data patterns.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] 3. Tier C — exploratory probe seeding: low-traffic slices with sporadic failures or new data patterns.


Retargeting procedure (operational steps)
---------------------------------------
1. Signal aggregation (automated): telemetry ingestion groups signals by slice and computes delta vs baseline windows (1h, 6h, 7d).
2. Candidate generation (automated): rule engine emits a ranked list of slices with suggested delta in probe allocation (e.g., +5%, +20%).
3. Safety checks (automated): budget guardrails and cross-slice collision detector run. If budget overcommit, scale via soft-throttling rules.
4. Dry-run & audit snapshot (automated): scheduler simulates retarget for 30 minutes with shadow metrics and writes a decision record.
5. Soft-retarget deployment (canary) (automated): apply change to 10% of probe-farm capacity with TTL=6h.
6. Monitoring window (automated + human): monitor quality and cost metrics. If metrics improve continue ramp; if regress beyond rollback thresholds, automatic rollback occurs.
7. Full rollout (automated with human signoff after first 24h if large changes): ramp to desired allocation.
8. Documentation & annotation (human): owner annotates the change in the decision log and links root-cause or contextual notes.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] 8. Documentation & annotation (human): owner annotates the change in the decision log and links root-cause or contextual notes.


Rollback and failure modes
-------------------------
- Immediate auto-rollback triggers:
  - Quality delta worsens by >10% relative to pre-retarget within 1h.
  - Token spend increases by >30% without measurable improvement in QA.
  - Unauthorized budget exceedance.
- Manual rollback: Oncall or Eval-Owners can initiate rollback via the eval CLI: `redwood-evalctl rollback --event-id <id>`

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] - Manual rollback: Oncall or Eval-Owners can initiate rollback via the eval CLI: `redwood-evalctl rollback --event-id <id>`


Retention and audit trail requirements
------------------------------------
- Decision records for retarget events must be persisted for 365 days with the following fields: event_id, timestamp, slices_affected, allocation_delta, signals_snapshot, initiating_rule, operator (system or user), dry_run_metrics, rollout_status, rollback_trigger_reason.
- Logs must be correlated with trace IDs from the scheduling service for triage.

Example scheduler config (YAML)
-----------------------------
| field | meaning | example |
|-------|---------|---------|
| policy_name | Friendly name for the retarget policy | "adaptive-hotspot-retarget-v1" |
| max_budget_pct | Maximum percent of total probe budget allowed for retarget ops | 30 |
| initial_canary_pct | Percent of capacity used for the first soft-retarget | 10 |
| rollback_thresholds | JSON-encoded thresholds for automatic rollback | '{"quality_pct_drop":10, "cost_pct_increase":30}' |

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] | rollback_thresholds | JSON-encoded thresholds for automatic rollback | '{"quality_pct_drop":10, "cost_pct_increase":30}' |


YAML snippet:
```
policy_name: adaptive-hotspot-retarget-v1
max_budget_pct: 30
initial_canary_pct: 10
rollout_steps:
  - canary: 10
  - ramp: 30
  - full: 100
enabled_signals: ["qa_score","rsi","embedding_drift","p99_latency","slo_burn_rate"]
```

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] ```


SQL telemetry examples
----------------------
- Top candidate slices by QA delta (example):
```
SELECT slice_id, avg(qa_score) as qa_1h, avg(qa_score) OVER (PARTITION BY slice_id ORDER BY ts ROWS BETWEEN 7 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) as qa_7d
FROM eval_telemetry
WHERE ts >= now() - interval '1 hour'
GROUP BY slice_id
ORDER BY qa_1h - qa_7d DESC
LIMIT 100;
```

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] ```


CLI examples
------------
- Dry-run a retarget plan and produce a decision record:
```
redwood-evalctl propose-retarget --policy adaptive-hotspot-retarget-v1 --dry-run --output decision-12345.json
```
- Apply a small retarget change (controlled):
```
redwood-evalctl apply-retarget --event decision-12345.json --canary 10
```

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] ```


Ownership and roles
-------------------
- Signal owners: Observability team (alerts & collection) — ensures signal quality and baseline windows.
- Scheduler owner: Eval orchestration team — implements the rule engine and rollout logic.
- Cost guardrail owner: Finance-ML partnership — enforces budget limits and approval flows.
- Oncall: Eval-oncall — authorized to manual rollback.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] - Oncall: Eval-oncall — authorized to manual rollback.


Operational metrics to track (dashboards)
--------------------------------------
- Probe efficiency: fraction of probes that detect actionable regressions per 1,000 probes.
- Retarget impact: delta in QA and RSI within 6h, 24h, 7d.
- Cost delta: token spend attributable to retarget actions.
- False-positive rate: proportion of retarget events that caused no meaningful triage within 7d.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] - False-positive rate: proportion of retarget events that caused no meaningful triage within 7d.


Safety review checklist (pre-deployment)
-------------------------------------
1. Policy has a max_budget_pct <= 30%.
2. Decision engine includes at least two orthogonal signals (example: QA + embedding drift).
3. Dry-run produces a decision record with no rollbacks flagged.
4. Observability dashboards and alerts are linked in the decision record.
5. Finance approved if expected cost delta > $500/day.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
section_header: Example retrospective template (fill after each retarget event)
text:
[...] 5. Finance approved if expected cost delta > $500/day.


Example retrospective template (fill after each retarget event)
--------------------------------------------------------------
- Event ID:
- Initiating signal(s):
- Rationale summary: 
- Was the retarget successful? (yes/no/mixed)
- Key metrics improved/worsened (6h/24h/7d):
- Cost impact:
- Root cause discovered: 
- Follow-ups and owners:

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] - Follow-ups and owners:


References and related playbooks
-------------------------------
- Eval lifecycle & private/dedicated integration playbook (link in related_pages).
- Probe-farm orchestration for rare regressions (see regression-triage folder).
- Prompt coverage matrix and drift repair guide.

title: adaptive-probe-retargeting-telemetry-feedback-playbook-2026
text:
[...] - Prompt coverage matrix and drift repair guide.


Appendix: small implementation notes
-----------------------------------
- Use decoupled components: signal-collector -> rule-engine -> scheduler -> probe-farm. Each component must emit the event_id to the event-log.
- Keep the rule-engine deterministic and versioned: store rule-versions with semantic versioning.
- Provide a readonly UI for decision review: columns must include signal deltas, suggested allocation delta, canary plan, and cost estimate.

Contact
-------
For questions or to propose a policy change, ping the eval-orchestration channel or email eval-platform@redwood.com.

title: Quantization Shadow Fidelity Simulator and Regression Hypothesis Bundle
text:
Quantization Shadow Fidelity Simulator and Regression Hypothesis Bundle

Overview

This document describes the Quantization Shadow Fidelity Simulator (QSFS) — a lightweight, modular evaluator used by the eval-harness to predict behavioral deltas introduced by quantized model artifacts before production rollout — and the accompanying Regression Hypothesis Bundle (RHB) for structured triage if the simulator surfaces risk. QSFS is intended for use during model-onboarding, quantization-profile generation, and pre-rollout canary design.

Why we built QSFS

- Reduce time and cost: simulate large-scale quantized behavior deterministically on small reference workloads rather than running full shadow fleets.
- Improve triage velocity: provide prioritized hypotheses and signal extraction to accelerate root cause identification for regressions tied to quant artifacts.
- Standardize handoffs: produce an artifact (RHB) consumed by regression teams, SRE, and model owners with uniform fields and triage runbooks.

Scope and non-goals

- In-scope: sequence-level fidelity simulation for text generation and reranking models (causal and encoder-decoder), token-level divergence metrics, prompt-conditioning sensitivity, and KV-cache interaction emulation.
- Out-of-scope: full GPU kernel performance estimation, detailed latency/throughput microbenchmarks, or end-to-end production traffic replay (use shadow-fleet or production mirror for that).

High-level architecture

title: Quantization Shadow Fidelity Simulator and Regression Hypothesis Bundle
section_header: QSFS consists of three coordinated modules
text:
[...] High-level architecture


QSFS consists of three coordinated modules:

1) Probe Fabricator: selects and prepares workload slices (priming context, few-shot examples, edge-case seeds).
2) Quantized Emulator: runs a quantized-inference deterministic emulator that applies quant noise models and cache-decay approximations to a floating-point baseline.
3) Analyzer & Hypothesis Composer: produces fidelity metrics, diffs, and structured hypotheses (RHB).

Each module can be executed independently in the eval-harness pipeline or as a single command. Ownership: eval-harness team owns the code; applied-ml stewards the probe catalog.

Probe Fabrication and Selection

- Inputs: workload fingerprint, slice definitions, probe budget, and tenant constraints.
- Strategy: stratified sampling across context lengths (short/medium/long), priming density (0/1/5 shots), and token-entropy buckets.

title: Quantization Shadow Fidelity Simulator and Regression Hypothesis Bundle
text:
[...] - Strategy: stratified sampling across context lengths (short/medium/long), priming density (0/1/5 shots), and token-entropy buckets.


Probe types generated:

- Baseline-stability probes: produce deterministic seeds used for consistency checks.
- Quant-stress probes: perturbations (token swap, suffix truncation, numeric precision stressors).
- Edge semantic probes: adversarially generated prompts targeting critical functionality (e.g., code generation, instruction-following).

Simulator configuration (table)

| parameter | description | default | notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| quant_profile | identifier for quant recipe (e.g., q4f, q8a) | q4f-default | maps to quantization-profiles catalog |
| seed_set | probe catalog id | small-core-100 | use larger for release candidate |
| kv_cache_modeling | none | "approximate" | options: none, approximate, exact-sim |
| temperature | sampling temp for stochastic probes | 0.0 | 0.0 forces deterministic sampling |
| rerun_count | number of emulator passes per probe | 3 | aggregate variance budget |
| divergence_threshold | alert-level for token-level divergence | 0.06 | used to generate RHB alerts |

Execution procedure (step-by-step)

1. Prepare context: map service-level workload fingerprint (from eval-mesh) to seed_set and override priming where necessary.
2. Run floating-point baseline (FP32 or BF16) to generate canonical outputs and internal trace (logits, top-k tokens per step).
3. Run Quantized Emulator against the same probes. If kv_cache_modeling=="exact-sim", emulate cache reads/writes; else apply cache-decay model.
4. Compute fidelity metrics: token-divergence (KL and token-mismatch rate), response-syntactic-distance (BLEU/ROUGE heuristics), and functioncall-structure-delta (for tool-calling).
5. If any metric breaches configured thresholds, auto-generate an RHB and escalate per policy.

Fidelity metrics and thresholds (examples)

- Token mismatch rate: fraction of sample tokens not in FP baseline top-1 (Alert if > divergence_threshold).
- KL over logits: mean KL across steps (Alert if > 0.18).
- Response structure flip: % of probes where functioncall schema or JSON validity changed (Alert if > 0.02).
- Latent-behavior drift: embedding cosine drift of final states (Alert if > 0.12).

Regression Hypothesis Bundle (RHB) schema

The RHB is a standardized artifact produced as a JSON-LD (consumed by issue trackers and triage dashboards). The key fields listed below must be present in the bundle; simplified here for human review.

Table: RHB fields and triage actions

| field | example | triage owner | action on fail |
|---|---|---|---|
| title | "q4f -> q4f-v2: functioncall fidelity regression on payments prompts" | model-owner | assign model regression ticket |
| probe_ids | ["seed-123","seed-54"] | applied-ml | reproduce locally |
| primary_signal | "token-mismatch-rate" | applied-ml | run targeted stress probes |
| delta_snapshot | "/artifacts/q4f/v2/compare-2026-02-10" | SRE | pull logs & kernel traces |
| top_hypotheses | ["quant-rounding-bias", "kv-cache-loss"] | applied-ml | prioritize by score |
| suggested_mitigations | ["increase precision for attention kernels", "use cache-stabilizer rewrite"] | model-owner | patch & re-run QSFS |
| impact_estimate | "0.6% requests downgraded for payments flow" | product | decide rollout delay |
| blocker_flag | "true" | release_coord | block rollout if true |

Hypothesis scoring and prioritization

- Each hypothesis scored by: signal-strength (0–1), reproducibility (0–1), surface-risk (0–1). Composite score = weighted sum (0.5*signal + 0.3*repro + 0.2*risk).
- Triage guidelines: focus first on hypotheses with composite score > 0.6 and reproducibility > 0.7.

Automated escalation rules

- If blocker_flag==true OR top_hypothesis composite score > 0.8 -> auto-create high-priority regression incident and tag SRE/model-oncall.
- If token-mismatch-rate > 2x divergence_threshold across critical probes -> notify product & customer-success.

Integration points with existing pipelines

title: Quantization Shadow Fidelity Simulator and Regression Hypothesis Bundle
text:
[...] 
Integration points with existing pipelines

- eval-harness: QSFS is a pluggable stage in pre-promotion pipelines. Use the eval-harness adapter to surface metrics to the eval-mesh and metrics-store.
- quantization-profiles catalog: QSFS reads canonical quant recipes; results write back suggested profile adjustments.
- regression-triage: RHBs are posted to the regression queue with labels: quant-shadow, simulator, auto-rhb.

CLI examples

title: Quantization Shadow Fidelity Simulator and Regression Hypothesis Bundle
section_header: Run a quick smoke sim against a small seed set
text:
[...] CLI examples


# Run a quick smoke sim against a small seed set
redwood-qsf simulate --profile q4f-default --seed-set small-core-100 --kv-cache-modeling approximate --temperature 0.0 --runcount 3 --out-dir /tmp/qsf/run-042

# Generate RHB and upload to triage queue
redwood-qsf package-rhb --in /tmp/qsf/run-042 --title "q4f-v2 smoke" --upload --notify applied-ml

Promotion checklist (pre-release gate)

1. QSFS run completed with rerun_count >= 3 for release-candidate seeds.
2. No metric breaches OR documented mitigations in RHB.
3. Mitigation verification run shows reduction in relevant signals by >= 50%.
4. RHB accepted by model owner and SRE.
5. Release coordination updated (if blocker_flag true, release blocked until root cause resolved).

Operational roles and SLAs

- QSFS owner: eval-harness team. Response SLA for triage requests: 24 business hours.
- Model owner: respond to RHB assignments with initial ack within 8 business hours.
- Applied-ML: investigative owner for hypotheses with reproducibility >= 0.7.

Failure modes and mitigation patterns (short list)

- Non-reproducible drift (low reproducibility score): increase rerun_count and switch kv_cache_modeling to exact-sim.
- Cryptic embedding drift: run embedding-level saliency probes and per-layer quant-activation histograms.
- Functioncall schema flips: add precision guard for decoder logits and widen functioncall-token threshold.

Appendix A — Example RHB snippet (human-readable)

{
  "title": "q4f-v2: rerank drift for billing prompts",
  "primary_signal": "token-mismatch-rate",
  "divergence": 0.083,
  "top_hypotheses": ["attention-scale-truncation","quantized-softmax-loss"],
  "suggested_mitigations": ["upgrade attention kernel to q8a for critical layers","apply softmax smoothing"]
}

Note: the actual RHB artifact is a structured JSON-LD object; the snippet above is for readability in this doc.

Related pages and references

- Quantization profile catalog and naming conventions: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quant-profile-synthesis-and-safety-playbook-2024
- Shadow-to-prod continuity guidelines: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/shadow-to-prod-eval-continuity-guidelines-2026
- Regression triage playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/causal-regression-investigation-handbook-2026

Change history

- 2025-11-12: Draft created (Maya Patel).
- 2025-12-15: Incorporated Evan Chen feedback: added kv_cache_modeling modes.
- 2026-02-10: Published: added automated escalation rules and RHB schema (Sofia Ramos review).

Contact and feedback

For questions, suggested improvements, or to add a seed set to the QSFS catalog, open a ticket in the eval-harness board or DM @Maya.Patel on internal chat.

title: Tokenization Robustness Testbed and Operational Procedures
text:
Tokenization Robustness Testbed and Operational Procedures

Overview

This page documents the Tokenization Robustness Testbed: a repeatable, automated evaluation harness and operational playbook for detecting, triaging, and preventing tokenization and byte-level drift across model onboarding, quantization, and runtime upgrades. The testbed focuses on mismatches between expected token sequences and runtime tokenization behavior, errors introduced by tokenizer-regression after merges, and failure modes that cause silent semantic changes (e.g., whitespace-sensitivity, Unicode normalization, byte-order-mark handling).

Why this matters

- Tokenization changes are a frequent, low-signal source of downstream quality regressions (classification label shifts, hallucination triggers, changed prompt completions).
- Quantized kernels and low-level runtime changes can subtly change token boundary behavior; these are often missed by standard semantic-only evals.
- For enterprise customers operating in regulated domains, tokenization determinism is part of certification and audit.

Scope and non-goals

- In scope: tokenizer behavior across model variants and runtime kernels, tokenizer + quantizer interactions, cross-locale Unicode normalization, and token-level privacy filters.
- Out of scope: overall semantic evaluation (covered by regular eval harness), dataset labeling policies, and user-facing prompt rewriting features.

Architecture and components

- eval-orchestrator: schedules runs, stores artifacts to S3-compliant storage (service: eval-runner).
- token-fuzzer: generator of edge-case input (emoji sequences, ZWJ clusters, bidi text, BOM prefixed inputs, mixed normalization).
- tokenizer-checker: deterministic reference tokenizer (pinned to model-catalog version) used as ground truth for token sequence comparisons.
- runtime-prober: executes tokenization inside the redwood-runtime and quantized kernels (service: simserv).
- analyzer: computes metrics, diffs, and triage signals, pushes alerts to Prometheus/Alertmanager and creates Jira tickets if thresholds cross.

Test definitions and canonical metrics

- Tokenization Mismatch Rate (TMR): fraction of inputs where runtime token sequence != reference token sequence. Alert threshold: 0.5% on a 10k sample (SLO: <0.1%).
- Boundary Shift Count (BSC): average number of tokens with different boundaries per mismatched input. Alert if mean BSC > 1.2 for failing sample set.
- Normalization Drift Rate (NDR): percent of inputs whose normalized form differs between reference and runtime normalization. Alert: >0.2%.
- Byte-Order Anomalies (BOA): count of inputs that gain or lose nonprinting bytes (BOM, ZWJ). Any nonzero BOA in production branch triggers immediate rollback candidate.

Config and requirements (reference)

| Parameter | Value | Rationale | Owner |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Sample size per run | 10,000 | Balanced sensitivity vs cost | Applied ML infra |
| Fuzzer variants | 24 families | Unicode, whitespace, markup, escaped JSON, long-repetition | Applied ML team |
| Run cadence | nightly + pre-merge on mainline | Detect regressions early and nightly drift | Eng-SRE |
| Retention | 90 days artifacts, 365 days metadata | For triage and audit | Data-Platform |

Test families (examples)

1. Unicode normalization bank: NFD vs NFC, mixed combining marks, sequenced modifiers.
2. Whitespace and delimiter stress: repeated spaces, nonbreaking spaces, soft hyphens.
3. Markup-edge cases: HTML entities, nested tags, incomplete XML.
4. Embedding/escape combos: JSON-escaped text, serialized protobuf fragments.
5. Long-tail concatenations: repeated rare tokens to stress KV cache and quant boundaries.
6. Locale-specific bidi and RTL stress tests.

Procedure: running a validation sweep (engineer-facing)

1. Prepare environment
   a. Checkout stable reference tokenizer: git checkout tags/tokenizer-ref-2025-11-01
   b. Ensure eval-orchestrator is pointed to the correct bucket: export EVAL_BUCKET=s3://redwood-evals/tokenization-testbed

2. Start a pre-merge check (CI integration)
   - CI step triggers `tokenization-check --ci --build $PR_BUILD --target runtime`
   - CI receives artifact with TMR and BSC. If TMR > 0.5% the PR blocks and posts a triage card.

3. Nightly full sweep
   - Cron job: `eval-orchestrator run --suite tokenization-robustness --size 10000`
   - Artifacts stored at: s3://redwood-evals/tokenization-testbed/YYYY-MM-DD/<run-id>/

title: Tokenization Robustness Testbed and Operational Procedures
text:
[...]    - Cron job: `eval-orchestrator run --suite tokenization-robustness --size 10000`
   - Artifacts stored at: s3://redwood-evals/tokenization-testbed/YYYY-MM-DD/<run-id>/

4. Alerting and automated actions
   - If TMR > 0.5% or BOA > 0: Alertmanager fires `RED-TOK-DRIFT` pager with run artifacts link.
   - If NDR > 0.2% but TMR < 0.5%: create low-priority Jira labeled `token-normalization` for follow-up.

On-call triage playbook

1. Acknowledge alert and locate run artifacts. Primary responder: Applied ML oncall (rotation in Eng-SRE).
2. Reproduce locally: run `runtime-prober --model $MODEL --input-file artifacts/inputs.json --dump-tokens` and compare with `tokenizer-checker --input artifacts/inputs.json` using the provided `diff-tokens.py` tool.
3. Determine root cause categories (select one):
   - Tokenization code change in tokenizer library (e.g., upgrade to new tokenizers binding).
   - Runtime kernel rounding or quantization effect that changes bytewise input handling.
   - Preprocessor change in ingestion layer (e.g., trimming, normalization).
   - Model packaging issue (wrong tokenizer artifact bundled).
4. Mitigation steps by category:
   - Tokenizer library change: revert or pin dependency; hotfix and trigger pre-merge checks.
   - Runtime kernel effect: roll to previous kernel variant in Dedicated and Private pools; create rollback run.
   - Ingestion preprocessor: patch normalization to align with reference behaviour; prepare minor release.
   - Packaging issue: repackage model with correct tokenizer and promote via shadow deployment.
5. Post-incident: create a regression ticket, attach artifacts, and schedule a postmortem if production customers were impacted.

Rollout and canary guidance

- Always run tokenization-check suite as part of model promotion pipeline (shadow stage).
- For runtime upgrades, run the testbed across 3 environments: dev (nightly), canary (10% traffic), and prod (full). Canary must pass TMR < 0.1% for 48 hours before wider rollout.

Integration with other evals and quant profiles

- The testbed emits per-input tags that downstream semantic evals can use to stratify failures (e.g., 'ZWJ-cluster', 'escaped-json'). This guarantees we can correlate semantic regressions to tokenization families.
- Before quantized promotion, run quant-tuner with tokenization-check enabled. Some quantization profiles (see quantization-profiles/kv-cache-quant-stability-audit-playbook-2025) modify byte handling for speed; these must be validated explicitly.

CLI examples and helper scripts

- Snapshot command to run a focused suite against a runtime image: 

```
eval-orchestrator run --suite tokenization-robustness --size 2000 --runtime-image redwood/runtime:canary-2026-01-15 --output ./artifacts/run-$(date +%F).tar.gz
```

- Quick local comparator: 

```
python tools/diff-tokens.py --ref artifacts/ref_tokens.json --hot artifacts/runtime_tokens.json --report out/report.html
```

Artifact format (recommended)

- inputs.jsonl: newline-delimited inputs with metadata tags.
- ref_tokens.jsonl: token lists from tokenizer-checker.
- runtime_tokens.jsonl: token lists from runtime-prober.
- metrics.json: small JSON with TMR, BSC, NDR, BOA values.

Example metrics.json content:

```
{ "run_id": "2026-02-03-01", "tmr": "0.0034", "bsc": "0.76", "ndr": "0.0012", "boa": "0" }
```

Ownership and SLAs

- Primary owner: Applied ML (tokenization testbed subteam).
- Secondary owner: Eng-SRE (alerting and infra).
- SLO: Nightly run completion within 2 hours of start 99% of the time.
- Alerting SLA: Critical tokenization pager must be acknowledged within 15 minutes.

title: Tokenization Robustness Testbed and Operational Procedures
text:
[...] - SLO: Nightly run completion within 2 hours of start 99% of the time.
- Alerting SLA: Critical tokenization pager must be acknowledged within 15 minutes.

Operational notes and limitations

- Synthetic fuzzers do not guarantee coverage of customer data distributions; any flagged regression must be verified against production customer seed sets before major rollbacks.
- Determinism requirement: tokenization-checker is pinned by hash in the model-catalog entry; ensure model packaging scripts include tokenizer-hash metadata.

Related pages and artifacts

- Serving simulation harness and orchestration: /applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/serving-simulation-eval-harness-2026
- KV-cache quant stability audit: /applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/kv-cache-quant-stability-audit-playbook-2025

Revision history

- 2025-11-18: Initial draft by Priya Nair.
- 2026-01-10: Added BOA handling and quick comparator script (review: Ethan Cole).
- 2026-02-03: Published with updated SLOs and integration notes (review: Lena Schmidt, Marcus Lee).

title: Eval Data Provenance and Reproducibility Standard
text:
Eval Data Provenance and Reproducibility Standard

Summary:

This standard defines the metadata, process, and automated checks required to ensure evaluation datasets and synthetic prompt collections used in Redwood Inference evaluation harnesses are traceable, auditable, and reproducible. It applies to all evaluation artifacts consumed by the eval-orchestrator and offline evaluation pipelines (interactive, batch, and shadow).

Scope and goals:

- Scope: ground-truth datasets, prompt playlists, synthetic generators, sampled mirrors of production traffic, and any derived evaluation artifacts used for model promotion, regression triage, or quality guardrails.
- Goals:
  - Produce a minimum provenance record for every eval artifact that permits exact rehydration in CI and on private/dedicated deployments.
  - Provide a reproducibility score and gating rules for model promotion and rollout.
  - Automate metadata capture, enforcement, and lineage visualization.

Definitions:

- artifact: any dataset, prompt set, synthetic generator output, or evaluation manifest.
- dataset_id: stable identifier for the upstream source (e.g., redwood/datasets/intent-probes-v2).
- snapshot_sha: git or object-store SHA for the exact file(s) used.
- manifest_uri: location in object storage or dataset registry.
- schema_hash: deterministic hash of the record schema (order + field names + types).
- provenance_level: low|standard|strict (see "Provenance Levels" below).

Provenance levels and requirements:

1) low (ad-hoc experiments)
- Required fields: dataset_id, created_by, timestamp, manifest_uri
- Recommendation: include seed when sampling
- Not allowed for promotion or production guardrails

2) standard (typical evals and benchmark runs)
- Required fields: dataset_id, snapshot_sha, manifest_uri, schema_hash, sample_strategy, seed, eval_suite_version, creator_contact
- Metadata must be stored in the central data-catalog entry and linked from the eval run
- Automated lint must pass (metadata-lint >= v1.3)

3) strict (promotion and safety-critical checks)
- All standard fields plus: lineage (upstream datasets used), annotation_guides_version, annotator_ids (or k-anonymized certificate), access_control_audit_uri, differential_privacy_parameters (if applied), reproducibility_score
- Required to use reproducibility_score >= 0.90 for model promotion to hosted or dedicated clusters

Minimal metadata schema (required keys):

| field | description | example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| dataset_id | canonical dataset name in data-catalog | redwood/datasets/intent-probes-v2 |
| snapshot_sha | SHA of the gzipped manifest or git tag | sha256:2a4b... |
| manifest_uri | s3 or gs URI pointing to the manifest | s3://redwood-evals/manifests/intent-probes-v2/2026-01-08.jsonl |
| schema_hash | deterministic hash of field names and types | hsh:8f12... |
| sample_strategy | full|stratified|random|production-mirror | stratified:user_segment |
| seed | integer seed for sampling/synth | 42 |
| eval_suite_version | semantic version of eval suite used | 2026.01.1 |
| creator_contact | owner email or oncall | nadia.chen@redwood.ai |
| provenance_level | low|standard|strict | standard |

Mandatory fields must be present as top-level keys in the artifact manifest and also as labels in the data-catalog entry. Implementations that fail to populate these keys should be rejected by eval-ci pre-submit checks.

Reproducibility score and computation:

The reproducibility_score is a number in [0.0, 1.0] that represents the confidence that an artifact + pipeline can be deterministically re-executed and yield equivalent evaluation outputs (accounting for known non-determinism). It is computed as a weighted average of:

- metadata_completeness (30%): fraction of mandatory keys present and valid.
- binary_stability (30%): whether binaries (evaluator, model surrogate) used are pinned with SHAs or immutable container digests.
- sample_determinism (20%): sampling seeds present and non-random components pinned.
- environment_pin (20%): runtime environment pinned (e.g., python packages, CUDA driver, runtime container digest).

Thresholds:

- < 0.6: exploratory only, cannot be used in regression triage or gated promotions.
- 0.6–0.89: acceptable for nightly bench and internal experiments.
- >= 0.90: eligible for promotion to canary/dedicated and for use in safety guardrails (strict artifacts require >=0.90).

Automation and enforcement:

- metadata-lint (CLI): runs on any manifest changes. Usage:
  - metadata-lint validate manifest.json --schema eval-provenance-v1
  - metadata-lint compute-score manifest.json
  - Exit codes: 0 pass, 2 warnings, 3 failure
- eval-ci (GitHub/GitLab integration):
  - Pre-merge hook validates manifest presence and reproducibility_score >= 0.6 (project config may raise this)
  - Promotion pipeline requires reproducibility_score >= 0.90 and provenance_level strict
- data-catalog (service: redwood-data-catalog): automatic registration API
  - POST /v1/artifacts register payload -> returns artifact_id and visual lineage graph link
- lineage-visualizer: internal service used by SRE and QA to render upstream/downstream dependencies

Promotion and gating workflow (numbered steps):

1. Author creates or updates evaluation manifest and pushes to the repo or uploads manifest to s3 with manifest_uri.
2. metadata-lint runs locally; author fixes schema or missing keys.
3. On PR, eval-ci validates manifest and computes reproducibility_score. PR blocked if score < team-configured threshold.
4. After merge, eval-orchestrator picks manifest and runs smoke evaluation.
5. If smoke passes and score >= 0.90, QA triggers strict-level human review (security/data-privacy).
6. Upon approval, artifact is marked in data-catalog as promotable and can be used in canary/regression suites.

Promotion checklist (must pass all items):

- metadata-lint: pass (exit 0)
- reproducibility_score >= 0.90
- smoke evaluation: no numeric regressions > 3% relative to baseline on key slices
- privacy review: DP parameters or annotator attestations present if data-sensitive
- storage: manifest_uri accessible with read-only service principal (audit log must show at least one access)

Operational considerations and edge cases:

- Large production mirrors: for privacy, store only stratified subsamples with schema_hash and random_seed, and store an access-controlled pointer to the full mirror.
- Synthetic generators: store generator commit hash, config, temperature/parameters, and random seed. If generator is non-deterministic (e.g., uses True RNG), mark provenance_level low unless additional determinism is introduced.
- Third-party datasets: require evidence of license and a scanned license_id field in manifest; provenance_level strict requires license_caveats documented.

Examples (manifest snippet):

{
  "dataset_id": "redwood/datasets/intent-probes-v2",
  "snapshot_sha": "sha256:2a4b...",
  "manifest_uri": "s3://redwood-evals/manifests/intent-probes-v2/2026-01-08.jsonl",
  "schema_hash": "hsh:8f12...",
  "sample_strategy": "stratified:user_segment",
  "seed": "42",
  "eval_suite_version": "2026.01.1",
  "creator_contact": "nadia.chen@redwood.ai",
  "provenance_level": "strict",
  "reproducibility_score": "0.93"
}

CLI quickstart for authors:

1) Create manifest: cp samples/manifest-template.json manifest.json && edit fields
2) Validate: metadata-lint validate manifest.json --schema eval-provenance-v1
3) Compute score: metadata-lint compute-score manifest.json
4) Register: curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" --data @manifest.json $DATA_CATALOG/register

Monitoring and alerts:

- Metrics exposed by eval-orchestrator: provenance_checks.total, provenance_checks.failed, reproducibility_score.avg (per-team metric).
- Alert: provenance_checks.failed > 5 in 1h -> page oncall-eval (pagerduty)
- Reports: weekly digest to applied-ml-and-evals channel with top failing keys and artifact churn

Ownership, support, and exceptions:

- Owner: Applied ML platform (team lead: Nadia Chen)
- Reviewers: Marcus Alvarez (SRE), Priya Kapoor (ML Research), Ethan Rivera (Privacy)
- Exceptions: requests for exceptions to the strict level must be submitted via the data-exceptions workflow and include a compensating controls checklist.

Appendix: example metadata-lint rule highlights:

- schema_hash must be computed using the deterministic_hasher tool (sha1 of field-name:type list)
- manifest_uri must be reachable by the CI runner and return 200 for HEAD
- snapshot_sha should be a 64-char hex (sha256) or a container digest (sha256:...)

Related pages and references:

- Evaluator interchange format: /applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/evaluator-interchange-format-and-federation-spec-2026
- Promotion playbook: /applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/inference-readiness-eval-matrix-and-promotion-playbook-2026
- Metadata tooling repo: git://git.redwood.ai/platform/metadata-lint (internal)

Revision history:

- 2025-11-20: initial draft (Nadia Chen)
- 2025-12-15: added reproducibility_score algorithm and thresholds (Marcus Alvarez)
- 2026-02-10: clarified strict level privacy requirements and added CLI quickstart (Priya Kapoor)

title: Probe Curriculum and Adaptive Priority Scheduler
text:
Probe Curriculum and Adaptive Priority Scheduler

Overview

This document describes the Probe Curriculum and Adaptive Priority Scheduler (PCAPS), a deterministic + stochastic scheduling layer for the eval-harness that prioritizes probes for continuous evaluation based on value (quality signal), cost, and reliability impact. PCAPS replaces the static TTL/fixed-priority queues with a curriculum-driven, budget-aware orchestrator to surface regressive or high-risk slices earlier while respecting eval budget and SLOs.

Goals
- Increase early detection rate for meaningful regressions (precision > 0.8 for P1 regressions in production-like shadow runs).
- Bound evaluator spend to a configurable daily budget while maximizing expected information gain.
- Smooth probe churn to avoid bursty load on the eval fleet and model-serving clusters.
- Provide transparent, auditable priorities and deterministic replay for postmortem triage.

When to use this document
- Onboarding new probe families into the evaluation mesh.
- Operationalizing continuous shadow or canary evals against a new model variant.
- Implementing cost-constrained, risk-focused probe sampling for private/dedicated deployments.

Scope and non-goals
- Scope: scheduling, scoring, and integration hooks into eval-orchestrator and probe-queue. Includes telemetry contract and rollout steps.
- Non-goals: model-side instrumentation, long-term labeling workflows, or annotator economics (see separate docs).

Architecture summary
- Components:
  - probe-fabricator: generates and mutates probe candidates from seed sets and telemetry anchors.
  - probe-priority-service: computes a score for each probe based on curriculum rules, risk signals, and cost model.
  - probe-queue: a tiered queue (hot, warm, cold) enforced by the scheduler.
  - eval-orchestrator: consumes probes from probe-queue and dispatches to evaluator workers.
  - backfill-manager: maintains probe budget and triggers historical replays.

Scoring function (design)
- Score(probe) = alpha * IG(probe) + beta * Risk(probe) - gamma * Cost(probe) + delta * FreshnessBoost
  - IG(probe): estimated information gain (expected reduction in uncertainty for target slices). Derived from historical detection rates and slice entropy.
  - Risk(probe): production telemetry signal (e.g., 95th-response-lag, error-rate spike probability, semantic-failure-probability).
  - Cost(probe): expected evaluator compute cost (tokens * model_cost_per_token * quant_penalty).
  - FreshnessBoost: a small time-decay bonus for recently observed production anomalies.
  - alpha/beta/gamma/delta are tunable per-curriculum profile.

Curriculum profiles
- Defensive (SLO-first): maximize Risk, high gamma to constrain cost. Use for mission-critical services.
- Exploratory (coverage-first): maximize IG, moderate gamma. Use for new-model onboarding.
- Cost-limited (budget-first): strong cost penalty and aggressive batching suggestions. Use for low-cost shadow environments.

Operational parameters (table)
- The table below lists default parameters used in prod rollout. Operators can override per-tenant or per-model.

Parameter | Default | Notes
---|---:|---
daily_eval_budget_usd | 1200 | Soft budget; backfill-manager enforces a hard cap with graceful pause.
hot_queue_capacity | 5k probes | Highest priority; targets P0/P1 signals.
warm_queue_capacity | 20k probes | For ongoing coverage and re-eval.
cold_queue_capacity | 200k probes | Low priority, best-effort sampling.
max_batch_tokens | 25k | Aggregation target used by probe-queue to group probes for efficient eval.
priority_decay_window_days | 7 | After this window, FreshnessBoost -> 0.
replay_retention_days | 90 | How long synthetic/historical probes are kept for deterministic replays.

Probe lifecycle (numbered procedure)
1. Fabrication: probe-fabricator creates candidate probes from seeds, telemetry anchors, and adversarial mutators. Each probe is annotated with metadata: seed_id, slice_id, telemetry_anchor, estimated_tokens, provenance_hash.
2. Scoring: probe-priority-service computes Score(probe) and assigns a TTL and suggested batch affinity.
3. Enqueue: probe is placed into hot/warm/cold tier according to score thresholds.
4. Dispatch: eval-orchestrator pulls from hot first, respecting batch and SLO constraints; warm as capacity allows; cold when spare budget exists.
5. Result ingestion: evaluator workers emit structured results (labels, evaluator_metrics, cost). backfill-manager updates cost ledger and recalculates IG for related probes.
6. Retire or recycle: probes are retired after exceeding retries or after replay_retention_days. Reuse is allowed for high-IG probes under a different model variant.

title: Probe Curriculum and Adaptive Priority Scheduler
text:
[...] 5. Result ingestion: evaluator workers emit structured results (labels, evaluator_metrics, cost). backfill-manager updates cost ledger and recalculates IG for related probes.
6. Retire or recycle: probes are retired after exceeding retries or after replay_retention_days. Reuse is allowed for high-IG probes under a different model variant.

Priority thresholds (example)
- hot: Score >= 0.72
- warm: 0.35 <= Score < 0.72
- cold: Score < 0.35
Thresholds are configurable and should be tuned during the canary rollout (see rollout steps).

Telemetry contract (JSON schema described in text)
- Each evaluator result MUST include the following fields: probe_id, model_variant, tokens_consumed, evaluator_time_ms, label_set_id, confidence_estimate, failure_class (none|semantic|safety|latency|runtime), trace_id.
- Telemetry should be exported to eval-metrics-store with the tag set: {model:variant, env:shadow|canary|prod, tenant}.

Cost model examples (YAML)
- sample_cost_profile:
    model_cost_per_token_usd: 0.0000009
    quant_penalty: 1.15
    avg_tokens_expected: 180
    min_tokens_floor: 16

- sampler_config:
    budget_window: daily
    budget_usd: 1200
    prefer_batching: true
    batch_min_utilization: 0.6

Integration points
- eval-orchestrator: implements a single API endpoint POST /v1/probes/claim which accepts a max_tokens budget and returns a batch. The scheduler must honor this contract.
- shadow-sequencer: when shadowing live traffic, the probe-priority-service listens to anomaly hooks and boosts FreshnessBoost for related probes.
- probe-audit-log: every score change emits an audit entry to enable deterministic postmortem replay.

Runbook: incident scenarios and mitigation (short playbook)
1) Unexpected budget exhaustion mid-day
  - Trigger: backfill-manager emits budget_exhaustion alert.
  - Immediate action: switch scheduler to cost-limited curriculum (owner: eval-platform oncall).
  - If critical production regressions suspected: temporarily raise hot_queue_capacity and create a manual emergency queue with must-run probes.
2) Hot queue starvation (latency backlog)
  - Trigger: hot_queue_latency_p95 > 3 minutes for 10m window.
  - Action: throttle warm/cold dispatch, enable aggressive batching, and spin up ephemeral evaluator workers (runbook: eng-sre procedure).
3) Priority inversion (low-risk probes running before high-risk)
  - Trigger: audit logs show score misassignment for >100 probes in 1h.
  - Action: ingest audit logs to simulate scoring, run deterministic replay, roll back priority-service to previous release.

Failure modes and mitigations
- Overconfident IG estimates: enforce conservative floor to reduce weight on IG during first 48 hours of a new curriculum.
- Cost underestimation for quantized artifacts: add quant_penalty per model and monitor cost drift metric: quant_cost_ratio > 1.25 triggers mitigation.
- Probe duplication loops: dedupe by provenance_hash; if >3 duplicates in 1h, mark probe family as noisy and reduce fabrication rate.

Rollout plan (canary -> ramp)
1. Internal canary (week 0): enable PCAPS in eval-staging with 10% of the evaluator pool; validate scoring reproducibility with deterministic seeds. Acceptance criteria: no increase in eval latency p95 > 5% and IG uplift >= 15% vs baseline.
2. Shadow pilot (weeks 1–2): route 1% production anomalies into PCAPS; track detection lead-time and false positive rate.
3. Tenant pilot (weeks 3–6): enable cost-limited curriculum for 3 non-critical tenant projects; monitor budget adherence and ROI (regressions detected per $1k spent).
4. Full rollout (week 8): enable for all internal services; provide a toggle per-model for external tenants.

Promotion checklist
- Unit tests for scoring function and curriculum selection.
- Telemetry schema validated by eval-metrics-store team (schema id: eval.telemetry.v3).
- Load test: sustained 10k probes/minute ingestion with 95th latency < 500ms for scoring API.
- Security review for probe content (no PII in probes).

Observability and dashboards
- Score distribution dashboard: histogram by curriculum and model_variant.
- Budget burn-down: daily and rolling-7d with per-tenant drilldown.
- Probe churn report: fabricated vs retired vs recycled counts.
- Regression catch rate: ratio of detected regressions to total labeled failures per week.

title: Probe Curriculum and Adaptive Priority Scheduler
text:
[...] - Probe churn report: fabricated vs retired vs recycled counts.
- Regression catch rate: ratio of detected regressions to total labeled failures per week.

Open questions and future work
- Multi-model joint probes: how to compute IG when a probe should be evaluated across many model families?
- Reinforcement-style curriculum updates: can we use evaluator outcomes to automatically tune alpha/beta/gamma?
- Pod-aware scheduling: incorporate hardware-aware batching benefits into Cost(probe) for TPU/GPU heterogeneity.

Ownership and contacts
- Primary owner: Applied ML infrastructure (owner contact: aisha@redwood.ai).
- Oncall: eval-platform (pager duty: ENG-EVAL-PLT).
- Reviewers: Ravi Patel (Evaluation team), Marta Gomez (SRE).

Appendix: example CLI
- Claim a batch (developer flow):
  curl -X POST https://eval-orchestrator.internal/v1/probes/claim -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' -d '{"max_tokens": 12000, "preferred_curricula": ["defensive"]}'

- Inspect a probe score:
  curl https://probe-priority-service.internal/v1/probes/score?probe_id=abc123

Appendix: deterministic replay snippet (pseudo-code)
- python replay.py --probe-ids path/to/probe_ids.txt --model model:canary-v42 --replay-window 2026-01-01:2026-02-01 --deterministic-seed 42

References and cross-links
- See eval-harness/eval-mesh-and-workload-fingerprinting-2026 for probe seed taxonomy.
- Related: quality-guardrails/response-stability-index-calibration-and-deployment-2026 for stability gating.

Document change log
- 2025-11-02: Created (Aisha Kapoor).
- 2026-01-18: Added cost model examples and updated daily budget defaults (Ravi Patel).
- 2026-02-14: Added production rollout acceptance criteria and deterministic replay snippet (Marta Gomez).

title: Adaptive Fidelity Anchor and Recovery Playbook
text:
Adaptive Fidelity Anchor and Recovery Playbook

Overview:

This playbook defines an anchored approach to fidelity regressions: detect deviations using lightweight sentinel probes (anchors), triage via signal fusion across eval and runtime telemetry, and execute a staged recovery that prioritizes customer-visible quality while minimizing compute and annotation costs. It unifies guidance across eval harness, prompt/probe generation, model onboarding gates, quality guardrails, and quantized deployment rollbacks.

Intended audience:
- ML SREs and Applied ML Engineers
- Model onboarding owners and Evaluator stewards
- Incident response and oncall engineers responsible for model quality

Key goals:
- Detect fidelity regressions at the tenant-slice level within 3x the normal probe cadence.
- Provide a deterministic triage workflow that identifies the likely root cause class (quantization, KV-cache interaction, prompt drift, model weight change, routing fallback).
- Automate safe rollback or soft-fallback to a higher-fidelity path with no more than 1% customer-visible error spike during remediation.
- Maintain an audit trail and recovery contract for post-incident learning and model promotion.

Scope and non-goals:
- This playbook covers detection, triage, synthetic probe resynthesis, automated remediations (auto-fallback/rollback), and hand-off to model-onboarding teams.
- It does NOT replace manual model certification; it augments onboarding with operational continuity measures.

Definitions and concepts:
- Anchor probe: a small, high-signal prompt from the canonical probe bank assigned to a tenant cohort and slice. Anchors are stable (low variance) and serve as early fidelity sentinels.
- Probe resynthesis: automatic generation of augmented probes for expanded coverage after a regression is detected.
- Quant-shadow cohort: a set of requests routed to a quantized variant in shadow for comparison against the reference model.
- Soft-fallback: route subset of traffic to a higher-precision variant or cached responses while diagnosis proceeds.

Monitoring and SLOs (thresholds):
- Probe pass rate SLO: >= 98% per slice over rolling 24h. Alert when drop > 4 percentage points compared to baseline.
- Semantic coherence (embedding drift) delta: alert when median cosine similarity to baseline decreases by > 0.06 for a slice.
- Latency-quality coupling: if 95th percentile latency improves by > 20ms but semantic score drops > 3%, flag for quality-first triage.
- Quant-fallback SLA: automated fallback must reinstate a verified path within 15 minutes of severe regression detection.

Signal sources for triage (ordered by priority):
1) Anchor probe failures (highest signal, low noise).
2) Eval harness cross-checks (shadow vs prod comparison on seeded probe set).
3) Live telemetry: token-level perplexity spikes, softmax entropy changes, embedding drift, and functioncall mismatch rates.
4) Runtime traces: KV cache hit/miss rate deltas, batch-size changes, kernel scheduling anomalies.
5) Deployment events: model version rollouts, quantization job completions, autoscaler events, routing policy changes.

Triage decision matrix (quick reference):
- Anchor fail + recent quantization rollout -> classify as Quantization-related and run Quant-Validity checks (Section: Quant triage).
- Anchor fail + KV cache miss spike (>= 12% increase) -> classify as Cache interaction; run priming-context replays.
- Anchor fail + prompt-template churn in telemetry -> classify as Prompt drift; trigger probe-resynthesis and annotator review.
- Anchor fail across multiple regions + routing policy changes -> classify as Routing/fallback; validate candidate model variant mapping.

Runbook: detection to remediation (step-by-step)
1) Detection (automated):
   a) Anchor probe scheduler runs every 10 minutes for high-priority slices and every hour for low-priority slices.
   b) If probe pass rate drops below Alert threshold, create incident with severity score computed as: 
      severity = max(5, ceil(10 * relative-drop * slice-weight)).
   c) Add telemetry tags: slice_id, tenant_id, anchor_id, recent_rollout_id.

2) Initial auto-triage (automated, ~2 minutes):
   a) Correlate recent deploy events (+/- 60 minutes) via deployment-log API.
   b) Run shadow replay for 500 recent requests or 1 hour of traffic, whichever is smaller.
   c) Compute probe delta matrix: semantic delta, token-level KL, functioncall delta.

3) Immediate containment (automated with manual approval flag):
   a) If quant-related evidence >= 0.8 confidence, initiate Quant soft-fallback: route 20% traffic to higher-precision variant and continue shadowing 100% for signals.
   b) If cache interaction evidence >= 0.75, enable priming bias reduction: temporarily disable prefix caching for affected slice and shadow results.
   c) If prompt drift evidence >= 0.7, pause recent prompt-template rollout and capture samples for annotators.

4) Deep triage (manual):
   a) Oncall runs the Triage Checklist (see below).
   b) If root cause still unclear after 30 minutes, escalate to Applied ML oncall and create a checkpointed replay job for 10k requests.

Triage Checklist (manual):
- Verify anchor probe reproducibility by running the anchor locally against reference and suspect variants.
- Run eval-harness microbench that replays context slices with varied tokenization seeds.
- Pull kernel-level profiler for suspect nodes and inspect scheduling/kerneltime anomalies.
- Check annotator sample: label 50 anchor failures for semantic correctness and edge-case mismatch.
- Review metadata for recent changes to model card, quant profile, or prompt templates.

Probe-resynthesis procedure (automated + human-in-loop):
1) Seed expansion: select top 10 failing anchors and generate 5 perturbations per anchor (typos, paraphrases, length variations).
2) Prioritize synthesized probes by entropy and slice coverage.
3) Submit top 50 synthesized probes to quick-label pipeline (human-in-the-loop) with 24h SLA for labels.
4) If labeled failure rate > 25%, mark slice as candidate for rollback until patch is validated.

Quant triage and validation steps:
- Run quant-shadow replay comparing float and quant variants across anchor bank.
- Metrics to compute: token divergence rate, stop-token mismatches, embedding cosine shifts, top-k probability reorderings.
- If token divergence rate > 2% on stable anchors, fail quant safety gate and trigger rollback or narrower quant profile.
- Use the following CLI to run a quant-shadow batch (example):

  # run in evaluator container
  eval-shadow-run --shadow-model rdl-1.3-quant8 --ref-model rdl-1.3-fp32 --probes anchors/tenant-42/top-500.json --out /tmp/qshadow.json --compare-metrics semantic,token-kl,emb-cosine

Rolling back a quantized deployment (safety steps):
1) Validate the rollback image passes smoke tests using the eval-harness smoke probe set.
2) Initiate staged rollback with 10% traffic cutover for 5 minutes, monitor anchored probes.
3) If probes pass for 15 minutes with no adverse telemetry, continue to 50% and then 100%.
4) Create a postmortem and a quant-profile adjustment ticket in the model-onboarding queue.

Automation recipes and config table:
- anchor-scheduler.yaml (example fields):
  name: anchor-scheduler
  cadence_high_priority: "10m"
  cadence_low_priority: "1h"
  sliding_window: "24h"
  alert_threshold_drop: "4%"
  eviction_policy: "age+staleness"

- quant-shadow-policy.json (example):
  {"shadow_fraction": "1.0", "compare_window": "60m", "metrics": ["token-kl","emb-cosine","funccall-mismatch"]}

Operational ownership matrix:
- Anchor bank stewardship: Evaluator/Probe team (owner: Priya Shah)
- Quant safety gates: Serving Runtime team (owner: Ming Zhao)
- Oncall triage and incident runbook owner: ML SRE (owner: Lucas Moreno)
- Annotator pipeline and labels: Data Ops (owner: Claire Roberts)

Communication and customer impact policy:
- If incident severity >= 7, notify affected customers via CS within 30 minutes with a templated message and mitigation steps.
- For severity 5–6, include a short note in the daily incident digest and add to the weekly QBR if unresolved after 48 hours.

Post-incident actions and promotion criteria:
1) Run a 5-point postmortem covering detection time, containment time, root cause, mitigation, and prevention items.
2) If quant-profile was rolled back, require a shadow-window of 72 hours and a 2000-anchor passing requirement before re-promoting the quant profile.
3) Add synthesized probes from the incident to the anchor bank with label provenance and an incident tag.

CI/CD integration and gating:
- Add a pre-deploy check that runs the anchor bank subset (100 probes) against the candidate model artifact with the candidate quant profile.
- Fail the pipeline if semantic delta > 0.02 on stable anchors or token divergence > 1% on anchor bank.

Examples and snippets:
- Example alert payload (JSON text for ops-tooling):
  {
    "incident_id": "INC-2026-1023",
    "slice_id": "payments-card-errors",
    "anchor_id": "anchor-2025-42",
    "metric": "probe_pass_rate",
    "baseline": "0.986",
    "current": "0.912",
    "recommended_action": "quant-soft-fallback"
  }

- Sample command to synthesize probes locally (developer):
  python tools/probe_synth.py --seed-file anchors/tenant-42/top-10.json --augmentations 5 --out /tmp/synth.json

Escalation matrix and SLAs:
- First response: ML SRE oncall within 15 minutes.
- Owner engagement: Applied ML owner within 1 hour for unresolved triage.
- CS notification: within 30 minutes if severity >= 7.

Metrics to add to dashboards (suggested panels):
- Anchor pass rate by slice (heatmap).
- Quant-shadow divergence ratio over time (line chart).
- Probe resynthesis queue length and label backlog (gauge).
- KV cache miss delta correlated with anchor failures (scatter).

Governance and change control:
- Any change to anchor bank or probe selection logic must be reviewed by the Evaluator steward and signed off by ML SRE.
- Quant profile changes require a recorded shadow run of at least 6 hours and an artifacts hash stored in model-onboarding.

Appendix A — Useful queries and endpoints:
- Fetch recent anchor failures (example SQL):
  SELECT slice_id, COUNT(*) AS failures, AVG(delta_semantic) AS avg_delta
  FROM probe_events
  WHERE ts > now() - interval '24 hours'
  GROUP BY slice_id
  ORDER BY failures DESC

- Runtime trace endpoint:
  https://observability.internal/api/v1/traces?service=serving-runtime&slice_id={slice_id}&since=60m

Appendix B — Templates
- Incident summary template (one-paragraph):
  Short description, anchor evidence, suspected cause, containment steps taken, next steps and owner.

- Customer notification snippet:
  "We detected a temporary reduction in response quality for [feature] affecting a small subset of requests. We have reverted the recent configuration change and are validating the recovery. We will follow up with a full root cause analysis within 48 hours."

Revision history:
- 2026-02-12: created by Asha Patel (draft).
- 2026-02-18: added quant triage recipes and CLI examples (reviewers: Lucas Moreno).
- 2026-03-02: published; tightened SLO deltas and added CI gating examples.

Contact and owners:
- Primary oncall: ml-sre-oncall@redwood.ai
- Probe bank steward: priya.shah@redwood.ai
- Quant safety owner: ming.zhao@redwood.ai

End of document.

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol

Summary:

This document defines a repeatable evaluation protocol for measuring prompt-level output stability when prompts are sharded (split across segments), when prefix and KV caches are active, and across quantized model variants. The protocol is intended for applied ML engineers onboarding new models into Redwood’s serving fleet and for regression triage when a change in runtime (kernels, batching, quantization) correlates with output drift.

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...] This document defines a repeatable evaluation protocol for measuring prompt-level output stability when prompts are sharded (split across segments), when prefix and KV caches are active, and across quantized model variants. The protocol is intended for applied ML engineers onboarding new models into Redwood’s serving fleet and for regression triage when a change in runtime (kernels, batching, quantization) correlates with output drift.


Goals:
- Provide a deterministic, low-noise benchmark to compare semantic stability across model variants and runtime configs.
- Surface regressions caused by caching differences, prompt segmentation, and quantization.
- Produce actionable diagnostics (delta examples, attention/kv-dumps, similarity matrices) to guide model/runtime remediation.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to autoregressive text generation and chat-style models (decoder-only and encoder-decoder where applicable).
- Not intended for pure embedding-only workloads (see embedding stability spec).
- Routine use: onboarding new model version, validating quantization profile, or triaging a regression flagged by QA or customers.

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...] - Routine use: onboarding new model version, validating quantization profile, or triaging a regression flagged by QA or customers.


Key terms and definitions:
- Prompt sharding: splitting a single prompt into multiple chunks that are sent as separate requests or streamed segments to the runtime. Common for very long contexts or when prefix caching is leveraged.
- Cache-aware mode: evaluation runs where the runtime is configured to populate and reuse prefix/KV cache between segments or across repeated requests.
- Stability: measured as semantic agreement between outputs given equivalent intent; metrics described below.

High-level protocol (steps):
1) Candidate selection
   - Pick a representative prompt set (N >= 200 recommended). Use a mix of short, medium, and long prompts and include edge cases: multi-turn chat fragments, code blocks, bullet lists, and HTML fragments.
   - Label prompts with modality tags and expected length bucket (short <128 tok, medium 128-2048, long >2048).

2) Baseline generation (canonical run)
   - Run prompts end-to-end on the reference model variant with cache disabled (cold start) to obtain baseline outputs. Record seeds, tokenizer, model hash, runtime version.
   - CLI example: python eval_runner.py --config configs/ps_stability_baseline.yaml --run-id baseline-2026-02-14

3) Sharded runs
   - For each prompt, generate outputs under two sharding strategies:
     a) Segment-by-sentence: split on sentence boundaries (try to keep segment sizes ~256–512 tokens)
     b) Fixed-window: split into equal token windows with 25% overlap
   - For each strategy, run in: cache-disabled and cache-enabled modes. Capture per-segment outputs and final assembled output.

4) Quantization and runtime variants
   - Repeat sharded runs on each model variant of interest (fp16, int8, quantized-llm-configA). Ensure identical decoding parameters (temperature, top_p, max_tokens, stop tokens).
   - Record kernel/runtime version, kernel flags (use_batched_kv, fused_attention), and hardware type.

5) Deterministic decoding and sampling controls
   - For stability-focused comparisons prefer deterministic decoding (temperature=0, do_sample=false). If sampling is required for downstream fidelity tests, run an additional sampled pass with fixed RNG seed.

6) Metrics and thresholds
   - Surface-level metrics (per-prompt):
     * Token-level edit distance (Levenshtein) normalized by output length
     * N-gram overlap (BLEU/ROUGE) between baseline and candidate assembled outputs
     * Semantic similarity: cosine(sim(embed(baseline)), embed(candidate)) using the reference embedding model (thresholds defined below)
     * Structural agreement: alignment of stop tokens, list lengths, JSON schema conformance (where applicable)
   - Aggregate metrics (recommended reporting): mean, median, 95th percentile of normalized edit distance; fraction of prompts with semantic similarity < 0.90; fraction with structural breaks (e.g., truncated JSON).
   - Alert thresholds (suggested defaults):
     * Mean normalized edit distance increase > 0.03 compared to baseline -> warning
     * >5% prompts with semantic similarity < 0.90 -> needs-investigation
     * Any prompt with structural break (JSON invalidation, or missing required fields) -> P0 triage

7) Diagnostics produced per failing prompt
   - Raw outputs for baseline and each variant (per-segment and assembled)
   - Token alignment visualization (side-by-side diff)
   - Attention/KV dump (where supported) for segments showing divergence (save to /mnt/evals/kv-dumps/{run-id}/{prompt-id})
   - Embedding similarity heatmap across prefixes and segments

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...]    - Embedding similarity heatmap across prefixes and segments


Configuration examples (YAML):

# configs/ps_stability_baseline.yaml
run_name: "prompt_shard_stability.baseline"
model: "gptx-7b-fp16"
max_tokens: 256
decoding:
  temperature: 0.0
  do_sample: false
shard_strategy: none
cache_mode: disabled
prompts_source: "datasets/prompt_shard_suite/2026-01.jsonl"
output_dir: "results/ps_baseline/"

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...] output_dir: "results/ps_baseline/"


# configs/ps_shard_cache_int8.yaml
run_name: "prompt_shard.int8.cache_enabled"
model: "gptx-7b-int8"
max_tokens: 256
decoding:
  temperature: 0.0
  do_sample: false
shard_strategy: fixed_window
shard_overlap: 0.25
cache_mode: enabled
kernel_flags: ["use_batched_kv", "fused_attention_v2"]
prompts_source: "datasets/prompt_shard_suite/2026-01.jsonl"
output_dir: "results/ps_int8_cache/"

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...] output_dir: "results/ps_int8_cache/"


Command-line runner example:

# run baseline
python eval_runner.py --config configs/ps_stability_baseline.yaml --workers 16 --seed 12345

# run int8 shard + cache
python eval_runner.py --config configs/ps_shard_cache_int8.yaml --workers 32 --seed 12345 --collect-kv-dumps

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...] python eval_runner.py --config configs/ps_shard_cache_int8.yaml --workers 32 --seed 12345 --collect-kv-dumps


Result artifacts:
- results/{run-id}/outputs.jsonl  # per-prompt assembled outputs
- results/{run-id}/segment_outputs/    # per-segment artifacts
- results/{run-id}/metrics_summary.csv
- results/{run-id}/kv-dumps/
- results/{run-id}/similarity_matrices/{prompt-id}.png

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...] - results/{run-id}/similarity_matrices/{prompt-id}.png


Analysis and triage playbook:
1) Automatic triage
   - The harness produces a ranked list of prompts by delta severity (composite score combining edit distance, semantic similarity, and structural break flags).
   - If any P0 flags (structural breaks) are present, page oncall-sre + model-owner.

2) Reproduce locally
   - Re-run the failing prompt with the exact run-id, kernel flags, and model hash on a dev cluster node with debug logging enabled.
   - Use --collect-kv-dumps and set REDWOOD_DEBUG=1 to capture runtime assertions.

3) Narrowing hypotheses
   - Compare cache-disabled vs cache-enabled runs to see if divergence appears only when cache is used -> suspect KV caching bug or cache key collision.
   - Compare fp16 vs quantized variants to isolate quantization-induced tokenization/rounding regression.
   - If divergence occurs only under sharding strategies, inspect segment assembly: ensure no dropped tokens at boundaries and consistent tokenizer state.

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
section_header: Quality gates for model onboarding (must pass all)
text:
[...]    - If divergence occurs only under sharding strategies, inspect segment assembly: ensure no dropped tokens at boundaries and consistent tokenizer state.


Ownership and SLAs for evaluation runs:
- Primary owner: Applied ML — Eval team (Priya Rao)
- Secondary: Runtime QA (Carlos Medina)
- Expected SLAs: onboarding runs (<= 2k prompts) should complete end-to-end within 24 hours on a standard eval cluster. Triage response for P0 stability regressions: 4 hours.

Quality gates for model onboarding (must pass all):
- No structural break alerts across a 1k prompt representative suite.
- Mean normalized edit distance delta <= 0.02 relative to baseline for FP16->candidate path.
- <2% prompts with semantic similarity < 0.90 for sampled suite.
- KV dump size within expected range (no unexpected MVCC growth).

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...] - KV dump size within expected range (no unexpected MVCC growth).


Troubleshooting checklist (common failure modes):
- Symptom: Assembled output truncated when sharded but fine in non-sharded run
  - Check shard_overlap settings; confirm tokenizer boundary splitting does not drop separators.
  - Confirm that the runtime passes the correct attention_mask/position_ids across segments.
- Symptom: Small token-level differences amplified in semantics for long outputs
  - Check floating point determinism flags and quantization config (per-layer scale mismatch).
- Symptom: Divergence only when cache_enabled
  - Reproduce with cache disabled to validate; if confirmed, capture KV dumps and compare cache keys for equality.

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...]   - Reproduce with cache disabled to validate; if confirmed, capture KV dumps and compare cache keys for equality.


Dashboarding and reporting:
- Add a stability dashboard tile in Redwood Console under "Evals / Prompt Shard Stability" that shows: mean normalized edit distance over time, % prompts with semantic similarity < 0.90, counts of structural breaks by model/version/runtime.
- Weekly triage report: top 10 failing prompts, diffs, and remediation status (fix merged / workaround / accept).

Privacy and data handling:
- The prompt suite may contain customer-derived traces for regression triage. Mark any dataset that contains customer data with confidentiality: restricted and follow the customer-masking checklist before sharing outside the triage group.

Appendix A: Example token-alignment diff output (text)
--- baseline: 
The list includes: 1) apple, 2) banana, 3) cherry.
--- candidate: 
The list includes: 1) apple, 2) banana 3) cherry.

Note the missing comma after 'banana' which causes JSON-serializers downstream to fail when the output is assembled into a list.

Appendix B: Minimal embed similarity script (bash)

python - <<'PY'
from openai_embedding_client import embed_text
import json
A = embed_text('baseline output')
B = embed_text('candidate output')
from numpy import dot
from numpy.linalg import norm
print(dot(A,B)/(norm(A)*norm(B)))
PY

title: Prompt sharding and cache-aware stability evaluation protocol
text:
[...] PY

- Production prompt catalog (see related page)
