title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual

Summary
-------
This manual describes the Probe Hedge Allocator (PHA): a runtime-aware subsystem in the eval harness that proactively allocates and schedules evaluation probes to detect, contain, and mitigate fidelity degradation across private, dedicated, and hosted inference tiers. The PHA is designed to hedge coverage for slices that are most likely to show regression when model variants, quantization profiles, or serving kernels change.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] This manual describes the Probe Hedge Allocator (PHA): a runtime-aware subsystem in the eval harness that proactively allocates and schedules evaluation probes to detect, contain, and mitigate fidelity degradation across private, dedicated, and hosted inference tiers. The PHA is designed to hedge coverage for slices that are most likely to show regression when model variants, quantization profiles, or serving kernels change.


Intro / Motivation
------------------
Given that regressions frequently emerge from interactions between model quantization, KV-cache behavior, and workload priming, we need an automated allocator that:
- Prioritizes probes by predicted information gain rather than raw frequency.
- Distributes probe replicas across runtime variants (float32, int8/4x, mixed-precision) and regions for early divergence detection.
- Triggers lightweight mitigations (dynamic fallback, probe re-synthesis) when drift is detected.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] - Triggers lightweight mitigations (dynamic fallback, probe re-synthesis) when drift is detected.


Scope
-----
This document covers:
- The allocation policy and scoring heuristics for probes.
- Operational runbook for handling hedged-probe alerts.
- Integration points with the eval harness, telemetry bridge, and model promotion pipelines.
- YAML config examples and a validation checklist.
Not covered: deep statistical investigation techniques (see regression-triage docs) and offline annotator calibration workflows.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] Not covered: deep statistical investigation techniques (see regression-triage docs) and offline annotator calibration workflows.


Terminology
-----------
- Probe: a single input (with metadata) used to evaluate model behavior.
- Hedge: creating multiple, staggered replicas of a probe across variants/regions.
- Fidelity delta: metric representing semantic divergence (see Metrics section).
- Probe-band: logical grouping of probes that share a slice/signature (e.g., “payment-instructions_en_US_long”).

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] - Probe-band: logical grouping of probes that share a slice/signature (e.g., “payment-instructions_en_US_long”).


Ownership
---------
- Product owner: Maya Patel (Applied ML)
- Implementing team: Eval Platform (eng-applied-ml)
- Oncall: Eval-Oncall rotation (see team wiki for calendar)
- Escalation: first to Eval Platform SRE, then to Runtime Model Ops if quantization or kernel root cause suspected.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] - Escalation: first to Eval Platform SRE, then to Runtime Model Ops if quantization or kernel root cause suspected.


Design goals and constraints
---------------------------
- Fast detection: median divergence detection within 15 minutes for high-priority slices.
- Low cost: hedge allocations should increase probe traffic by no more than 8% for top 500 slices during normal hedging.
- Isolation: hedged probes must not affect production customer traffic; use separate shadow tokens and rate-limits.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
section_header: Primary metrics (collected per probe-band and model-version)
text:
[...] - Isolation: hedged probes must not affect production customer traffic; use separate shadow tokens and rate-limits.


Metrics and SLOs
---------------
Primary metrics (collected per probe-band and model-version):
- Fidelity Delta (FD): symmetric token-level and embedding cosine divergence composite. (lower is better)
- Divergence Rate (DR): fraction of probes exceeding FD > threshold.
- Time-to-detect (TTD): time from a change (deployment/quantization) to when DR crosses alert threshold.
- Hedge Cost Uplift (HCU): additional token/compute cost from hedging.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] - Hedge Cost Uplift (HCU): additional token/compute cost from hedging.


SLOs (targets):
- TTD: 95th percentile <= 20 minutes for priority bands.
- DR alert threshold: FD >= 0.22 for textual slices or embedding cosine <= 0.88.
- HCU: <= 8% for top-500 bands, <= 15% for all bands in a confined rollout window.

Allocation policy (high-level)
-----------------------------
1. Score probe-bands by a combination of:
   - Historical volatility (weight 0.35)
   - Business priority (weight 0.30)
   - Cross-variant sensitivity (weight 0.25)
   - Recent production error signals (weight 0.10)
2. Select top-K bands per evaluation window (K configurable).
3. For each selected band, allocate hedged replicas across a taxonomy of runtime variants:
   - canonical-fp32 (baseline)
   - quant-int8 (default quant profile)
   - quant-int4-mixed (aggressive)
   - low-latency-kernel (kernel variant B)
   - region-mirror (if multi-region enabled)
4. Orchestrate staggered execution and compare outputs using fidelity rules described below.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] 4. Orchestrate staggered execution and compare outputs using fidelity rules described below.


Pseudocode (policy)
-------------------
```
for window in rolling_windows:
  bands = fetch_band_scores(window)
  selected = top_k(bands, k=WINDOW_CONFIG.k)
  for band in selected:
    variants = select_variant_set(band)
    for v in variants:
      schedule_probe_replica(band, variant=v, stagger=rand(0,STAGGER_MAX))
```

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] ```


Fidelity comparison rules
-------------------------
- Primary comparator: composite FD = 0.6 * normalized_token_edit_distance + 0.4 * (1 - cosine_embed).
- For function-call or structured outputs: apply field-level equivalence with stricter thresholds (FD_field <= 0.12).
- Acceptable variance window: if replica outputs diverge but pass semantic equivalence (QA passes), do not alert.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
section_header: Automated actions when divergence conditions met
text:
[...] - Acceptable variance window: if replica outputs diverge but pass semantic equivalence (QA passes), do not alert.


Shutdown and mitigation strategies
--------------------------------
Automated actions when divergence conditions met:
- Soft-mitigation (automated): increase sampling to 3x for the affected band and capture extended debug trace (KV-cache snapshot, seed, full telemetry).
- Autoswap: route subsequently scheduled probes to fallback variant (canonical-fp32) while human review occurs.
- Canary-quarantine: mark latest model/quant artifact as quarantined in the artifact registry and block promotion pipelines.
- Alert owner and create incident with prefilled triage template.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] - Alert owner and create incident with prefilled triage template.


Operational runbook (step-by-step)
---------------------------------
1. Alert fires: DR > threshold for a band.
2. Oncall: acknowledge within 10 minutes in PagerDuty and open a triage ticket.
3. Run lightweight triage commands:
   - CLI: evalctl probe inspect --band <band> --last 60m
   - CLI: evalctl probe compare --band <band> --variants all --format json
4. If FD confirms regression in quant variants but not canonical-fp32:
   - Trigger autoswap: evalctl hedger autoswap --band <band> --target canonical-fp32
   - Notify Model-Ops and set artifact quarantine flag: model-registry tag set --id <artifact> --tag quarantine
5. If all variants diverge from expected golden outputs: escalate to Applied ML for root cause hypothesis (prompt sensitivity, seed changes, tokenization drift).
6. Postmortem: attach probe traces, hedger logs, and telemetry to the incident and link to regression-triage docket.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] 6. Postmortem: attach probe traces, hedger logs, and telemetry to the incident and link to regression-triage docket.


Validation & test harness integration
-----------------------------------
- Shadow run mode: PHA must be exercised in shadow for every rollout. Test checklist:
  1. End-to-end scheduling works under synthetic load (5000 probes/s for 10m).
  2. Comparators produce stable FD values under controlled perturbations.
  3. Autoswap triggers in the sandbox and respects rate-limits.
- Unit tests: score normalization, variant selection heuristics, and stagger calculation.
- Integration tests: gated in CI with a smoke job that runs PHA across a minimal model set.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
section_header: Below is a representative hedger config used by the eval orchestrator (values annotated)
text:
[...] - Integration tests: gated in CI with a smoke job that runs PHA across a minimal model set.


YAML config example
-------------------
Below is a representative hedger config used by the eval orchestrator (values annotated):

```yaml
hedger:
  window_seconds: 600            # evaluation window
  k_top_bands: 250              # number of bands to hedge each window
  max_stagger_seconds: 45       # jitter to reduce simultaneous stress
  variant_sets:
    default: ["canonical-fp32", "quant-int8", "low-latency-kernel"]
  cost_budget_pct: 0.08         # HCU budget
  alerting:
    fidelity_threshold: 0.22
    min_alert_volume: 3
```

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] ```


CLI examples
------------
- Inspecting allocations in last window:
  evalctl hedger status --window 10m --json
- Forcing a temporary hedge for a band:
  evalctl hedger force-hedge --band payment-instructions_en_US_long --variants quant-int8,low-latency-kernel --ttl 1h

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
section_header: Dashboard panels to include in the default hedger view
text:
[...]   evalctl hedger force-hedge --band payment-instructions_en_US_long --variants quant-int8,low-latency-kernel --ttl 1h


Alerting and observability
-------------------------
Dashboard panels to include in the default hedger view:
- Probe-band heatmap (DR over time)
- FD distribution histogram by variant
- Hedge Cost Uplift time series and budget burn rate
- Replica failure rate and scheduling latency

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] - Replica failure rate and scheduling latency


Baked alert rules (examples):
1. hedger.fd.high: when DR for any priority band > 0.05 sustained for 15m.
2. hedger.hcu.burn: when HCU > configured budget for 30m.
3. hedger.autoswap.rate: when autoswap events exceed 5 in 1 hour (indicates instability).

Telemetry & audit
----------------

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
section_header: Captured artifacts for every hedged replication
text:
[...] Telemetry & audit
----------------

Captured artifacts for every hedged replication:
- full request/response (sanitized PII)
- model artifact id and runtime kernel id
- KV-cache diagnostics snapshot (if available)
- execution trace id and machine metadata
All artifacts stored in probe-archive with 90-day retention for internal investigations.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
section_header: Before enabling PHA for a new model-family or quant-profile
text:
[...] All artifacts stored in probe-archive with 90-day retention for internal investigations.


Release checklist & promotion gates
----------------------------------
Before enabling PHA for a new model-family or quant-profile:
- Run a 48-hour shadow trial with full telemetry enabled.
- Confirm HCU remains below specified budget in a representative workload sample.
- Ensure automated autoswap reversions are tested end-to-end in staging.
- Obtain sign-off from Applied ML, Runtime, and SRE owners (names recorded in sign-off log).

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] - Obtain sign-off from Applied ML, Runtime, and SRE owners (names recorded in sign-off log).


Appendix A: Sample triage template (prefilled)
---------------------------------------------
Title: [Auto] Hedger alert - <band> - <artifact-id>
Severity: P2
Summary: DR crossed threshold in quant variants; canonical-fp32 shows [pass|fail].
Hypotheses:
- quant-precision drift (quantization mapping mismatch)
- priming/seed variation affecting KV-cache
- tokenization or normalization bug in preprocessor
Immediate actions taken: autoswap -> canonical-fp32; captured extended traces; quarantined artifact.
Next steps: run focused replay job, re-evaluate quant-profile sensitivity, roll forward with revert if necessary.

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] Next steps: run focused replay job, re-evaluate quant-profile sensitivity, roll forward with revert if necessary.


Appendix B: Quick debugging commands
-----------------------------------
- Compare sample outputs: evalctl probe compare --band <band> --variants quant-int8,canonical-fp32 --sample 20 --output table
- Dump KV cache snapshot for a replica: evalctl probe debug --trace <trace-id> --dump-kv

Contact and escalation
----------------------
- Primary: Eval Platform Slack channel #eval-hedger (pings to PagerDuty)
- Model-Ops lead: Diego Ramos (diego@redwood.com)
- Applied ML lead: Maya Patel (maya@redwood.com)

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] - Applied ML lead: Maya Patel (maya@redwood.com)


Change log
----------
- 2026-09-15: Initial draft (Asha Menon)
- 2026-10-10: Added autoswap flow and YAML config defaults (Diego Ramos)
- 2026-11-02: Finalized FD comparator and SLO targets; publish (Maya Patel)

title: Probe Hedge Allocator and Degradation Mitigation Manual
text:
[...] - 2026-11-02: Finalized FD comparator and SLO targets; publish (Maya Patel)


Related reading
---------------

End of manual

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook

Overview:
This cookbook defines the recommended topology, slotting strategies, and operational patterns for the eval mesh used across Redwood’s evaluation fleet. The document covers: mesh topology patterns, slot types and sizing; the slot allocation algorithm; cost and fidelity accounting; backfill and rebalancing strategies; safety and privacy gates for shadow workloads; and an operational runbook for deploying topology changes.

Goals:
- Provide a predictable, cost-aware mapping from eval workloads (probes, shadow traffic, backfills) to scarce evaluation capacity.
- Minimize eval latency for high-priority probes while maximizing overall probe coverage within budget.
- Ensure safe rollouts and auditable chargebacks for tenant-aware evals.

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...] - Ensure safe rollouts and auditable chargebacks for tenant-aware evals.


Audience:
- Evaluation platform engineers (owners of eval-orch and shadow-mesh).
- Onboarding engineers preparing probe manifests and budgets.
- SREs responsible for eval fleet capacity and cost allocation.

Key services and components:
- eval-orch: central orchestrator that assigns probes to slots and emits allocation events.
- probe-fabricator: generates synthetic probes and seeds shadow traffic.
- shadow-mesh: per-cluster runtime that executes shadow workloads; supports isolation modes (tenant, mixed, anonymized).
- telemetry-ingest: collects token-level latency/cost/time-series for evals.
- eval-ledger: accounting service that records slot consumption and cost attributions.

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...] - eval-ledger: accounting service that records slot consumption and cost attributions.


Definitions:
1) Slot: a reserved execution slot on the eval mesh. Slots have attributes: tier, cpu/gpu profile, qps limit, and cost weight.
2) Probe class: a logically grouped set of probes (e.g., functioncall-stability, longcontext-coherence, embedding-drift).
3) Fidelity budget: the allocation of high-fidelity slots for a probe class over a time window.

Topology patterns (recommended):
A) Hierarchical mesh (default) — used for most workloads:
   - Global control plane (eval-orch) + regional shadow-mesh clusters.
   - Each regional mesh exposes three slot pools: fast (low-latency), steady (balanced), bulk (throughput-optimized).
   - Routing policy: prefer local fast -> local steady -> cross-region steady -> bulk.

B) Isolated shadow mesh (sensitive tenants) — for regulated customers:
   - Dedicated shadow cluster with tenant-only slots and KMS-protected probes.
   - No cross-tenant colocation; telemetry scrubbed at source.

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...]    - No cross-tenant colocation; telemetry scrubbed at source.


Slot types and sizing (reference table):
| slot_type | cpu/gpu | qps_limit | median_latency_target | cost_weight | recommended_use_cases |
| fast      | 1xA100 | 50       | < 40ms                | 5x         | interactive probes, micro-canaries |
| steady    | 1xA100 | 200      | < 120ms               | 2x         | baseline continuous probes        |
| bulk      | 2xA100 | 1000     | best-effort            | 1x         | backfills, large-scale simulations|

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...] | bulk      | 2xA100 | 1000     | best-effort            | 1x         | backfills, large-scale simulations|


Slot allocation algorithm (summary):
1. Ingest probe manifest with metadata: priority (P0..P3), fidelity_required (high/medium/low), probe_weight, tenant_id, SLA_window.
2. Compute effective score = priority_weight * fidelity_multiplier / expected_tokens_per_probe.
3. Run candidate selection: try to place in same-region fast pool if score >= fast_threshold; otherwise steady; if no fit and probe is low-fidelity, schedule into bulk with sampling.
4. Perform cost check against probe class fidelity budget in eval-ledger; if budget overrun, enqueue for token-sampled run or reject with recommendation.

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...] 4. Perform cost check against probe class fidelity budget in eval-ledger; if budget overrun, enqueue for token-sampled run or reject with recommendation.


Allocation heuristics and thresholds (tunable knobs):
- priority_weight: P0=1.0 (critical), P1=0.6, P2=0.3, P3=0.1
- fidelity_multiplier: high=1.0, medium=0.6, low=0.2
- fast_threshold: 0.7
- steady_threshold: 0.3
- backfill_sampling_rate (for bulk): default 0.05 (5%)

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...] - backfill_sampling_rate (for bulk): default 0.05 (5%)


Cost and fidelity accounting:
- Eval-ledger records: slot_id, probe_manifest_id, start_ts, end_ts, tokens_processed, cost_estimate.
- Monthly billing export includes: tenant_id, probe_class, total_tokens, slot-weighted-cost.
- Fidelity budget alerts: generate when projected spend > 80% of allocated monthly fidelity budget for probe class.

Backfill and rebalancing policy:
- Trigger conditions: underutilization > 20% for 15m OR overload (queue depth > 2000).
- Backfill steps:
  1) Identify candidate low-priority low-fidelity probes for bulk migration.
  2) Apply progressive sampling ramp (0% -> 5% -> 25% over 30m) with telemetry gates.
  3) Monitor fidelity signal (response-stability-index, latency-tail) — abort if degradation > configured delta.
- Rebalancing (auto): every 10 minutes, eval-orch computes target distribution and migrates slots with live cutover window of 2 minutes.

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...] - Rebalancing (auto): every 10 minutes, eval-orch computes target distribution and migrates slots with live cutover window of 2 minutes.


Safety, privacy, and gating:
- All shadow traffic must be redacted by probe-fabricator before entering telemetry-ingest.
- Sensitive-probe flag triggers isolated shadow-mesh placement.
- Rollout gating: new topology rules must pass canary on 3 regional meshes for at least 24h with no fidelity or latency regression > 2x baseline.

Operational runbook: topology change flow
1) Draft topology change PR (config: pools, thresholds, weightings) and post to #eval-ops.
2) Create an experiment manifest in eval-orch with scope=canary (regions: us-west-2, eu-central-1).
3) Dry-run allocation for 24h (simulation mode) and gather: slot fill curves, projected ledger spend, telemetry delta.
4) If dry-run passes, enable canary with 1% of global probe volume for 24h.
5) Escalation criteria (immediately rollback if any fires):
   - fidelity_delta > 3% for P0 probes
   - latency tail (p99) increase > 150ms for fast slots
   - spending rate > 2x projection
6) Gradually increase canary (1% -> 5% -> 20% -> 100%) with 12h observation windows between steps.

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...] 6) Gradually increase canary (1% -> 5% -> 20% -> 100%) with 12h observation windows between steps.


Example slotting config (YAML example):
---
slot_pools:
  - name: fast
    gpu: a100-40gb
    concurrency: 50
    weight: 5
    allocation_threshold: 0.7
    owner: eval-platform@redwood.ai
  - name: steady
    gpu: a100-40gb
    concurrency: 200
    weight: 2
    allocation_threshold: 0.3
    owner: eval-platform@redwood.ai
  - name: bulk
    gpu: 2xa100
    concurrency: 1000
    weight: 1
    sampling_rate: 0.05

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...]     weight: 1
    sampling_rate: 0.05

    owner: eval-platform@redwood.ai
---

Probe manifest fields (example):
- probe_id: pc-2026-485
- probe_class: longcontext-coherence-v2
- priority: P1
- fidelity_required: high
- expected_tokens: 1200
- sensitive: false
- owner: search-team@redwood.ai

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
section_header: Telemetry and alerting knobs to configure for topology rollouts
text:
[...] - owner: search-team@redwood.ai


Telemetry and alerting knobs to configure for topology rollouts:
- response-stability-index (RSI) delta alert: > 2x baseline -> sev-2
- p99 latency increase (fast slots): > 150ms -> sev-2
- fidelity-budget burn rate: projected > 80% -> paging to cost-ops
- slot-fill anomaly (< 10% utilization across 30m) -> investigate potential overprovisioning

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
section_header: Debugging checklist (when probes are not scheduled or fail)
text:
[...] - slot-fill anomaly (< 10% utilization across 30m) -> investigate potential overprovisioning


Debugging checklist (when probes are not scheduled or fail):
1) Check eval-orch allocation logs for allocation_reject reasons.
2) Query eval-ledger for probe_manifest_id and cost budget status.
3) Inspect shadow-mesh metrics for slot health (oom_count, preemptions, avg_qps).
4) If privacy gating blocked allocation, confirm probe redaction passed (probe-fabricator logs).

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...] 4) If privacy gating blocked allocation, confirm probe redaction passed (probe-fabricator logs).


Rollout example (real case study):
- Goal: reduce cost for continuous functioncall-stability probes by 18% while preserving p99 latency for P0 canaries.
- Action: introduced a new steady pool with 1.5x concurrency and weight=1.8, enabled progressive bulk sampling for low-fidelity sub-slices.
- Result: after staged rollout (1% -> 20% -> 100%), observed 17.9% cost reduction and p99 for P0 canaries remained within 8ms of baseline.

Ownership and contacts:
- Owners: eval-platform team (primary), evaluation-costing (secondary).
- Oncall: eval-sre rotation (pager: #eval-oncall).
- Escalation path: eval-oncall -> eval-platform -> platform-sre -> eng-lead.

Appendix A — tuning knobs and recommended defaults:
- fast_threshold: 0.7
- steady_threshold: 0.3
- backfill_sampling_rate: 0.05
- fidelity_alert_pct: 80
- rebalancing_interval_minutes: 10
- canary_step_windows: [24h, 12h, 12h]

Appendix B — links to related docs:
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quant-shadow-fidelity-simulator-2026
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/telemetry-driven-prompt-surgeon-and-repair-playbook-2025
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/eval-ledger-and-billing-export-spec-2026 (internal)

title: Eval mesh topology and slotting cookbook
text:
[...] - /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/eval-ledger-and-billing-export-spec-2026 (internal)


Change log:
- 2025-11-12: initial draft (Aisha Patel)
- 2025-12-10: added backfill sampling recipe and cost accounting section (Miguel Santos)
- 2026-01-15: tightened rollout escalation thresholds and added example YAML (Lena Zhou)

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
text:
Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies

Summary:
This document describes the evaluation protocol used to measure and qualify model variants and serving configurations for latency-sensitive workloads. The goal is to produce an operational signal that directly informs traffic routing and automatic fallback policies in Redwood's smart routing layer. It covers metric definitions, workload synthesis, test harness configuration, pass/fail thresholds, and an incident triage runbook for latency regressions.

Background and motivation:
- Many customer-facing features require a sub-100ms median response time or predictable tail latency to maintain UX.
- Model choices (size, quantization), runtime kernel selection, KV-cache hit patterns, and batching behavior all interact to create latency regressions that are invisible to token-count-only evaluations.
- Automated routing and fallback should be driven by a reproducible eval signal that closely mimics production request characteristics.

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
text:
[...] - Automated routing and fallback should be driven by a reproducible eval signal that closely mimics production request characteristics.


Scope:
- Applies to: model variants intended for low-latency endpoints (chat-completion, single-turn generation with short context), runtime tunables that affect single-request latency (batching, prefetch, kernel/path selection), and routing/fallback policy parameters.
- Excludes: large-batch throughput-only benchmarks, long-context (> 8k) full-document generation stress tests (covered by separate bench).

Key definitions and metrics:
1) Per-request P95 latency: end-to-end time from API ingress to first-byte-of-response (ms).
2) Cold-start latency: latency when no warm worker has the model loaded or KV cache is empty (ms).
3) Steady-state latency: median and P95 after warmup period (ms).
4) Amplification factor (AF): ratio of P95 under production-mixed traffic to P95 under synthetic single-client baseline. Used to quantify sensitivity to interleaving workloads.
5) SLO delta: change in SLO attainment compared to baseline model/config (percentage points).

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
section_header: Minimum SLOs used by this protocol
text:
[...] 5) SLO delta: change in SLO attainment compared to baseline model/config (percentage points).


Minimum SLOs used by this protocol:
- Target median (50th): <= 80ms for short-chat endpoints (configurable per product).
- Target P95: <= 180ms.
- Acceptable cold-start: <= 400ms.
- AF threshold for auto-rollback: > 1.5 triggers investigation; > 2.0 blocks rollout.

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
text:
[...] - AF threshold for auto-rollback: > 1.5 triggers investigation; > 2.0 blocks rollout.


Test corpus and traffic profiles:
- Primary prompt set: 1k anonymized short-turn prompts sampled from production (max tokens: 64). Prompts are stratified by client product, region, and token distribution.
- Adversarial prompt subset: 200 prompts with long-token prefixes, syntactic noise, and content that stresses decoding loops.
- Synthetic client mix: a multi-client interleaving generator with 50 concurrent virtual clients, configurable think-time distribution (exponential mean = 200ms).
- Background noise profile: 10% of requests are long-context (> 2048 tokens) to simulate multi-tenant interference.

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
text:
[...] - Background noise profile: 10% of requests are long-context (> 2048 tokens) to simulate multi-tenant interference.


Harness configuration (example YAML):
---
harness: latency-sensitivity-v1
clients: 50
think_time_ms_mean: 200
prompt_set: s3://redwood-evals/latency/short-chat-1k-v2.json
model_variant: rwinf-7b-q8
batch_policy: continuous
kv_cache_mode: enabled
warmup_requests: 500
measurement_window_sec: 600

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
section_header: collect: [p50, p90, p95, p99, mean, throughput, cpu, gpu_util, memory]
text:
[...] warmup_requests: 500
measurement_window_sec: 600

collect: [p50, p90, p95, p99, mean, throughput, cpu, gpu_util, memory]
---

Execution steps (numbered procedure):
1. Provision test cluster matching the target deployment class (hosted/dedicated/private). Use the infra profile documented in /confluence/eng-infra/gpu-fleet-and-capacity/test-cluster-profiles.
2. Pre-load models and warm KV caches using warmup_requests. Confirm worker processes are up and the model is resident in GPU memory.
3. Start the synthetic client generator with the primary prompt set and background noise profile.
4. Run for measurement_window_sec (recommended 10 minutes for small variants, up to 30 minutes for larger configs).
5. Aggregate metrics and compute AF relative to baseline. Baseline is the currently promoted model+config for the route.
6. If metrics cross thresholds defined under 'Pass/fail criteria', mark the run as failed and trigger the triage workflow.

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
section_header: How latency signals map to routing and fallback policies
text:
[...] 6. If metrics cross thresholds defined under 'Pass/fail criteria', mark the run as failed and trigger the triage workflow.


Pass/fail criteria and gating rules:
- Hard fail: P95 > 200ms OR AF > 2.0 OR cold-start > 600ms.
- Soft fail (requires engineering review): P95 between 180-200ms OR AF between 1.5-2.0 OR median degradation > 10%.
- Canary gating: For canary rollouts, combine this harness signal with production real-user telemetry for 1 hour before advancing traffic if any soft-fail metrics present.

How latency signals map to routing and fallback policies:
- If hard fail: route traffic away from the model variant immediately (automatic rollback) and mark variant unhealthy in the routing registry.
- If soft fail: reduce traffic to variant by 50% and add a retry fallback (fast-path to baseline variant) for requests with strict latency budget.
- If AF indicates susceptibility to multi-tenant interference: set conservative batching knobs and enable per-customer QoS isolation in Dedicated deployments.

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
text:
[...] - If AF indicates susceptibility to multi-tenant interference: set conservative batching knobs and enable per-customer QoS isolation in Dedicated deployments.


Quantization and kernel interaction notes:
- Always run the harness for both float and quantized builds. Quantization may improve median latency but can worsen P95 due to kernel path mismatches.
- Record kernel selection logs (kernel_id, torch/llvm path) and include in eval artifact. Use the profiling-playbook to map slow paths back to kernel candidates.

Artifact and reporting requirements:
- Each run must produce a single artifact tarball with: harness-config.yaml, raw-metrics.jsonl, aggregated-summary.csv, kernel-selection.log, and reproducer-script.sh. Upload to s3://redwood-evals/artifacts/latency/<run-id>/.
- Create a Confluence run summary page using the template in /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/run-summary-template. Required fields: run-id, cluster-profile, model-variant, pass/fail, owner, link to artifacts.

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
section_header: Incident triage runbook (when a promoted variant shows a latency regression in prod)
text:
[...] - Create a Confluence run summary page using the template in /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/run-summary-template. Required fields: run-id, cluster-profile, model-variant, pass/fail, owner, link to artifacts.


Incident triage runbook (when a promoted variant shows a latency regression in prod):
1. Immediate mitigation (first 5 minutes): increase routing weight to baseline model; if autos-rollback is enabled, the router will reduce traffic automatically. Notify on-call via Slack channel #prod-latency.
2. Collect traces: enable request tracing for affected route and collect first-byte timing, kernel selection trace, and KV-cache hit logs for a 5-minute window.
3. Re-run harness with identical cluster profile and collect AF. If AF reproduces: escalate to serving-runtime oncall.
4. If AF does not reproduce: capture production request samples (privacy-preserving sampling; redact PII) and run through the harness with identical concurrency/think-time distribution.
5. Postmortem: owner creates a postmortem page including root cause hypothesis, timeline, and remediation (config change, kernel patch, model rollback). Link to regression triage playbook and schedule a follow-up regression test after remediation is applied.

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
text:
[...] 5. Postmortem: owner creates a postmortem page including root cause hypothesis, timeline, and remediation (config change, kernel patch, model rollback). Link to regression triage playbook and schedule a follow-up regression test after remediation is applied.


Ownership and responsibilities:
- Eval owner: Applied ML Evaluations team (primary contact: Elena Gupta).
- Serving runtime owner: eng-serving-runtime (contacts: Marcus Lee).
- Platform & routing owner: eng-platform (contacts: Tara O'Connell).
- On-call: per the oncall rota in /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/oncall-rotations.

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
section_header: Checklist before promoting a model/config (pre-promotion verification)
text:
[...] - On-call: per the oncall rota in /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/oncall-rotations.


Checklist before promoting a model/config (pre-promotion verification):
- [ ] Pass latency harness under target cluster profile
- [ ] Quantized and float builds evaluated and artifacts uploaded
- [ ] Canary: run 50% traffic for 1 hour with live telemetry and no hard-fail events
- [ ] Kernel-selection review for uncommon slow-paths
- [ ] Owner sign-off and release notes recorded

Appendix: example CLI commands
- Run harness locally (short smoke test):
  ./eval-harness run --config harness-config.yaml --dry-run=false --warmup 100 --window 60

- Upload artifacts:
  aws s3 cp artifacts/<run-id>.tar.gz s3://redwood-evals/artifacts/latency/<run-id>/ --acl private

- Query aggregated metrics (jq):
  cat aggregated-summary.csv | jq -R -s -c 'split("\n") | .[1:-1] | map(split(","))'

title: Latency Sensitivity Eval Protocol for Routing and Fallback Policies
text:
[...]   cat aggregated-summary.csv | jq -R -s -c 'split("\n") | .[1:-1] | map(split(","))'


References and related pages:
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/inference-readiness-eval-matrix-and-promotion-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-platform/rollouts/canary-deployment-guidelines

Revision history:
- 2025-11-15: created initial draft (Elena Gupta)
- 2025-12-10: added AF metric and gating rules after review
- 2026-02-03: tightened P95 thresholds for short-chat endpoints and clarified artifact requirements

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook

Summary
-----
This playbook describes a federated evaluation orchestration pattern and an accompanying privacy budgeting model for running distributed evals across Dedicated, Private, and Hosted environments. It targets teams that need to: (1) evaluate models across siloed customer workloads, (2) preserve differential privacy and data minimization guarantees, and (3) preserve capacity and cost SLOs while obtaining statistically useful signals.

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...] This playbook describes a federated evaluation orchestration pattern and an accompanying privacy budgeting model for running distributed evals across Dedicated, Private, and Hosted environments. It targets teams that need to: (1) evaluate models across siloed customer workloads, (2) preserve differential privacy and data minimization guarantees, and (3) preserve capacity and cost SLOs while obtaining statistically useful signals.


Motivation
----------
As Redwood supports federated and private deployments, evaluators increasingly need aggregated signals from heterogenous tenants without moving raw user data to central storage. A federated eval approach reduces data movement, improves regulatory compliance, and enables per-tenant calibration. However, it introduces new operational concerns: orchestration latency, heterogeneous compute availability, skewed sample distributions, and the need to account for privacy "budget" consumption across many small experiments. This document defines patterns, configuration, and an operational runbook.

Goals and non-goals
-------------------
- Goals:
  - Provide a repeatable orchestration pattern for federated evals that works across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployment modes.
  - Define a privacy budgeting abstraction that teams can use to reason about cumulative information leakage from repeated evals.
  - Offer scheduling and cost controls so evals do not violate production SLOs or incur unexpected costs.
- Non-goals:
  - This playbook is not a full cryptographic DP primer. Use this alongside the Security team's DP guidance for formal proofs.
  - We do not prescribe model training workflows. This targets evaluation and telemetry only.

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...]   - We do not prescribe model training workflows. This targets evaluation and telemetry only.


Key concepts and terminology
----------------------------
- Federated Eval Node: customer-controlled evaluator instance that runs workload probes against a model and emits sanitized, aggregated metrics.
- Coordinator: centralized orchestration service that schedules federated eval jobs, collects metadata, and enforces privacy budgets and quotas.
- Privacy Budget: a per-customer and per-experiment quota (measured in epsilon-like units) that limits how much information an eval can reveal about raw user data.
- SLO Guardrails: limits on CPU/GPU utilization, request QPS, and latency impact so eval runs do not impair production serving.

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...] - SLO Guardrails: limits on CPU/GPU utilization, request QPS, and latency impact so eval runs do not impair production serving.


High-level architecture
-----------------------
1) Coordinator service (runs in Redwood control plane):
   - Registers eval specs, assigns job IDs, tracks budgets, aggregates metadata, and stores per-job manifests.
   - API endpoints: POST /v1/fed-evals (create), GET /v1/fed-evals/{id} (status), POST /v1/fed-evals/{id}/complete (submit summary).
2) Federated Eval Agent (runs in customer Dedicated/Private environment or on sandboxed host):
   - Pulls job manifests, executes probes locally, computes local aggregates, applies noise/sanitization, and submits only summary artifacts.
3) Result Aggregator (control plane):
   - Accepts per-node summaries and computes final population-level metrics, confidence intervals, and cross-tenant reports.

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...]    - Accepts per-node summaries and computes final population-level metrics, confidence intervals, and cross-tenant reports.


Orchestration and scheduling policies
------------------------------------
- Priority classes:
  - critical: must-run for compliance or safety, preemptible only with explicit owner approval.
  - standard: routine evals for performance/cost monitoring.
  - exploratory: low-priority experiments with strict cost caps.
- Backoff and retry policy:
  1) Coordinator schedules up to 3 attempts spaced with exponential backoff (initial 30s).
  2) If nodes report resource contention, Coordinator reschedules to a softer window (off-peak) and reduces sample size.
- Capacity-aware placement:
  - Agents report available GPU minutes and QPS headroom. Coordinator adjusts sample sizes and selects nodes that meet requested headroom thresholds.

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...]   - Agents report available GPU minutes and QPS headroom. Coordinator adjusts sample sizes and selects nodes that meet requested headroom thresholds.


Privacy budgeting model
----------------------
- Each federated eval job declares: privacy_label (purpose), sensitivity_level (low | medium | high), and requested_epsilon (numeric string).
- Budget enforcement rules:
  - Per-tenant rolling budget window: 30 days.
  - Default epsilon caps: low=0.5, medium=0.2, high=0.05.
  - Coordinator rejects or asks for explicit owner approval when requested_epsilon exceeds remaining budget.
- Sanitization primitives recommended on Agents:
  - Local aggregation with counts and clipped contributions.
  - Add Laplace/Gaussian noise calibrated to requested_epsilon.
  - Thresholding: require minimum local sample size Nmin before releasing any summary.

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...]   - Thresholding: require minimum local sample size Nmin before releasing any summary.


Operational runbook: day-of-run checklist
----------------------------------------
1) Create eval manifest with fields: job_id, probes[], privacy_label, requested_epsilon, target_nodes[].
2) Coordinator validates resource quotes and budgets -> returns scheduled window and sample_size suggestion.
3) Notify tenant SRE/owner and request acceptance if requested_epsilon > 0.1.
4) Agents pull manifest and perform a dry-run smoke check (3 probe samples) against a read-only test endpoint.
5) Execute full run. Agents upload compressed summary (sha256) to aggregator.
6) Aggregator computes population-level statistics and stores job artifact.
7) Auto-generate report and flag if any metric deviates > 3 sigma from baseline.

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...] 7) Auto-generate report and flag if any metric deviates > 3 sigma from baseline.


Incident / failure modes and mitigation
--------------------------------------
- Node fails mid-run:
  - Mitigation: Coordinator marks node as partial and rebalances sample to other participants while respecting budget.
- Excessive latency on Agents causing production impact:
  - Mitigation: Immediate agent throttling, abort job, alert oncall, rollback probe frequency.
- Privacy budget overrun attempt:
  - Mitigation: Coordinator refuses job and records audit event; require manual override with compliance sign-off.

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...]   - Mitigation: Coordinator refuses job and records audit event; require manual override with compliance sign-off.


Metrics, SLOs, and dashboards
-----------------------------
- Orchestration SLOs:
  - Job acceptance latency: 99th percentile < 4s for manifest validation.
  - Completion success rate: >= 97% across scheduled nodes for standard jobs.
- Privacy and correctness KPIs:
  - Budget violation rate: target 0%.
  - Fraction of reports passing sanity checks: >= 99%.
- Recommended dashboards:
  - Fed Eval Overview (jobs by privacy_label, budget consumption heatmap).
  - Agent Health (node CPU/GPU headroom, last heartbeat).
  - Aggregation Integrity (submitted SHA256 vs canonical).

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...]   - Aggregation Integrity (submitted SHA256 vs canonical).


Configuration examples
----------------------
- Eval manifest (YAML):
```yaml
job_id: fed-eval-2026-187
privacy_label: usage-telemetry
sensitivity_level: medium
requested_epsilon: "0.15"
priority: standard
target_nodes: [tenant-az-1, tenant-eu-3]
probes:
  - name: answer-quality-probe
    samples: 200
    path: /v1/generate
    timeout_ms: 5000
```

- Agent local constraints table:
| key | description | default | notes |
|-----|-------------|---------|-------|
| gpu_headroom_minutes | Minutes of GPU available for evals | "120" | Agents must report as integer minutes |
| min_local_sample | Minimum local samples before reporting | "50" | Prevents low-N leakage |
| noise_mechanism | Laplace|Gaussian | "Gaussian" | Calibrated per requested_epsilon |

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...] | noise_mechanism | Laplace|Gaussian | "Gaussian" | Calibrated per requested_epsilon |


Privacy review checklist (pre-launch)
------------------------------------
1) Confirm privacy_label and business justification.
2) Ensure requested_epsilon <= remaining budget or signed exception exists.
3) Verify Nmin and clipping thresholds configured on agents.
4) Confirm audit logging to central control plane is enabled.
5) Register the job in the security review queue if sensitivity_level == high.

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
section_header: Appendix: common CLI commands and snippets
text:
[...] 5) Register the job in the security review queue if sensitivity_level == high.


Appendix: common CLI commands and snippets
------------------------------------------
- Submit a federated eval (example):
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/fed-evals -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d @manifest.yaml

- Retrieve job status:
  curl https://api.redwood.ai/v1/fed-evals/fed-eval-2026-187/status

- Quick sanity check (agent):
  python -m redwood.fed.agent \n    --manifest manifest.yaml \n    --dry-run

title: Federated Eval Orchestration and Privacy Budgeting Playbook
text:
[...]   python -m redwood.fed.agent \n    --manifest manifest.yaml \n    --dry-run


Related docs and references
---------------------------
- See Eval Mesh and Workload Fingerprinting (internal) for node capability discovery.
- Security DP primer and the Security team's DP evidence repo for formal guarantees.

Ownership and next steps
------------------------
- Owners: Applied ML Eval Platform (primary), Security (privacy budget policy), Eng SRE (agent health).
- Next steps (roadmap):
  - Q2 2026: automated budget reconciliation API and per-tenant quota UI.
  - Q3 2026: integrate cryptographic aggregation primitives (secure aggregation) as opt-in.

Contact
-------
If you have questions about implementing federated evals or need an exception for privacy budgets, contact Maya Patel (maya.patel@redwood.ai) or raise an issue in #eval-platform on Slack.

title: Shadow Consistency Checker and Stochastic Seed Policy
text:
Shadow Consistency Checker and Stochastic Seed Policy

This playbook defines the Shadow Consistency Checker (SCC) and the stochastic seed policy used for repeatable, low-latency shadow validation. It standardizes sampling, seeding, metric computation, and alert thresholds for detecting subtle regressions introduced by quantization, kernel changes, or prompt rewrites.
Context: Shadow deployments are a primary mechanism for detecting regressions before promotion to production. However, nondeterminism (random seeds, sampling noise, tokenization nondeterminism) and under-specified sampling policies have produced intermittent false positives and missed regressions. The SCC is designed to make shadow comparisons deterministic where possible, quantify stochastic variance, and produce actionable triage signals.
Applies to: all model shadow runs executed by the eval harness for hosted, dedicated, and private deployments. Includes shadow runs tied to: quantized artifacts, runtime kernel upgrades, decoder-only architecture changes, prompt-layer rewrites, and batch/latency policy changes. Excludes: synthetic unit tests for tokenizers and low-level kernel microbenchmarks (handled by runtime team).
- Shadow run: a replication of production traffic routed to candidate model(s) without affecting user-visible output.
- Deterministic seed: a chosen PRNG seed selected per probe to minimize sampling variance.
- SCC delta: a signed metric describing difference between baseline and candidate (see metrics_and_thresholds).
- Owner: eval-harness team (primary contact: Anika Verma).
- Observability: eng-platform (dashboards, traces).
- On-call triage: applied-ml oncall rotation.
- Release authority: model-onboarding product owner for promotions.
- Reviewers: runtime, quantization, infra SRE for any SCC alerts classified high severity.

title: Shadow Consistency Checker and Stochastic Seed Policy
section_header: Primary metrics (computed per-probe and aggregated by slice)
text:
[...] - Release authority: model-onboarding product owner for promotions.
- Reviewers: runtime, quantization, infra SRE for any SCC alerts classified high severity.

Primary metrics (computed per-probe and aggregated by slice):
1) Semantic Delta (BLEU/ROUGE/embedding-cosine difference normalized) — threshold: alert if median delta > 0.035 across probes for 1 hour.
2) Deterministic Variance (inter-seed variance of metric) — acceptable if variance < 0.005; flag if > 0.01.
3) Latency Shift (P95 candidate - P95 baseline) — soft warn at +15ms, alert at +50ms.
4) Token Failure Rate (generation aborts, malformed JSON) — soft warn at 0.05%, alert at 0.2%.
5) Response Stability Index (RSI): aggregated stability score used by quality guardrails; regression if RSI drop > 7 points.
All thresholds are configurable per-customer slice; default global thresholds above apply to platform-level promotions.
1) Probe selection: use combined prioritized-probe list (production-derived probes + targeted adversarial probes). Default sample size: 10k probes per 24h per slice; minimum 1k for rarely-seen slices.
2) Seeding: each probe receives a stable deterministic seed derived from probe_id and epoch (seed = fnv1a128(probe_id + epoch)). For stochastic operations internal to the model runtime, the SCC requests the runtime to initialize PRNG using the provided seed via /v1/internal/start-seed header.
3) Epoch cadence: daily epochs (00:00 UTC) for broad coverage; micro-epochs (1-hour rolling) for high-risk changes (quantization/critical kernel patches).
4) Sampling distribution: stratified by intent-slice, length-bin, and frequency-decile to ensure long-context and low-frequency corner cases are evaluated.
5) Probe annotation: each probe must carry metadata: intent, slice, source (prod/annotated), expected_tokens, and json-schema (if structured).

title: Shadow Consistency Checker and Stochastic Seed Policy
text:
[...] 4) Sampling distribution: stratified by intent-slice, length-bin, and frequency-decile to ensure long-context and low-frequency corner cases are evaluated.
5) Probe annotation: each probe must carry metadata: intent, slice, source (prod/annotated), expected_tokens, and json-schema (if structured).

Shadow run steps (checklist):
1) Preflight: verify candidate artifact provenance and quant-annotations.
2) Seed injection: include header X-SCC-Seed: <seed> for each request.
3) Run shadow traffic through the shadow sequencer with mirroring factor 1:1 for functional probes and 1:0.1 for heavy-cost probes.
4) Collect token-level telemetry: token_latencies, kv_cache_hit_rate, prng_seed_hash, and model_logs.
5) Post-run aggregation: compute per-probe metrics and inter-seed variance.
6) SCC compares baseline vs candidate with bootstrapped confidence intervals (default 1000 bootstrap samples).
7) Generate badge: PASS / WEAK_REGRESSION / STRONG_REGRESSION based on metric thresholds.
8) If STRONG_REGRESSION, open a ticket in 'applied-ml-regression' with payload: probe-slice, top 10 probes with highest delta, seed values, and link to trace.
9) If PASS, annotate artifact with SCC:pass:<epoch> and allow next-stage promotion if other gates are green.
- Dashboard: SCC Overview (owner: eng-platform). Panels: pass rate, RSI trend, top delta probes, P95 latency shift heatmap, seed collision rate.
- Alerts:
  * SCC-STRONG-REGRESSION: triggered when STRONG_REGRESSION count > 50 probes in 1h — Severity: P2 — paging to applied-ml oncall + runtime oncall.
  * SCC-LATENCY-REGRESSION: P95 shift > 50ms for top 5 slices — Severity: P1 if customer impact high.
  * SCC-HIGH-VARIANCE: inter-seed variance > threshold for any core slice — Severity: P3 — email to eval-harness squad.
- Telemetry tags: include scc_epoch, scc_seed, scc_slice, model_artifact_id, quant_profile_id.
Promotion gating: artifact may be promoted to production only when: SCC badge == PASS for 3 consecutive epochs OR PASS with WEAK_REGRESSION if risk acceptance form is signed by product and runtime.
Rollback criteria: immediate rollback if production RSI drop > 10 points within 30 minutes, or Token Failure Rate > 0.3% sustained for 15 minutes.
Canary policy: small canary (1% traffic) for 2 hours after promotion with accelerated SCC checks (micro-epochs every 15 minutes).
Example seed header: curl -X POST https://shadow-sequencer.internal/v1/generate -H 'Authorization: Bearer <key>' -H 'X-SCC-Seed: 0x9f8b7c2d' -d '{"probe_id":"p-2314","input":"Summarize the article" }'
Sample bootstrap command for SCC metric: python scc/compute_delta.py --baseline baseline.json --candidate candidate.json --bootstrap 1000 --output delta_report.json
Sample returned JSON snippet (flattened): {
  "probe_id": "p-2314",
  "seed": "0x9f8b7c2d",
  "semantic_delta": "0.041",
  "rsi": "72",
  "p95_latency_ms": "152"
}
Runbook CLI for triage: ./scripts/scc_triage --ticket create --art artifact_id --epoch 2026-03-10 --attach delta_report.json
Known limitations: deterministic seeding cannot eliminate all sources of nondeterminism: GPU kernel fp rounding, multithreaded scheduler order, and third-party tokenizer nondeterminism. For high-confidence checks, pair SCC with microbench kernel-level determinism tests.
Change log: 2026-01-15 initial draft published; 2026-02-20 added RSI integration; 2026-03-10 updated latency thresholds and canary cadence.
References: internal RFC: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/latency-sensitivity-eval-routing-fallback-protocol-2026, quality guardrails RSI spec.

title: Evaluation lifecycle for Private & Dedicated deployments: integration, promotion, and monitoring playbook
text:
Evaluation lifecycle for Private & Dedicated deployments: integration, promotion, and monitoring playbook

A cross-functional playbook describing the end-to-end evaluation lifecycle for models promoted to Redwood Dedicated or Private deployments. Covers integration points with the eval harness, deployment acceptance gates, post-promotion observability, regression triage triggers, and rollback criteria tailored for constrained/private environments.
Purpose

This playbook defines the evaluation lifecycle and operational controls required to safely promote and operate models in Redwood Dedicated and Private deployments. It is intended for applied-ML engineers, release engineers, SRE, and solutions engineers who onboard customer-specific inference workloads.

Scope

- Applies to any model variant promoted from staging to Dedicated (reserved capacity) or Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments.
- Covers integration with the eval harness, prompt set validation, quantization/kv-cache stability checks, and monitoring/alerting templates.
- Does NOT replace deployment infra runbooks (see eng-private-deployments/* for network and packaging specifics).

Key principles

1) Evaluate in-situ: run a representative eval harness against the exact runtime (quantization, kernel set, sequence length distributions) that will be used in the target deployment.
2) Define acceptance gates that combine quality, latency, and stability—do not rely solely on aggregate metrics.
3) Observe for asymptotic regressions post-promotion with an elevated monitoring window before relaxing to standard SLOs.
4) Automate rollbacks where conservative criteria are violated; require human triage for borderline failures.

Pre-promotion checklist (must pass all items)

- Model artifact verification:
  - SHA256 of model files matches signed artifact manifest.
  - Config (tokenizer, special_tokens, model config) validated against onboarding matrix.
- Eval harness integration:
  - Run full eval harness with the target runtime binary and config. Include unit prompt sets (safety, hallucination, regressions), customer prompt samples, and stress workloads.
  - Generate the standardized report: eval-report-<model>-<runtime>-<date>.json and upload to artifact store.
- Latency & throughput baselines:
  - P50/P95/P99 token latency baselines measured at target batch sizes and sequence lengths.
  - Maximum sustainable throughput (tokens/sec) at 95th percentile latency budget.
- Quantization & KV-cache checks (if applicable):
  - Run quant-stability-suite (see CLI snippet below) across representative context lengths (0, 512, 2048).
  - Validate KV-cache warm-up behavior: 1000 warm-up sequences, then measure subsequent steady-state latencies and output KL divergence vs FP16 run.
- Regression detection vector:
  - Compare top-level metrics vs baseline using the regression-delta thresholds in the table below.

Acceptance thresholds (example - teams should tune per workload)

| Metric | Acceptance threshold | Measurement window | Action if violated |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Quality Delta (composite score: accuracy+hallucination penalty) | <= 2% relative drop | 1000 eval prompts | Block promotion; open triage | 
| Embedding cosine change (mean) | <= 0.015 absolute | 2000 samples | Block promotion; run embedding recalibration | 
| P95 latency | < 1.6x baseline (target infra) | 1 hr synthetic traffic | Block promotion / capacity review |
| Token error rate (malformed tokens) | < 0.1% | 10k tokens | Block promotion |
| KV-cache hit stabilization | Reach >85% hit rate within 200 warm-up sequences | Warm-up run | Investigate caching policy/priming |

Automated CLI snippets

- Run quant stability suite:
  ```bash
  redwood-eval quant-stability --model ./artifacts/model-v3.q4_0 --runtime /opt/redwood/runtime-dedicated --prompts ./tests/kv-cache-warmup.txt --out ./reports/qstab.json
  ```

- Launch in-situ eval harness against private runtime (example uses ephemeral test node in customer VPC):
  ```bash
  eval-harness run --runtime-endpoint https://10.0.0.12:8443 --auth-token-file ~/.secrets/customer-token --suite canonical,safety,customer-sample --out ./reports/promote-eval.json
  ```

Promotion procedure (recommended automated pipeline)

title: Evaluation lifecycle for Private & Dedicated deployments: integration, promotion, and monitoring playbook
text:
[...] 
Promotion procedure (recommended automated pipeline)

1) CI gating: push artifact to staging. CI triggers eval-harness + quant-stab + perf-bench suites.
2) If all automated gates pass, create a promotion candidate in the deployment service and run a canary (1-2 nodes) for 24 hours with synthetic traffic at 10% of expected peak.
3) Canary monitoring window: elevated alerts enabled (see alerting templates). If any critical alert fires, automatic rollback to previous model; open regression ticket.
4) If canary is healthy, gradually ramp to target capacity with staged rollout (25% -> 50% -> 100%), each stage at least 2 hours or 10M tokens, whichever is later.

Post-promotion monitoring & elevated observation window

- Observation window: first 72 hours post-promotion are considered elevated. During this time:
  - Data retention: keep full request/response traces and KV-cache logs.
  - Sampling: increase prompt sampling to 5% of traffic routed to the promoted model for manual evaluation queues.
  - Alerts: use stricter thresholds (see alerting template).

Alerting templates (examples to add to monitoring stacks)

- Critical alert (SEV 1): Quality composite drop > 3% relative over 1 hour OR P95 latency > 2x baseline for > 10 minutes. Pager to SRE + applied-ML oncall.
- High alert (SEV 2): Embedding mean shift > 0.03 OR token error rate > 0.5% for 30 minutes. Notify product owner + release engineer.
- Medium alert: Elevated KV-cache miss rate > 30% for 1 hour. Create ticket for performance team.

Alert routing and ownership

- Pager rota: SRE primary -> applied-ML secondary during first 72 hours. After 72 hours, revert to standard routing.
- Incident ownership: the team that owns the model (owner_team in model manifest) is primary for triage. SRE owns rollback execution and infra traces.

Regression triage flow (on alert or automated detection)

1) Triage lead assigned (automatically by pager duty runbook).
2) Collect artifacts: eval-report, perf-bench outputs, recent traces (last 6 hours), sampling set (200 examples flagged by monitoring).
3) Quick checks (first 30 minutes): verify artifact integrity, confirm whether problem exists in staging, compare kernel/driver versions between staging and prod nodes.
4) Isolation: if steady-state latency or quality regressions are observed, shift traffic to a compatible fallback model variant (automated via routing policy).
5) Deep diagnosis: run the regression-forensics suite (see related doc) and annotate failing prompts; open a regression ticket with label regression/severity.

Rollback criteria (automatic)

- Any SEV1 alert during promotion/canary should trigger automatic rollback.
- If more than 5% of sampled prompts show quality regressions beyond the acceptance threshold within 4 hours, rollback.
- If P95 latency exceeds 2x baseline for 20 consecutive minutes under measured load, rollback.

Sampled prompt policies for private deployments

- Customer prompt sampling: collect 1 in N requests (configurable, default N=200) but redact PII and store only model inputs and outputs for evaluation. Use the builtin redaction library before storage.
- Synthetic edge-case prompts: maintain a local prompt-store per deployment with 500 targeted adversarial prompts for regular replay tests.

Quantization-specific guidance

- For on-prem hardware with diverse kernels, always include a kernel compatibility matrix in the promotion artifact.
- When enabling aggressive int4 quantization: require a double-run regression check where outputs from the quantized path are compared against FP16 baseline using semantic and token-level metrics.
- If deterministic replay differs frequently, pin the runtime to the exact kernel revision used in successful evals.

Operational artifacts to attach to promotion ticket

- eval-report-<model>-<runtime>-<date>.json
- perf-baseline-<model>-<runtime>-<date>.csv
- quant-stab-report.json
- kernel-compat-matrix.yaml
- signed-artifact-manifest.txt

Related pages

- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/serving-simulation-eval-harness-2026 (integration example)
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/kv-cache-quant-stability-audit-playbook-2025 (stability checks)
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/model-regression-forensics-playbook-2026 (forensics runbook)

title: Evaluation lifecycle for Private & Dedicated deployments: integration, promotion, and monitoring playbook
text:
[...] - /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/kv-cache-quant-stability-audit-playbook-2025 (stability checks)
- /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/model-regression-forensics-playbook-2026 (forensics runbook)

Appendix: example monitoring rule (Prometheus-style pseudo-yaml)

```yaml
alert: EvalQualityRegression
expr: |
  (sum(rate(eval_quality_drop_total[15m])) / sum(rate(eval_checks_total[15m]))) > 0.03
for: 10m
labels:
  severity: page
annotations:
  summary: "Composite eval quality drop >3% over 15m"
  runbook: https://confluence.internal/runbooks/eval-quality-regression
```

Owner and contact list

- Playbook owner: Ava Chen (applied-ML infra) - ava.chen@redwood.ai
- SRE lead: Diego Ramirez - diego.ramirez@redwood.ai
- Quantization SME: Priya Menon - priya.menon@redwood.ai

Revision history

- 2026-01-12: Created (Ava Chen)
- 2026-02-05: Added quantization warm-up guidance and KV-cache thresholds (Priya Menon)
- 2026-03-02: Expanded regression triage flow and automated rollback criteria (Diego Ramirez)

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol

Summary
-------
This document defines a repeatable evaluation protocol, harness components, workload recipes, and triage playbook for measuring stability and fidelity of interactive streaming responses when the model invokes function/tool calls. The protocol is specifically targeted at cross-cutting failure modes introduced by quantization, KV-cache interactions, and continuous-batching in production serving. It is intended for applied-ML engineers, runtime owners, and SREs validating model promotions and platform changes that affect streaming or function-call behavior.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] This document defines a repeatable evaluation protocol, harness components, workload recipes, and triage playbook for measuring stability and fidelity of interactive streaming responses when the model invokes function/tool calls. The protocol is specifically targeted at cross-cutting failure modes introduced by quantization, KV-cache interactions, and continuous-batching in production serving. It is intended for applied-ML engineers, runtime owners, and SREs validating model promotions and platform changes that affect streaming or function-call behavior.


Scope
-----
- Model families: decoder-only & encoder-decoder topologies used in Redwood hosted/dedicated/private serving.
- Modes: streaming token-by-token output with optional function/tool call markers, non-streaming baseline for comparison.
- Interventions: quantization changes (8/4-bit paths), KV-cache warm/cold patterns, batching policy changes, streaming buffer/window tuning.
- Excludes: offline embedding-only workloads and strictly batch, non-interactive evaluation.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] - Excludes: offline embedding-only workloads and strictly batch, non-interactive evaluation.


Goals
-----
1) Detect and quantify stability regressions introduced by runtime changes that affect streaming partial outputs or function-call boundaries.
2) Measure end-to-end fidelity (intent and argument correctness) of function-call outputs across quantized/float variants.
3) Provide deterministic triage steps and thresholds for blocking promotions or triggering rollback.
4) Produce actionable artifacts (diffs, heatmaps, replay bundles) that developers can use to debug.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] 4) Produce actionable artifacts (diffs, heatmaps, replay bundles) that developers can use to debug.


Definitions
-----------
- Partial-stream divergence: token-level Levenshtein distance between prefix streams produced by baseline and candidate at matched prefix lengths.
- Call-boundary shift: a change in the token index where the model emits the function_call start token (or structured marker). Measured as delta in tokens.
- Argument fidelity: semantic equivalence score for the structured arguments in function calls (using domain-specific equality rules).
- Streaming latency SLOs: p50/p95 of time-to-first-byte and time-between-chunks measured at the API gateway.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
section_header: Use these primary metrics in the evaluation dashboard and for automated gating
text:
[...] - Streaming latency SLOs: p50/p95 of time-to-first-byte and time-between-chunks measured at the API gateway.


Metrics and thresholds
----------------------
Use these primary metrics in the evaluation dashboard and for automated gating:
- Partial-stream divergence (median per-example): acceptable < 0.08 normalized edit distance.
- Partial-stream divergence (95th percentile): acceptable < 0.20.
- Call-boundary shift: median < 3 tokens; 95th percentile < 12 tokens.
- Argument fidelity: exact-match >= 98% for deterministic argument schemas; semantic-score >= 94% for freeform args.
- Streaming latency regressions: p95 time-to-first-byte regression < 25% vs baseline; p95 inter-chunk delay < 200ms.
- Regression alert: any single high-severity sample where partial-stream divergence > 0.6 or argument fidelity < 80% must be considered a blocking failure.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] - Regression alert: any single high-severity sample where partial-stream divergence > 0.6 or argument fidelity < 80% must be considered a blocking failure.


Test harness architecture
------------------------
1) Controller: orchestrates experiments, schedules traffic, applies environment overrides (quant flags, cache priming).
2) Workload generator: replay-based and synthetic workload engines that can produce interleaved streaming clients and function-call stubs.
3) Proxy recorder & replayer: records wire-level chunk timing and payloads (timestamps, chunk sizes, and markers) to enable deterministic replays.
4) Analyzer: computes token-level diffs, boundary shifts, semantic scoring of structured outputs, and produces per-example artifacts.
5) Dashboard: stores metrics, example traces, heatmaps, and links to replay bundles in object storage.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] 5) Dashboard: stores metrics, example traces, heatmaps, and links to replay bundles in object storage.


Workload generation recipes
--------------------------
- Real-traffic shadow: sample 72 hours of production streaming sessions (anonymized) with a 1% sampling rate; keep conversation context lengths stratified (0-128, 128-512, 512+ tokens).
- Synthetic edge-probe set: 1,200 queries designed to exercise: long-running partial outputs, multi-step tool invocation, nested JSON output, ambiguous prompt completions, and adversarial truncation.
- Cold-cache churn: attack pattern that alternates sessions across 10,000 unique user prefixes to simulate worst-case KV cache miss rate.
- Burst streaming: simulate 500 concurrent lightweight streaming clients issuing 1–3 requests each, with varied inter-chunk arrival rates.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] - Burst streaming: simulate 500 concurrent lightweight streaming clients issuing 1–3 requests each, with varied inter-chunk arrival rates.


Experiment config template (YAML)
--------------------------------
```yaml
experiment_name: interactive-stream-fcall-canary-2026-02
baseline_model: redwood-1.0-fp16
candidate_model: redwood-1.0-8bit
workload_sets:
  - name: real_shadow
    duration: 72h
    sample_rate: 0.01
  - name: synthetic_edge_probes
    iterations: 10
interventions:
  - quant_flags: [enable_8bit_kernels]
  - kv_cache: [warm, cold, partial]
  - batching_policy: [continuous, fixed-window]
metrics_to_record: [partial_stream_divergence, call_boundary_shift, argument_fidelity, ttfb_p95, inter_chunk_p95]
analysis_outputs: [per_example_diff, top_100_regressions, heatmap_stream_divergence, replay_bundle_url]
```

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] ```


Run procedure
-------------
1) Prepare baseline: pin baseline model and capture a 24-hour shadow snapshot to serve as the canonical baseline if not already available.
2) Configure candidate: deploy candidate runtime variant (quant or batching change) to an isolated staging cluster with identical routing rules.
3) Prime caches: run the "warm" phase for 1 hour to stabilize KV-cache state, then run the cold-cache phase by cycling unique prefixes.
4) Execute workload: start the controller; run real_shadow and synthetic_edge_probes concurrently for the configured duration.
5) Collect artifacts: ensure proxy recorder saves chunk timings, structured call outputs, and client-side partial streams to object storage.
6) Analysis pass: run analyzer; generate diffs and heatmaps; compute gating metrics.
7) Decide: compare metrics to thresholds and follow triage guidance below.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] 7) Decide: compare metrics to thresholds and follow triage guidance below.


Result analysis and artifacts
----------------------------
- Per-example diff bundle (plain text + token-level annotations).
- Call argument table (baseline vs candidate) with semantic similarity scores.
- Heatmap: examples (rows) vs prefix-length (columns) colored by normalized edit distance.
- Timing waterfall per example showing time-to-first-byte and inter-chunk deltas.
- Replay bundle (runnable): includes recorded traffic + runtime recipe to reproduce the trace in a local staging cluster.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
section_header: If the experiment shows any metric violating the regression alert thresholds
text:
[...] - Replay bundle (runnable): includes recorded traffic + runtime recipe to reproduce the trace in a local staging cluster.


Regression triage playbook
-------------------------
If the experiment shows any metric violating the regression alert thresholds:
1) Reproduce the top-10 failing examples with the proxy replayer locally against both baseline and candidate; save logs.
2) Run a narrow micro-benchmark toggling only one intervention at a time (e.g., quant flag only, then cache pattern only, then batching policy only).
3) If the failure is tied to quantization: run the candidate with mixed-precision (FP16 tokens for logits accumulation) and run targeted per-layer profiling.
4) If the failure is tied to KV-cache: compare warm vs cold traces; inspect cache keys, sequence offsets, and KV eviction events.
5) If the failure is tied to streaming chunkization: instrument the runtime to capture internal chunk buffer boundaries and confirm whether function_call markers align with chunk boundaries.
6) Produce a root-cause note with: minimal repro, diffs, profiler flame snapshots, and recommended rollback or mitigation (e.g., fall back to fp16 for model X, increase chunk buffer to 64KB, or adjust batching to preserve deterministic tokenization).
7) Postmortem: tag the release and link to the CI job that introduced the change; propose permanent guardrail (see guardrail suggestions below).

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] 7) Postmortem: tag the release and link to the CI job that introduced the change; propose permanent guardrail (see guardrail suggestions below).


Guardrail suggestions (examples)
------------------------------
- Safety gate: any candidate that increases call-boundary shift at the 95th percentile by > 5 tokens must fail automated promotion.
- Canary window: require a minimum 7-day rolling run of the streaming canary with no blocking regressions before promoting quantized runtime to dedicated clusters.
- Auto-fallback rule: when argument_fidelity < 90% for more than 1% of traffic in the canary, automatically route 10% of traffic to baseline model until triage completes.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] - Auto-fallback rule: when argument_fidelity < 90% for more than 1% of traffic in the canary, automatically route 10% of traffic to baseline model until triage completes.


Ownership and cadence
---------------------
- Primary owner: applied-ml infra team (OWNERS: @applied-ml-infra).
- Secondary owner: serving-runtime team for instrumentation and replay tooling.
- Run cadence: automated nightly canary runs for non-production changes; full 72-hour runs for any model candidate promotion or runtime kernel change.
- On-call: severity-2 incident for blocking regressions discovered in production within the first 72 hours after release.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
section_header: Start a local replay against candidate runtime (example)
text:
[...] - On-call: severity-2 incident for blocking regressions discovered in production within the first 72 hours after release.


Appendix: example CLI commands
-----------------------------
# Start a local replay against candidate runtime (example)
redwood-eval run --experiment interactive-stream-fcall-canary-2026-02 --target candidate --replay-bundle gs://eval-replays/bundle-20260210.tar.gz

# Compute token-level diff summary
redwood-eval analyze --input gs://eval-replays/bundle-20260210 --output gs://eval-results/interactive-canary-20260210 --metrics partial_stream_divergence,call_boundary_shift

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] redwood-eval analyze --input gs://eval-replays/bundle-20260210 --output gs://eval-results/interactive-canary-20260210 --metrics partial_stream_divergence,call_boundary_shift


Frequently asked questions (short)
---------------------------------
Q: Why test streaming and function calls together?
A: Function-call boundaries are often emitted mid-stream; chunkization and truncation interact with where structured markers appear, which is a source of high-severity regressions.

Q: Can this protocol be used for private deployments?
A: Yes — the harness supports private replay bundles and local staging clusters. Masking/anonymization hooks must be enabled for customer data.

title: Interactive streaming + function-call stability eval protocol
text:
[...] A: Yes — the harness supports private replay bundles and local staging clusters. Masking/anonymization hooks must be enabled for customer data.


Change log
----------
- 2025-11-12: Created initial draft (Aisha Park).
- 2026-01-15: Added heatmap outputs and gating thresholds (Omar Chen).
- 2026-02-10: Finalized triage steps and canary cadence; published (Lucia Morales).

title: Operational probe economics and priority contract
text:
Operational probe economics and priority contract

Overview

This document defines the operational contract between evaluation owners (model teams, product teams, and the eval platform) for probe prioritization, sampling budgets, and the per-probe cost accounting model used by the Redwood eval harness. It codifies: how probes are scored for execution priority, how budget allocation works across workloads (hosted/private/dedicated), and the runbook for triaging cost-driven failures or regressions that originate from probe scheduling and sampling.

Scope

- Applies to all probes scheduled through the eval harness (shadow, continuous, and targeted stress probes)
